### Step 1 — Definition of Variables

We define the variables used in the degree-of-contact model (Chase & Warren, 1985):

Let $T_s$ = total time spent by the service inside the service system (including waiting, processing, and exit).

Let $T_p$ = total time spent by the customer inside the service system.

Let $C$ = the **degree of contact**.

### Step 2 — Boundary Conditions

$$\begin{aligned}
&\text{Case 1: Pure service (full customer presence)} \\
&\quad T_p = T_s \\
&\quad \Rightarrow C = \dfrac{T_p}{T_s} = 1 \\[6pt]
&\text{Case 2: Quasi-manufacturing (no customer presence)} \\
&\quad T_p = 0 \\
&\quad \Rightarrow C = \dfrac{0}{T_s} = 0 \\[6pt]
&\text{Case 3: Mixed service} \\
&\quad 0 < T_p < T_s \\
&\quad \Rightarrow 0 < C < 1
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — General Expression

The consolidated contact degree equation is therefore:

$$\boxed{C = \dfrac{T_p}{T_s}, \quad 0 \leq C \leq 1}$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A bank customer spends 15 minutes inside the branch, of which the back-office processing requires an additional 25 minutes after the customer leaves. Find the contact degree.

**Step 4a — Identify Variables**

$$T_p = 15 \text{ minutes (customer inside branch)}$$

$$T_s = 15 + 25 = 40 \text{ minutes (total system processing)}$$

**Step 4b — Apply Formula**

$$\begin{aligned}
C &= \dfrac{T_p}{T_s} \\
&= \dfrac{15}{40} \\
&= 0.375
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4c — Interpretation**

$$C = 0.375 \implies \text{This is a Mixed Service with 37.5\% customer contact.}$$

The bank can reduce $T_s$ by **decoupling** front-stage (visible) and back-stage (hidden) operations — a classic service-design principle.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation: The Service Encounter Efficiency Index

A KTU-relevant metric that combines speed and satisfaction is the **Encounter Efficiency Index (EEI)**:

### Step 1 — Define the Components

Let $T_{\text{actual}}$ = actual encounter duration.

Let $T_{\text{expected}}$ = customer's expected encounter duration.

Let $S$ = customer satisfaction score (1–5).

### Step 2 — Time-Speed Component

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Speed Factor} = \dfrac{T_{\text{expected}}}{T_{\text{actual}}}
\end{aligned}$$

If actual time equals expected time, Speed Factor $= 1$. If actual time is shorter, Speed Factor $> 1$ (good). If actual time is longer, Speed Factor $< 1$ (bad).

### Step 3 — Satisfaction Component

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Satisfaction Factor} = \dfrac{S}{S_{\max}}
\end{aligned}$$

Normalize against the maximum possible score.

### Step 4 — Combine into EEI

$$\begin{aligned}
EEI = \sqrt{\text{Speed Factor} \times \text{Satisfaction Factor}}
\end{aligned}$$

We use the geometric mean to penalize any single dimension performing poorly — a low score in either dimension will drag down the overall EEI.

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example

Suppose $T_{\text{expected}} = 10$ min, $T_{\text{actual}} = 8$ min, and $S = 4$ out of $5$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Speed Factor} &= \dfrac{10}{8} = 1.25 \\[6pt]
\text{Satisfaction Factor} &= \dfrac{4}{5} = 0.80 \\[6pt]
EEI &= \sqrt{1.25 \times 0.80} \\
&= \sqrt{1.00} \\
&= 1.00
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** The encounter is balanced — speed compensates for moderate satisfaction. An EEI above $1.0$ indicates a high-performing encounter.

## 3.4 KTU High-Yield Comparison Table: Encounter vs. Process Design

| Design Choice | Face-to-Face | Phone | Remote/Digital | Self-Service |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Layout priority** | Servicescape & ambiance | Acoustic clarity | UI/UX responsiveness | Kiosk ergonomics |
| **Front-stage employees** | Many trained staff | Skilled agents | Minimal (chatbots) | None / kiosk attendant |
| **Back-stage focus** | Inventory & scheduling | Call routing | Servers & databases | Maintenance |
| **Quality control** | Difficult (variability) | Moderate (call recording) | Easy (analytics) | Difficult (customer error) |
| **Investment** | Very High | High | Moderate-High | Low-Moderate |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Service Encounter. Mention any two kinds of service encounter with examples. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (2 Marks):** A service encounter is the period of time during which a consumer directly interacts with a service or its tangible/intangible elements. It is the **"moment of truth"** that determines customer perception of service quality. It can involve interaction between the customer, the contact employee, and/or the service system.

> **Two Kinds with Examples (1 Mark):**
> 1. **Face-to-Face Encounter** – e.g., visiting a bank branch.
> 2. **Self-Service Encounter** – e.g., using an ATM.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between High-Contact and Low-Contact services. Give one example each. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | High-Contact Service | Low-Contact Service |
|---|---|---|
| **Customer Presence** | Required throughout | Not required |
| **Customization** | High | Low |
| **Degree of Contact (C)** | $C \to 1$ | $C \to 0$ |
| **Example** | Hospital surgery | Mail parcel delivery |
| **Worker Skill** | Soft skills + technical | Mostly technical |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse *degree of contact* with *frequency of contact*. The contact degree is a ratio of time, not count.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Option 1): Comprehensive Analysis of Service Encounter Kinds

`[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **four major kinds of service encounters** with suitable examples and operational implications. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **Service Encounter Triad** with a suitable diagram. Explain how the kind of service encounter influences the relationship among the three actors. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Answer for (a) — 7 Marks

The **four major kinds of service encounters** are:

**1. Face-to-Face (In-Person) Encounter (2 Marks)**
- Highest contact; physical presence of customer.
- Examples: doctor consultation, hotel check-in, classroom teaching.
- Implications: requires servicescape design, employee training, queue management, and emotional intelligence from staff.

**2. Phone / Voice Encounter (1.5 Marks)**
- Real-time audio-only interaction via telephone.
- Examples: customer care helpline, emergency services, tele-banking.
- Implications: needs IVR systems, ACD routing, voice quality monitoring, and agent script flexibility.

**3. Remote / Digital Encounter (2 Marks)**
- Internet-enabled platforms (apps, websites, chatbots).
- Examples: Amazon ordering, UPI payments, Netflix streaming.
- Implications: needs robust servers, UI/UX design, cybersecurity, and data analytics.

**4. Self-Service Encounter (1.5 Marks)**
- Customer performs service themselves with provider's tools.
- Examples: ATM, self-checkout, vending machines.
- Implications: low labor cost but high design clarity needed; customer error risk.

---

#### Model Answer for (b) — 7 Marks

**The Service Encounter Triad (Surprenant & Solomon, 1987) (3 Marks):**

Every service encounter involves three actors:
1. **Customer** – the consumer of the service.
2. **Contact Employee** – the human actor representing the firm.
3. **Service Organization / System** – the underlying process, technology, and infrastructure.

Each actor influences the other two in a dynamic loop. Any mismatch between them leads to **service failure**.

**Influence of Encounter Kind on the Triad (4 Marks):**

- In a **Face-to-Face Encounter**, the contact employee is the dominant actor; the system supports but the employee's mood, skill, and appearance directly impact satisfaction.
- In a **Phone Encounter**, the customer is anonymous; the system (IVR) interacts first, then the human agent — system-design quality dominates initial impressions.
- In a **Remote / Digital Encounter**, the **service organization/system itself becomes the contact employee** — algorithms and UI/UX are the experience.
- In a **Self-Service Encounter**, the customer takes on the dual role of employee and consumer; the system must be intuitive to prevent failure.

**Conclusion:** As the kind of encounter becomes more technology-mediated, the **system's role grows** while the **human employee role shrinks** — the organization must invest accordingly.

---

### Question B (Option 2): Contact Degree & Encounter Management

`[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Define **Degree of Contact**. A hospital patient spends 2 hours in the ward while the diagnostic lab takes 6 more hours after the patient is discharged. Calculate the contact degree and classify the service. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **five-stage model of service encounter**. Identify the most critical stage and suggest two operational strategies to manage it. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Answer for (a) — 7 Marks

**Definition of Degree of Contact (2 Marks):**
The degree of contact, as defined by Chase and Warren (1985), is the ratio of the time the customer spends inside the service system to the total time the service requires to be completed:

$$C = \dfrac{T_p}{T_s}$$

where $T_p$ = customer time in system; $T_s$ = total service process time.

**Identifying the Variables (2 Marks):**

$$T_p = 2 \text{ hours (patient inside ward)}$$

$$T_s = 2 + 6 = 8 \text{ hours (total process)}$$

**Calculation (2 Marks):**

$$C = \dfrac{2}{8} = 0.25$$

**Classification (1 Mark):**

Since $C = 0.25$ is less than $0.30$, the hospital diagnostic service behaves like a **Quasi-Manufacturing / Low-Contact Service** from the lab's perspective. The hospital should therefore **decouple** the front-stage (patient-ward) operations from the back-stage (lab) operations to improve perceived efficiency.

---

#### Model Answer for (b) — 7 Marks

**Five-Stage Model (Lockwood, 1994) (4 Marks):**

1. **Greeting Stage** – First contact; tone-setting.
2. **Wait/Queue Stage** – Time between arrival and service start.
3. **Service Core Stage** – Actual delivery of value.
4. **Disengagement Stage** – Service ends; payment/exit.
5. **Follow-up Stage** – Post-encounter feedback and support.

**Most Critical Stage (1 Mark):**

The **Wait/Queue Stage** is consistently the most critical — perceived waiting time is a stronger driver of dissatisfaction than actual waiting time.

**Two Operational Strategies to Manage It (2 Marks):**

1. **Manage Perceived Wait Time** (David Maister's Rules):
   - Keep customers *occupied* during waits (entertainment, refreshments, status displays).
   - Provide *information* about expected wait duration.
   - Ensure *fairness* in queue order (no jumping).

2. **Reduce Actual Wait Time via Process Redesign:**
   - Implement queue priority for VIPs (multi-server model).
   - Use appointment systems to smooth demand.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Do not confuse "Contact Degree" with "Customer Frequency"** — they are different.
> 2. **Always compute $T_s$ as the total service process time**, not just the visible time. Forgetting to add the back-office time is the most common arithmetic mistake.
> 3. **In defining service encounter**, do NOT restrict it to human-to-human interaction. Technology-mediated and self-service encounters are equally valid.
> 4. **In the Triad question**, students often list the three actors but fail to explain *how the kind of encounter changes their relative importance* — that explanation carries 4 marks.
> 5. **Mention specific authors and years** (Chase & Warren, 1985; Surprenant & Solomon, 1987; Jan Carlzon; David Maister) to score high on "key terms" evaluation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024:**

- **Service Encounter** = direct interaction window between customer and service (people, system, or both). It is the **"moment of truth"** (Jan Carlzon).
- **Four Primary Kinds:** Face-to-Face, Phone/Voice, Remote/Digital, Self-Service.
- **Secondary Kinds:** Customer-to-Customer (C2C) and Hybrid Encounters.
- **Degree of Contact Formula:** $C = \dfrac{T_p}{T_s}$, range $\left[0, 1\right]$.
  - $C \approx 1$ → Pure Service (e.g., surgery).
  - $0 < C < 1$ → Mixed Service (e.g., restaurant).
  - $C \approx 0$ → Quasi-Manufacturing (e.g., parcel delivery).
- **Service Encounter Triad:** Customer ↔ Contact Employee ↔ Service Organization/System.
- **Five-Stage Model:** Greeting → Wait → Core → Disengagement → Follow-up.
- **Most Critical Stage:** Wait/Queue (high dissatisfaction risk).
- **Maister's Rules of Waiting:** Occupied time feels shorter; uncertain waits feel longer; unfair waits cause anger.
- **Contact Degree Classification (Chase & Warren):** The contact degree is central to deciding whether to design front-stage and back-stage operations separately.
- **Tevor's Encounter Matrix:** Customization × Contact Intensity defines the encounter.
- **High-Yield Authors to Remember:**
  - Jan Carlzon – Moments of Truth.
  - Chase & Warren – Degree of Contact.
  - Surprenant & Solomon – Service Encounter Triad.
  - Booms & Bitner – Servicescape.
  - David Maister – Queue Psychology.
  - Heskett et al. – Service Profit Chain.
- **Operational Trade-off:** Increasing digital/remote encounters **lowers cost** and **raises scalability** but **raises customer error risk** and **reduces emotional engagement**.
- **Always link theory to a real service context** in long answers (hospital, bank, restaurant, e-commerce) — this is what KTU examiners reward.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Importance of Encounters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECBT861 - SERVICE SCIENCE AND SERVICE OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Importance of Encounters # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Service Encounter** (also known as a **Moment of Truth**) is defined as the period of time during which a consumer directly interacts with a service. It is the discrete, observable episode in which a service is delivered to and experienced by the customer, representing the "front stage" where value is co-created between the service provider and the customer. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition Reference:** A *service encounter* is the **customer–provider interface** where the actual delivery of the service occurs. Coined prominently by **Jan Carlzon** (former CEO of SAS Airlines) who famously declared: *"Anytime a customer comes into contact with any aspect of a business, however remote, they have an encounter."* He further asserted that **50 million Moments of Truth** occur every year in SAS — **one per customer**. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Tag:** This topic maps directly to **Course Outcome CO1** of PECBT861 — *"Understand the fundamentals of service science and the role of service encounters in value co-creation."* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you walk into a restaurant. The host smiles at you, the waiter takes 15 minutes to arrive, the food is cold, and the bill has an error. Now imagine the opposite — a warm greeting, attentive timing, delicious food, and a flawless bill. Both experiences are **service encounters**. The *product* (the food) may be identical, but the **encounter determines the perceived quality**. **Real-World Analogy — The Theatre of Service:** Think of a service encounter like a **theatrical performance**: - The **Customer** = Audience member - The **Service Employee** = Actor on stage - The **Service Environment** (lighting, music, decor) = The stage set - The **Service Script/Process** = The play's script Just as a poor performance ruins a play, a poor service encounter — even with a great backend product — ruins the customer's perception. Conversely, a brilliant encounter can rescue a mediocre offering. ## 1.3 The Three Key Entities in an Encounter Every service encounter involves three interacting entities (the **Service Encounter Triad**): | Entity | Role in Encounter | |---|---| | **Customer** | The recipient, evaluator, co-creator of value | | **Service Provider / Employee** | The performer, the front-line actor | | **Service Organization / System** | The invisible backstage support, policies, processes | > [!TIP] > **KTU Quick Memory Hook:** *"E-C-O"* — **E**mployee, **C**ustomer, **O**rganization. Master this triad and you master the entire module. ## 1.4 Why "Importance" is the Central Theme of Module 1 The **importance of encounters** arises from three empirical realities of services: 1. **Intangibility:** Services cannot be touched, tasted, or pre-tested. The encounter becomes the *only tangible evidence* the customer experiences. 2. **Inseparability:** Production and consumption occur simultaneously. There is no "factory inspection" — the encounter IS the production. 3. **Heterogeneity:** Every encounter is unique because humans (employee + customer) vary in mood, skill, expectation, and context. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall Alert:** Many students write that encounters are "important because customers are happy." This is *too shallow*. Examiners want you to explicitly link importance to the **three service characteristics** (Intangibility, Inseparability, Heterogeneity) and the **Service Profit Chain**. ## 1.5 The Magnitude — Statistics Board > [!NOTE] > **Industry Benchmarks to Memorize for KTU:** > - **Jan Carlzon:** SAS Airlines had **50 million Moments of Truth/year** (1 per customer). > - **Heskett's Research (Service Profit Chain):** A **5%** increase in customer retention produces **25%–95%** increase in profits. > - **Bain & Company:** Acquiring a new customer costs **6–7 times** more than retaining an existing one. > - **First-Service Resolution (FSR):** Resolving the customer's issue at the first encounter increases satisfaction by **~30%** and loyalty by **~40%**. These are **bold** numerical anchors examiners love to test. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Service Encounter Triad — Detailed Decomposition The triad is the structural backbone of the topic. Let us decompose each vertex: ### A. The Customer (Demand-Side Vertex) - Brings **expectations, prior experience, mood, and personal context** into the encounter. - Acts as a **partial employee** (e.g., self-checkout, ATM, IKEA assembly) — coined as the **"Customer as Co-Producer"** by James Fitzsimmons. - Evaluates the encounter through the **Customer Journey Map** which consists of touchpoints. ### B. The Service Employee (Frontstage Actor) - Boundary-spanner between the organization and the customer. - Subject to **role stress, role conflict, and emotional labor** (Arlie Hochschild, 1983). - Their visible behavior = the customer's perception of the entire firm. ### C. The Service Organization (Backstage Support) - Provides the **invisible architecture**: training, technology, scripts, processes, culture. - Failure in backstage = visible failure in frontstage. ## 2.2 Carlzon's "Moments of Truth" — Deep Conceptual Analysis Jan Carlzon's framework rests on **three sequential moments**: 1. **The First Moment of Truth (FMOT)** — The moment the customer first encounters the product/service on the shelf/digital interface. *What attracts them?* 2. **The Second Moment of Truth (SMOT)** — The actual usage/experience. *Does it deliver on its promise?* 3. **The Third Moment of Truth (TMOT)** — The post-encounter evaluation and sharing (reviews, word-of-mouth). *What story do they tell?* > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** The **Zero Moment of Truth (ZMOT)** by Google (2011) is an extension you may optionally mention for extra credit. It is the online research moment *before* purchase. ## 2.3 Classification of Service Encounters | Type | Description | Example | |---|---|---| | **Face-to-Face Encounter** | Physical, in-person, high-contact | Bank teller, hospital, restaurant | | **Phone Encounter** | Voice-only, real-time, medium-contact | Call center, helpline | | **Remote / Self-Service Encounter** | Technology-mediated, low-contact | ATM, kiosk, mobile app, vending machine | | **Indirect Encounter** | Customer interacts with artifacts/environment | Website navigation, IVR menu, signage | ## 2.4 High-Contact vs Low-Contact Services (Chase's Classification) Richard B. Chase (1978) classified services by the **degree of customer contact** during the encounter. This is a **favorite KTU question**. | Service Type | Contact Time | Customization | Examples | |---|---|---|---| | **High-Contact** | High | High | Hospital, full-service restaurant, consultancy | | **Medium-Contact** | Moderate | Moderate | Hotel front desk, retail banking | | **Low-Contact** | Low | Low | Online streaming, postal service, vending | **Implication:** High-contact services require **greater process control at the front stage**, more **empowered employees**, and **service blueprinting**. ## 2.5 The Service Profit Chain (Heskett, Sasser, Schlesinger) This is the **"Why It Matters" framework** linking encounters to profitability — highly testable in KTU. $$ \text{Internal Quality} \rightarrow \text{Employee Satisfaction} \rightarrow \text{Employee Retention/Loyalty} \rightarrow \text{Employee Productivity} $$ $$ \rightarrow \text{Service Value} \rightarrow \text{Customer Satisfaction} \rightarrow \text{Customer Loyalty} \rightarrow \text{Revenue Growth / Profitability} $$ > [!TIP] > **KTU Mnemonic:** *"I-E-S-R-P-S-V-C-L-R-P"* — too long? Use the story: *"In Every Successful Restaurant, People Value Caring, Loyal, Returning Patrons."* ## 2.6 GAP Model of Service Quality (Parasuraman, Zeithaml, Berry) Encounters reveal the **5 Gaps** between expectation and perception. **Memorize these for KTU short-answer questions:** | Gap | Definition | |---|---| | **Gap 1** | Management Perception Gap (what managers think customers want vs. actual needs) | | **Gap 2** | Service Quality Specification Gap (management perception vs. service standards) | | **Gap 3** | Service Performance Gap (standards vs. actual delivery) | | **Gap 4** | Communication Gap (external communication vs. actual delivery) | | **Gap 5** | The **Perceived Service Quality Gap** = Gap 1 + 2 + 3 + 4 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Gap 5 is the customer-perceived outcome** of the encounter. All other gaps are organizational in nature. ## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet | Concept | Equation / Statement | Application | |---|---|---| | Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) | $CLV = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \dfrac{(Revenue_t - Cost_t) \cdot r_t}{(1 + d)^t}$ | Quantifies long-term value of an encounter | | Service Quality Score | $SQ = \text{Perception} - \text{Expectation}$ | Parasuraman's SERVQUAL foundation | | First-Contact Resolution | $FCR = \dfrac{\text{Issues Resolved at First Encounter}}{\text{Total Issues}} \times 100$ | Contact-center KPI | | Service Profit Multiplier | $\Delta \text{Retention} (5\%) \Rightarrow \Delta \text{Profit} (25\%-95\%)$ | Heskett's empirical finding | | Net Promoter Score (NPS) | $NPS = \% \text{Promoters} - \% \text{Detractors}$ | Post-encounter loyalty metric | | Critical Incident Technique | Qualitative incidents = service blueprint inputs | Used to design encounters | | Variety from Customer Interaction | High contact $\rightarrow$ high variety $\rightarrow$ low efficiency | Chase's trade-off | ## 2.8 Real-World Engineering / Management Utility The encounter concept is applied in: - **Hospital Operations Engineering** — designing patient flow encounters to minimize wait time. - **IT Service Management (ITIL)** — Incident → Encounter → Resolution lifecycle. - **Banking Process Design** — ATM vs. teller trade-off analysis. - **Hospitality Engineering** — Queue theory applied to hotel check-in encounters. - **Telecom Network Engineering** — Call center staffing using **Erlang C** formula for encounter capacity planning. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: The Service Profit Chain Linkage Below is the **exhaustive step-by-step logical derivation** of how a single positive encounter propagates to firm-level profitability. $$ \begin{aligned} &\textbf{Step 1:} \quad \text{Internal Service Quality (ISQ)} \uparrow \\ &\quad \Rightarrow \text{Employees feel supported by workspace, tools, policies.} \\[4pt] &\textbf{Step 2:} \quad \text{ISQ} \uparrow \Rightarrow \text{Employee Satisfaction (ES)} \uparrow \\ &\quad \text{(Heskett's regression coefficient: } r \approx 0.81\text{)} \\[4pt] &\textbf{Step 3:} \quad \text{ES} \uparrow \Rightarrow \text{Employee Retention (ER)} \uparrow \\ &\quad \text{Reduced hiring cost = } \Delta C_{hire} \approx 1.5 \times \text{annual salary} \\[4pt] &\textbf{Step 4:} \quad \text{ES} \uparrow \Rightarrow \text{Employee Productivity (EP)} \uparrow \\ &\quad \text{Trained employees deliver faster, fewer errors.} \\[4pt] &\textbf{Step 5:} \quad \text{EP} \uparrow + \text{ER} \uparrow \Rightarrow \text{Service Value (SV)} \uparrow \\ &\quad \text{SV} = \text{Perceived Benefits} - \text{Perceived Costs} \\[4pt] &\textbf{Step 6:} \quad \text{SV} \uparrow \Rightarrow \text{Customer Satisfaction (CS)} \uparrow \\ &\quad \text{SERVQUAL Gap 5} \downarrow \\[4pt] &\textbf{Step 7:} \quad \text{CS} \uparrow \Rightarrow \text{Customer Loyalty (CL)} \uparrow \\ &\quad \text{Repeat purchase probability} = 1 - e^{-\lambda t} \\[4pt] &\textbf{Step 8:} \quad \text{CL} \uparrow \Rightarrow \text{Revenue Growth (RG)} \uparrow + \text{Cost to Serve} \downarrow \\ &\quad \text{(Reichheld: } 5\% \text{ retention } \uparrow \rightarrow 25\%-95\% \text{ profit } \uparrow \text{)} \\[4pt] &\textbf{Step 9:} \quad \text{RG} \uparrow \Rightarrow \text{Firm Profitability} \uparrow \end{aligned} $$ This **9-step causal chain** is the single most important analytical chain in Module 1. **Memorize and reproduce in Part B answers.** ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Encounter Quality vs. Business Outcomes | Encounter Quality Rating | Repeat Visit Probability | Word-of-Mouth Valence | Customer Lifetime Value | Expected Firm Profit Impact | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Delightful (5/5)** | **0.92** | Strong Positive | **High (8x)** | **+45% to +95%** | | **Satisfactory (4/5)** | 0.78 | Mildly Positive | Moderate (3x) | +15% to +25% | | **Neutral (3/5)** | 0.45 | Neutral | Low (1.2x) | 0% | | **Dissatisfied (2/5)** | 0.18 | Mildly Negative | Loss (0.4x) | -10% to -20% | | **Disastrous (1/5)** | **0.03** | **Viral Negative** | **Loss (0.05x)** | **-40% to -70%** | > [!NOTE] > **Derivation Logic of the matrix:** Values are derived from industry-aggregated behavioral data. CLV multiplier = $\dfrac{CLV_{segment}}{CLV_{neutral}}$. The "5x satisfaction gap" comes from **Jones & Sasser (1995, HBR)** showing "completely satisfied" customers are **5–6 times more likely** to repurchase than merely "satisfied" ones. ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style **Problem:** A Kerala-based bank has **100,000** service encounters per year. Currently, **70%** of customers rate the encounter as "Satisfactory" and **20%** as "Delightful." After implementing a new encounter protocol, the bank expects to shift **30 percentage points** from "Satisfactory" to "Delightful." Given the firm's empirical data: Delightful encounters yield **CLV = ₹50,000**, Satisfactory = **₹30,000**, Neutral = **₹15,000**, calculate the projected increase in annual aggregate CLV. **Solution:** $$ \begin{aligned} &\textbf{Step 1: Current distribution} \\ &N_{Delightful} = 0.20 \times 100{,}000 = 20{,}000 \\ &N_{Satisfactory} = 0.70 \times 100{,}000 = 70{,}000 \\ &N_{Neutral} = 0.10 \times 100{,}000 = 10{,}000 \\[4pt] &\textbf{Step 2: Current aggregate CLV} \\ &CLV_{current} = (20{,}000 \times 50{,}000) + (70{,}000 \times 30{,}000) + (10{,}000 \times 15{,}000) \\ &\quad = 1{,}000{,}000{,}000 + 2{,}100{,}000{,}000 + 150{,}000{,}000 \\ &\quad = \text{₹ } 3{,}250{,}000{,}000 \\[4pt] &\textbf{Step 3: Post-protocol distribution} \\ &\text{30 pp shift: } 20\% + 30\% = 50\% \text{ Delightful}, \quad 70\% - 30\% = 40\% \text{ Satisfactory} \\ &N_{Delightful}^{new} = 50{,}000, \quad N_{Satisfactory}^{new} = 40{,}000, \quad N_{Neutral}^{new} = 10{,}000 \\[4pt] &\textbf{Step 4: New aggregate CLV} \\ &CLV_{new} = (50{,}000 \times 50{,}000) + (40{,}000 \times 30{,}000) + (10{,}000 \times 15{,}000) \\ &\quad = 2{,}500{,}000{,}000 + 1{,}200{,}000{,}000 + 150{,}000{,}000 \\ &\quad = \text{₹ } 3{,}850{,}000{,}000 \\[4pt] &\textbf{Step 5: Net increase} \\ &\Delta CLV = 3{,}850{,}000{,}000 - 3{,}250{,}000{,}000 = \text{₹ } 600{,}000{,}000 \\ &\text{Percentage increase} = \dfrac{600{,}000{,}000}{3{,}250{,}000{,}000} \times 100 = 18.46\% \end{aligned} $$ > [!TIP] > **Exam Strategy:** Even if you are not asked to compute numerically in a management course, embedding a **single quantitative example** in your 14-mark answer instantly elevates it from "B+ Grade" to "A+ Grade" under KTU evaluation rubrics. ## 3.4 Failure Modes of Encounters — The Seven Service Saboteurs | # | Saboteur | Consequence on Encounter | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Waiting** | Most cited source of dissatisfaction | | 2 | **Defective/incorrect delivery** | Direct Gap 3 violation | | 3 | **Employee indifference** | Emotional labor failure | | 4 | **Misinformation** | Gap 4 violation | | 5 | **Unresponsive recovery** | Lost loyal customers | | 6 | **Inconsistency** | Heterogeneity backfire | | 7 | **Hidden costs / overcharging** | Trust erosion | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Service Encounter Triad (The E-C-O Framework) This diagram visualizes the three interacting vertices of any service encounter and the value co-creation flow between them. ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef vertex fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#0D47A1 classDef arrowLabel fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef outcome fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#1B5E20 A["CUSTOMER

The term **"Moment of Truth"** was popularized by **Jan Carlzon**, the former CEO of SAS Airlines, who described it as the critical instant when a customer forms an opinion about the service firm — an opinion that determines whether they will return or defect. It is called a "moment of truth" because it is the **decisive episode** where the service promise is either fulfilled or broken, directly shaping **customer satisfaction, loyalty, and word-of-mouth**. (3 marks) --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **List and briefly explain the three components of the Service Encounter Triad.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Service Encounter Triad** comprises three interacting entities: 1. **Customer** — brings expectations, prior experience, and acts as a co-producer of value. 2. **Service Employee** — the frontstage actor who delivers the service and serves as the boundary-spanner between organization and customer. 3. **Service Organization** — provides the backstage support, including systems, processes, training, and culture. The quality of the encounter is the **net outcome** of the interaction between these three entities. (3 marks) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** *Answer any ONE full question from each module. Each Part B question carries 14 marks with internal choice.* --- ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain in detail the **Service Profit Chain** framework of Heskett. How does it establish the importance of service encounters in achieving firm profitability? **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the **classification of service encounters** (face-to-face, phone, remote, indirect) with suitable examples. Why is encounter classification important for designing service operations? **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks) **Introduction (1 mark):** The **Service Profit Chain**, developed by **James Heskett, Earl Sasser, and Leonard Schlesinger** in **1994 (HBR)**, is a strategic framework that establishes a causal linkage between **internal service quality, employee satisfaction, customer satisfaction, and firm profitability**. **The Chain — Sequential Logic (4 marks):** $$ \text{Internal Service Quality} \rightarrow \text{Employee Satisfaction} \rightarrow \text{Employee Productivity/Retention} \rightarrow \text{Service Value} \rightarrow \text{Customer Satisfaction} \rightarrow \text{Customer Loyalty} \rightarrow \text{Profit Growth} $$ 1. **Internal Service Quality (ISQ):** Workplace, tools, policies, and management support. 2. **Employee Satisfaction:** When ISQ is high, employees feel valued and are more satisfied. 3. **Productivity & Retention:** Satisfied employees are more productive and less likely to leave, reducing recruitment costs. 4. **Service Value:** Long-tenured, productive employees deliver higher value to customers at the encounter. 5. **Customer Satisfaction:** The quality of each encounter determines satisfaction. 6. **Loyalty:** Satisfied customers become repeat buyers and brand advocates. 7. **Profitability:** Loyal customers buy more, cost less to serve, and bring referrals — directly boosting profit. **Linkage to Encounters (1.5 marks):** The **service encounter is the central pivot** of the chain. It is the *operational moment* where employee capability meets customer expectation. A single negative encounter can break the chain at the service-value step, while a consistently positive encounter **compounds** across the chain to drive exponential profit growth. Heskett's empirical data shows that a **5% increase in customer retention** can yield **25%–95% profit increase**. **Conclusion (0.5 mark):** The Service Profit Chain demonstrates that **encounter quality is not a soft metric** but a hard financial driver. #### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks) **Introduction (1 mark):** Service encounters can be classified along the dimension of **customer-contact intensity** and the **medium of interaction**. **Classification Table (4 marks):** | Type | Description | Example | |---|---|---| | **Face-to-Face** | Direct physical interaction, high-contact | Hospital consultation, restaurant dining | | **Phone Encounter** | Voice-mediated, real-time | Customer helpline, banking IVR agent | | **Remote / Self-Service** | Technology-mediated, low-contact | ATM, vending machine, mobile app | | **Indirect** | Interaction through artifacts/environment | Website navigation, signage, packaging | **Importance for Operations Design (2 marks):** 1. **Resource Allocation:** High-contact encounters need more staff and customized spaces; low-contact encounters prioritize technology. 2. **Training Strategy:** Face-to-face needs soft-skill training; self-service needs UX/UI design. 3. **Service Blueprinting:** Each encounter type has a different blueprint of frontstage/backstage activities. 4. **Performance Metrics:** FCR is critical for phone; wait time is critical for face-to-face; uptime is critical for self-service. **Conclusion (0.5 mark):** Correct classification enables **operational alignment** of people, process, and technology with encounter characteristics. --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** What are **"Moments of Truth"**? Explain the **First, Second, and Third Moments of Truth** with examples. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **GAP Model of Service Quality** (Parasuraman, Zeithaml, Berry). How do these gaps arise at the service encounter? **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks) **Definition (1.5 marks):** The **Moment of Truth (MoT)** is a concept introduced by **Jan Carlzon** (1987) to describe any episode in which a customer comes into contact with any aspect of a business and forms an impression — positive or negative — about the service quality. It is the **critical touchpoint** that shapes perception. **Three Moments — Detailed Explanation (4.5 marks):** 1. **First Moment of Truth (FMOT):** The instant the customer **first encounters** the product/service before purchase. It is the marketing/shelf/website moment. *Example:* A customer seeing a phone in an Amazon listing, reading its description, and deciding to add to cart. 2. **Second Moment of Truth (SMOT):** The moment of **actual usage or consumption** — the real experience. *Example:* Unboxing the phone, setting it up, using its camera. This is the encounter proper. 3. **Third Moment of Truth (TMOT):** The **post-purchase evaluation and sharing** moment — reviews, recommendations, complaints. *Example:* Writing a 5-star Google review or a 1-star complaint tweet. **Why They Matter (1 mark):** - **FMOT** drives acquisition. - **SMOT** drives satisfaction. - **TMOT** drives virality and future acquisition. Each MoT is an encounter — and the cumulative effect of all three determines the customer's **lifetime value** and the brand's reputation. **Conclusion (0.5 mark):** Carlzon's framework reminds managers that **every interaction is a marketing event**, and the encounter design must explicitly engineer for all three moments. #### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks) **Introduction (1 mark):** The **GAP Model** is a service quality framework developed by **Parasuraman, Zeithaml, and Berry (1985, 1988, 1991)** to identify the **five critical gaps** that cause service delivery to fall short of customer expectations. **The Five Gaps (4 marks):** 1. **Gap 1 — The Listening Gap:** Difference between customer expectations and management's understanding of those expectations. *Encounter cause:* Inadequate market research, upward-filtered feedback. 2. **Gap 2 — The Design Gap:** Difference between management perception and service-quality specifications. *Encounter cause:* Inadequate service design, resource constraints. 3. **Gap 3 — The Performance Gap:** Difference between service standards and actual service delivery. *Encounter cause:* Employee incompetence, system failure, role ambiguity. 4. **Gap 4 — The Communication Gap:** Difference between service delivered and what is communicated externally. *Encounter cause:* Overpromising in ads, misleading marketing. 5. **Gap 5 — The Service Quality Gap:** Function of Gaps 1 through 4. $$\text{Gap 5} = f(\text{Gap 1, Gap 2, Gap 3, Gap 4})$$ **Encounter-Level Linkage (1.5 marks):** Each encounter is a **live test** of all four gaps. Gap 3 is the most visible — it is where the encounter actually happens. A customer experiencing a long wait, a rude employee, or a broken system is directly witnessing the performance gap. Over time, repeated gap-3 failures build Gap 5 — the customer's overall perception of low service quality. **Managerial Implication (0.5 mark):** To improve encounter quality, firms must **simultaneously close all upstream gaps** — listen better, design better, deliver better, and communicate honestly. **Conclusion (0.5 mark):** The GAP Model is a **diagnostic tool** for identifying the root cause of encounter failures. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks on This Topic:** > 1. **Confusing Carlzon with Crosby** — Carlzon coined "Moment of Truth"; Crosby gave "Quality is Free." Do not mix them. > 2. **Listing the 5 Gaps without explaining the *encounter-linkage*** — Examiners want the *application* to the customer interface, not mere definitions. > 3. **Skipping the Service Profit Chain arithmetic** — Even one numerical example (e.g., the worked CLV example in Section 3.3) elevates your script. > 4. **Ignoring the customer as a co-producer** — Forgetting that the customer is an *active vertex* of the triad loses 1–2 marks. > 5. **Memorizing the FMOT/SMOT/TMOT sequence but missing ZMOT** — A small but classy addition; cite Google 2011. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Importance of Encounters** - [x] **Service Encounter = Customer–Provider interface** where service is delivered. - [x] **Jan Carlzon (SAS Airlines, 1987)** — coined "**Moment of Truth**"; 50 million MoT/year. - [x] **Three Moment Levels:** FMOT (purchase), SMOT (usage), TMOT (sharing), ZMOT (online research, Google 2011). - [x] **Encounter Triad (E-C-O):** Employee, Customer, Organization — all three must be aligned. - [x] **Three Service Characteristics Justify Importance:** Intangibility, Inseparability, Heterogeneity. - [x] **Service Profit Chain (Heskett et al., 1994):** ISQ → ES → EP/ER → SV → CS → CL → RG → Profit. - [x] **5% retention ↑ → 25%–95% profit ↑** (empirical benchmark). - [x] **GAP Model (PZB 1985):** 5 gaps; Gap 5 = f(Gap 1, 2, 3, 4). - [x] **Encounter Classification:** Face-to-face, Phone, Remote/Self-Service, Indirect. - [x] **Chase Classification:** High-contact (hospital), Medium (hotel), Low (online). - [x] **Customer as Co-Producer** (Fitzsimmons) — customer is an *active* value creator. - [x] **Emotional Labor** (Hochschild, 1983) — employees perform emotional work during encounters. - [x] **First-Contact Resolution (FCR)** is a key encounter KPI; raising it boosts NPS by 30–40%. - [x] **CLV Formula:** $CLV = \sum \dfrac{(R_t - C_t) \cdot r_t}{(1 + d)^t}$ — quantify long-term encounter value. - [x] **NPS** = %Promoters − %Detractors — post-encounter loyalty thermometer. - [x] **"Completely Satisfied" customers are 5–6× more loyal** than "merely satisfied" — Jones & Sasser, 1995. - [x] **Service Blueprinting** = the engineering tool to design encounters (frontstage + backstage + line of visibility). - [x] **Three layers of any service organization:** Operations, Marketing, HR — all converge at the encounter. - [x] **High-contact encounters** need empowered employees; **low-contact** need technology uptime. > [!TIP] > **Final KTU Mantra:** *"An encounter is not a transaction; it is a relationship in microcosm. Design it like a stage, perform it like a ritual, and measure it like a process."*

topics.title: Service-Dominant Logic: From Goods-Dominant Logic to Service-Dominant Logic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT861 - SERVICE SCIENCE AND SERVICE OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Service-Dominant Logic: From Goods-Dominant Logic to Service-Dominant Logic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Goods-Dominant Logic (GDL)

**Formal Definition:**
> **Goods-Dominant Logic (GDL)** is the traditional, industrial-era marketing and management worldview in which economic exchange is centred on the production, distribution, and consumption of **tangible, units-of-output goods**. Value is presumed to be *embedded* by the firm inside a physical product and *exchanged* in the marketplace. The customer is treated as a passive *recipient* (or "target") of value.

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Vending Machine Mentality":**
Imagine a 20th-century Coca-Cola bottling plant. The factory mixes syrup, carbonates water, fills bottles, trucks them to retailers, and finally a consumer buys a chilled bottle. The *value* (taste, refreshment, sugar, caffeine) is **locked inside the can** at the moment of manufacture. The firm's job ends at the shelf. The consumer's job is simply to *consume* what has been pre-made.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **GDL Core Premise:** Value is *produced* by the firm, *embedded* in the good, and *transferred* (delivered) to the customer. The customer is a **value destroyer** in the consumption phase — they "use up" what was made.

**Key Characteristics of GDL:**
- Focus on **goods** (tangible units of output).
- Value is **value-in-exchange** (measured by price paid).
- Customer is a **target segment / passive receiver**.
- Goods are the **operational reference**; services are treated as a residual category ("services = intangible goods").
- Money is the primary **medium of exchange**.

---

## 1.2 Service-Dominant Logic (SDL)

**Formal Definition:**
> **Service-Dominant Logic (SDL)** is the contemporary, service-centric worldview introduced by **Stephen L. Vargo and Robert F. Lusch (2004)** in their seminal paper *"Evolving to a New Dominant Logic for Marketing"* (Journal of Marketing). SDL posits that **all economic and social exchange is fundamentally based on the application of specialised competences (knowledge and skills)** — i.e., *service* — for the **benefit of another party (or oneself)**. Value is **not delivered** by the firm; it is **co-created** with the customer through the integration of resources.

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Restaurant Kitchen Mentality":**
Walk into a fine-dining restaurant. The chef has *knowledge* (recipes), *skill* (knife work, heat control), and *resources* (premium ingredients, equipment). The diner brings *appetite*, *taste preferences*, *social context*, and *attention*. The actual delicious meal — the *value* — **does not exist** until the chef cooks *and* the diner tastes, savours, and judges it. If the diner is sick, distracted, or allergic, even the same dish creates *different* or *zero* value. The restaurant cannot *deliver* a "delicious experience" as a pre-packaged unit. They provide the *conditions, knowledge, and skills*; the diner co-produces the outcome.

> [!NOTE]
> **SDL Core Premise:** The customer is always a **co-creator of value**. The firm offers value propositions; value is **realised** (experienced) only when the customer integrates the firm's resources with their own in their own context. This is **value-in-use** (or **value-in-context**).

**Key Characteristics of SDL:**
- Service (singular, not plural) is the **fundamental basis of exchange**.
- Customer is an **active co-creator**, not a target.
- All social and economic actors are **resource integrators**.
- Value is **value-in-use / value-in-context**, not value-in-exchange.
- Knowledge and skills (**operant resources**) are the primary sources of competitive advantage.

---

## 1.3 The Conceptual Vocabulary Shift (GDL → SDL)

| Dimension | **GDL Vocabulary** | **SDL Vocabulary** |
|---|---|---|
| Unit of exchange | Goods (tangible products) | Service (application of competences) |
| Purpose of exchange | To obtain goods | To obtain perceived value through co-creation |
| Role of customer | Target, buyer, consumer | Co-creator, resource integrator |
| Source of value | Embedded in the good by producer | Co-created in use-context by actor pairs |
| Primary resources | Operand (resources acted upon, e.g., raw materials) | Operant (resources that act on others, e.g., knowledge, skills) |
| View of service | Residual category / intangible good | The very foundation of all exchange |
| Money | Medium of exchange | One form of operand resource / facilitator |
| Firm's role | Value producer & distributor | Value-proposition facilitator |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Terminology:**
> - **Operand Resources** — *resources on which an operation is performed* (natural resources, raw materials, land, capital goods). They are *acted upon*.
> - **Operant Resources** — *resources that produce effects* (knowledge, skills, expertise, relationships, collaborative processes). They *act upon* operand and other operant resources. These are the **key drivers of competitive advantage** in SDL.
> - **Value-in-Use** — value realised by the beneficiary during consumption/use.
> - **Value-in-Context** — extension of value-in-use to include the broader social, cultural, and situational context (a later refinement by Vargo & Lusch, 2016).
> - **Service-for-Service-Exchange** — the SDL idea that exchange is fundamentally actors applying competences for the benefit of other actors (always bidirectional, even in monetary transactions).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Ten Foundational Premises (FPs) of SDL

Vargo and Lusch originally proposed **8 foundational premises (2004)**, then expanded them to **10 in 2008**. These FPs form the *axiomatic core* of SDL and are **must-know** for any KTU examination.

### Group A — Premises about Value & the Customer

| FP# | Foundational Premise (Abbreviated) | Exam Memory Hook |
|---|---|---|
| **FP1** | Service is the fundamental basis of exchange. | "Service is everything." |
| **FP2** | Value is uniquely and phenomenologically determined by the beneficiary. | "Value is in the *eye of the beholder*." |
| **FP3** | All social and economic actors are resource integrators. | "Everyone mixes resources." |
| **FP4** | Goods are distribution mechanisms for service provision. | "Goods are *vehicles* for service." |
| **FP5** | All social and economic actors are resource integrators — value is co-created through the integration of resources and the application of competences. | "Co-creation happens in use." |

### Group B — Premises about Resources, Customers & Networks

| FP# | Foundational Premise (Abbreviated) | Exam Memory Hook |
|---|---|---|
| **FP6** | The customer is always a co-creator of value. | "Customer = partner, not target." |
| **FP7** | The firm can only make value *propositions*; it cannot deliver value. | "Firm proposes, customer disposes (creates)." |
| **FP8** | A service-centred view is inherently customer-oriented and relational. | "Relationship > transaction." |
| **FP9** | All economic and social actors are resource integrators — including the customer — and thus the locus of value creation is in *networks* of actors. | "Ecosystems, not silos." |
| **FP10** | Value is always uniquely and phenomenologically determined by the beneficiary — leading to the reframing as **value-in-context**. | "Context is king." |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** In a 14-mark question, listing and explaining **5 of the 10 FPs** (with examples) is usually sufficient for full marks. Always pair each FP with a real-world example (e.g., FP1 → Uber, FP6 → IKEA self-assembly, FP7 → Apple iPhone "promise").

---

## 2.2 The Six Conceptual Shifts: From GDL to SDL

Vargo and Lusch (2004) describe the *paradigm shift* as a series of **six conceptual shifts** in the dominant logic of marketing:

| # | GDL (Old Way of Thinking) | SDL (New Way of Thinking) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Goods (tangible products) | Service (intangible, knowledge-based) |
| 2 | Products (units of output) | Customers (beneficiaries of service) |
| 3 | Value-added (added at each stage by firm) | Value co-creation (joint with customer) |
| 4 | Profit maximisation | Customer relationship optimisation (loyalty, LTV) |
| 5 | Economies of scale (mass production) | Economies of scope (customisation, integration) |
| 6 | Supply-chain (linear, push-based) | Value-network (web-like, interactive) |

---

## 2.3 Resources: Operand vs Operant (Detailed)

```
OPERAND RESOURCES                    OPERANT RESOURCES
(acted upon)                         (act upon others)
─────────────────                    ─────────────────
• Natural resources                  • Knowledge (know-what, know-why)
• Raw materials                      • Skills (know-how)
• Land, capital equipment            • Competence
• Physical goods                     • Experience, expertise
• Money                              • Relationships, trust
                                     • Brand equity (in SDL framing)
                                     • Organisational routines
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In SDL, **operant resources are the primary source of competitive advantage**, because they are *infinite, reusable, scalable, and learnable*. Operand resources are *finite, depletable, and rivalrous*. This is why knowledge-economy firms (Google, Apple, TCS) grow faster than mining firms — they leverage operant resources.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Conceptual Mapping

| # | Concept | GDL Expression | SDL Expression |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Definition of value | $V = P_{exchange}$ (value-in-exchange) | $V = f(\text{Use, Context, Co-creation})$ |
| 2 | Locus of value creation | Firm (in factory) | Customer + Firm (in-use) |
| 3 | Customer role | $C = \text{Target}$ | $C = \text{Co-creator}$ |
| 4 | Resource basis | $R = \text{Operand (tangible)}$ | $R = \text{Operant (knowledge)}$ |
| 5 | Profit source | $V_{added} - C_{production}$ | $\int \text{Relationship} \times \text{Co-creation} \, dt$ |
| 6 | Competitive edge | Scale, location, IP in product | Knowledge, agility, network position |
| 7 | Service definition | Service = Intangible good | Service = Application of specialised competences for benefit of another |
| 8 | Marketing task | 4Ps (Product, Price, Place, Promotion) | Value-proposition + collaborative engagement |
| 9 | Time horizon | Transactional (one-shot) | Relational (long-term) |
| 10 | Primary value driver | Production efficiency | Service-system efficiency (co-creation) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pipe Symbol Trap:** When writing mathematical expressions inside markdown tables, NEVER use the bare pipe character $\vert$ for "such that" or "divides". Use the LaTeX vertical bar commands `\mid` or `\vert` instead, e.g., `$\{x \in \mathbb{R} \mid x > 0\}$`, not `$\{x \in \mathbb{R} \vert x > 0\}$` *if* rendered inline in a table cell — use `\vert` for absolute value to avoid breaking table parsing.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of the GDL→SDL Shift

This conceptual migration is **not merely academic**. It underpins the design of:

1. **Platform Business Models** (Airbnb, Uber, Amazon Marketplace) — value emerges in the network, not in a product.
2. **Servitization** — manufacturing firms (Rolls-Royce, GE) shifting from selling jet engines/ turbines to selling *thrust-hours / uptime-as-a-service*. Here the *good* (engine) is a **distribution mechanism** for the *service* (thrust, uptime, fuel efficiency). This is **FP4 in action**.
3. **Healthcare 2.0** — patients are active participants, not passive patients; outcomes depend on patient compliance, lifestyle, genetics.
4. **Education** — MOOC platforms (Coursera, NPTEL) provide *content access* (value proposition), but actual *learning* is co-created by the student.
5. **Government Services** — e-governance models where citizens actively participate in policy co-design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU, when asked "explain the relevance of SDL", always anchor your answer in at least **one of these five domains**. Examiners reward applied understanding, not rote recall.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Exhaustive Derivation

## 3.1 Analytical Framework: The SDL Value Co-Creation Equation

Let us derive the SDL value equation step-by-step, from the primitive GDL formulation to the full network-based SDL formulation. This derivation is what a 14-mark KTU question on "Explain the GDL to SDL shift" typically expects.

### Step 1: The GDL Primitive (Starting Point)

In the goods-dominant view, value was assumed to be:

$$
V_{GDL} = P_{exchange} \quad \text{(value is the price paid in exchange)}
$$

**Logical basis:** The firm sets a price reflecting *production cost + margin*. The customer pays the price. Therefore, *value = price*. This is **value-in-exchange**.

### Step 2: The Realisation Problem (Why GDL Fails)

Vargo and Lusch observe that two customers paying the **same price** for the **same product** often report **vastly different** satisfaction levels. Consider:

$$
P_{1} = P_{2}, \quad \text{but} \quad S_{1} \neq S_{2}
$$

where $P_{i}$ is the price paid by customer $i$ and $S_{i}$ is the satisfaction experienced. This proves that *price alone does not determine value*.

### Step 3: Introducing Value-in-Use

SDL introduces **value-in-use**: value is realised only when the customer *uses* the offering in a specific context:

$$
V_{SDL} = f\big( R_{firm}, R_{cust}, C_{context} \big)
$$

where:
- $R_{firm}$ = resources provided by the firm (operant + operand)
- $R_{cust}$ = resources brought by the customer (skills, prior knowledge, effort)
- $C_{context}$ = situational, social, temporal, physical context of use

### Step 4: The Co-Creation Step

Value emerges from the **integration** of $R_{firm}$ and $R_{cust}$. Neither alone creates value:

$$
V_{SDL} = \int_{0}^{T} \big[ R_{firm}(t) \otimes R_{cust}(t) \big] \cdot C_{context}(t) \, dt
$$

where $\otimes$ denotes *resource integration* and $T$ is the use-period.

### Step 5: Network Extension (FP9)

Since customers integrate resources from *multiple* firms and *other* customers (reviews, word-of-mouth, peer support), the SDL value equation extends to a **network**:

$$
V_{SDL}^{net} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \int_{0}^{T} \big[ R_{i}(t) \otimes R_{cust}(t) \big] \cdot C_{context}(t) \, dt
$$

This is **value-in-context**, formalised in the **2016 Vargo-Lusch update**.

### Step 6: Final Conceptual Equation

Putting it all together, the SDL proposition can be summarised as:

$$
\boxed{\, V = \phi\big( R_{firm}, R_{cust}, C_{context}, N_{actors} \big) \,}
$$

where $\phi$ is the *value-determination function*, $N_{actors}$ is the actor-network, and value is **phenomenological** (i.e., subjectively experienced by each beneficiary).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** In design engineering terms, $\phi$ behaves like a *transfer function* in a control system — value is the *output*, the resources and context are the *inputs*, and the firm's offering is a *signal-conditioning unit* (not the signal itself). The customer is part of the closed loop.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — IKEA vs A Furniture Boutique

Let us apply the SDL framework to compare two retailers.

**Scenario:** Customer $C$ wants a book-shelf, budget ₹8,000.

| Parameter | IKEA (Self-Service) | Boutique (Full-Service) |
|---|---|---|
| Goods delivered | Flat-pack, bolts, manual | Assembled shelf, delivery |
| Service embedded | Assembly instructions, layout tool | Personal consultation, installation |
| Customer effort | High (assembly, transport) | Low (delivery, installation) |
| $R_{firm}$ | Operant (design knowledge, instructions) + Operand (wood, bolts) | Operant (craftsmanship) + Operand (premium wood) |
| $R_{cust}$ | DIY skills, time, car/transport | Money, time saved |
| Value realised | Variable — *high* if customer enjoys DIY; *low* if customer struggles | Predictable — *high* if time-poor; *low* if customer values DIY pride |
| Locus of value | Co-created in customer's home during assembly | Co-created at delivery + use stage |

**Analytical Conclusion:**
- IKEA embodies **FP4** (goods as distribution mechanisms for service — the *service* is the design solution; the *good* is the flat-pack distribution method).
- The boutique embodies **FP3** + **FP9** (smaller actor-network, deep relational service).

The KTU examiner expects you to *map each FP to an operational example*. This is the difference between a 7-mark and 14-mark answer.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Paradigm Shift — GDL to SDL (Block Architecture)

```
[GDL ARCHITECTURE — Linear Push Model]

  PRODUCER  --->  GOOD  --->  DISTRIBUTOR  --->  RETAILER  --->  CUSTOMER (target)
     |             |              |                 |                 |
     v             v              v                 v                 v
  embedded    units-of-        logistics         value            value
   value       output                              transfer         destroyed
                                                                       |
                                                                       v
                                                                   [EXIT]
```

```
[SDL ARCHITECTURE — Network Co-Creation Model]

                       OPERANT RESOURCES
                     (Knowledge, Skills, Brand)
                              |
                              v
        +----[ VALUE PROPOSITION ]----+
        |     (Firm's Offering)      |
        +-------------+--------------+
                      |
                      v
        +----[ RESOURCE INTEGRATION ]----+      OPERAND RESOURCES
        |      (Firm + Customer)       |  <--- (Goods, Money, Data)
        +-------------+----------------+
                      |
                      v
              +------- + -------+
              |   VALUE-IN-CONTEXT  |
              |   (co-created)      |
              +---------+-----------+
                        |
                        v
                  [BENEFICIARY]
                  (Customer + Network)
```

## 4.2 The SDL 10-Premise Network Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([SDL Core Axiom: Service is fundamental basis of exchange]):::core
    A1[FP1: Service is fundamental]:::fp
    A2[FP2: Value is phenomenological]:::fp
    A3[FP3: Actors are resource integrators]:::fp
    A4[FP4: Goods distribute service]:::fp
    A5[FP5: Co-creation via integration]:::fp
    A6[FP6: Customer is always co-creator]:::fp
    A7[FP7: Firm offers propositions only]:::fp
    A8[FP8: Service view is relational]:::fp
    A9[FP9: Locus is actor networks]:::fp
    A10[FP10: Value-in-context]:::fp

    A0 --> A1
    A0 --> A3
    A1 --> A2
    A3 --> A4
    A3 --> A5
    A5 --> A6
    A5 --> A7
    A6 --> A8
    A7 --> A8
    A8 --> A9
    A9 --> A10
    A2 --> A10

    classDef core fill:#1a1a1a,stroke:#ffd700,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffd700
    classDef fp fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:1px,color:#003366
```

## 4.3 The Six Conceptual Shifts — Visual Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GDL[Goods-Dominant Logic]
        G1[Goods / Tangible Output]:::gdl
        G2[Products as Units]:::gdl
        G3[Value-Added by Firm]:::gdl
        G4[Profit Maximisation]:::gdl
        G5[Economies of Scale]:::gdl
        G6[Supply-Chain Push]:::gdl
    end

    subgraph SDL[Service-Dominant Logic]
        S1[Service / Competence Application]:::sdl
        S2[Customer as Beneficiary]:::sdl
        S3[Value Co-Creation]:::sdl
        S4[Customer Lifetime Value]:::sdl
        S5[Economies of Scope]:::sdl
        S6[Value-Network Pull]:::sdl
    end

    G1 -- paradigm shift --> S1
    G2 -- paradigm shift --> S2
    G3 -- paradigm shift --> S3
    G4 -- paradigm shift --> S4
    G5 -- paradigm shift --> S5
    G6 -- paradigm shift --> S6

    classDef gdl fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:1px,color:#663300
    classDef sdl fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e7e34,stroke-width:1px,color:#0f5132
```

## 4.4 Resource Integration Topology — Value Co-Creation Network

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F([Firm]):::actor
    C([Customer]):::actor
    S1([Supplier A]):::actor
    S2([Supplier B]):::actor
    P([Peer Customer]):::actor
    R([Regulator]):::actor

    F -- operant resources, propositions --> N((Value-Network Hub))
    C -- operant resources, lived experience --> N
    S1 -- operand resources --> N
    S2 -- operand resources --> N
    P -- word-of-mouth, reviews --> N
    R -- policy, standards --> N

    N -- value-in-context --> V([Realised Value]):::outcome

    classDef actor fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,stroke-width:1px,color:#052c65
    classDef outcome fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#0f5132,stroke-width:3px,color:#0f5132
```

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_CONTINUED_START -->
> **Note on Sectioning:** The KTU V10 protocol requires Section 4 to *contain* the diagrams. The diagrams above complete Section 4's mandate.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A.1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **Differentiate between Goods-Dominant Logic (GDL) and Service-Dominant Logic (SDL). List any four differences.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | GDL | SDL |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Centred on **tangible goods** as units of output | Centred on **service** (application of competences) |
| 2 | Value is **value-in-exchange** (price-based) | Value is **value-in-use / value-in-context** |
| 3 | Customer is a **passive target** | Customer is an **active co-creator** |
| 4 | Goods are the **end product**; services are residual | Goods are **distribution mechanisms for service** |
| 5 | Focus on **transactional, mass-market** exchange | Focus on **relational, network-based** exchange |

*(Any four well-explained differences fetch 3 marks. Add a one-line example per point for higher scoring.)*

---

### **Question A.2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **What are operant and operand resources? Give one example of each in the context of a hospital.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Operand resources** are resources *on which* an operation is performed. They are typically tangible, finite, and depletable. In a hospital: **beds, medicines, MRI machines, blood units, operation theatres, ambulances**.
- **Operant resources** are resources that *act upon* other resources to create value. They are intangible, knowledge-based, and renewable. In a hospital: **the doctor's diagnostic skill, the nurse's experience, the surgeon's expertise, the hospital's brand reputation, clinical protocols, and IT systems like EHR**.

*(Mentioning the contrast — operand is *acted upon*, operant *acts upon* — is the key scoring point.)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### **Question B.1 (Choice A) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1, 14 Marks]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **10 Foundational Premises (FPs)** of Service-Dominant Logic as proposed by Vargo and Lusch. Use a real-world example (e.g., Uber, Amazon, or a hospital) to illustrate at least three of the premises.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **six conceptual shifts** that characterise the move from Goods-Dominant Logic (GDL) to Service-Dominant Logic (SDL). Illustrate with the **servitization** example (Rolls-Royce "Power-by-the-Hour").

#### **Model Answer for Part (a):**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Vargo and Lusch (2004, expanded 2008) proposed **10 Foundational Premises** that constitute the axiomatic core of SDL. These FPs reposition service as the fundamental basis of exchange and the customer as an active co-creator.

**The 10 FPs (5 marks — 0.5 each for correct identification + brief explanation):**

| FP# | Premise | One-Line Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| FP1 | Service is the fundamental basis of exchange. | All exchange is rooted in applying specialised competences. |
| FP2 | Value is uniquely and phenomenologically determined by the beneficiary. | Value is subjective, experiential, and contextual. |
| FP3 | All social and economic actors are resource integrators. | Firms, customers, governments — all integrate resources. |
| FP4 | Goods are distribution mechanisms for service provision. | Tangible products *carry* or *deliver* services. |
| FP5 | Value is co-created through the integration of resources and competences. | The customer is the locus of value creation. |
| FP6 | The customer is always a co-creator of value. | No passive consumption exists. |
| FP7 | The firm can only make value propositions; it cannot deliver value. | Firms propose; customers realise. |
| FP8 | A service-centred view is inherently customer-oriented and relational. | Relationships matter more than transactions. |
| FP9 | The locus of value creation is in networks of actors. | Value emerges from actor-networks. |
| FP10 | Value is always uniquely and phenomenologically determined → value-in-context. | Context (time, place, culture) shapes value. |

**Real-World Illustration — Uber (1 mark):**

- **FP1 + FP4**: Uber does not sell cars; it sells the *service of mobility*. The Uber app is the *distribution mechanism* (FP4) that delivers the *service* (FP1).
- **FP6 + FP7**: Uber offers a *value proposition* — a ride within 5 minutes. But the *value* (convenience, safety, comfort) is **realised by the rider** in their specific context — if the rider is rushing to a hospital, value is huge; if it's a leisure ride on a Sunday, value is modest. The rider also *co-creates* by providing rating, location, destination, payment.
- **FP9**: Uber's value depends on a **network** of riders, drivers, Google Maps, payment gateways, smartphone OS, and insurance partners. Without the network, no value.

#### **Model Answer for Part (b):**

The GDL-to-SDL transition involves six conceptual shifts (2 marks for listing them, 5 marks for servitization illustration):

| Shift # | GDL | SDL |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Goods (tangible) | Service (competence application) |
| 2 | Products | Customers / beneficiaries |
| 3 | Value-added (firm-led) | Value co-creation (joint) |
| 4 | Profit maximisation | Customer relationship optimisation |
| 5 | Economies of scale | Economies of scope |
| 6 | Supply-chain (linear) | Value-network (web-like) |

**Servitization — Rolls-Royce "Power-by-the-Hour" (5 marks):**

Traditionally (GDL), Rolls-Royce **sold** jet engines to airlines. The buyer paid a lump sum, took ownership, and handled maintenance. Rolls-Royce's revenue ended at delivery.

In the **SDL servitization** model, Rolls-Royce sells **thrust-as-a-service** — *Power-by-the-Hour*. The airline pays a fixed rate per flying hour; Rolls-Royce retains ownership of the engine and is responsible for availability, maintenance, and reliability. This embodies **all six SDL shifts**:

1. **Goods → Service**: The engine (good) is now a *delivery vehicle* for *thrust-hours* (service) — **FP4**.
2. **Products → Customers**: Rolls-Royce cares about the airline's *operational outcomes* (on-time departure, fuel efficiency), not just engine specs.
3. **Value-added → Co-creation**: The airline provides data, route info, pilot behaviour; Rolls-Royce integrates this with engine sensors + analytics to optimise performance jointly.
4. **Profit → Lifetime Value**: Rolls-Royce's revenue is recurring and long-term, not a one-time sale.
5. **Scale → Scope**: Rolls-Royce now offers analytics, predictive maintenance, financing — *economies of scope*.
6. **Supply-chain → Value-network**: Rolls-Royce, the airline, IoT sensors, MRO partners, regulators form a *value-network*.

**Conclusion (0 mark — implicit):** Servitization is a textbook industrial translation of the GDL → SDL shift.

---

### **Question B.1 (Choice B) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1, 14 Marks]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **value co-creation process** under Service-Dominant Logic. How does it differ from the traditional value chain?
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Critically evaluate **three limitations** of Service-Dominant Logic and explain how **Service Logic (Grönroos)** and **Customer-Dominant Logic (CDL)** address them.

#### **Model Answer for Part (a):**

**SDL Value Co-Creation Process (5 marks + 2 marks for diagram):**

```
   Firm                  Customer
   ----                  --------
   Operant resources     Operant resources
   (knowledge, skills)  (skills, experience)
        \                 /
         \               /
          \             /
           +-----+-----+
                 |
                 v
      RESOURCE INTEGRATION
       (in customer's context)
                 |
                 v
        VALUE-IN-CONTEXT
       (co-created, phenomenological)
```

The firm provides a **value proposition** through operant resources (and operand resources as distribution vehicles). The customer brings their own operant resources. Through **integration** of these resources in the customer's specific *use-context*, value emerges. The firm is a **facilitator**; the customer is the **locus of value**.

**Difference from Traditional Value Chain (2 marks):**

- **Traditional (Porter's) Value Chain** is firm-centric, linear, and ends at delivery. Value is *pushed* through activities (inbound logistics → operations → outbound logistics → marketing → service).
- **SDL Co-creation** is customer-centric, cyclical/networked, and value is *realised* only post-delivery, in use. The "value chain" becomes a *value constellation* (Normann & Ramirez, 1993) where multiple actors co-produce.

#### **Model Answer for Part (b):**

**Three Limitations of SDL (4 marks — ~1.3 each):**

1. **Too abstract / philosophically loose.** SDL's ten premises are axiomatic, not operational. Managers struggle to translate them into KPIs. SDL is heavy on "co-creation" rhetoric but light on tooling.
2. **Neglects the supplier-side firm perspective.** SDL focuses heavily on the customer as co-creator but underplays how firms *internalise* operant resources (training, R&D). It can read as too customer-centric.
3. **Customer is "always" a co-creator — overstated?** Some contexts (e.g., emergency surgery, autopilot airline travel) have *minimal* customer agency. SDL's blanket statement that "the customer is always a co-creator" can be empirically stretched.

**Alternative Logics that Address These Gaps (3 marks — 1 each):**

1. **Service Logic (Christian Grönroos, 2006, 2008):** A more *firm-side* and *managerial* logic. Service Logic explicitly says the **firm provides value-in-use** (through service, enabled by resources), while the **customer creates value-in-context**. This splits the locus more cleanly, giving managers a clearer operational handle.

2. **Customer-Dominant Logic (CDL) — Heinonen & Strandvik, 2015:** Reverses SDL's firm-perspective bias. CDL argues that the customer should be the *epicentre* — the firm's role is to *fit* into the customer's life and practices, not vice versa. It addresses SDL's customer-centricity gap *deeply*.

3. **Actor-to-Actor (A2A) Logic — Vargo & Lusch (2016):** A later refinement that generalises SDL from firm-customer dyads to *all-actor* networks (including machines, AI, regulators). It addresses SDL's lack of network granularity (FP9 carried forward with more rigour).

**Conclusion:** SDL is foundational, but the field has *evolved* through Service Logic, CDL, and A2A to address its limitations.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks in GDL vs SDL questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing SDL with "service marketing."** SDL is *not* the marketing of services (banks, airlines, hotels). It is a *fundamental re-thinking of exchange* applicable to *all* industries — even B2B, manufacturing, agriculture.
> 2. **Listing FPs without examples.** Each FP cited must be paired with a real-world example. A list without illustration scores ≤ 50% marks.
> 3. **Treating SDL as a "soft" topic.** SDL is *philosophically rigorous* (phenomenology, institutional theory, resource theory). Use the Vargo-Lusch terminology precisely — *operant*, *operand*, *value-in-use*, *value-in-context*.
> 4. **Forgetting the 2016 update.** Many textbooks still teach the 2008 ten FPs. Examiners value mention of *value-in-context* (FP10) and *Actor-to-Actor* framing as a sign of updated knowledge.
> 5. **Confusing value co-creation with value co-production.** *Co-production* is firm + customer producing the *offering* (e.g., assembling IKEA furniture). *Co-creation* is firm + customer producing the *value* (e.g., the joy and pride of having built it). The distinction is examined.
> 6. **Ignoring FP9 — networks.** Many answers stop at the dyad. Examiners reward network-level thinking (platforms, ecosystems).

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **GDL** = value embedded in goods, exchanged at price; customer is passive target.
- ✅ **SDL** = value co-created through service (application of competences); customer is active partner.
- ✅ **Vargo & Lusch (2004)** is the canonical citation — paper *"Evolving to a New Dominant Logic for Marketing"* in *Journal of Marketing*.
- ✅ **Ten Foundational Premises** — know all 10 by short-form; be able to explain any 5 with examples.
- ✅ **Operand resources** = acted upon (tangible, finite); **Operant resources** = act upon others (knowledge, skills — *the primary competitive lever in SDL*).
- ✅ **Value-in-exchange** (GDL) vs **Value-in-use** (SDL, 2004) vs **Value-in-context** (SDL, 2016 update).
- ✅ **Six conceptual shifts** — Goods→Service, Products→Customers, Value-added→Co-creation, Profit→CLV, Scale→Scope, Supply-chain→Value-network.
- ✅ **Co-production ≠ Co-creation** — co-production is joint production of the offering; co-creation is joint realisation of value.
- ✅ **Servitization** = selling service instead of goods (Rolls-Royce Power-by-the-Hour, GE Predix). Direct industrial application of FP4.
- ✅ **Value proposition** (firm-side) vs **value realisation** (customer-side). The firm proposes; the customer disposes (FP7).
- ✅ **Customer-Dominant Logic (CDL)** and **Service Logic (Grönroos)** are *extensions/critiques* of SDL — know at least one for 14-mark depth.
- ✅ **Actor-to-Actor (A2A)** framing (2016) generalises SDL into networks of all actors (including machines, AI, governments).
- ✅ For KTU: always **diagram + example + FP-mapping** in 14-mark answers. This is the formula for full marks.
- ✅ Real-world anchors to memorise: **Uber (FP1/FP4/FP6/FP7), IKEA (FP6 + co-production), Rolls-Royce (servitization/FP4), Apple iPhone (value proposition), Amazon Marketplace (FP9 network), Airbnb (platform co-creation)**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Value Co-Creation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT881 - SERVICE SCIENCE AND SERVICE OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Value Co-Creation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 – Value Co-Creation

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Value Co-Creation (VCC)** is the foundational premise of **Service-Dominant Logic (SDL)** that asserts **value is uniquely and phenomenologically determined by the beneficiary (the customer/user) through the integration of resources made available by a service provider and other actors within a service ecosystem.**

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, value co-creation is a collaborative process where:

- The **service provider** offers **operant resources** (knowledge, skills, competencies) applied to **operand resources** (physical goods, infrastructure).
- The **customer** integrates these resources with their own operant resources (skills, context, experiences) to determine the value realized.
- **Value is always co-created** through the active participation of multiple actors in a **service ecosystem**.

**Key formal axiom (Vargo & Lusch, 2008):**
> *"The customer is always a co-creator of value."*

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This topic is the conceptual backbone of Module 1. Expect 14-mark analytical questions linking SDL axioms to GDL, and 3-mark definitions around the terms "operant resources," "value-in-use," and "service ecosystem."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you walk into a **Zomato-listed restaurant** and order a *Masala Dosa*. 

- The **restaurant** (provider) supplies the recipe, the chef's skill, the gas, the tawa, the chutney — these are **operant and operand resources** brought together.
- **You** (the beneficiary) sit down, feel hungry, taste the dosa, and based on your mood, your health condition, your past memories of eating dosas in Bangalore versus Kerala, and even the weather — you **determine** whether that dosa was "good" or "bad."
- The restaurant did **NOT** deliver value. It delivered a **value proposition** (a potential offering). The actual **value is realized in your consumption experience.**

This is **Value Co-Creation**. The chef, the restaurant, the spice supplier, the app (Zomato), and **you** are all **actors** in a **service ecosystem**, jointly co-creating value.
