> | Feature | **CCR Model** | **BCC Model** |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | **Introduced By** | Charnes, Cooper, Rhodes (1978) | Banker, Charnes, Cooper (1984) |
> | **Returns to Scale** | Constant Returns to Scale (**CRS**) | Variable Returns to Scale (**VRS**) |
> | **Key Constraint** | None beyond $\lambda \geq 0$ | $\sum \lambda_j = 1$ (convexity) |
> | **Efficiency Type** | **Technical Efficiency** (overall) | **Pure Technical Efficiency** |
> | **Frontier Shape** | Single straight ray through origin | Piecewise linear convex hull |
> | **Efficiency Score** | $\theta_{CRS}$ | $\theta_{VRS}$ |
>
> **(2 marks for the table; 1 mark for stating the key distinguishing constraint $\sum \lambda_j = 1$.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]*

**A hospital wants to evaluate the relative efficiency of its 4 Intensive Care Units (ICUs) using DEA. The data is given below:**

| DMU (ICU) | Input 1: Doctors ($x_1$) | Input 2: Beds ($x_2$) | Output 1: Patients Treated ($y_1$) | Output 2: Surgeries ($y_2$) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| A | 5 | 10 | 20 | 15 |
| B | 8 | 12 | 30 | 20 |
| C | 6 | 9 | 25 | 18 |
| D | 7 | 11 | 28 | 22 |

**(a)** Formulate the **CCR input-oriented envelopment model** for DMU A.  
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain how you would interpret the efficiency score $\theta$, peer weights $\lambda_j$, and slack variables $s^{-}, s^{+}$. If DMU A is found to be inefficient, write the **target projection formulas**.  
**(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Formulation (7 marks):**

For DMU A: $x_o = (5, 10)^T$, $y_o = (20, 15)^T$.

The CCR input-oriented envelopment LP for DMU A is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min_{\theta, \lambda_1, \lambda_2, \lambda_3, \lambda_4} \quad & \theta \\[4pt]
\text{s.t.} \quad & 5\lambda_1 + 8\lambda_2 + 6\lambda_3 + 7\lambda_4 \leq 5\theta \quad & (\text{Doctors}) \\[4pt]
& 10\lambda_1 + 12\lambda_2 + 9\lambda_3 + 11\lambda_4 \leq 10\theta \quad & (\text{Beds}) \\[4pt]
& 20\lambda_1 + 30\lambda_2 + 25\lambda_3 + 28\lambda_4 \geq 20 \quad & (\text{Patients}) \\[4pt]
& 15\lambda_1 + 20\lambda_2 + 18\lambda_3 + 22\lambda_4 \geq 15 \quad & (\text{Surgeries}) \\[4pt]
& \lambda_j \geq 0, \quad j = 1, 2, 3, 4
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic (1 mark):**  
> - The objective minimizes the radial contraction factor $\theta$ applied to all inputs of DMU A.  
> - Constraints (1) and (2) ensure that the *composite* DMU built from convex combination of all ICUs uses no more doctors / beds (after scaling by $\theta$) than DMU A.  
> - Constraints (3) and (4) ensure the composite DMU produces *at least* as many patients / surgeries as DMU A.  
> - $\lambda_j \geq 0$ are the **peer weights** that identify the reference set.

**(b) Interpretation and Target Projection (7 marks):**

| Variable | Interpretation (2 marks) |
| :--- | :--- |
| $\theta$ | Efficiency score: $\theta = 1 \Rightarrow$ efficient; $\theta < 1 \Rightarrow$ inefficient. It is the proportional amount by which DMU A can reduce all inputs while still producing the same output level. |
| $\lambda_j$ | Peer weights: an inefficient DMU A is benchmarked against the convex combination $\sum \lambda_j DMU_j$. If $\lambda_B = 0.4, \lambda_D = 0.6$, A should mimic a 40-60% blend of B and D. |
| $s^{-}$ | Input slack — additional non-radial reduction in input $i$ possible *after* the $\theta$-contraction. |
| $s^{+}$ | Output slack — additional non-radial increase in output $r$ possible. |

**Target Projection Formulas (3 marks):**

For each input $i$ and output $r$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_i^{\text{target}} &= \theta^{\ast} \, x_{i o} - s_i^{-\ast} \\[4pt]
y_r^{\text{target}} &= y_{r o} + s_r^{+\ast}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical example (2 marks):** If for DMU A the LP gives $\theta^{\ast} = 0.8$, $s_1^{-\ast} = 0.5$ (doctors), $s_2^{-\ast} = 1$ (beds), $s_1^{+\ast} = 2$ (patients), $s_2^{+\ast} = 0$:

- **Target doctors:** $0.8 \times 5 - 0.5 = 3.5$ (i.e., reduce doctors from 5 to 3.5)
- **Target beds:** $0.8 \times 10 - 1 = 7$ (reduce beds from 10 to 7)
- **Target patients treated:** $20 + 2 = 22$
- **Target surgeries:** $15 + 0 = 15$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]*

**(a)** Derive the **Charnes-Cooper transformation** that converts the fractional CCR multiplier form into the equivalent linear program.  
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **Technical Efficiency (TE)**, **Pure Technical Efficiency (PTE)**, and **Scale Efficiency (SE)**. How is SE computed? What managerial insights does it provide?  
**(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Charnes-Cooper Transformation Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Original fractional program (2 marks):**

$$
\max_{u, v} \; h_o = \frac{\sum_{r=1}^{s} u_r y_{r o}}{\sum_{i=1}^{m} v_i x_{i o}} \quad \text{s.t.} \quad \frac{\sum_{r=1}^{s} u_r y_{r j}}{\sum_{i=1}^{m} v_i x_{i j}} \leq 1, \quad \forall j
$$

**Step 2 — Impose normalization (1 mark):**  
Because $h_o$ is **scale-invariant** in $(u, v)$ jointly, fix

$$\sum_{i=1}^{m} v_i x_{i o} = 1$$

**Step 3 — Substitute (2 marks):**  
Let $t = 1 / \sum_i v_i x_{i o}$, $\mu_r = t u_r$, $\nu_i = t v_i$. Then:

- The objective becomes $\max \mu^T y_o$.
- The constraints $u^T y_j / v^T x_j \leq 1$ become $\mu^T y_j - \nu^T x_j \leq 0$.
- The normalization becomes $\nu^T x_o = 1$.

**Step 4 — Resulting LP (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\max_{\mu, \nu} \quad & \mu^T y_o \\[4pt]
\text{s.t.} \quad & \mu^T y_j - \nu^T x_j \leq 0, \quad \forall j \\[4pt]
& \nu^T x_o = 1 \\[4pt]
& \mu, \nu \geq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

> This is a standard LP. Its dual is the **envelopment form** used in the computational algorithms.

**(b) Efficiency Decomposition (7 marks):**

**Definitions (3 marks):**

- **Technical Efficiency (TE) — $\theta_{CRS}$:** The overall efficiency obtained from the **CCR model** (CRS). It captures *both* pure operational efficiency and scale effects.
- **Pure Technical Efficiency (PTE) — $\theta_{VRS}$:** The efficiency obtained from the **BCC model** (VRS). It captures *only* operational efficiency, free from the influence of scale size.
- **Scale Efficiency (SE):** The portion of technical efficiency attributable purely to operating at the *optimal scale*.

**Computation (2 marks):**

$$SE = \frac{TE}{PTE} = \frac{\theta_{CRS}}{\theta_{VRS}}, \quad SE \in [0, 1]$$

**Managerial Insights (2 marks):**
- If $SE = 1$ and $PTE < 1$: the DMU is operating at the right scale but is **managerially inefficient** (poor process, waste, or skill gaps).
- If $SE < 1$ and $PTE = 1$: the DMU is **technically efficient** but operating at the wrong scale. The manager should consider expansion (if IRS) or contraction (if DRS).
- If both $SE < 1$ and $PTE < 1$: the DMU must improve **both** operations *and* scale.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks in DEA Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Inputs and Outputs** — A 1-mark penalty is typical. Always re-read the question: "DMU A uses 5 doctors and produces 20 patients" — doctors are *inputs*, patients are *outputs*.
> 2. **Forgetting the convexity constraint in BCC** — The constraint $\sum \lambda_j = 1$ must appear explicitly, or you will be solving the CCR model by mistake.
> 3. **Writing $\lambda \geq 0$ only without stating it is the *peer weight* vector** — Examiners expect a sentence defining $\lambda_j$.
> 4. **Not mentioning slacks** — A complete answer must include $s^{-}$ and $s^{+}$; efficiency score $\theta = 1$ does NOT automatically mean the DMU is fully efficient in the slacks-minimized sense (Phase II of DEA).
> 5. **Not stating that DEA is *relative*, not absolute** — A common 2-mark conceptual error.
> 6. **Missing the "$\varepsilon$ non-Archimedean" condition** for $u_r, v_i$ in the multiplier form — adding a small $\varepsilon > 0$ prevents zero-weight degeneracy.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — DEA**

- **DEA** = non-parametric LP technique to measure **relative** efficiency of homogeneous **DMUs**.
- **Charnes-Cooper-Rhodes (1978)** introduced the **CCR** model under **Constant Returns to Scale (CRS)**.
- **Banker-Charnes-Cooper (1984)** introduced the **BCC** model under **Variable Returns to Scale (VRS)** via the convexity constraint $\sum \lambda_j = 1$.
- **Efficiency Score** $\theta \in [0, 1]$: $\theta = 1$ → on the frontier (efficient); $\theta < 1$ → below frontier (inefficient).
- **Two Equivalent Forms**:
  - *Multiplier form* — chooses best weights $(u, v)$ to maximize $h_o$.
  - *Envelopment form* — chooses peer weights $\lambda$ to minimize radial input contraction $\theta$.
- **Charnes-Cooper Transformation** converts the fractional multiplier form into a standard LP using the normalization $\nu^T x_o = 1$.
- **Slacks** $s^{-}$ (input excess) and $s^{+}$ (output shortfall) capture **non-radial** inefficiencies and require a **Phase-II** model.
- **Scale Efficiency** $SE = \theta_{CRS} / \theta_{VRS}$ — measures the inefficiency due to wrong operating scale.
- **Reference Set** of an inefficient DMU consists of all DMUs with $\lambda_j > 0$ in the optimal LP solution.
- **Managerial Target Projection**: $x^{\text{target}} = \theta^{\ast} x_o - s^{-\ast}$, $y^{\text{target}} = y_o + s^{+\ast}$.
- **Returns to Scale** (for BCC-efficient units): $\sum \lambda_j < 1$ → **IRS**; $\sum \lambda_j = 1$ → **CRS**; $\sum \lambda_j > 1$ → **DRS**.
- **Practical Applications in Service Operations**: bank branches, hospital ICUs, retail outlets, courier hubs, telecom service centers, e-commerce warehouses.
- **Limitations to Note**: DEA is sensitive to outliers, requires homogeneity, and gives relative (not absolute) efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: New Service Development: NSD Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT861 - SERVICE SCIENCE AND SERVICE OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** New Service Development: NSD Cycle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**New Service Development (NSD)** is the structured, sequential process through which service organizations generate, design, introduce, and improve service offerings to meet evolving customer needs, leverage emerging technologies, and maintain competitive advantage in dynamic markets. The **NSD Cycle** refers to the iterative, multi-stage framework that systematically transforms a nascent service idea into a fully operational, market-ready service that delivers value to customers and the provider.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECBT861 - Module 2):**
> *New Service Development is the total process from idea generation to commercialization of a new or improved service, encompassing strategic planning, design, testing, and launch activities. Unlike physical products, NSD emphasizes intangibility, customer co-production, and process simultaneity.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Restaurant Analogy

Imagine a chef who wants to introduce a new dish to the restaurant menu. The chef does not just cook it one day and put it on the menu immediately. Instead, the chef:

1. **Brainstorms** ideas (combining spices, ingredients, cuisines)
2. **Tests** the concept on family and friends (concept testing)
3. **Analyzes** if the dish will be profitable given ingredient costs (business analysis)
4. **Develops** the exact recipe and presentation style (service design)
5. **Pilots** the dish for a week as a "chef's special" to gather feedback (pilot testing)
6. **Launches** it officially on the permanent menu (commercialization)
7. **Monitors** customer satisfaction and modifies if needed (post-launch review)

This entire sequence — from idea to mature, refined offering — is exactly the **NSD Cycle** in service management. Just as a chef iterates on the dish, service firms iterate on their service offerings to achieve service excellence.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Product Development (NPD):** Services are intangible, inseparable (produced and consumed simultaneously), heterogeneous, and perishable. Therefore, the NSD cycle is more iterative, customer-involved, and flexible than the traditional new product development (NPD) cycle.

### 1.3 Why NSD is Critical for Service Firms

- **Customer Expectations Evolve:** Modern customers demand personalized, technology-enabled, omni-channel experiences.
- **Competitive Pressure:** Disruptive service models (e.g., Uber, Ola, Zomato) redefine industries rapidly.
- **Service Innovation Drives Growth:** Studies indicate that **more than 65% of service firm growth** comes from new or improved services.
- **Risk Mitigation:** A structured NSD process reduces the high failure rate of new service launches.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics and Benchmarks

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Benchmark Metrics in NSD:**
> - **NSD Success Rate:** Typically **40%–60%** for structured processes vs. **<20%** for unstructured launches.
> - **Development Cycle Time:** Ranges from **6 months (simple services)** to **3+ years (complex financial/health services)**.
> - **Time-to-Market:** A **1-month delay** can reduce revenue potential by approximately **10%–15%**.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The NSD Cycle: Stage-by-Stage Operational Logic

The NSD cycle is not strictly linear — modern frameworks treat it as an **iterative loop** with feedback arrows. The most widely cited NSD models in KTU curriculum include **Scheuing & Johnson**, **Edvardsson & Olsson**, and the **Fitzsimmons Service Design Process**.

Below is the consolidated 8-stage NSD cycle (synthesized from the above models for KTU examination purposes):

#### **Stage 1: Service Strategy & Idea Generation**
- **Why:** To align new service ideas with the firm's overall mission, market positioning, and strategic goals.
- **How:** Internal brainstorming, customer surveys, competitor benchmarking, technology scouting, and employee suggestion systems.
- **Output:** A pool of potential service concepts and a strategic direction document.

#### **Stage 2: Idea Screening**
- **Why:** To filter out ideas that are infeasible, unprofitable, or misaligned with strategy.
- **How:** Use feasibility checklists, scoring models, and Go/No-Go decision matrices.
- **Output:** A shortlist of viable ideas.

#### **Stage 3: Concept Development & Testing**
- **Why:** To translate abstract ideas into tangible service concepts and validate them with target customers.
- **How:** Develop a service concept statement (positioning, target segment, value proposition) and test it via focus groups, in-depth interviews, and conjoint analysis.
- **Output:** A refined service concept with validated customer appeal.

#### **Stage 4: Business Analysis**
- **Why:** To assess the financial viability of the proposed service.
- **How:** Estimate costs, revenues, break-even point, ROI, and risk factors.
- **Output:** A business case document with financial projections.

#### **Stage 5: Service Design & Process Engineering**
- **Why:** To blueprint the service process, customer journey, supporting infrastructure, and staff roles.
- **How:** Use **Service Blueprinting** (a detailed flow diagram showing customer actions, frontstage/ backstage activities, and support processes).
- **Output:** Service blueprint, process maps, and SOPs (Standard Operating Procedures).

#### **Stage 6: Service Testing & Pilot Launch**
- **Why:** To validate the service in a real or simulated environment before full rollout.
- **How:** Conduct internal testing (employees act as customers), followed by a limited market pilot (e.g., one branch, one city).
- **Output:** Pilot results, customer feedback, defect logs.

#### **Stage 7: Full Commercial Launch**
- **Why:** To introduce the service to the broader market.
- **How:** Develop marketing campaigns, train staff, set up delivery infrastructure, and monitor KPIs.
- **Output:** A market-launched service with performance tracking.

#### **Stage 8: Post-Launch Evaluation & Continuous Improvement**
- **Why:** To assess actual performance vs. forecasts and identify improvements.
- **How:** Use customer satisfaction surveys (CSAT, NPS), service quality metrics (SERVQUAL), and financial audits.
- **Output:** Improvement action plans, leading to **feedback into Stage 1** (closing the cycle).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following quantitative models are essential for KTU numerical/analytical questions on NSD.

| **Metric / Formula** | **Mathematical Form** | **Description & Unit** |
|---|---|---|
| **Return on Investment (ROI)** | $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{\text{Net Profit from Service}}{\text{Total Investment}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage (\%); measures financial return |
| **Break-Even Point (Units)** | $\text{BEP} = \dfrac{\text{Fixed Costs}}{\text{Price per Unit} - \text{Variable Cost per Unit}}$ | Number of service units |
| **Break-Even Point (Revenue)** | $\text{BEP}_{\text{Rev}} = \dfrac{\text{Fixed Costs}}{1 - \dfrac{\text{VC}}{\text{Sales}}}$ | Currency units (₹) |
| **Net Present Value (NPV)** | $\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{\text{CF}_{t}}{(1+r)^{t}}$ | Currency units; t = time, r = discount rate |
| **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)** | $\text{CLV} = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{(R_{t} - C_{t}) \cdot r_{\text{ret}}}{(1+d)^{t}}$ | Currency units; long-term customer worth |
| **Service Success Rate** | $\text{SSR} = \dfrac{\text{Successful NSD Launches}}{\text{Total NSD Launches}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage |
| **Concept Test Score** | $\text{CTS} = \dfrac{\sum (\text{Weighted Ratings})}{\text{Max Possible Score}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage |
| **Time-to-Market Index** | $\text{TTM} = \dfrac{\text{Planned Duration}}{\text{Actual Duration}}$ | Ratio; > 1 = early, < 1 = delayed |
| **Service Quality Gap (SERVQUAL)** | $\text{Gap}_5 = P - E$ | P = Perception, E = Expectation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** In 14-mark questions, the service blueprinting stage (Stage 5) is the **most frequently tested**. Always remember to distinguish between **Line of Visibility** and **Line of Interaction** in the blueprint.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility

The NSD cycle is applied extensively in:
- **Banking** (e.g., mobile wallet launches by HDFC, ICICI)
- **Healthcare** (e.g., teleconsultation platforms like Practo)
- **Hospitality** (e.g., new room concepts by Marriott)
- **IT/ITES** (e.g., AI-powered service desks)
- **Education** (e.g., NPTEL's online certification services)

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: NSD Models Comparison (KTU High-Yield)

| **Dimension** | **Scheuing & Johnson (1989)** | **Edvardsson & Olsson (1996)** | **Fitzsimmons & Fitzsimmons** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Number of Stages** | 15 stages | 8 stages | 6 stages |
| **Focus** | Detailed procedural model | Customer-focused, quality-driven | Strategic & operational |
| **Customer Involvement** | High (concept testing) | Very high (co-creation) | Moderate |
| **Iteration Loops** | Limited | Strong iteration | Moderate |
| **Best Suited For** | Manufacturing-led services | Knowledge-intensive services | Strategic service design |
| **KTU Weightage** | High (definition questions) | Moderate (comparison Qs) | High (blueprint Qs) |

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: Break-Even Analysis for a New Service

**Problem Context:** A new cloud-based training service requires an initial investment of ₹50,00,000 (Fixed Costs). The variable cost per subscriber is ₹500. The subscription price is ₹2,000 per user. Calculate:
1. The Break-Even Point in units.
2. The Break-Even Point in revenue.
3. The expected profit at 5,000 subscribers.

**Step 1: Identify the Given Parameters**
- Fixed Cost (FC) = ₹50,00,000
- Variable Cost per unit (VC) = ₹500
- Selling Price per unit (P) = ₹2,000

**Step 2: Calculate the Contribution Margin per Unit**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Contribution Margin (CM)} &= P - VC \\
&= 2{,}000 - 500 \\
&= \text{₹}1{,}500 \text{ per unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate the Break-Even Point in Units**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BEP}_{\text{units}} &= \frac{FC}{P - VC} \\
&= \frac{50{,}00{,}000}{1{,}500} \\
&= 3{,}333.33 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since we cannot have fractional subscribers, we round up to **3,334 subscribers** to fully recover fixed costs.

**Step 4: Calculate the Break-Even Point in Revenue**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BEP}_{\text{Rev}} &= \text{BEP}_{\text{units}} \times P \\
&= 3{,}334 \times 2{,}000 \\
&= \text{₹}66{,}68{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Calculate the Expected Profit at 5,000 Subscribers**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Revenue (TR)} &= 5{,}000 \times 2{,}000 = \text{₹}1{,}00{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Total Variable Cost (TVC)} &= 5{,}000 \times 500 = \text{₹}25{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Profit} &= TR - TVC - FC \\
&= 1{,}00{,}00{,}000 - 25{,}00{,}000 - 50{,}00{,}000 \\
&= \text{₹}25{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Validate Using NPV (assuming 3-year horizon, 10% discount rate, constant cash flow of ₹25 lakh)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV} &= \sum_{t=1}^{3} \frac{25{,}00{,}000}{(1.10)^{t}} - 50{,}00{,}000 \\
&= \frac{25{,}00{,}000}{1.10} + \frac{25{,}00{,}000}{1.21} + \frac{25{,}00{,}000}{1.331} - 50{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 22{,}72{,}727 + 20{,}66{,}116 + 18{,}78{,}287 - 50{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 62{,}17{,}130 - 50{,}00{,}000 \\
&= \text{₹}12{,}17{,}130 \text{ (Positive — project is viable)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The new service becomes profitable after the 3,334th subscriber. At 5,000 subscribers, the firm earns ₹25 lakh in profit, and the 3-year NPV is positive, confirming financial viability.

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Service Blueprint Construction Logic

A **Service Blueprint** is a critical NSD output. The construction steps are:

1. **Identify Customer Actions:** Map all steps the customer takes (e.g., enters bank, fills form, receives tokens).
2. **Identify Frontstage Employee Actions:** Visible to customer (e.g., teller serves the customer).
3. **Identify Backstage Employee Actions:** Invisible to customer (e.g., clerk verifies documents, IT system processes request).
4. **Identify Support Processes:** Internal systems that enable service delivery (e.g., core banking system, server infrastructure).
5. **Draw the Lines of Interaction:**
   - **Line of Interaction:** Separates customer from service provider.
   - **Line of Visibility:** Separates frontstage from backstage.
   - **Line of Internal Interaction:** Separates backstage from support processes.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The 8-Stage NSD Cycle (Iterative Loop)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Stage 1: Service Strategy and Idea Generation"] --> B["Stage 2: Idea Screening"]
    B --> C["Stage 3: Concept Development and Testing"]
    C --> D["Stage 4: Business Analysis"]
    D --> E["Stage 5: Service Design and Process Engineering"]
    E --> F["Stage 6: Service Testing and Pilot Launch"]
    F --> G["Stage 7: Full Commercial Launch"]
    G --> H["Stage 8: Post Launch Evaluation and Improvement"]
    H -- Continuous Improvement Feedback --> A
    F -- Pilot Defects Found --> E
    C -- Concept Rejected --> B
    D -- Financial Non-Viability --> B
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Service Blueprint Architecture (Block-Level View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CustomerJourney["CUSTOMER JOURNEY ZONE"]
        CA1["Step 1: Enter Branch"]
        CA2["Step 2: Submit Documents"]
        CA3["Step 3: Await Service"]
        CA4["Step 4: Receive Service"]
    end

    subgraph FrontStageZone["FRONTSTAGE ZONE"]
        FS1["Greet Customer"]
        FS2["Verify Identity"]
        FS3["Process Request"]
        FS4["Deliver Service"]
    end

    subgraph BackStageZone["BACKSTAGE ZONE"]
        BS1["Check Database"]
        BS2["Approve Transaction"]
        BS3["Update Records"]
    end

    subgraph SupportZone["SUPPORT PROCESS ZONE"]
        SP1["IT Infrastructure"]
        SP2["Compliance Systems"]
        SP3["HR and Training"]
    end

    CA1 --> FS1
    CA2 --> FS2
    CA3 --> FS3
    CA4 --> FS4
    FS1 --> BS1
    FS2 --> BS2
    FS3 --> BS3
    BS1 --> SP1
    BS2 --> SP2
    BS3 --> SP3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Blueprint:**
> - The horizontal dashed line between the two zones represents the **Line of Interaction** (separates customer from provider).
> - The line between Frontstage and Backstage is the **Line of Visibility**.
> - The line between Backstage and Support is the **Line of Internal Interaction**.

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: NSD Stage-to-Outcome Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    NSD1["Idea Generation"] --> NSD1O["Output: Idea Pool"]
    NSD2["Screening"] --> NSD2O["Output: Shortlisted Ideas"]
    NSD3["Concept Testing"] --> NSD3O["Output: Validated Concept"]
    NSD4["Business Analysis"] --> NSD4O["Output: Business Case"]
    NSD5["Service Design"] --> NSD5O["Output: Service Blueprint"]
    NSD6["Pilot Launch"] --> NSD6O["Output: Pilot Report"]
    NSD7["Commercial Launch"] --> NSD7O["Output: Market Entry"]
    NSD8["Post Launch Review"] --> NSD8O["Output: Improvement Roadmap"]
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

#### **Question 1: Define New Service Development (NSD) and list its first four stages.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** New Service Development (NSD) is the systematic, multi-stage process of creating, designing, and launching new or improved services to meet customer needs and achieve organizational objectives. It encompasses the entire journey from idea conception to post-launch evaluation.

**First Four Stages:**
1. **Service Strategy & Idea Generation** — Identifying new service opportunities aligned with the firm's strategic goals.
2. **Idea Screening** — Filtering out infeasible ideas using feasibility criteria.
3. **Concept Development & Testing** — Translating ideas into tangible concepts and validating them with customers.
4. **Business Analysis** — Assessing financial viability (ROI, NPV, break-even).

---

#### **Question 2: Differentiate between NSD and NPD (New Product Development).** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| **Basis** | **NSD (Service Development)** | **NPD (Product Development)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature of Output** | Intangible (service) | Tangible (product) |
| **Customer Involvement** | High (co-production) | Low (consumption only) |
| **Testing** | Difficult to test in isolation | Lab-based testing feasible |
| **Patents** | Rare (process-oriented) | Common |
| **Iteration** | Highly iterative | More linear |
| **Inventory** | No inventory (perishability) | Inventory management required |
| **Customization** | High (heterogeneity) | Standardized |

---

### **PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]`

**"Explain the complete 8-stage New Service Development (NSD) cycle with a suitable real-world example. Also, discuss the role of Service Blueprinting in the design stage."**

**Sub-part (a): Describe the 8-Stage NSD Cycle with a real-world example. (7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

The NSD cycle is a structured, iterative process used by service firms to develop new offerings. Consider the example of **Zomato launching its "Zomato Pro" subscription service** for free delivery and exclusive discounts.

1. **Stage 1 – Service Strategy & Idea Generation:** Zomato identified that frequent users wanted lower delivery costs. Strategic brainstorming led to the idea of a subscription model.

2. **Stage 2 – Idea Screening:** Multiple ideas (subscription, loyalty points, premium restaurants) were screened using feasibility analysis. The subscription model was selected.

3. **Stage 3 – Concept Development & Testing:** The "Zomato Pro" concept (₹200/month, unlimited free delivery) was tested via focus groups in Delhi and Mumbai. Customer feedback refined the pricing.

4. **Stage 4 – Business Analysis:** Financial projections estimated break-even at 8 lakh subscribers. ROI was projected at 35% in Year 1.

5. **Stage 5 – Service Design & Blueprinting:** The customer journey was mapped: app open → Pro banner → order placement → free delivery. SOPs for restaurants and delivery partners were drafted.

6. **Stage 6 – Pilot Launch:** Initially rolled out in 5 cities (Delhi, Mumbai, Bangalore, Chennai, Hyderabad) to gather real-world data.

7. **Stage 7 – Commercial Launch:** Pan-India rollout with marketing campaigns featuring celebrities.

8. **Stage 8 – Post-Launch Evaluation:** NPS scores, churn rates, and customer feedback were analyzed quarterly. Improvements (e.g., adding OTT benefits) were implemented, feeding back to Stage 1.

**Sub-part (b): Discuss the role of Service Blueprinting in the design stage. (7 Marks)**

Service Blueprinting is a critical tool in **Stage 5 (Service Design)** that visually maps the entire service process. It consists of four zones:

1. **Customer Actions:** All activities the customer performs.
2. **Frontstage Actions:** Visible employee activities.
3. **Backstage Actions:** Invisible internal activities.
4. **Support Processes:** Infrastructure and systems enabling the service.

**Key Roles of Service Blueprinting:**

- **Clarity:** Provides a clear visual representation of the service process, reducing ambiguity.
- **Gap Identification:** Highlights potential failure points (e.g., long wait times at "backstage" handoffs).
- **Customer Experience Focus:** Keeps customer actions central, ensuring the design prioritizes UX.
- **Cross-Functional Alignment:** Aligns marketing, operations, HR, and IT teams on a single process view.
- **Training Aid:** Acts as a reference document for training new employees.
- **Service Standardization:** Ensures consistent service delivery across branches/locations.

In the Zomato Pro example, the blueprint would clearly delineate the app interface (frontstage), the restaurant order flow (backstage), and the payment gateway (support process), enabling seamless service design.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Supplementary]`

**"What is a Service Blueprint? Explain its components, the key lines of separation, and its significance in NSD with a banking example."**

**Sub-part (a): Explain Service Blueprint components and the key lines of separation. (7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

A **Service Blueprint** is a detailed diagram that visualizes the sequence of activities in a service process, distinguishing between what the customer experiences and the internal processes that support it.

**Key Components of a Service Blueprint:**

1. **Customer Actions:** The steps a customer takes to receive the service.
2. **Frontstage Employee Actions:** Activities visible to the customer.
3. **Backstage Employee Actions:** Activities invisible to the customer but essential.
4. **Support Processes:** Internal systems and infrastructure.

**Three Critical Lines of Separation:**

1. **Line of Interaction:** Separates the customer from the service organization. It marks the points where direct customer–provider contact occurs (e.g., when a customer speaks to a bank teller).
2. **Line of Visibility:** Separates frontstage (visible) from backstage (invisible) activities. Everything above this line is visible to the customer; everything below is not.
3. **Line of Internal Interaction:** Separates backstage activities from support processes. It distinguishes the service operations from the underlying IT/systems infrastructure.

**Sub-part (b): Significance in NSD with a banking example. (7 Marks)**

Consider the example of a bank launching a new **"Instant Personal Loan"** service:

- **Customer Actions:** Customer logs into mobile app → selects "Instant Loan" → enters amount → submits KYC documents → receives loan approval.
- **Frontstage Actions:** The mobile app interface (digital teller), SMS confirmations.
- **Backstage Actions:** Document verification, credit score analysis by loan officers.
- **Support Processes:** Core Banking System (CBS), CIBIL API integration, payment gateway.

**Significance in NSD:**

- **Clarity in Process Design:** The blueprint clarifies who does what at each stage, reducing operational confusion.
- **Customer Journey Mapping:** Helps identify moments of truth where customer satisfaction is decided.
- **Failure Point Identification:** Reveals bottlenecks (e.g., credit score API delays) before launch.
- **Cross-Department Coordination:** Aligns IT, operations, compliance, and marketing teams.
- **Quality Control:** Enables the setting of SLAs (Service Level Agreements) for each process step.
- **Cost Optimization:** Identifies non-value-adding activities that can be eliminated.
- **Scalability Planning:** Helps design the service for future scale (e.g., 1 lakh to 10 lakh customers).

Thus, the service blueprint is the cornerstone of the **Service Design stage (Stage 5)** in the NSD cycle.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing NSD with NPD:** Many students mix up service and product development. Always highlight the four unique service characteristics (IHIP: Intangibility, Heterogeneity, Inseparability, Perishability).
> 2. **Skipping the Iteration Aspect:** The NSD cycle is **iterative**, not linear. Always draw feedback arrows from post-launch review back to idea generation.
> 3. **Blueprint Labeling Errors:** Students often forget to label the **three lines** (Interaction, Visibility, Internal Interaction) explicitly. The KTU examiner awards 2 marks specifically for this.
> 4. **Neglecting Customer Co-Creation:** In service design, the customer is a co-producer. Failing to mention this loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Formula Units:** In break-even/ROI calculations, always mention the **units** (₹, %, units) explicitly. Omitting units costs a half-mark.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- ✅ **NSD Cycle = 8 Stages:** Strategy → Screening → Concept → Business Analysis → Design → Pilot → Launch → Review (with feedback loops).
- ✅ **Key Models:** Scheuing & Johnson, Edvardsson & Olsson, Fitzsimmons — know the differences.
- ✅ **Service Blueprinting** is the most critical Stage 5 deliverable — includes **3 lines** and **4 zones**.
- ✅ **IHIP Characteristics** of services differentiate NSD from NPD.
- ✅ **Key Formulas:** ROI, BEP (units & revenue), NPV, CLV, SERVQUAL Gap 5.
- ✅ **Iterative Nature:** Always emphasize feedback loops in diagrams.
- ✅ **Customer Co-Creation:** Customers are active participants, not passive consumers, in NSD.
- ✅ **Pilot Launch** is non-negotiable in service NSD due to high heterogeneity.
- ✅ **Post-Launch Evaluation** uses CSAT, NPS, and SERVQUAL metrics.
- ✅ **Real-World Examples:** Always use KTU-friendly examples (Zomato, HDFC, Practo, Marriott) to earn application marks.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Service Blueprinting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT861 - SERVICE SCIENCE AND SERVICE OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Service Blueprinting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Service Blueprinting

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Service Blueprinting** is a customer-focused process analysis and design technique that visually maps the sequence of activities, interactions, and physical evidence involved in a service delivery process. It is a *process-flow diagram* that displays the *front-stage* and *back-stage* activities performed by the service provider, the *customer actions*, and the *support processes* required to deliver the service.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Wording):**
> *"Service Blueprinting is a detailed flow chart or process map that depicts the service delivery process, distinguishing between what the customer experiences (visible front-stage), what service employees do (invisible back-stage), and what support systems are required to make the service possible. It is an essential service design tool used to identify potential failure points, optimize processes, and standardize service quality."*

The technique was pioneered by **G. Lynn Shostack** in 1982 in the *Harvard Business Review* and later refined by **Richard B. Chase** and **Warren J. Erikson** in 1985.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🍽️ The Restaurant Analogy — "Service Blueprinting is the Architectural Floor Plan of a Service"**
>
> Imagine you walk into a restaurant:
> - You (the **customer**) see the menu, the waiter, the dining area, and your food arriving — that is the **front stage**.
> - Behind the swinging kitchen door, chefs are cooking, dishwashers are cleaning, and managers are scheduling — that is the **back stage**.
> - But the kitchen cannot function without the **support processes**: gas supply, refrigeration, procurement of raw materials, and IT-based billing.
>
> A **Service Blueprint** is exactly like the *blueprint of a building* — it shows the customer exactly what they will see, and reveals to the designer what must happen behind the walls to make the customer's experience flawless. The blueprint's horizontal *lines* are the equivalent of the walls, ceilings, and plumbing diagrams that an architect uses.

## 1.3 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics in Service Blueprinting

While Service Blueprinting is primarily a *qualitative* design tool, the following standardized **service process metrics** are integrated into blueprinted services:

- **Cycle Time (CT)** — Standard unit: **minutes** or **seconds**.
- **Customer Wait Time (CWT)** — Standard unit: **minutes**.
- **Service Capacity (μ)** — Standard unit: **customers/hour**.
- **Process Touchpoints** — Standard count: number of customer–provider interactions.
- **Failure Point Rate (λ)** — Standard unit: **failures per 1000 transactions**.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Cue:** Always quote the *three key lines* of a Service Blueprint by exact name:
> 1. **Line of Interaction**
> 2. **Line of Visibility**
> 3. **Line of Internal Interaction**

## 1.4 Importance in KTU Curriculum (Syllabus Highlight)

| Aspect | Application in Service Operations |
|---|---|
| **Process Design** | Standardizes service delivery across employees and branches |
| **Quality Control** | Identifies *failure points* where service failure can occur |
| **Customer Experience (CX)** | Maps every *moment of truth* in the customer journey |
| **Training Tool** | New employees learn the entire service system in one diagram |
| **Innovation** | Reveals opportunities for *self-service technologies* and automation |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Core Components of a Service Blueprint

A Service Blueprint is structured into **five horizontal zones**, separated by **three critical lines**. Understanding each component is essential for any KTU 14-mark design question.

### Component 1: Customer Actions (Top Zone)
- All steps the customer performs *before*, *during*, and *after* consuming the service.
- Examples: filling a form, waiting in queue, making payment, providing feedback.

### Component 2: Frontstage / Front-Office Actions
- The visible actions performed by the service employee *in direct contact* with the customer.
- These occur **above the line of visibility**.
- Examples: greeting, taking order, serving food.

### Component 3: Line of Visibility (Separator 1)
- The horizontal line that **separates what the customer sees from what is hidden**.
- Above the line = customer-visible.
- Below the line = customer-invisible (backstage).

### Component 4: Backstage / Back-Office Actions
- The invisible activities performed by service employees **not in direct contact** with the customer.
- Examples: cooking food, processing data, preparing the bill.

### Component 5: Line of Internal Interaction (Separator 2)
- The horizontal line that separates **backstage contact employees** from **support process staff**.

### Component 6: Support Processes (Bottom Zone)
- The internal company activities that enable the frontline and back-office staff to deliver the service.
- Examples: IT systems, HR, procurement, training, facility management.

### Component 7: Line of External Interaction (Top Line)
- The horizontal line that **separates the customer from the service organization** — this is the **Line of Interaction**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these lines matter (the "Why" logic):**
> The three lines convert a vague service description into a *precise operational schematic*. The *Line of Visibility* answers the question *"What does the customer actually see?"* — critical for branding. The *Line of Internal Interaction* answers *"Where does the employee-to-employee handoff occur?"* — critical for accountability.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following formulas are routinely tested in the KTU ESE for service process analysis. They are derived from **Operations Management** principles applied to services.

| # | Formula / Metric | Symbolic Form | Unit | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Total Cycle Time** | $CT_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i$ | minutes | Total time a customer spends in the system |
| 2 | **Value-Added Time** | $VAT = \sum T_{VA}$ | minutes | Time that actually creates customer value |
| 3 | **Process Efficiency Ratio** | $\eta = \dfrac{VAT}{CT_{total}} \times 100\%$ | percent | Measures service process leanness |
| 4 | **Customer Wait Time** | $CWT = CT_{total} - VAT$ | minutes | Non-value-added time; KPI for operations |
| 5 | **Service Capacity** | $\mu = \dfrac{1}{T_{server}}$ | customers/hour | Maximum throughput per server |
| 6 | **Failure Rate (Poisson)** | $P(k) = \dfrac{\lambda^k e^{-\lambda}}{k!}$ | dimensionless | Probability of k failures in a service cycle |
| 7 | **Service Gap (SERVQUAL)** | $Gap_5 = Perception - Expectation$ | score (1–7) | Overall service quality gap |
| 8 | **Utilization (ρ)** | $\rho = \dfrac{\lambda_{arrival}}{\mu_{service}}$ | dimensionless | Server load (0 ≤ ρ < 1 for stability) |
| 9 | **Little's Law** | $L = \lambda \cdot W$ | entities | Avg. number in system = arrival rate × wait time |
| 10 | **Throughput Time Index** | $TTI = \dfrac{CWT}{CT_{total}}$ | ratio | Higher = worse customer experience |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Isolation Rule:** Always leave one blank line before and after every `$$` block to prevent markdown rendering breaks.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility of Service Blueprinting

- **Healthcare Engineering:** Mapping patient flow from admission → diagnostics → surgery → discharge to reduce average length of stay (ALOS).
- **Hospitality Industry:** Designing hotel check-in processes to minimize guest wait time below 5 minutes (industry KPI).
- **Banking & FinTech:** Blueprinting digital onboarding journeys to reduce customer drop-off rates.
- **Aviation:** Mapping passenger experience from ticket booking → security → boarding → in-flight → baggage claim.
- **IT Service Management (ITSM):** Used in ITIL framework to design *Incident Management* and *Service Request Fulfillment* processes.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for the 3 Lines (top to bottom):**
> **"I-V-I"** → **I**nteraction → **V**isibility → **I**nternal Interaction
> This sequence is the most frequently asked KTU 2-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Seven-Step Service Blueprinting Methodology (Exhaustive Derivation)

The KTU 2024 syllabus specifies a structured methodology. Below is the **complete step-by-step derivation** of how a service blueprint is constructed.

### Step 1: Identify the Service Process to be Blueprinted

This is the **boundary-setting step**. The engineer must clearly define:
- The **scope** of the service (e.g., "passenger check-in at airport counter").
- The **start point** (customer enters the queue) and the **end point** (customer receives boarding pass).
- The **stakeholders** involved.

Mathematically, we define the service process as a tuple:

$$
S = \{A, T, C, E\}
$$

where:
- $A$ = set of all activities
- $T$ = set of timestamps
- $C$ = set of customers
- $E$ = set of environmental constraints

### Step 2: Identify the Customer Segments

Customers are not homogeneous. We segment them by:
- **Demographic** factors (age, income).
- **Behavioral** factors (frequency of use).
- **Need-based** factors (purpose of service).

### Step 3: Map the Customer Journey (Top Zone of Blueprint)

For each customer segment, list every action the customer performs:

$$
A_{customer} = \{a_1, a_2, a_3, \ldots, a_n\}
$$

Each action $a_i$ has an associated time $t_i$ (in minutes).

### Step 4: Map the Frontstage Employee Actions (Line of Visibility Above)

For each customer action, identify the *visible* employee activity:

$$
A_{front} = \{f_1, f_2, \ldots, f_m\}
$$

These are direct contact moments — also called **"Moments of Truth"** (Carlzon, 1987).

### Step 5: Map the Backstage Employee Actions (Line of Internal Interaction Above)

These are invisible activities:

$$
A_{back} = \{b_1, b_2, \ldots, b_p\}
$$

### Step 6: Map the Support Processes (Bottom Zone)

$$
A_{support} = \{s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_q\}
$$

### Step 7: Add the Three Lines and Identify Failure Points

For each interaction, attach a **failure point (FP)** wherever the service could fail.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why we need Failure Points (FPs):**
> A failure point is a *specific step* in the service process where a service defect is likely to occur. By mapping FPs in the blueprint, managers can design *poka-yokes* (mistake-proofing) controls.

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation: Process Efficiency of a Blueprinted Service

Let us derive the **Process Efficiency Ratio** $\eta$ for a typical blueprinted service.

### Given Data (Example: Coffee Shop Service)

| Step | Activity | Zone | Time (min) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Customer enters & queues | Customer | 2.0 |
| 2 | Barista greets | Frontstage | 0.5 |
| 3 | Order taken | Frontstage | 1.0 |
| 4 | Customer waits | Customer | 4.0 |
| 5 | Coffee prepared | Backstage | 3.0 |
| 6 | Payment processed | Frontstage | 1.5 |
| 7 | Customer receives coffee | Frontstage | 0.5 |
| 8 | Customer leaves | Customer | 1.0 |

### Step 1: Calculate Total Cycle Time

$$
\begin{aligned}
CT_{total} &= T_1 + T_2 + T_3 + T_4 + T_5 + T_6 + T_7 + T_8 \\
&= 2.0 + 0.5 + 1.0 + 4.0 + 3.0 + 1.5 + 0.5 + 1.0 \\
&= 13.5 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Identify Value-Added Time (VAT)

Value-added steps are those the customer *perceives* as creating value:
- Step 2: Greeting (0.5) — *Customer values the welcome*
- Step 3: Order taking (1.0) — *Customer values the interaction*
- Step 5: Coffee preparation (3.0) — *Core value creation*
- Step 7: Receiving coffee (0.5) — *Final value delivery*

$$
\begin{aligned}
VAT &= 0.5 + 1.0 + 3.0 + 0.5 \\
&= 5.0 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Calculate Process Efficiency Ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \dfrac{VAT}{CT_{total}} \times 100\% \\
&= \dfrac{5.0}{13.5} \times 100\% \\
&\approx 37.04\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Calculate Customer Wait Time (CWT)

$$
\begin{aligned}
CWT &= CT_{total} - VAT \\
&= 13.5 - 5.0 \\
&= 8.5 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Service Engineering Insight:**
> Only **37%** of the customer's time is value-adding. The remaining **63%** (8.5 min) is *pure waste* — wait, queue, payment, and transit. **KTU-Standard Industry Benchmark:** A world-class service should have $\eta \geq 60\%$. The blueprint reveals clear improvement opportunities: introduce *mobile ordering* (eliminates queue and order-taking) and *contactless payment* (reduces step 6).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: The 5 Zones × Failure Point Mapping

The following matrix is the **KTU High-Yield Analytical Table** for blueprint-based service failure analysis.

| Blueprint Zone | Typical Failure Point | Root Cause | Poka-Yoke Solution |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Customer Actions** | Customer arrives late | Miscommunication | SMS/email reminders |
| **Frontstage** | Wrong order taken | Language barrier | Visual menu tablets |
| **Line of Visibility** | Customer misperception of wait | No progress indicator | Digital queue display |
| **Backstage** | Stock-out of raw materials | Poor inventory mgmt. | Real-time POS integration |
| **Support Process** | IT system crash | Hardware failure | Cloud-redundant architecture |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Service Blueprint Architecture (Five-Zone Layout)

This diagram maps the **complete structural architecture of a Service Blueprint**, showing all five zones, the three lines, and the directional flow of interactions.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Zone1["CUSTOMER ZONE - Customer Actions"]
        C1["Step 1: Enters Service Area"]
        C2["Step 2: Submits Request"]
        C3["Step 3: Waits for Service"]
        C4["Step 4: Receives Service"]
        C5["Step 5: Provides Feedback"]
        C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4 --> C5
    end

    subgraph Zone2["FRONTSTAGE ZONE - Visible Employee Actions"]
        F1["Employee Greets Customer"]
        F2["Employee Records Request"]
        F3["Employee Delivers Output"]
        F4["Employee Seeks Feedback"]
    end

    subgraph Zone3["BACKSTAGE ZONE - Invisible Employee Actions"]
        B1["Process Planning"]
        B2["Resource Allocation"]
        B3["Quality Check"]
    end

    subgraph Zone4["SUPPORT PROCESS ZONE - Internal Systems"]
        S1["IT Infrastructure"]
        S2["HR and Training"]
        S3["Supply Chain"]
        S4["Finance and Billing"]
    end

    Line1["LINE OF INTERACTION"]:::line
    Line2["LINE OF VISIBILITY"]:::line
    Line3["LINE OF INTERNAL INTERACTION"]:::line

    Zone1 -. interacts via .-> Line1
    Line1 -. separates .-> Zone2
    Zone2 -. visible above .-> Line2
    Line2 -. hidden below .-> Zone3
    Zone3 -. internal handoff .-> Line3
    Line3 -. enables .-> Zone4

    C2 -.- F2
    C4 -.- F3
    C5 -.- F4
    F2 --- B1
    F3 --- B3
    B1 --- S3
    B3 --- S1

    classDef line fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Process Flow: Building a Service Blueprint (Seven-Step Methodology)

This flowchart depicts the **engineering procedure** to construct a service blueprint from scratch — a frequent KTU 14-mark question.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Step 1: Define Service Scope"] --> B["Step 2: Identify Customer Segments"]
    B --> C["Step 3: Map Customer Journey"]
    C --> D["Step 4: Map Frontstage Actions"]
    D --> E["Step 5: Map Backstage Actions"]
    E --> F["Step 6: Map Support Processes"]
    F --> G["Step 7: Draw the 3 Lines"]
    G --> H["Step 8: Identify Failure Points"]
    H --> I["Step 9: Add Time Standards"]
    I --> J["Step 10: Validate with Customers"]
    J --> K["FINAL SERVICE BLUEPRINT"]
    K --> L{"Service Acceptable?"}
    L -- No --> M["Redesign Process"]
    M --> A
    L -- Yes --> N["Deploy and Monitor"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Blueprint of a Coffee Shop (Practical Illustration)

This block diagram simulates a real coffee shop blueprint, the same example used in Section 3.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TopZone["TOP - CUSTOMER ACTIONS"]
        CA1["Enters Shop"]
        CA2["Joins Queue"]
        CA3["Reads Menu"]
        CA4["Places Order"]
        CA5["Pays Bill"]
        CA6["Receives Coffee"]
        CA7["Exits Shop"]
        CA1 --> CA2 --> CA3 --> CA4 --> CA5 --> CA6 --> CA7
    end

    subgraph FrontZone["FRONTSTAGE - Visible to Customer"]
        FA1["Greets with Smile"]
        FA2["Takes Order on Tablet"]
        FA3["Processes Payment"]
        FA4["Hands Over Coffee"]
    end

    subgraph BackZone["BACKSTAGE - Hidden from Customer"]
        BA1["Grinds Coffee Beans"]
        BA2["Brews Espresso"]
        BA3["Steams Milk"]
    end

    subgraph SupportZone["SUPPORT PROCESSES - Internal Systems"]
        SA1["POS Software"]
        SA2["Inventory Mgmt System"]
        SA3["Staff Scheduling App"]
        SA4["Supplier Network"]
    end

    TopZone --- LineA["LINE OF INTERACTION"]:::lineStyle
    FrontZone --- LineB["LINE OF VISIBILITY"]:::lineStyle
    BackZone --- LineC["LINE OF INTERNAL INTERACTION"]:::lineStyle

    CA4 -.- FA2
    CA5 -.- FA3
    CA6 -.- FA4
    FA2 --- BA1
    FA2 --- BA3
    BA1 --- SA2
    BA2 --- SA1
    SA3 --- SA4

    classDef lineStyle fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:4px,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define Service Blueprinting. List any two of its key components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Definition (2 marks):* Service Blueprinting is a visual process-mapping technique that details the service delivery process from the customer's perspective. It distinguishes between customer actions, frontstage (visible) employee actions, backstage (invisible) employee actions, and support processes, separated by three lines: the Line of Interaction, Line of Visibility, and Line of Internal Interaction.

*Two Key Components (1 mark):*
1. **Customer Actions** – steps performed by the customer.
2. **Frontstage Actions** – visible activities of service employees in direct contact with the customer.

> [!TIP]
> **Mark Allocation Cue:** Definition = 2 marks, Components = 1 mark. Don't exceed 4 lines.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is the "Line of Visibility" in a Service Blueprint? Why is it important?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Definition (1.5 marks):* The **Line of Visibility** is the horizontal line in a Service Blueprint that separates the frontstage (customer-visible) activities from the backstage (customer-invisible) activities performed by the service organization.

*Importance (1.5 marks):*
1. It helps managers decide **what aspects of the service to expose** to the customer (e.g., open kitchen concept in restaurants).
2. It enables **strategic concealment** of complex, unattractive, or error-prone backstage activities to maintain customer confidence.
3. It is critical for **service branding and customer perception management**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the **five components** of a Service Blueprint with a neat diagram. Discuss the significance of the three lines used in the blueprint. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Construct a **Service Blueprint** for a *Hotel Check-in Process*. Identify the failure points and suggest poka-yoke (mistake-proofing) solutions. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The five components are:

1. **Customer Actions** (Top Zone): All steps the customer performs from the moment they engage with the service until disengagement. *Example:* Walking to the hotel reception, presenting ID, signing the register.

2. **Frontstage Actions:** Visible activities of the receptionist in direct contact with the guest. *Example:* Greeting, verifying ID, handing over the room key.

3. **Line of Visibility:** Horizontal line separating frontstage from backstage.

4. **Backstage Actions:** Invisible activities. *Example:* Room allotment by the manager, housekeeping preparation, system entry of guest details.

5. **Support Processes:** Internal systems enabling the service. *Example:* Property Management System (PMS), housekeeping software, billing module.

**Significance of the Three Lines:**

- **Line of Interaction:** Separates the customer from the service organization; defines the boundary of the service encounter.
- **Line of Visibility:** Distinguishes visible service elements from hidden ones — guides the *aesthetic* and *branding* of the service.
- **Line of Internal Interaction:** Separates contact employees (backstage) from support staff — clarifies **internal accountability** and handoffs.

*(A neatly drawn blueprint diagram should accompany this answer — see Section 4.3 for reference structure.)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Hotel Check-in Service Blueprint:**

| Zone | Step | Activity |
|---|---|---|
| **Customer Actions** | 1 | Guest arrives at hotel lobby |
| | 2 | Approaches reception desk |
| | 3 | Presents ID & reservation |
| | 4 | Signs registration form |
| | 5 | Receives room key |
| | 6 | Proceeds to room |
| **Frontstage** | A | Receptionist greets guest |
| | B | Verifies booking in PMS |
| | C | Processes check-in form |
| | D | Hands over key & welcome kit |
| **Backstage** | E | Manager confirms room availability |
| | F | Housekeeping verifies room readiness |
| **Support** | G | Property Management System (PMS) |
| | H | Housekeeping coordination software |

**Identified Failure Points (FPs) and Poka-Yoke Solutions:**

| FP# | Failure Point | Poka-Yoke Solution |
|---|---|---|
| FP1 | Wrong room key issued | Barcode scan of key tag matches PMS record |
| FP2 | Room not ready | PMS auto-blocks dirty rooms from allocation |
| FP3 | Long check-in time | Pre-check-in via mobile app with QR code |
| FP4 | Lost reservation | Cloud-synced PMS with double-redundancy |
| FP5 | Guest dissatisfaction | Automated post-check-in feedback SMS |

---

### 📗 Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **(a)** Differentiate between **Frontstage** and **Backstage** activities in a Service Blueprint. Illustrate with the example of an **airline check-in process**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A hospital records the following data for its **Out-Patient Department (OPD)** consultation process. Calculate the **(i) Total Cycle Time, (ii) Value-Added Time, (iii) Process Efficiency Ratio, and (iv) Customer Wait Time**. Suggest two improvements based on the blueprint logic. **(7 marks)**

| Step | Activity | Zone | Time (min) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Patient arrives and registers | Customer | 3 |
| 2 | Nurse records vitals | Frontstage | 4 |
| 3 | Patient waits for doctor | Customer | 25 |
| 4 | Doctor consults patient | Frontstage | 8 |
| 5 | Doctor writes prescription | Backstage | 3 |
| 6 | Patient pays bill | Customer | 5 |
| 7 | Pharmacist dispenses medicine | Frontstage | 4 |
| 8 | Patient exits | Customer | 2 |

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Difference between Frontstage and Backstage:**

| Parameter | Frontstage | Backstage |
|---|---|---|
| **Visibility** | Above the Line of Visibility | Below the Line of Visibility |
| **Customer Contact** | Direct | Indirect / None |
| **Aesthetic Standards** | High (uniforms, scripts) | Functional |
| **Employee Skills** | Soft skills, communication | Technical / specialized |
| **Examples in Airline** | Check-in agent, gate staff | Baggage handlers, loaders |

**Airline Check-in Example:**

- **Frontstage:** Check-in agent greets passenger, verifies ticket, weighs luggage, issues boarding pass, directs to gate.
- **Backstage:** Baggage tagging and routing, seat assignment algorithm, crew briefing, flight-deck preparation, ground fueling.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**(i) Total Cycle Time (CT):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CT &= 3 + 4 + 25 + 8 + 3 + 5 + 4 + 2 \\
&= 54 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Value-Added Time (VAT):**
Value-added steps (customer-perceivable value): Step 2 (vitals = 4), Step 4 (consultation = 8), Step 7 (medicine = 4).

$$
\begin{aligned}
VAT &= 4 + 8 + 4 \\
&= 16 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Process Efficiency Ratio (η):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \dfrac{VAT}{CT} \times 100\% \\
&= \dfrac{16}{54} \times 100\% \\
&\approx 29.63\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Customer Wait Time (CWT):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CWT &= CT - VAT \\
&= 54 - 16 \\
&= 38 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Two Suggested Improvements:**

1. **Reduce Step 3 wait (25 min):** Implement an *online token system* and digital queue display, parallel consultation rooms, and triage-based priority routing.
2. **Parallelize Steps 1 & 2:** Use *self-service kiosks* for registration (BP, weight, temperature auto-recorded), allowing vitals recording and registration to happen simultaneously.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mistake 1:** Students often write only *one* improvement and skip the second → lose 1 mark. Always provide **two** distinct improvements.
> 2. **Mistake 2:** Forgetting to **include the Line of Visibility in the diagram** → loses 1 mark. Even a hand-drawn sketch must show the three lines.
> 3. **Mistake 3:** Confusing *Customer Actions* with *Frontstage Actions* — they are **two different zones** of the blueprint.
> 4. **Mistake 4:** Failing to convert minutes to hours when computing service capacity $\mu$ → unit-mismatch penalty (-0.5 mark).
> 5. **Mistake 5:** Not identifying *at least 3 failure points* in a 7-mark blueprint question → loses 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **📌 High-Density Revision Summary — Service Blueprinting**

- ✅ **Definition:** Service Blueprinting is a process-flow diagram that visually maps the service delivery system across five zones.
- ✅ **Originator:** **G. Lynn Shostack** (1982) — refined by **Chase & Erikson** (1985).
- ✅ **Five Zones (top to bottom):**
   1. Customer Actions
   2. Frontstage (Visible) Employee Actions
   3. Backstage (Invisible) Employee Actions
   4. Support Processes
   5. Physical Evidence (sometimes a sixth lateral column)
- ✅ **Three Critical Lines (top to bottom):**
   1. **Line of Interaction** — separates customer from service provider.
   2. **Line of Visibility** — separates visible from invisible activities.
   3. **Line of Internal Interaction** — separates contact employees from support staff.
- ✅ **Failure Point (FP):** A specific step in the blueprint where a service defect is likely. Poka-yoke solutions should be proposed.
- ✅ **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
   - $CT = \sum T_i$
   - $\eta = \dfrac{VAT}{CT} \times 100\%$
   - $CWT = CT - VAT$
   - Little's Law: $L = \lambda W$
   - Utilization: $\rho = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu}$
- ✅ **Process Efficiency Benchmark:** World-class services target $\eta \geq 60\%$.
- ✅ **"Moments of Truth":** Every frontstage interaction is a Moment of Truth (Jan Carlzon, SAS Airlines).
- ✅ **Application Domains:** Healthcare, hospitality, banking, aviation, retail, IT services (ITIL).
- ✅ **Difference from Process Flowchart:** A blueprint is *customer-centric*, while a process flowchart is *internal-centric*.
- ✅ **Always draw three lines** in the diagram for full marks.
- ✅ **Always attach time standards** to each step (minutes) for 7-mark questions.
- ✅ **Always propose poka-yoke** (mistake-proofing) for each failure point identified.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Elements of Service Delivery System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECBT861 - SERVICE SCIENCE AND SERVICE OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Elements of Service Delivery System # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Service Delivery System (SDS) Definition:** > A *Service Delivery System* is the **integrated configuration of people, processes, technology, physical evidence, and customer interfaces** that collectively converts service inputs (customer needs, information, materials) into service outputs (value, experiences, solutions) at a defined point of consumption. It is the **operational backbone** that determines how a service offering is *conceived, designed, produced, transmitted, and consumed*. The Service Delivery System is studied under **Service Operations Management** as the mechanism by which *value-in-exchange* is created and delivered across the *Service-Profit Chain*. In the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned), the SDS is treated as an **outcome-driven system** mapped directly to the course outcome **CO2**: *"Analyze the elements and process flows of a service delivery system."* ### 1.2 The Five Canonical Elements of a Service Delivery System According to the consolidated framework adapted from **Chase & Hayes (1991)**, **Booms & Bitner's Services Marketing Triangle**, and the **Fitzsimmons Service Model**, the Service Delivery System consists of five interrelated elements: | # | Element | Core Function | |---|---------|---------------| | **1** | **Customers (The Recipient)** | Source of demand, co-creator of value | | **2** | **Service Providers (The People)** | Human interface executing the service | | **3** | **Service Process (The Workflow)** | Sequence of activities transforming input → output | | **4** | **Service Technology (The Enablers)** | Tools, platforms, automation, IT systems | | **5** | **Servicescape (The Physical Evidence)** | Facility, equipment, ambiance, environment | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A service delivery system is **not a list of parts** — it is a **dynamic configuration** where failure in one element cascades into all others. For example, a brilliant process fails without trained people; excellent people fail without proper technology. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant" Intuition Imagine a **fine-dining restaurant** in Kochi. What makes your meal arrive hot, tasty, and on time? - **The Customer (You)** walk in, order, and eat → you are the *recipient* and *co-producer* of the meal. - **The Chef & Waiter** → they are the *service providers*, performing intangible acts of skill. - **The Kitchen Workflow** (prep → cook → plate → serve) → this is the *process*. - **The Oven, POS System, Smart Kitchen Display** → the *technology*. - **The Ambiance (lighting, music, table setting, aroma)** → the *servicescape* or *physical evidence*. If any one of these five is broken — burnt food, rude waiter, broken oven, dirty restroom, chaotic billing — the **entire service experience collapses**, even if the other four are perfect. This is the essence of a Service Delivery System. > [!TIP] > **Memory Trick (for KTU exams):** Remember **"C-P-P-T-S"** → **C**ustomer, **P**eople, **P**rocess, **T**echnology, **S**ervicescape. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework ## 2.1 Element-Wise Theoretical Breakdown ### Element 1: **Customers as Service Co-Producers** Unlike manufacturing, the customer is **inside** the service delivery system. Value is created in the *customer's experience* during consumption (the "moment of truth" — Jan Carlzon, SAS Airlines). **Why it matters:** - Customer participates in service production (filling a form, queuing, self-checkout). - Customer mood, knowledge, and effort affect service quality. - Customer heterogeneity creates *variability* in output. **How it manifests in KTU problems:** - Customer *role ambiguity* leads to service failure. - *Self-service technologies* (ATM, mobile banking) shift the customer into the operational process. --- ### Element 2: **Service Providers (People Element)** People provide the *humanic* element of the service. They are the **brand on the inside** (Leornard Berry). **Sub-components:** 1. **Frontstage employees** (visible to customer — receptionist, teller, cabin crew) 2. **Backstage employees** (invisible — kitchen staff, IT support, back-office) 3. **Contact vs. Non-contact staff** (by degree of customer interaction) 4. **Service culture & training systems** > [!NOTE] > **The "Service-Profit Chain" Link (Heskett et al., HBR 1994):** > *Internal Service Quality → Employee Satisfaction → Employee Productivity → Service Value → Customer Satisfaction → Customer Loyalty → Profit Growth* > This chain demonstrates that **the People element is causally upstream of all value outcomes**. --- ### Element 3: **Service Process (Operational Workflow)** The process is the **blueprint of activity sequencing** that transforms the customer's need into a fulfilled service outcome. **Key Process Characteristics (Chase, Jacobs & Aquilano):** - **Degree of customer contact** (high vs. low) - **Degree of customization** (mass vs. bespoke) - **Degree of divergence** (number of paths through the system) - **Degree of complexity** **Process Flow Categories:** - **Linear flow** (assembly-line style — fast food) - **Branching flow** (multiple service options — hospital OPD) - **Hub-and-spoke flow** (central triage with peripheral specialization — airline hub) - **Iterative flow** (feedback loops — software development / consulting) --- ### Element 4: **Service Technology (Enabling Infrastructure)** Technology augments or replaces human labor in the SDS. In the **Katz et al. (2003)** classification, service technology has three roles: 1. **Substitution** — replacing manual steps (ATM for bank teller) 2. **Augmentation** — assisting workers (CRM dashboards) 3. **Transformation** — fundamentally redesigning the service (cloud computing, AI chatbots) **Kerala 2024 Context:** UPI-based payments, e-Sanjeevani telemedicine, KSRTC online booking, Cochin International Airport digital boarding — all are SDS technology integrations. --- ### Element 5: **Servicescape (Physical Evidence)** Coined by **Booms & Bitner (1981)** and extended by **Bitner (1992)**. The servicescape is the **physical environment** in which the service is delivered and consumed. **Three Dimensions of Servicescape:** | Dimension | Examples | |-----------|----------| | **Ambient conditions** | Temperature, lighting, music, scent, noise | | **Spatial layout & functionality** | Furniture, signage, queuing lines, equipment arrangement | | **Signs, symbols & artifacts** | Logos, certificates, brochures, staff uniforms | > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Servicescape affects:** > (a) Customer emotions & satisfaction, > (b) Employee behavior & productivity, > (c) Approach/avoidance behaviors. > In a hospital, dim lighting calms patients; in a fast-food outlet, bright lighting speeds up turnover. The *same* element, different effect — a classic KTU 14-mark discussion point. --- ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Conceptual Map (Cheat Sheet) | Concept | Definition | Key Theorist | Formula / Metric | |---------|-----------|--------------|------------------| | Service Encounter | Moment of interaction between customer & service system | Surprenant & Solomon (1987) | *Service Encounter Quality = f(Provider, Customer, Environment)* | | Moment of Truth | Instance when customer evaluates service | Jan Carlzon (1987) | One encounter ≈ Brand image (50/year in airline) | | Servicescape | Physical environment of service | Booms & Bitner | Affects satisfaction, behavior, perception | | Service Blueprint | Visual diagram of process flow | Shostack (1984) | Line of Visibility, Line of Interaction | | Service-Profit Chain | Link from employees to profit | Heskett et al. | Linear causal chain of 7 variables | | Co-Production | Customer participation in service creation | Prahalad & Ramaswamy | Value = f(Provider input × Customer input) | | Flower of Service | Core service + 8 supplementary services | Christopher Lovelock | Petals around core benefit | | Gap Model | 5 gaps causing service failure | Parasuraman et al. | Gap 5 = f(Gap 1, 2, 3, 4) | > [!TIP] > **For 3-mark questions:** Memorize the **Flower of Service** (core + petals) and **Service-Profit Chain** order. They are the two highest-frequency KTU 14-mark triggers in this module. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering & Management Utility - **Healthcare SDS:** AIIMS OPD uses triage (process), EHR (technology), nurses (people), reception (servicescape), and patients (customers). - **E-Governance:** Kerala MVD vehicle registration is a digitally-mediated SDS where technology has *substituted* much of the human interface. - **Banking:** SBI YONO platform integrates all 5 elements; failure of *one* element (e.g., server crash) halts the entire value chain. - **Education:** KTU's own LMS, exam cell, and digital valuation are an SDS in action. # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Service Design Models ## 3.1 The **Service Triangle (Booms & Bitner, 1981)** The Service Triangle is the **canonical 2D framework** that maps all five elements of the SDS onto a closed value-creation loop. ### Structural Composition: The triangle has **four nodes** and **six edges**: - **Nodes:** Company, Customer, Provider, Service System (you, the designer) - **Edges:** Marketing, Internal Marketing, Interactive Marketing, Design, Development, Quality Control ### Analytical Walkthrough: **Step 1 — Identify the nodes:** - **N1 = Company** (the service firm, e.g., Apollo Hospitals) - **N2 = Customer** (the patient) - **N3 = Service Provider** (the doctor / nurse) - **N4 = Service System** (the designer / management) **Step 2 — Identify the relationships (edges):** 1. **External Marketing:** N1 → N2 (promise made to customer) 2. **Internal Marketing:** N1 → N3 (training, equipping provider) 3. **Interactive Marketing:** N3 ↔ N2 (moment of service encounter) 4. **Service Design & Development:** N4 → N1, N3, N2 (systematic creation) 5. **Quality Assurance Loops:** feedback edges from all three actors to N4 **Step 3 — Evaluate integrity:** The system is in equilibrium when: $$\text{Promise} = \text{Perception} = \text{Delivery}$$ Where the "perception filter" function is: $$P_{final} = \int_{0}^{T} (\text{ProviderBehavior}(t) \cdot \text{CustomerFilter}(t)) \, dt$$ **Step 4 — Identify failure points:** - If **External Marketing** over-promises (e.g., "5-minute doctor consultation") and provider cannot deliver → **Gap 5** of Parasuraman's model occurs. --- ## 3.2 The **Service Blueprint (Shostack, 1984)** The Service Blueprint is the **engineering blueprint** of a service, separating visible from invisible operations. ### Standard Blueprint Zones (with vertical lines): | Line | Name | Separates | |------|------|-----------| | **Line 1** | **Line of Interaction** | Customer ↔ Service Provider | | **Line 2** | **Line of Visibility** | Frontstage ↔ Backstage | | **Line 3** | **Line of Internal Interaction** | Service Provider ↔ Support System | | **Line 4** | **Line of Implementation** | Service System ↔ External Suppliers | ### Blueprint Components (rows, top → bottom): 1. **Customer actions** (above Line 1) 2. **Frontstage / Onstage actions** (between Lines 1 and 2) 3. **Backstage actions** (between Lines 2 and 3) 4. **Support processes** (between Lines 3 and 4) 5. **Physical evidence** (visible cues throughout) ### Mathematical Representation of Process Time: Total service delivery time: $$T_{total} = T_{customer} + T_{front} + T_{back} + T_{support} + T_{wait}$$ Service efficiency: $$\eta_{service} = \frac{T_{value\text{-}added}}{T_{total}}$$ **Step-by-step application (Example: KSRTC Online Ticket Booking):** | Step | Zone | Actor | Activity | Time | |------|------|-------|----------|------| | 1 | Customer | Customer | Visit website / app | 0–60 s | | 2 | Customer | Customer | Enter journey details | 60–90 s | | 3 | Frontstage | UI / App | Display options, seats | 90–100 s | | 4 | Backstage | Server | Query database | 100–120 s | | 5 | Support | Payment gateway | Verify UPI payment | 120–150 s | | 6 | Frontstage | UI | Show confirmation | 150–155 s | | 7 | Customer | Customer | Download ticket | 155–180 s | The blueprint makes the *invisible* (server query) visible — a critical KTU analytical skill. --- ## 3.3 The **Flower of Service (Lovelock & Wirtz, 2011)** A service is rarely a single atom. It is a **core service surrounded by supplementary services (petals)**. ### Mathematical Formulation: $$S_{total} = S_{core} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot S_i$$ Where: - $S_{total}$ = Total perceived service value - $S_{core}$ = Core service benefit - $S_i$ = *i*-th supplementary service - $w_i$ = Weight of importance for that petal - $n$ = Number of petals (typically 8) ### The 8 Petals (Supplementary Services): | # | Petal | Example (Airline) | |---|-------|------------------| | 1 | **Information** | Flight status, fare queries | | 2 | **Consultation** | Travel advice, seat selection | | 3 | **Order-taking** | Booking reservation | | 4 | **Hospitality** | Food, beverages, lounges | | 5 | **Safekeeping** | Baggage handling, insurance | | 6 | **Exceptions** | Refunds, complaints, delays | | 7 | **Billing** | Invoicing, payment processing | | 8 | **Payment** | UPI, card, cash processing | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Insight:** A failure in any petal **negates the value of the core service**. A 5-star meal served to a customer who was overcharged for parking = perceived failure. This is the **integrated** nature of the SDS. --- ## 3.4 The **Service-Profit Chain (Heskett, Sasser & Schlesinger, 1994)** A **causal mathematical model** linking internal elements to financial outcomes. ### Algebraic Form: $$\text{Profit} \propto \sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{CustomerLifetimeValue}_i - \text{Cost}_i$$ Decomposed through the chain: $$\text{Profit} = f(LS) \cdot g(EP) \cdot h(SV) \cdot k(CS) \cdot p(CL)$$ Where: - $LS$ = *Internal Service Quality* (work environment) - $EP$ = *Employee Satisfaction & Productivity* - $SV$ = *Service Value Delivered to Customer* - $CS$ = *Customer Satisfaction* - $CL$ = *Customer Loyalty* - $f, g, h, k, p$ = positive monotonic functions ### Step-by-step evaluation: **Step 1:** Improve workplace conditions (pay, tools, culture) → $LS \uparrow$ **Step 2:** Satisfied employees serve better → $EP \uparrow$ **Step 3:** Higher productivity lowers cost → $\text{Price} \downarrow$, value $\uparrow$ **Step 4:** Customer perceives more value → $SV \uparrow$ **Step 5:** Satisfied customers return → $CS \uparrow$, $CL \uparrow$ **Step 6:** Lower acquisition cost, higher referrals → $\text{Profit} \uparrow$ This is **the single most cited model** in KTU module-2 examinations for 14-mark questions. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Service Triangle (Booms & Bitner Model) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[COMPANY]:::firmNode B[CUSTOMER]:::custNode C[SERVICE PROVIDER]:::provNode D[SERVICE SYSTEM DESIGNER]:::designNode A -- EXTERNAL MARKETING
Promise --> B A -- INTERNAL MARKETING
Training and Tools --> C C -- INTERACTIVE MARKETING
Service Encounter --> B D -- SERVICE DESIGN --> A D -- SERVICE DEVELOPMENT --> C D -- CUSTOMER RESEARCH --> B B -. FEEDBACK .-> D C -. FEEDBACK .-> D A -. QUALITY METRICS .-> D classDef firmNode fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#0c1e35,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef custNode fill:#c0392b,stroke:#7a1f12,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef provNode fill:#27ae60,stroke:#145a32,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef designNode fill:#e67e22,stroke:#7a4710,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px ``` **Reading the diagram:** The four nodes interact via six relational edges, with the Service System Designer acting as the central integrator of feedback. The flow proceeds clockwise: design → develop → train → encounter → evaluate → redesign. --- ## 4.2 The Five Elements of Service Delivery System — Integrated Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB INPUT[Customer Needs
and Expectations]:::inputNode OUTPUT[Value Delivery
