and Customer Satisfaction]:::outputNode E1[CUSTOMER
Co-producer of value]:::elem1 E2[SERVICE PROVIDERS
Frontstage and Backstage staff]:::elem2 E3[SERVICE PROCESS
Workflow and activities]:::elem3 E4[SERVICE TECHNOLOGY
IT and Automation]:::elem4 E5[SERVICESCAPE
Physical evidence]:::elem5 FB[Feedback Loop
Continuous Improvement]:::fbNode INPUT --> E1 INPUT --> E2 INPUT --> E3 INPUT --> E4 INPUT --> E5 E1 --> OUTPUT E2 --> OUTPUT E3 --> OUTPUT E4 --> OUTPUT E5 --> OUTPUT OUTPUT --> FB FB --> E1 FB --> E2 FB --> E3 FB --> E4 FB --> E5 classDef inputNode fill:#34495e,stroke:#1b2631,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef outputNode fill:#16a085,stroke:#0e6b5a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef elem1 fill:#c0392b,stroke:#7a1f12,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef elem2 fill:#27ae60,stroke:#145a32,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef elem3 fill:#2980b9,stroke:#1a5276,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef elem4 fill:#8e44ad,stroke:#4a235a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef elem5 fill:#d35400,stroke:#6e2c00,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef fbNode fill:#f39c12,stroke:#7d6608,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px ``` **Interpretation:** Every element receives the *same input* (customer needs) and *contributes* to the same *output* (satisfaction). The Feedback Loop ensures the system is **adaptive and self-correcting** — a hallmark of mature service operations. --- ## 4.3 The Service-Profit Chain Causal Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR N1[INTERNAL SERVICE QUALITY
Workplace tools and culture]:::stage1 N2[EMPLOYEE SATISFACTION
Fair pay and respect]:::stage2 N3[EMPLOYEE PRODUCTIVITY
Service output per hour]:::stage3 N4[EXTERNAL SERVICE VALUE
Perceived by customer]:::stage4 N5[CUSTOMER SATISFACTION
Meets expectations]:::stage5 N6[CUSTOMER LOYALTY
Repeat purchase and advocacy]:::stage6 N7[REVENUE GROWTH
Profitability]:::stage7 N1 --> N2 --> N3 --> N4 --> N5 --> N6 --> N7 N7 -. REINVEST .-> N1 N5 -. FEEDBACK .-> N2 N6 -. ADVOCACY .-> N4 classDef stage1 fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#0c1e35,color:#ffffff classDef stage2 fill:#283747,stroke:#0c1e35,color:#ffffff classDef stage3 fill:#21618c,stroke:#0c1e35,color:#ffffff classDef stage4 fill:#2874a6,stroke:#154360,color:#ffffff classDef stage5 fill:#2e86c1,stroke:#1b4f72,color:#ffffff classDef stage6 fill:#3498db,stroke:#1a5276,color:#ffffff classDef stage7 fill:#85c1e9,stroke:#21618c,color:#000000 ``` **Reading the chain:** The diagram shows the linear causality (left → right) and the two critical feedback loops: **Revenue → Internal Service Quality** (reinvestment) and **Customer Loyalty → External Service Value** (advocacy amplifies perception). --- ## 4.4 The Service Blueprint — Schematic Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB L1["LINE OF INTERACTION
Customer Touchpoint"]:::line1 L2["LINE OF VISIBILITY
Frontstage Boundary"]:::line2 L3["LINE OF INTERNAL INTERACTION
Support Boundary"]:::line3 L4["LINE OF IMPLEMENTATION
Supplier Boundary"]:::line4 R1["CUSTOMER ACTIONS
Visit, Order, Pay, Consume"]:::row1 R2["FRONTSTAGE ACTIONS
Greet, Take Order, Serve"]:::row2 R3["BACKSTAGE ACTIONS
Cook, Process, Verify"]:::row3 R4["SUPPORT PROCESSES
Inventory, HR, IT, Training"]:::row4 R1 --- L1 R1 --- L2 R1 --- L3 R1 --- L4 R2 --- L1 R2 --- L2 R2 --- L3 R2 --- L4 R3 --- L1 R3 --- L2 R3 --- L3 R3 --- L4 R4 --- L1 R4 --- L2 R4 --- L3 R4 --- L4 PE[PHYSICAL EVIDENCE
Signage, Ambiance, Equipment, Uniforms]:::peNode PE -. VISIBLE THROUGHOUT .-> R1 PE -. VISIBLE THROUGHOUT .-> R2 PE -. HIDDEN FROM CUSTOMER .-> R3 PE -. HIDDEN FROM CUSTOMER .-> R4 classDef line1 fill:#c0392b,stroke:#7a1f12,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px classDef line2 fill:#e67e22,stroke:#7a4710,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px classDef line3 fill:#27ae60,stroke:#145a32,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px classDef line4 fill:#8e44ad,stroke:#4a235a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px classDef row1 fill:#fdebd0,stroke:#7a4710,color:#000000 classDef row2 fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#145a32,color:#000000 classDef row3 fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1a5276,color:#000000 classDef row4 fill:#f4ecf7,stroke:#4a235a,color:#000000 classDef peNode fill:#f7dc6f,stroke:#7d6608,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px ``` **Interpretation:** The four horizontal **lines of demarcation** correspond exactly to Shostack's blueprint zones. The Physical Evidence layer (yellow) is a *transverse layer* — it appears across all rows but is observed differently by frontstage vs. backstage actors. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *List any five elements of a service delivery system with a one-line example for each.* **Model Answer (5 × 1 = 5 lines max in exam):** 1. **Customer** — co-producer of value (passenger booking flight) 2. **Service Provider** — human interface (cabin crew) 3. **Service Process** — workflow (check-in → boarding → landing) 4. **Service Technology** — automation tools (self check-in kiosk) 5. **Servicescape** — physical environment (airport lounge ambiance) --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Define "Moment of Truth" and explain its relevance in a service delivery system.* **Model Answer:** A **Moment of Truth** (Carlzon, 1987) is the precise instant when a customer interacts with the service and forms an impression. With **50 million moments of truth per year** for SAS Airlines, the cumulative impact defines brand image. **Relevance:** In an SDS, every moment — be it a phone call, a smile, a delivery — is an opportunity to *create or destroy* customer loyalty. Managing each moment systematically is the goal of the service delivery system. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - Students **commonly** write 1–2 pages of generic "services are intangible" text → **0 marks** (irrelevant to the question). > - Students forget to *integrate* all five elements with the chosen model — losing 3–4 marks on **completeness**. > - Diagrams (Service Triangle, Blueprint, Profit Chain) are **mandatory** for full 14 marks; skip them → lose 2–3 marks. > - Use **Indian examples** (KSRTC, SBI YONO, AIIMS, Cochin Airport) — examiners reward local context. > - **Always end** with a 2-line conclusion linking back to **CO2** ("integrate the elements of an SDS…"). --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2 Choice A]** *Explain the concept of the Service Delivery System in detail. Using a real-world example (e.g., hospital, airline, restaurant, bank), describe the five elements of the service delivery system and how they interact to create value for the customer.* #### Sub-Part (a) — 7 Marks *Define Service Delivery System and explain its five elements with reference to a chosen example.* **Step-by-step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Definition (2 marks):** A **Service Delivery System (SDS)** is an *integrated configuration of people, processes, technology, physical evidence, and customers* that converts service inputs into value-creating service outputs at the point of consumption (Chase & Hayes, 1991). It is the operational mechanism that operationalizes the firm's service concept. **Step 2 — Five Elements with Example (5 × 1 = 5 marks):** Using **Apollo Hospitals, Chennai** as the example: | # | Element | Application | |---|---------|-------------| | 1 | **Customer (Patient)** | Co-produces value by providing symptoms, history, consent, and adhering to post-op care | | 2 | **Service Provider** | Doctors, nurses, technicians, billing staff — frontstage (receptionist) and backstage (lab technician) | | 3 | **Service Process** | Registration → Triage → Consultation → Diagnostics → Treatment → Discharge → Follow-up | | 4 | **Service Technology** | Electronic Health Records, MRI/CT, telemedicine, robotic surgery, Apollo 24/7 app | | 5 | **Servicescape** | Cleanliness, signage, calm lighting, comfortable waiting area, wheelchair access | #### Sub-Part (b) — 7 Marks *Using the Booms & Bitner Service Triangle, illustrate how the five elements interact to create value.* **Step-by-step Model Solution:** **Step 3 — Service Triangle Application (4 marks):** - **Company → Customer:** External marketing (Apollo promises "advanced care with empathy") - **Company → Provider:** Internal marketing (continuous medical education, training, competitive pay) - **Provider ↔ Customer:** Interactive marketing (the consultation moment — diagnosis explanation, reassurance) - **Service System Designer:** Designs SOPs, conducts patient surveys, monitors outcomes **Step 4 — Interaction Dynamics (2 marks):** The five elements are *not sequential*; they are *interlocking gears*. The doctor's competence (Provider) is amplified by the EHR system (Technology), guided by the consultation protocol (Process), delivered in a calming room (Servicescape), and evaluated by the patient's perception (Customer). If the doctor is brilliant but the EHR crashes, value collapses. **Step 5 — Conclusion (1 mark):** The integrated value equation is: $$V_{perceived} = (ProviderQuality + TechnologyUptime + ProcessEfficiency + ServicescapeAtmosphere) \times CustomerEngagement$$ > [!TIP] > **Closing line to write in exam:** "Thus, the Service Delivery System of Apollo Hospital is a value-creation ecosystem where the failure of any one element cascades into system-wide dissatisfaction, reinforcing the principle of *integrated service operations management* aligned with CO2." --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2 Choice B]** *"A service is not a single act but a system of interconnected elements."* Critically analyze this statement with reference to the **Service-Profit Chain** and the **Flower of Service** models. Use appropriate diagrams. #### Sub-Part (a) — 7 Marks *Explain the Service-Profit Chain. How does it establish the link between internal service quality and profitability?* **Step-by-step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Origin (1 mark):** Proposed by **Heskett, Sasser & Schlesinger (1994)** in *Harvard Business Review*, the Service-Profit Chain (SPC) is a causal sequence linking 7 variables. **Step 2 — Sequential Explanation (5 marks):** $$LS \rightarrow EP \rightarrow SV \rightarrow CS \rightarrow CL \rightarrow \text{Revenue} \rightarrow \text{Profit}$$ - **Internal Service Quality (ISQ):** Workplace design, tools, training, and culture. - **Employee Satisfaction (ES):** Driven by ISQ; high ES = low attrition. - **Employee Productivity (EP):** Engaged employees serve faster and better. - **External Service Value (ESV):** Customer-perceived value per visit. - **Customer Satisfaction (CS):** Outcome meets expectation. - **Customer Loyalty (CL):** Repeat purchase + advocacy (lower CAC). - **Profit & Growth (P&G):** Higher revenue + lower cost = higher margin. **Step 3 — Linkage Justification (1 mark):** The chain is *non-bypassable*. Firms that try to cut ISQ to save costs see EP fall, CS fall, CL fall, and ultimately revenue collapse. Empirical studies (Sears Roebuck) show a **4% increase in CS → 0.5% revenue uplift** within a quarter. #### Sub-Part (b) — 7 Marks *Describe the Flower of Service model. Using a banking or airline example, show how a failure in any supplementary service negates the value of the core service.* **Step-by-step Model Solution:** **Step 4 — Flower of Service Origin (1 mark):** Devised by **Christopher Lovelock (1992)** to show that a service offering is a *core service* surrounded by 8 *supplementary services* (petals). **Step 5 — Mathematical Representation (1 mark):** $$S_{total} = S_{core} + \sum_{i=1}^{8} w_i \cdot S_i$$ **Step 6 — Banking Example (5 marks):** - **Core Service:** Money withdrawal from a bank account. - **8 Petals:** | # | Petal | Bank Example | |---|-------|--------------| | 1 | Information | Balance inquiry, statement download | | 2 | Consultation | Financial advisor, loan guidance | | 3 | Order-taking | Cheque book request, new account | | 4 | Hospitality | AC lobby, water, friendly staff | | 5 | Safekeeping | Locker, insurance, fraud alert | | 6 | Exceptions | Dispute resolution, loan restructuring | | 7 | Billing | SMS alert, e-statement | | 8 | Payment | UPI, NEFT, IMPS, card swipe | **Step 7 — Failure Demonstration (write explicitly in exam):** If a customer successfully withdraws ₹50,000 (core service OK) but receives a **wrong SMS amount** (Billing petal fails) and the bank refuses to correct it (Exception petal fails), the *entire perceived service value collapses* — even though the core was delivered. This proves the statement in the question: services are *integrated systems*, not isolated acts. > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Diagram Required:** Draw the **Flower diagram with 8 petals** and a labeled center. In the 14-mark question, a correctly drawn diagram carries 2 marks by itself. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **Save this section for last-night revision before KTU exam.** - [x] **SDS = 5 elements** → Customer, Service Provider, Process, Technology, Servicescape (Mnemonic: **C-P-P-T-S**). - [x] **Booms & Bitner Service Triangle** has 4 nodes (Company, Customer, Provider, Designer) and 6 edges. - [x] **Moment of Truth** is the *evaluation instant* — coined by Jan Carlzon of SAS Airlines. - [x] **Servicescape** is a **physical environment** concept, NOT a process concept (common exam trap). - [x] **Service-Profit Chain** is **linear and causal** — break ISQ, the entire chain breaks. - [x] **Flower of Service** has **8 petals** + 1 core = 9 components. Know all 8. - [x] **Service Blueprint** has **4 demarcation lines**: Interaction, Visibility, Internal Interaction, Implementation. - [x] **Customer is INSIDE the SDS** — co-producer, not external recipient. - [x] **Co-Production** is a defining feature of services — Prahalad & Ramaswamy. - [x] **Heskett et al. (1994)** is the standard citation for SPC. - [x] **Shostack (1984)** is the standard citation for Service Blueprint. - [x] **Bitner (1992)** extended servicescape to *employee behaviors*. - [x] **Lovelock (1992)** created the Flower of Service. - [x] Always draw a **diagram** in 14-mark answers (Triangle / Blueprint / Flower / SPC). - [x] Always use **Indian examples** (SBI, KSRTC, Apollo, AIIMS, Cochin Airport) — adds 1–2 extra marks. - [x] **CO2 alignment:** Conclude with — "the integrated nature of these elements demonstrates the systemic character of service operations." - [x] **Avoid these common errors:** - Writing only definitions, no examples → −3 marks - Forgetting the diagram → −2 marks - Listing elements but not explaining their *interaction* → −2 marks - Mixing up Servicescape with Process → −1 mark - Writing the SPC in the wrong order → −2 marks - Not mentioning the specific theorist → −1 mark

topics.title: Service Design: Customer Journey and Service Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT861 - SERVICE SCIENCE AND SERVICE OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Service Design: Customer Journey and Service Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Service Design?

**Service Design** is the activity of planning and organizing a business's resources (people, processes, technology, infrastructure, and communication) in order to (1) directly improve the employee's experience, and (2) indirectly improve the customer's experience, thereby creating value for both the provider and the customer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Stickdorn & Schneider):**
> "Service Design is a holistic, co-creative, and iterative approach to orchestrate people, processes, technology, and physical evidence to co-create value for and with stakeholders throughout the service experience."

In simpler terms, **Service Design** is the art and science of designing experiences that are useful, usable, desirable, and effective for customers, while being efficient and sustainable for the organization.

## 1.2 What is a Customer Journey?

A **Customer Journey** is the complete sum of experiences that a customer goes through when interacting with a company and its products/services. It encompasses every touchpoint, channel, and interaction from the moment a need is triggered until the post-purchase phase and beyond.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> The Customer Journey is the end-to-end sequence of interactions a customer has with a service provider across all channels, touchpoints, and stages of the service consumption process, including pre-purchase, purchase, and post-purchase phases.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The Theatre Experience

Imagine you are going to watch a **movie in a cinema hall**:

| Service Design Element | Cinema Analogy |
|----------------------|----------------|
| **Pre-service stage** | Checking movie timings online, booking tickets on BookMyShow |
| **Service encounter** | Arriving at the theatre, buying popcorn, finding the seat |
| **Visible contact员工** | The ticket checker, the popcorn vendor, the usher |
| **Backstage processes** | Projector room, AC system, kitchen preparing popcorn |
| **Physical evidence** | The big screen, surround sound, comfortable seats |
| **Post-service** | Exiting, sharing reviews on Google, loyalty card renewal |

Just like a theatre carefully designs every aspect — from the parking lot to the closing credits — to ensure you leave happy and come back, **Service Design** plans every customer interaction deliberately.

## 1.4 Why Service Design and Customer Journey Matter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Industry Statistics (Industry Standard Benchmarks):**
> - A **5% increase in customer retention** can boost profitability by **25% to 95%** (Bain & Co.)
> - Customers who have an emotional connection with a brand have a **306% higher lifetime value** (Motista)
> - **86% of buyers** will pay more for a better customer experience (Walker)

The customer journey acts as the **"GPS of the service experience"** — it tells you exactly where the customer is, what they need, what they're feeling, and how you can serve them better at each point.

## 1.5 Key Terminology You Must Know

- **Touchpoint**: Any point of interaction between the customer and the service provider (e.g., website, call center, billing).
- **Moment of Truth (MoT)**: The specific instance when a customer forms an impression of the service (coined by Jan Carlzon, former CEO of SAS Airlines).
- **Service Encounter**: The period of time during which a consumer directly interacts with a service.
- **Frontstage**: The visible parts of the service that the customer sees and experiences.
- **Backstage**: The hidden parts of the service (internal processes, employees) that the customer does not see.
- **Customer Journey Map (CJM)**: A visual representation of the customer journey.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Principles of Service Design

According to **Stickdorn and Schneider (2010-2018 framework, used in KTU syllabus)**, service design is guided by five interconnected principles:

### Principle 1: User-Centered (Customer-Centricity)
- The customer is at the **heart of every design decision**.
- Empathize with customers, understand their needs, pains, and gains.
- Use personas, empathy maps, and ethnographic research.

### Principle 2: Co-Creative
- All **stakeholders** (customers, employees, partners) must be involved in the design process.
- Co-design workshops, focus groups, and feedback loops.
- Service value is created **with** customers, not just **for** them.

### Principle 3: Sequencing (The Journey View)
- Visualize the service as a **chronological journey** of interconnected steps.
- Each step should be meaningful and contribute to the overall experience.

### Principle 4: Evidencing (Tangible Cues)
- Make the **intangible service tangible** through physical evidence, communication, and behavior.
- Customers judge services by what they can see, hear, feel, and smell.

### Principle 5: Holistic (Whole-System View)
- Consider the **entire ecosystem**, not just isolated parts.
- Balance customer needs, business goals, technology, and employee well-being.

> [!NOTE]
> **Remember the Mnemonic: "U-S-C-E-H"** → User-centered, Sequencing, Co-creative, Evidencing, Holistic.

## 2.2 The Customer Journey — Stages in Detail

The **end-to-end customer journey** is typically broken into **five stages**:

### Stage 1: Awareness (Need Recognition)
- The customer becomes aware of a need or problem.
- Triggers: advertising, social media, word-of-mouth, search engines.
- **Customer Question:** "Do I have a problem? Who can solve it?"

### Stage 2: Consideration (Information Search)
- The customer researches options, compares alternatives, reads reviews.
- Channels: Google search, YouTube reviews, comparison sites, social media.
- **Customer Question:** "What are my options? Which is the best for me?"

### Stage 3: Purchase / Decision (Service Consumption)
- The customer makes the transaction and experiences the core service.
- Touchpoints: website checkout, store visit, app purchase, payment.
- **Customer Question:** "Is this safe? Is this worth my money?"

### Stage 4: Retention (Post-Purchase Experience)
- The customer evaluates the experience and may repurchase or churn.
- Channels: customer support, follow-up emails, loyalty programs.
- **Customer Question:** "Was I satisfied? Will I come back?"

### Stage 5: Advocacy (Loyalty and Recommendation)
- The customer becomes a promoter or detractor.
- Channels: reviews, social media shares, referrals, testimonials.
- **Customer Question:** "Should I tell others about this?"

## 2.3 Moments of Truth — Jan Carlzon's Framework

**Jan Carlzon** (former SAS Airlines CEO) introduced the **Moments of Truth (MoT)** concept in his 1987 book *"Moments of Truth"*. Every time a customer interacts with a service is a "moment of truth" because it forms or modifies their impression.

### The Three Moments of Truth

| Moment | Stage | What Happens | Example (Amazon) |
|--------|-------|--------------|------------------|
| **Zero Moment of Truth (ZMOT)** | Pre-purchase | Customer researches online before buying | Reading Amazon reviews, watching unboxing videos |
| **First Moment of Truth (FMOT)** | At-purchase | Customer encounters the product/service on shelf or screen | Seeing the product page, opening the package |
| **Second Moment of Truth (SMOT)** | Post-purchase | Customer uses the product and forms a final opinion | Using the product, contacting support, returning items |
| **Ultimate Moment of Truth (UMOT)** | Advocacy | Customer shares experience publicly | Writing a review, posting on social media |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Definition:**
> A *Moment of Truth* is "the precise instant at which a customer comes into contact with any aspect of the company and gets an impression of the quality of its service."

## 2.4 Service Blueprinting (Bitner et al.)

A **Service Blueprint** is a visual diagram that maps the service process from the customer's perspective, distinguishing between what the customer experiences and what happens behind the scenes.

### Components of a Service Blueprint

1. **Customer Actions**: The steps the customer takes.
2. **Frontstage Actions**: Visible interactions between customer and service employee.
3. **Backstage Actions**: Invisible employee actions supporting the frontstage.
4. **Support Processes**: Internal systems, technology, and processes enabling service delivery.
5. **Physical Evidence**: Tangible elements the customer sees at each step.

### The Line of Visibility
The horizontal line separating the customer's view from the backstage is the **Line of Visibility** (also called the Line of Interaction at the top, and Line of Internal Interaction lower down).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Conceptual Metrics)

| Metric / Concept | Definition / Formula | Unit | KTU Relevance |
|------------------|----------------------|------|---------------|
| **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)** | $\text{CLV} = \text{Average Purchase Value} \times \text{Purchase Frequency} \times \text{Customer Lifespan}$ | Currency | High-yield for 14-mark numerical questions |
| **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** | $\text{NPS} = \%\text{Promoters} - \%\text{Detractors}$ | Score (-100 to +100) | Common 3-mark question |
| **Customer Satisfaction (CSAT)** | $\text{CSAT} = \frac{\text{Sum of Satisfaction Scores}}{\text{Number of Responses}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) | Direct 3-mark definition |
| **Customer Effort Score (CES)** | Average rating of ease of interaction | 1-7 scale | Conceptually tested |
| **Churn Rate** | $\text{Churn} = \frac{\text{Customers Lost in Period}}{\text{Total Customers at Start}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) | Linked to retention stage |
| **Touchpoint Effectiveness** | $\text{TE} = \frac{\text{Successful Interactions}}{\text{Total Interactions}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) | Service blueprinting metric |
| **Moment of Truth Conversion** | $\text{MoT Conversion} = \frac{\text{Conversions at MoT}}{\text{Visitors at MoT}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) | Carlzon framework |

> [!WARNING]
> In the KTU exam, **do NOT use vertical pipes `|` inside markdown tables** for absolute value notation. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead. The table above uses standard formatting, so it is safe.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Service Industries

- **Healthcare**: Designing patient journey from appointment → consultation → diagnosis → treatment → follow-up.
- **Banking**: Reducing queue time at branches, designing mobile-first onboarding.
- **Hospitality**: Hotel check-in journey (welcome drink, fast Wi-Fi, room upgrade recognition).
- **E-commerce**: Cart abandonment recovery, last-mile delivery experience.
- **Public Services**: Passport renewal, driving license, e-governance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) — The Gold Metric

**Why is CLV important?** Because in service design, retaining a customer is **5 to 7 times cheaper** than acquiring a new one. CLV quantifies the total revenue a business can expect from a single customer account throughout the business relationship.

### The Basic CLV Formula

$$
\text{CLV} = \text{APV} \times \text{APF} \times \text{ACL}
$$

Where:
- $\text{APV}$ = Average Purchase Value (total revenue / number of purchases)
- $\text{APF}$ = Average Purchase Frequency (purchases per year)
- $\text{ACL}$ = Average Customer Lifespan (in years)

### Step-by-Step Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** A telecom company has the following data for its postpaid customers:
- Average monthly bill: ₹800
- Average customer lifespan: 4 years
- Customers typically upgrade plans once every 2 years, increasing bill by 20% with each upgrade

**Find the CLV and recommend service design improvements.**

**Step 1: Identify the parameters.**
- Monthly revenue per customer: ₹800
- Annual purchase frequency: 12 (monthly billing)
- Average customer lifespan: 4 years

**Step 2: Calculate Annual Purchase Value (APV).**

$$
\text{APV}_{\text{annual}} = 800 \times 12 = 9{,}600 \text{ INR/year}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Average Purchase Frequency over lifespan.**

$$
\text{APF} = 12 \text{ purchases/year} \times 4 \text{ years} = 48 \text{ purchases}
$$

**Step 4: Account for the 20% plan upgrade at year 2.**

$$
\text{Year 1 Revenue} = 9{,}600
$$

$$
\text{Year 2 Revenue} = 9{,}600 \times 1.20 = 11{,}520
$$

$$
\text{Year 3 Revenue} = 9{,}600 \times 1.20 = 11{,}520
$$

$$
\text{Year 4 Revenue} = 9{,}600 \times 1.44 = 13{,}824 \text{ (compounded upgrade)}
$$

**Step 5: Sum the total revenue over 4 years.**

$$
\text{Total CLV} = 9{,}600 + 11{,}520 + 11{,}520 + 13{,}824
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total CLV} &= 9{,}600 + 11{,}520 + 11{,}520 + 13{,}824 \\
&= 46{,}464 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Service Design Recommendations based on CLV.**

- With **CLV = ₹46,464**, the company can spend up to **₹4,646 (10% of CLV)** on customer acquisition.
- Invest in **onboarding journey** to reduce early churn.
- Design **loyalty touchpoints** at year 2 to lock in upgrades.
- Use **proactive support** during moments of truth to extend customer lifespan.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Service Design vs. Product Design

| Dimension | Product Design | Service Design |
|-----------|---------------|----------------|
| **Output** | Tangible physical good | Intangible experience/process |
| **Customer Role** | Passive consumer | Active co-creator |
| **Storage** | Can be inventoried | Cannot be inventoried (perishability) |
| **Quality Control** | Done before delivery | Done during delivery |
| **Time** | Single moment of use | Extended journey over time |
| **Variation** | Standardized | Heterogeneous (varies by customer) |
| **Primary Focus** | Features and function | Experience and emotion |
| **Success Metric** | Sales, defect rate | NPS, CSAT, CLV |

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Customer Journey Stage vs. Service Touchpoint

| Stage | Customer Goal | Key Touchpoints | Service Design Focus | Key Metric |
|-------|---------------|-----------------|----------------------|------------|
| **Awareness** | Discover need | Ads, social media, search engines | Brand storytelling | Reach, impressions |
| **Consideration** | Compare options | Website, reviews, sales calls | Information clarity | Bounce rate, time-on-site |
| **Purchase** | Complete transaction | Checkout, payment, store | Friction reduction | Conversion rate, cart abandonment |
| **Retention** | Get ongoing value | Support, loyalty programs | Relationship building | CSAT, churn rate |
| **Advocacy** | Share experience | Reviews, social media, referrals | Delight moments | NPS, referral rate |

## 3.4 NPS Calculation — Step-by-Step

**Problem:** A restaurant surveyed 500 customers. 350 rated 9-10 (Promoters), 100 rated 7-8 (Passives), and 50 rated 0-6 (Detractors). Find the NPS.

**Step 1: Calculate % Promoters.**

$$
\%\text{Promoters} = \frac{350}{500} \times 100 = 70\%
$$

**Step 2: Calculate % Detractors.**

$$
\%\text{Detractors} = \frac{50}{500} \times 100 = 10\%
$$

**Step 3: Apply the NPS formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPS} &= \%\text{Promoters} - \%\text{Detractors} \\
&= 70 - 10 \\
&= 60
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation.**

An **NPS of 60** is considered **excellent** (Apple and Tesla typically score 60-70). The service design is performing well, but the **Passives (100 customers)** represent an opportunity to convert into Promoters through targeted touchpoint improvements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTP Exam Tip:** Always state the formula first, then substitute values, and finally interpret the result in the context of the service scenario.

## 3.5 Customer Effort Score (CES) — Derivation Logic

CES is the **"ease of doing business"** metric. The lower the effort, the higher the loyalty.

$$
\text{CES} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Effort Score}_i}{n}
$$

Where effort score is rated on a **1-7 scale** (1 = very low effort, 7 = very high effort).

**Key Insight (Gartner Research):** 96% of customers with a high-effort service interaction become **disloyal**, compared to only 9% with low-effort interactions. Hence, **service design must minimize customer effort** at every touchpoint.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Complete Customer Journey Map (CJM) — Mermaid Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Customer Aware: Need Triggered] --> A2[Customer Consider: Research Options]
    A2 --> A3[Customer Decides: Chooses Brand]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Service Design. List the Five Principles of Service Design. [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**Model Answer:**

**Service Design** is a holistic, human-centered approach to planning and organizing people, infrastructure, communication, and material components of a service to improve its quality, the interaction between service provider and customer, and the customer's overall experience (Stickdorn & Schneider).

**The Five Principles of Service Design:**

1. **User-Centered** — Design from the customer's perspective.
2. **Co-Creative** — Include all stakeholders in the design process.
3. **Sequencing** — Visualize the service as a journey of interconnected steps.
4. **Evidencing** — Make the intangible service tangible through physical cues.
5. **Holistic** — Consider the entire service ecosystem.

### Question 2: What are the Three Moments of Truth in Service Marketing? [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

The **Three Moments of Truth** were introduced by **Jan Carlzon**, former CEO of SAS Airlines, in his 1987 book *"Moments of Truth"*. They represent critical instances when customers form opinions about a service.

1. **Zero Moment of Truth (ZMOT):** Occurs *before* purchase. The customer researches the product/service online, reads reviews, compares options, and forms a first impression.
2. **First Moment of Truth (FMOT):** Occurs *at* the moment of purchase. The customer encounters the product on the shelf, the website, or the service provider, and decides whether to buy.
3. **Second Moment of Truth (SMOT):** Occurs *after* purchase. The customer uses the product/service and forms a lasting opinion based on actual experience.

> [!NOTE]
> Mark these clearly in the exam with definitions and one real-world example each (e.g., Amazon, Zomato, Apple Store).

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

### Question A: Service Design Analysis (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme — Question A]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Customer Journey Mapping (CJM)** in detail. Describe its stages with a suitable example from the hospitality industry (hotel). (7 Marks)

**(b)** Discuss the **Service Blueprint** as a tool for service design. Draw a service blueprint for an online food delivery service like Zomato. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a)

**Customer Journey Mapping (CJM)** is a visual storytelling tool that captures the complete experience a customer has with a service provider, from initial awareness to post-purchase advocacy. It includes the customer's actions, thoughts, emotions, and pain points at each stage.

**Stages of the Customer Journey (Hotel Industry Example: Taj Hotels):**

| Stage | Customer Action | Touchpoint | Emotion | Hotel Service Design |
|-------|----------------|------------|---------|----------------------|
| **1. Awareness** | Searches for luxury stay in Goa | Google, Instagram, YouTube ads | Inspired, aspirational | Beautiful destination videos, celebrity endorsements |
| **2. Consideration** | Compares Taj vs. Oberoi vs. Marriott | Website, TripAdvisor, review sites | Evaluating, comparing | 360° room tours, transparent pricing, chat support |
| **3. Booking** | Reserves a deluxe room | Taj website, app, call center | Excited, anticipating | One-click booking, instant confirmation, room preferences saved |
| **4. Pre-Arrival** | Looks forward to the trip | Email confirmation, WhatsApp updates | Reassured, excited | Welcome email, weather updates, airport pickup option |
| **5. Arrival** | Reaches the hotel | Valet, lobby, front desk | Welcomed, impressed | Traditional aarti, garland, refreshing drink, fast check-in |
| **6. Stay** | Uses hotel facilities | Room service, spa, pool, restaurant | Pampered, delighted | Personalized greetings, remembering guest names, surprise upgrades |
| **7. Departure** | Checks out, leaves review | Checkout, email, review portal | Nostalgic, satisfied | Express checkout, handwritten thank-you note, loyalty points credited |
| **8. Advocacy** | Shares experience, returns | Google review, social media, Taj website | Proud, loyal | Loyalty rewards, referral program, personalized birthday offers |

**Benefits of CJM:**
- Identifies **pain points** and **moments of delight**.
- Aligns internal teams around the customer's perspective.
- Enables **data-driven service improvements**.
- Reveals **gaps** between customer expectations and actual delivery.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b)

**Service Blueprint** is a detailed diagram, first developed by **G. Lynn Shostack (1984)** and later refined by **Mary Jo Bitner et al. (2008)**, that visualizes the service process, separating customer-facing activities from internal operations.

**Key Components of a Service Blueprint:**

1. **Customer Actions** — What the customer does.
2. **Frontstage Interactions** — Visible contact between customer and employee.
3. **Backstage Interactions** — Hidden employee activities supporting the service.
4. **Support Processes** — Internal systems and infrastructure.
5. **Physical Evidence** — Tangible cues at each step.

**Service Blueprint for Zomato (Online Food Delivery):**

| Layer | Step 1: Browse | Step 2: Select | Step 3: Pay | Step 4: Track | Step 5: Receive | Step 6: Review |
|-------|----------------|----------------|-------------|--------------|-----------------|----------------|
| **Customer Action** | Opens app, searches "biryani" | Picks restaurant, views menu | Places order, pays online | Tracks delivery on map | Receives food at door | Rates restaurant & delivery |
| **Frontstage** | App UI, push notifications | Restaurant listings, photos, ratings | Payment gateway, offers | Live tracking, ETA | Delivery executive, packaging | Rating screen, photo upload |
| **Backstage** | Recommendation algorithm | Restaurant partner network | Payment processing, KYC | Dispatch system, route optimization | Order handoff at restaurant | Review moderation team |
| **Support Processes** | AWS servers, data analytics | Restaurant onboarding team | Banking partners, fraud detection | Logistics tech, GPS | Training, quality audits | Customer support, content team |
| **Physical Evidence** | Mobile screen, app icon | Menu photos, customer reviews | Order confirmation screen | Map view, delivery partner photo | Branded packaging, sealed bag | Star ratings, written reviews |

**The Line of Visibility** separates the frontstage (visible to customer) from the backstage (hidden). Every service design improvement must consider all three lines of interaction.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake in Blueprint Questions:**
> Many students confuse **Service Blueprint** with **Customer Journey Map**. Remember: **CJM is from the customer's perspective** (emotions, thoughts), while **Service Blueprint is from the organization's perspective** (processes, systems, employees). Drawing them in the same diagram is a common error that costs 2-3 marks.

---

### Question B: Service Design Thinking & Customer Journey Optimization (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme — Question B (Alternative Choice)]**

**(a)** Explain the **Double Diamond model of service design** with a case study of an Indian bank redesigning its digital onboarding journey. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A retail brand has 10,000 customers. 6,500 are Promoters, 2,500 are Passives, and 1,000 are Detractors based on the NPS survey. Calculate the **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** and suggest three service design interventions to improve it. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a)

**The Double Diamond Model** (popularized by the **UK Design Council**) is a four-phase service design framework that balances divergent (broad) and convergent (narrow) thinking.

**The Four Phases:**

1. **Discover (Divergent):** Understand the problem space — research customers, markets, and trends.
2. **Define (Convergent):** Synthesize insights into a clear problem statement.
3. **Develop (Divergent):** Brainstorm multiple solutions and prototypes.
4. **Deliver (Convergent):** Test, refine, and launch the best solution.

**Case Study: HDFC Bank Digital Onboarding Redesign**

| Phase | Activity | Outcome |
|-------|----------|---------|
| **Discover** | HDFC researched why 40% of new customers abandoned the mobile onboarding journey. Conducted 50 user interviews, analyzed heatmaps, and observed 100 app sessions. | Found that users struggled with KYC document upload, video verification, and signature steps. |
| **Define** | The team defined the problem: "How might we reduce onboarding drop-off by 50% while maintaining regulatory compliance?" | Clear design challenge aligned with business goals. |
| **Develop** | Prototyped multiple solutions: pre-filled forms, AI-driven document scanning, video KYC simplification, and instant digital account opening. | Three prototypes tested with 200 users each. |
| **Deliver** | Launched the simplified "Zero-Contact Account Opening" feature in 6 months. | Result: 70% drop in abandonment, 3x increase in new accounts, NPS improved from 35 to 58. |

**Why Double Diamond Works:**
- Balances **breadth and depth** in design thinking.
- Encourages **co-creation** with stakeholders.
- Reduces the risk of building the wrong solution.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b)

**Given Data:**
- Total customers surveyed = 10,000
- Promoters (rated 9-10) = 6,500
- Passives (rated 7-8) = 2,500
- Detractors (rated 0-6) = 1,000

**Step 1: Calculate the percentage of Promoters.**

$$
\%\text{Promoters} = \frac{6{,}500}{10{,}000} \times 100 = 65\%
$$

**Step 2: Calculate the percentage of Detractors.**

$$
\%\text{Detractors} = \frac{1{,}000}{10{,}000} \times 100 = 10\%
$$

**Step 3: Apply the NPS formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPS} &= \%\text{Promoters} - \%\text{Detractors} \\
&= 65 - 10 \\
&= 55
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation.**

An **NPS of 55** is **excellent** (industry average is 30-40). The retail brand has strong customer loyalty, but **25% are Passives** who could shift either way.

**Three Service Design Interventions to Improve NPS:**

1. **Convert Passives into Promoters via Personalized Engagement:**
   - Use CRM data to send personalized offers on birthdays and anniversaries.
   - Implement a **loyalty tier program** (Silver → Gold → Platinum) with exclusive perks.
   - Target Passives with a "We Value You" campaign offering early access to sales.

2. **Reduce Detractor Pain Points via Service Recovery:**
   - Establish a **24/7 omnichannel complaint resolution** (chat, call, in-store).
   - Train frontline staff in **service recovery scripts** to apologize and offer immediate resolution.
   - Implement a **closed-loop feedback system** where every Detractor is contacted within 48 hours.

3. **Proactively Delight at Moments of Truth:**
   - Redesign the **post-purchase journey** with surprise gifts, handwritten thank-you notes, and exclusive previews.
   - Use **predictive analytics** to identify at-risk customers and offer proactive support.
   - Create an **online community forum** where loyal customers can share experiences and earn rewards.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> In numerical questions like NPS or CLV, students often forget to **state the formula first** before substitution. This alone costs 1 mark. Also, **do not skip the interpretation step** — KTU examiners award 1-2 marks for "interpretation" of the result. Finally, ensure units are mentioned where applicable (e.g., CLV in INR, NPS as a pure number).

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Quick-Recall Checklist (High-Density Revision)

- **Service Design Definition:** Holistic, co-creative, iterative approach to orchestrate people, processes, technology, and physical evidence.
- **Customer Journey Definition:** End-to-end sequence of customer interactions with a service provider.
- **Five Principles of Service Design:** User-Centered, Co-Creative, Sequencing, Evidencing, Holistic. (Mnemonic: **U-S-C-E-H**)
- **Five Stages of Customer Journey:** Awareness → Consideration → Purchase → Retention → Advocacy.
- **Moments of Truth (Jan Carlzon, 1987):** ZMOT, FMOT, SMOT (+ UMOT for digital age).
- **Service Blueprint Inventor:** G. Lynn Shostack (1984), refined by Mary Jo Bitner et al. (2008).
- **Service Blueprint Components:** Customer Actions, Frontstage, Backstage, Support Processes, Physical Evidence.
- **Key Line:** Line of Visibility (separates frontstage from backstage).
- **Double Diamond Model:** Discover → Define → Develop → Deliver (UK Design Council).
- **NPS Formula:** `%Promoters − %Detractors`, range −100 to +100.
- **CSAT Formula:** `(Sum of scores / Number of responses) × 100`.
- **CLV Formula:** `APV × APF × ACL` (in currency).
- **Churn Rate Formula:** `(Customers lost / Total customers at start) × 100`.
- **CES Range:** 1-7 scale; lower effort = higher loyalty.
- **Customer Effort Insight:** 96% high-effort customers become disloyal (Gartner).
- **CLV Insight:** 5% retention increase → 25-95% profit boost (Bain & Co.).
- **Real-World Example:** Amazon (ZMOT reviews, FMOT product page, SMOT delivery, UMOT reviews).
- **Tools Used in Service Design:** Empathy maps, personas, journey maps, blueprints, storyboards, prototypes.
- **Difference to Remember:** CJM = customer's view (emotions); Blueprint = organization's view (processes).

### KTU Exam Writing Strategy (Final Tips)

1. **Always start 3-mark answers with a definition**, then list or explain.
2. **In 14-mark answers**, use headings, subheadings, tables, and diagrams (Mermaid or hand-drawn).
3. **For numerical questions**, follow the **5-step format**: Given → To Find → Formula → Substitution → Answer + Interpretation.
4. **Cite at least one real-world example** (Amazon, Zomato, HDFC, Taj, Apple) to gain the **"Application" mark** that KTU examiners reserve.
5. **Link to Bloom's Taxonomy**: Show Understanding (define), Application (example), Analysis (breakdown), Evaluation (justify).
6. **Time allocation**: 3-mark = 4 minutes; 14-mark = 18-20 minutes in a 3-hour exam.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Reminder:** Service Design is a **management/operations topic**, NOT a programming topic. Do not include any code blocks (Python, C++, etc.) in your exam answers. Use Mermaid diagrams, tables, and structured prose instead.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Thinking Methods to Aid Service Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT861 - SERVICE SCIENCE AND SERVICE OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design Thinking Methods to Aid Service Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Design Thinking** is a human-centered, iterative problem-solving methodology that blends analytical reasoning with creative intuition to develop innovative, feasible, and desirable solutions — particularly for complex, ill-defined (often called *"wicked"*) service problems where user needs, technology feasibility, and business viability intersect.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standardized):**
> *Design Thinking is a discipline that uses designers' sensibility and methods to match people's needs with what is technologically feasible and commercially viable as a service outcome.*

In the context of **Service Design**, design thinking shifts focus from a physical product to an **intangible service experience** — emphasizing co-creation with users, journey mapping, prototyping of service touchpoints, and iterative refinement before large-scale deployment.

### 1.2 The Three Lenses of Design Thinking

Every service designed using this methodology is evaluated through three overlapping lenses:

- **Desirability** (Human Lens): Does the user *want* it?
- **Feasibility** (Technology Lens): Can we *actually build* it?
- **Viability** (Business Lens): Will it *sustain* the business?

$$ \text{Successful Service Design} = f(\text{Desirability}, \text{Feasibility}, \text{Viability}) $$

> [!NOTE]
> **The Sweet Spot of Innovation**: The intersection of these three lenses is where truly breakthrough service innovations (like Uber, Netflix, or Zomato) are born. Missing any one lens leads to service failure.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of **Design Thinking** like a **doctor diagnosing a complex patient**:

| Medical Process | Design Thinking Stage |
|---|---|
| Listening to patient symptoms | **Empathize** with users |
| Diagnosing the disease | **Define** the problem |
| Brainstorming treatments | **Ideate** solutions |
| Trying a small treatment first | **Prototype** the service |
| Monitoring recovery | **Test** and iterate |

Just as a doctor does not prescribe medicine without first understanding the patient, a service designer must deeply understand the *user's world* before designing any service. The doctor may need multiple consultations (iteration), and the treatment is constantly refined based on the patient's response.

### 1.4 Why Design Thinking Matters for Services

Services are **intangible, heterogeneous, perishable, and inseparable** (the famous IHIP characteristics). Unlike a physical product you can return, a poorly designed service creates immediate, irreversible bad experiences.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Highlight:** Services cannot be prototyped like a physical product. They must be **simulated, role-played, or piloted** before full rollout — making design thinking's iterative testing phase critical.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Methods Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Stages of Design Thinking (Stanford d.school Framework)

Design Thinking is **non-linear and iterative**. Stages may be revisited multiple times based on new insights.

#### Stage 1: Empathize
- **Goal:** Understand users' needs, emotions, motivations, and pain points through observation, immersion, and engagement.
- **Methods/Tools Used:**
  - **Empathy Maps** (Says / Thinks / Does / Feels)
  - **User Interviews** (semi-structured, contextual)
  - **Shadowing** (observing users in their natural environment)
  - **Extreme User Interviews** (novices, experts, edge cases)
  - **Photo/Diary Studies**
- **Why:** In service design, customers co-produce the service. Without deep empathy, services fail to address real user needs.

#### Stage 2: Define
- **Goal:** Synthesize empathy findings into a clear, actionable **Problem Statement** (Point of View).
- **Methods/Tools Used:**
  - **Point of View (POV) Statement:** `[User] · [Need] · [Insight]`
  - **Affinity Diagrams** (clustering insights)
  - **User Personas** (archetypal user representations)
  - **How-Might-We (HMW) Questions**
- **Why:** A well-defined problem is half-solved. This stage prevents *solution-jumping* before the problem is truly understood.

#### Stage 3: Ideate
- **Goal:** Generate a wide, divergent set of creative solutions without judgment.
- **Methods/Tools Used:**
  - **Brainstorming** (quantity over quality)
  - **SCAMPER Technique** (Substitute, Combine, Adapt, Modify, Put to another use, Eliminate, Reverse)
  - **Worst Possible Idea** (to unlock creativity)
  - **Storyboarding** (visual narratives of service)
  - **Analogous Inspiration** (borrowing from other industries)
- **Why:** Service innovation requires breaking conventional industry patterns.

#### Stage 4: Prototype
- **Goal:** Build quick, low-cost, tangible representations of ideas to test assumptions.
- **Methods/Tools Used:**
  - **Paper Prototypes / Wireframes**
  - **Role-Playing / Service Walkthroughs**
  - **Storyboards**
  - **Mock Service Encounters**
  - **Pilot Programs / Beta Tests**
- **Why:** Tangibility helps teams and users give feedback. Services are prototyped through **staged interactions**.

#### Stage 5: Test
- **Goal:** Validate prototypes with real users, gather feedback, and refine.
- **Methods/Tools Used:**
  - **Usability Testing**
  - **A/B Testing**
  - **Feedback Loops**
  - **Service Safari** (experiencing competitor services)
  - **Pilot Rollouts with KPIs**
- **Why:** Real user feedback exposes mismatches between intent and experience.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Design Thinking Methods Cheat Sheet

| Stage | Primary Method | Output | Key Metric / Output Format |
|---|---|---|---|
| Empathize | Empathy Map | 4-quadrant user insight | Says / Thinks / Does / Feels |
| Empathize | User Interview | Verbatim transcripts | Thematic insights |
| Define | POV Statement | Problem framing | `[User] · [Need] · [Insight]` |
| Define | Persona | Archetype document | Demographic + behavioral profile |
| Define | HMW Question | Divergent prompt | Open-ended challenge |
| Ideate | SCAMPER | 7 transformation lenses | Modified idea matrix |
| Ideate | Brainstorm | Quantity of ideas | ≥ 50 ideas/session |
| Prototype | Storyboard | Sequential sketches | 6–8 panel flow |
| Prototype | Role-Play | Simulated service | Choreographed touchpoints |
| Test | A/B Test | Comparative data | Conversion %, NPS, CSAT |

> [!NOTE]
> **No mathematical formulas apply directly** to Design Thinking as a methodology. However, **key performance indicators (KPIs)** that quantify successful service design outcomes include: **NPS (Net Promoter Score)**, **CSAT (Customer Satisfaction)**, **CES (Customer Effort Score)**, and **FCR (First Contact Resolution)** — all expressed as percentage scores between **0%** and **100%**.

### 2.3 Design Thinking × Service Design: The Critical Intersection

Service Design adds three service-specific concerns on top of classical Design Thinking:

- **Service Blueprinting** — maps frontstage (customer-visible) and backstage (internal) service activities.
- **Customer Journey Mapping** — traces user emotions across each service touchpoint.
- **Service Ecosystem Mapping** — identifies all stakeholders involved in value co-creation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Whenever asked *"How does Design Thinking aid Service Design?"*, the answer **must** explicitly reference: *co-creation, journey mapping, prototyping intangible touchpoints, and iterative testing* — these are the four pillars examiners reward with marks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Methodology & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Step-by-Step Procedure: Applying Design Thinking to a Service

The following is the **canonical 10-step methodology** for applying design thinking to a service design problem (e.g., redesigning a university cafeteria service):

---

**Step 1: Frame the Service Challenge**
Write a clear, broad challenge statement.
*Example:* "How might we improve the campus dining experience for working professionals?"

**Step 2: Conduct Empathy Research**
Conduct 8–10 user interviews with diverse stakeholders (students, staff, kitchen crew, vendors). Observe behaviors, capture emotions.

**Step 3: Synthesize Findings into Empathy Maps**
Plot observations on a 4-quadrant empathy map (Says / Thinks / Does / Feels).

**Step 4: Cluster Insights Using Affinity Diagrams**
Group similar observations into 4–6 thematic clusters (e.g., "Long Wait Times", "Limited Healthy Options", "Payment Friction").

**Step 5: Define the Point of View (POV) Statement**
$$ \text{POV} = \text{[User]} \cdot \text{[Need]} \cdot \text{[Insight]} $$
*Example:* "Busy working professional students · need a fast, healthy, predictable meal experience · because they cannot afford to lose 30 minutes between classes."

**Step 6: Formulate How-Might-We Questions**
$$ \text{HMW} = \text{"How might we..."} + \text{[POV reframed as opportunity]} $$
*Example:* "How might we reduce waiting time without expanding infrastructure?"

**Step 7: Ideate Divergently**
Generate 50+ ideas using SCAMPER, Worst Possible Idea, and analogical inspiration.

**Step 8: Prototype Selected Ideas**
Build low-fidelity prototypes: paper layouts of a "grab-and-go" healthy station, role-play the queue experience, sketch digital payment flow.

**Step 9: Test Prototypes with Real Users**
Run 2–3 prototype sessions, capture feedback using observation + post-test interviews. Measure: wait time, NPS, repeat usage.

**Step 10: Iterate or Implement**
Refine based on feedback. If successful, scale to full rollout. Return to earlier stages if tests reveal new insights.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Design Thinking Methods × Service Touchpoints

The following matrix maps **which Design Thinking method best applies to each service touchpoint**:

| Service Touchpoint | Best Design Thinking Method | Why This Method Fits |
|---|---|---|
| First contact (entry) | Empathy Map + Persona | Captures initial user state |
| Information search | Customer Journey Map | Traces emotional arc |
| Booking / ordering | Storyboard + Prototype | Visualizes sequence |
| Service delivery | Service Blueprint + Role-Play | Reveals frontstage/backstage gaps |
| Payment | A/B Testing | Quantifies preference |
| Post-service follow-up | Feedback Loops + NPS | Captures long-term value |

### 3.3 Quantitative Scoring Framework for Service Design Concepts

When evaluating multiple service concepts, students can use the **Desirability-Feasibility-Viability (DFV) Scoring Matrix**:

$$ \text{Concept Score} = (0.4 \times D) + (0.4 \times F) + (0.2 \times V) $$

Where:
- **D** = Desirability score (1–10, from user empathy data)
- **F** = Feasibility score (1–10, from technical assessment)
- **V** = Viability score (1–10, from business case)

$$ \text{Threshold for Approval} \geq 7.0 $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these weights?** *Desirability* and *Feasibility* are weighted higher than *Viability* because a service that users love and can be built will eventually find a business model. A service that is merely profitable but unlovable will fail.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Design Thinking Process (Iterative Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 1 Empathize Understand Users] --> B[Stage 2 Define Frame the Problem]
    B --> C[Stage 3 Ideate Generate Solutions]
    C --> D[Stage 4 Prototype Build Tangible Models]
    D --> E[Stage 5 Test Validate with Users]
    E --> F{Insights Gained Validated?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Implement Service at Scale]
    F -->|No| A
    D -.Iterate Back.- A
    C -.Refine POV.- B
    E -.Refine Prototype.- D
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Visualization Note:** The dashed back-edges represent the **iterative nature** of design thinking — it is not a one-way waterfall. The arrows often loop back to earlier stages based on new insights.

### 4.2 Service Blueprint: A Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CUST[Customer Actions]
        CA1[Searches for Service]
        CA2[Submits Request]
        CA3[Receives Service]
        CA4[Gives Feedback]
    end

    subgraph FS[Frontstage Interactions]
        FS1[Website or App Interface]
        FS2[Customer Service Agent]
        FS3[Delivery or Service Channel]
        FS4[Feedback Form or Survey]
    end

    subgraph BS[Backstage Internal Processes]
        BS1[Server and Database]
        BS2[Ticket Routing Engine]
        BS3[Operations Team]
        BS4[Analytics Dashboard]
    end

    subgraph SUP[Support Processes]
        SUP1[IT Infrastructure]
        SUP2[Training and Quality]
        SUP3[Logistics and Supply]
        SUP4[Data Warehouse]
    end

    subgraph L1[Line of Internal Visibility]
    end

    CA1 --> FS1
    CA2 --> FS2
    CA3 --> FS3
    CA4 --> FS4

    FS1 -.Visible.  L1
    FS2 -.Visible.  L1
    FS3 -.Visible.  L1
    FS4 -.Visible.  L1

    FS1 --> BS1
    FS2 --> BS2
    FS3 --> BS3
    FS4 --> BS4

    BS1 --> SUP1
    BS2 --> SUP2
    BS3 --> SUP3
    BS4 --> SUP4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Blueprint:** Everything *above* the **Line of Internal Visibility** is what the customer experiences directly (frontstage). Everything *below* is the invisible machinery making the service work (backstage + support). Design thinking focuses on prototyping the frontstage while ensuring backstage feasibility.

### 4.3 The Design Thinking T-Model (Mindset vs Process)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph TM[Design Thinking T Model]
        V[Vertical Bar Mindset and Values]
        H[Horizontal Bar Process and Stages]
    end

    V --> M1[Human Centered]
    V --> M2[Empathy Driven]
    V --> M3[Bias Toward Action]
    V --> M4[Radical Collaboration]
    V --> M5[Iterative Mindset]

    H --> P1[Empathize]
    H --> P2[Define]
    H --> P3[Ideate]
    H --> P4[Prototype]
    H --> P5[Test]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define Design Thinking. List any four core principles of Design Thinking.*

**Model Answer:**
Design Thinking is a human-centered, iterative problem-solving approach that integrates user needs, technological feasibility, and business viability to co-create innovative services.

**Four core principles:**
1. **Human-Centered** — Solutions begin and end with the user.
2. **Empathy Driven** — Deep understanding of user emotions and contexts.
3. **Bias Toward Action** — Rapid prototyping over endless discussion.
4. **Iterative Refinement** — Continuous improvement through feedback loops.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for principles — typically ½ mark each.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*What is an Empathy Map? Mention its four quadrants.*

**Model Answer:**
An Empathy Map is a visual tool used during the Empathize stage to capture what users say, think, do, and feel.

**Four Quadrants:**
1. **Says** — Verbatim quotes from the user.
2. **Thinks** — Internal thoughts and beliefs (often unsaid).
3. **Does** — Observable actions and behaviors.
4. **Feels** — Emotional state — anxieties, fears, joys.

*(3 marks: 1 for tool identity + 2 for the four quadrants.)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q3(a). [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
*Explain the five stages of Design Thinking in detail. For each stage, name one tool/method used in Service Design.*

**Model Answer:**

**Stage 1: Empathize**
The goal is to deeply understand users by observing, engaging, and immersing oneself in their context. In service design, this is critical because services are co-produced by users.
*Tool:* **Empathy Map** — captures Says/Thinks/Does/Feels.

**Stage 2: Define**
Synthesize empathy findings into a clear, actionable problem statement. Prevents premature solution-jumping.
*Tool:* **Point of View (POV) Statement** — `[User] · [Need] · [Insight]`.

**Stage 3: Ideate**
Generate a wide range of creative ideas without judgment. Quantity over quality.
*Tool:* **SCAMPER** — applies 7 transformation lenses to existing services.

**Stage 4: Prototype**
Build low-cost, tangible representations of ideas. In services, this means paper prototypes, role-play, or pilot programs.
*Tool:* **Storyboard** — visual sequence of the service experience.

**Stage 5: Test**
Validate prototypes with real users and iterate based on feedback.
*Tool:* **A/B Testing** or **Usability Testing** with feedback loops.

*(7 marks: ~1.4 marks per stage × 5 stages.)*

---

**Q3(b). [7 Marks]**
*Illustrate how Design Thinking aids the design of an innovative banking service. Apply at least three specific Design Thinking methods in your answer.*

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Empathize with Bank Customers:**
Conduct empathy interviews and shadowing with senior citizens, working professionals, and first-time account holders. Build an **Empathy Map** revealing frustrations like long queues, complex forms, fear of digital fraud.

**Step 2 — Define the Core Problem:**
POV Statement: *"Time-constrained working professionals · need a frictionless way to perform routine banking transactions · because they cannot visit branches during business hours."*

HMW Question: *"How might we make routine banking invisible yet secure?"*

**Step 3 — Ideate Service Innovations:**
Use **SCAMPER** to generate ideas:
- **Substitute** physical tokens with mobile queue tokens.
- **Combine** biometric login with AI-based fraud detection.
- **Eliminate** branch visits for routine transactions via conversational AI.

**Step 4 — Prototype:**
Build a paper prototype of a "Voice-First Banking Assistant" with role-play scenarios. Test the prototype with 5 users in a simulated environment.

**Step 5 — Test & Iterate:**
Run a 2-week pilot measuring **NPS**, **CSAT**, and **FCR**. Iterate based on feedback (e.g., add multi-language support if observed).

**Conclusion:** Design Thinking transforms banking from a process-driven experience to a human-centered service by embedding empathy, prototype-driven validation, and continuous iteration.

*(7 marks: breakdown — 2 for empathize, 2 for define/ideate, 2 for prototype/test, 1 for conclusion.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q4(a). [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper]**
*Explain Service Blueprinting as a Design Thinking method. With the help of a diagram, describe its key components.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Service Blueprinting is a Design Thinking tool used in the Prototype stage that maps the entire service process from the customer's perspective while exposing internal operations.

**Key Components:**

1. **Customer Actions** — Steps the customer takes during service consumption.
2. **Frontstage Interactions** — Customer-visible touchpoints (staff, digital interfaces).
3. **Backstage Interactions** — Internal staff/processes invisible to the customer.
4. **Support Processes** — Internal systems supporting the service (IT, HR, supply chain).
5. **Line of Internal Visibility** — The crucial demarcation between frontstage and backstage.
6. **Line of Interaction** — Direct interactions between customer and service provider.

**Block-Level Architecture Diagram:**

```
[Customer Actions]  →  [Frontstage]  |  [Backstage]  →  [Support Processes]
                              ↑
                  Line of Internal Visibility
```

**Utility:**
Service blueprints reveal gaps, inconsistencies, and bottlenecks — making them essential for service redesign.

*(7 marks: 2 for definition, 3 for components, 2 for diagram and utility.)*

---

**Q4(b). [7 Marks]**
*Compare and contrast Design Thinking with Traditional Problem-Solving approaches in the context of service design. Why is Design Thinking more suitable for complex services?*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Traditional Problem-Solving | Design Thinking |
|---|---|---|
| **Starting Point** | Pre-defined problem | User empathy first |
| **Approach** | Linear, analytical | Iterative, experimental |
| **User Involvement** | Limited / passive | Active co-creator |
| **Failure Tolerance** | Low — failure avoided | High — fail fast, learn fast |
| **Solution Generation** | Narrow, expert-driven | Divergent, collaborative |
| **Output** | Optimized process | Innovative experience |
| **Best For** | Well-defined problems | Wicked, ambiguous problems |

**Why Design Thinking Suits Complex Services:**

1. **Service Intangibility** — Services cannot be touched or returned. Prototyping simulates the experience before launch.
2. **Service Heterogeneity** — Every customer interaction differs. Empathy-led design accounts for variability.
3. **Co-Creation** — Customers actively participate. Design thinking formalizes this through journey maps.
4. **Wicked Problems** — Service problems (e.g., healthcare access, urban mobility) have no single solution; design thinking explores many.
5. **Rapid Iteration** — Service environments evolve fast; design thinking's loops keep pace with change.

*(7 marks: 3 for comparison table, 4 for the five reasons.)*

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Conflating Design Thinking with Brainstorming** — Brainstorming is *one tool* within Ideate, not the whole process. Avoid defining Design Thinking as just "idea generation."
> 2. **Skipping the Empathize Stage** — Many students jump to solutions. Examiners *specifically deduct marks* if empathy is missing from service design answers.
> 3. **Listing methods without explaining context** — Naming "Empathy Map" alone is insufficient; explain *when* and *why* it is used.
> 4. **Forgetting Iteration** — Drawing Design Thinking as a *straight line* (waterfall) loses the iterative marks.
> 5. **Ignoring Service Specificity** — Writing generic product-design answers without linking to **service characteristics (IHIP)** loses 2–3 marks easily.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Design Thinking = Human-centered + Iterative + Collaborative + Prototype-driven.**
- **Five Stages:** Empathize → Define → Ideate → Prototype → Test (non-linear).
- **Three Lenses:** Desirability, Feasibility, Viability — the *sweet spot* defines innovation.
- **Key Tools:** Empathy Map, POV Statement, HMW Question, Persona, SCAMPER, Storyboard, Service Blueprint, Customer Journey Map, A/B Testing.
- **Design Thinking is non-linear** — every stage can loop back to any earlier stage based on new insights.
- **Service-Specific Tools:** Service Blueprint, Customer Journey Map, Persona — these extend classical design thinking to intangible services.
- **Evaluation Formula:** `Concept Score = 0.4D + 0.4F + 0.2V` (Threshold ≥ 7.0).
- **KPIs to Remember:** NPS, CSAT, CES, FCR (all 0–100% scales).
- **Core Principle:** *Empathy before Solution.* Without empathy, the design is not thinking — it is guessing.
- **Exam Tip:** Always link answers back to **service characteristics (IHIP)** to score full marks on KTU Board exams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Locating Facilities and Designing their Layout: Models of Facility Locations (Huff's Retail Model)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT861 - SERVICE SCIENCE AND SERVICE OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Locating Facilities and Designing their Layout: Models of Facility Locations (Huff's Retail Model)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Huff's Retail Model** is a **probabilistic, gravity-based spatial interaction model** developed by **David L. Huff (1963, 1964)** that is used to determine:
1. The probability that a customer located at a given origin will patronize a particular retail facility.
2. The **trade area (market area)** boundary of a retail store.
3. The **market share** of competing retail facilities within a given geographic region.

The model is fundamentally used in **service operations management** for *retail facility location planning*, *store network design*, and *competitive retail strategy*. It explicitly couples two key variables: the **attractiveness** of the destination facility and the **distance/travel time impedance** from the customer origin to that facility.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic falls under *Module 2 – Locating Facilities and Designing their Layout* and is a **high-frequency Part B (14-mark) question** due to its quantitative nature and direct relevance to the *Service Operations* domain.

The general functional form of Huff's model is given by:

$$P_{ij} = \frac{\dfrac{A_j^{\alpha}}{d_{ij}^{\beta}}}{\sum_{k=1}^{n} \dfrac{A_k^{\alpha}}{d_{ik}^{\beta}}}$$

where the parameters govern the *attractiveness effect* and the *distance decay effect*, respectively.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are hungry and you see **three restaurants** in your neighborhood:
- **Restaurant A** is small, cheap, and only 200 m away.
- **Restaurant B** is a large, premium outlet with a beautiful ambience, but it is 1.5 km away.
- **Restaurant C** is medium-sized and 800 m away.

**Where will you most likely go?**

You will subconsciously perform a *trade-off*:
- If you are very hungry and want quick food → you'll weigh **distance heavily** → go to **A**.
- If you are celebrating and want a memorable experience → you'll weigh **attractiveness (size, quality, ambience) heavily** → go to **B**.
- If you want a balance → you'll go to **C**.

Huff's model **mathematically formalizes this very trade-off**. It says:
> *"The probability of a customer choosing a particular store is directly proportional to how attractive that store is, and inversely proportional to how far (or how difficult) it is to reach."*

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Meaning:** The bigger and more attractive the store, the more it *pulls* customers (like gravity). The farther the store, the more it *pushes* customers away (like friction). Huff's model is essentially a *shopping gravity equation*.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board Terminology)

| Term | Meaning in Retail Context |
|---|---|
| **Origin (i)** | The location of the customer / demand point (e.g., a residential colony). |
| **Destination (j)** | The retail store / facility under consideration. |
| **Attractiveness (S)** | A measure of how appealing a store is; usually **floor area in square feet**. |
| **Travel Time / Distance (T or d)** | Impedance between origin and destination. |
| **Distance Decay Parameter (λ)** | Sensitivity of customers to distance (typically **1.5 to 3.5**). |
| **Trade Area** | Geographic region from which a store draws most of its customers. |
| **Market Share** | Proportion of total potential customers who patronize a particular store. |

### 1.4 Assumptions of Huff's Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a complete KTU answer, always state the underlying assumptions before applying the model.

1. Customers are **utility-maximizers** who choose the store offering the highest perceived utility.
2. **Attractiveness** is exogenously given and quantifiable (commonly by store floor area).
3. **Travel time/distance** is the only disutility considered.
4. The **distance decay parameter (λ)** is the same for all customers (homogeneous behavior).
5. Customers have **perfect information** about all available stores.
6. The model is **probabilistic**, not deterministic — a probability value is assigned to each store.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Underlying Logic — Step by Step

Huff's model rests on three pillars of reasoning:

**Pillar 1 — The Gravity Analogy (Retail Gravity)**
Just as Newton's law of gravitation states that the force between two bodies is proportional to their masses and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them, Huff hypothesized that a similar "gravitational pull" exists between customers and stores:
- A larger store (greater "mass") attracts more customers.
- A store that is farther away (greater "distance") attracts fewer customers.

**Pillar 2 — Relative, Not Absolute, Choice**
A customer does not evaluate a store in isolation. They compare it against **all available competing stores**. Therefore, the probability of choosing store $j$ is **relative** to the sum of "attractiveness-to-impedance" ratios of all stores. This is why the formula uses a **denominator that sums over all competing destinations**.

**Pillar 3 — The Distance Decay Function**
Not all products/services are equally sensitive to distance. Buying groceries is highly distance-sensitive (low willingness to travel), while buying a luxury car is far less distance-sensitive. The parameter **λ (lambda)** captures this sensitivity. Higher λ implies customers are *more* averse to distance.

### 2.2 The Three Core Formulae of Huff's Model

> [!NOTE]
> Memorize these three formulae. They are the **backbone** of every Part B question on this topic.

**Formula 1 — Probability of Patronage (Core Huff Equation):**

$$P_{ij} = \frac{\dfrac{S_j}{T_{ij}^{\lambda}}}{\sum_{k=1}^{n} \dfrac{S_k}{T_{ik}^{\lambda}}}$$

**Formula 2 — Market Share of Store j:**

$$MS_j = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( P_{ij} \cdot W_i \right)$$

**Formula 3 — Trade Area Boundary (Equi-Probability Line):**
The boundary between two stores $j$ and $k$ is the locus where $P_{ij} = P_{ik}$. For two competing stores with equal perceived attractiveness at the boundary, this reduces to a **perpendicular bisector logic** modified by λ.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` instead of `|` in tables below to avoid markdown parsing errors. All formulae use **dimensionless** probability output.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variable Meaning | Typical Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Probability of Patronage** $P_{ij}$ | $\dfrac{S_j \,/\, T_{ij}^{\lambda}}{\sum_{k=1}^{n} S_k \,/\, T_{ik}^{\lambda}}$ | $S_j$ = store size; $T_{ij}$ = travel time/distance; $\lambda$ = distance decay parameter | $0 \leq P_{ij} \leq 1$ |
| 2 | **Market Share** $MS_j$ | $\sum_{i=1}^{m} P_{ij} \cdot W_i$ | $W_i$ = weight (e.g., population) at origin $i$ | Number of customers or share % |
| 3 | **Total Demand Captured** $D_j$ | $\sum_{i=1}^{m} P_{ij} \cdot W_i \cdot \bar{S}$ | $\bar{S}$ = average spending per customer | Currency units |
| 4 | **Distance Decay Range** | $1.5 \leq \lambda \leq 3.5$ | $\lambda = 1$ (linear); $\lambda = 2$ (Newtonian) | Empirical, calibrated per industry |
| 5 | **Trade Area Boundary** | $P_{ij} = P_{ik} = 0.5$ | Equi-probability contour | Geographic line / polygon |
| 6 | **Conditional on Selection** | $\sum_{j=1}^{n} P_{ij} = 1$ | Sum of probabilities from one origin = 1 | Always holds (axiom) |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Service Utility

Huff's model is not merely academic — it is the **operational engine** behind:

- **GIS-based retail site selection** in supermarkets (Walmart, Reliance Retail, Big Bazaar use gravity models extensively).
- **Bank branch location planning** — banks use Huff-type models to determine where to open a new branch.
- **Hospital catchment area analysis** — public health departments use it to allocate resources.
- **Mall anchor tenant placement** — shopping mall developers use it to position anchor stores optimally.
- **E-commerce last-mile delivery hub placement** — even digital service companies use gravity models for hyperlocal warehousing.
- **Logistics warehouse selection** — service operations managers use it to choose distribution centers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why KTU loves this question:** It directly tests three COs — *CO2: Analyse service design models*, *CO3: Apply quantitative tools to location problems*, and *CO4: Evaluate facility layout decisions*. A 14-mark question on Huff's model typically combines a numerical problem with a conceptual explanation of assumptions and managerial implications.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Huff's Model from First Principles

Huff built his model by **extending the Reilly's Law of Retail Gravitation (1931)**, which stated that two cities attract retail trade from an intermediate town in proportion to their populations and inversely with the square of the distance. Huff's innovation was to convert this deterministic *breakpoint* into a **probabilistic, continuous function**.

Let us derive it from a **utility-maximization perspective**.

**Step 1 — Define the perceived utility of a store.**
The utility $U_{ij}$ that a customer at origin $i$ derives from store $j$ is assumed to be:

$$U_{ij} = \alpha \cdot \ln(S_j) - \lambda \cdot \ln(T_{ij})$$

where the natural log transformation allows the model to be multiplicative in nature (a property of the gravity analogy).

**Step 2 — Convert utilities to choice probabilities using the multinomial logit form.**
