# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **The following questions mirror KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE) patterns, including Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) with internal choice.**

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Service Guarantee. List any FOUR characteristics of an effective service guarantee. [3 Marks]
**Model Answer:**

A service guarantee is a formal, written promise by a service provider specifying the level of service to be delivered and the compensation the customer will receive if that level is not met.

**Four characteristics of an effective service guarantee:**
1. **Unconditional** — no fine print, no "ifs and buts."
2. **Meaningful** — addresses what the customer truly cares about.
3. **Easy to understand and invoke** — simple wording, simple process.
4. **Credible and quantifiable** — measurable, believable standard.

---

### Question 2: What is the "Service Recovery Paradox"? [3 Marks]
**Model Answer:**

The Service Recovery Paradox is a phenomenon (documented by Hart, Heskett, and Sasser, **1990**) in which a customer who has experienced a service failure but receives an excellent recovery is *more loyal* than a customer who never experienced any failure at all. The recovery, when handled well, **deepens trust and brand attachment** because the customer sees the organization acting with integrity under stress.

Mathematically, the loyalty of a recovered customer is often modeled as:

$$
L_{recovered} \;=\; L_{normal} \times (1 + m)
$$

where $m$ is the recovery paradox multiplier, typically between **1.5 and 2.0**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A: Service Guarantee Design and Implementation [14 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the strategic role of service guarantees in a service organization. Discuss with suitable examples.

**Model Answer:**

Service guarantees play **four strategic roles** in any service organization:

1. **Internal Benchmarking Tool** — A guarantee forces the organization to define, measure, and standardise service quality. For example, before Domino's could promise "30 minutes or free," it had to redesign its kitchen workflow, mapping every step from order to delivery.

2. **Customer Empowerment Instrument** — The guarantee shifts power to the customer. For example, a "no questions asked" refund policy at L.L. Bean gives the customer total confidence in remote purchases.

3. **Quality Control Catalyst** — Employees become more vigilant because the cost of failure is real and visible. FedEx's overnight delivery guarantee made every dispatcher accountable.

4. **Marketing Signal** — The guarantee acts as a credible brand statement. JetBlue's "Customer Bill of Rights" reduced customer-perceived risk in low-cost air travel.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A hospital offers a guarantee: "If your waiting time in the emergency department exceeds 30 minutes, your consultation fee is waived." The hospital serves 200 patients per day, and historically 5% of patients wait more than 30 minutes. Calculate:
1. The expected number of patients invoking the guarantee per day.
2. The daily cost to the hospital if the consultation fee is ₹500.
3. Comment on whether the guarantee is financially viable, assuming that each recovered patient generates ₹2,000 in long-term loyalty value.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Expected number of patients invoking the guarantee.**

$$
N \times f \;=\; 200 \times 0.05
$$

$$
\text{Expected Claims} \;=\; 10 \text{ patients/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Daily cost to the hospital.**

$$
\text{CoG} \;=\; 10 \times 500 \;=\; ₹5{,}000 \text{ per day}
$$

**Step 3 — Long-term loyalty value (assuming all 10 invoke the guarantee and stay loyal).**

$$
\text{Loyalty Benefit} \;=\; 10 \times 2{,}000 \;=\; ₹20{,}000 \text{ per day}
$$

**Step 4 — Net Benefit.**

$$
\text{Net Benefit} \;=\; 20{,}000 \;-\; 5{,}000 \;=\; ₹15{,}000 \text{ per day}
$$

**Step 5 — Viability comment.**

$$
\boxed{\text{The guarantee is financially viable — net positive benefit of ₹15,000/day.}}
$$

The 4:1 benefit-to-cost ratio confirms that the guarantee is a sound investment, *provided* the service standards are operationally feasible.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice): Service Recovery and the 3Rs [14 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **3Rs of Service Recovery** with reference to a real-world service failure scenario. Why is the order of the 3Rs important?

**Model Answer:**

The **3Rs of Service Recovery** are:

1. **R1 — Recognize:** The organization must first *detect* that a service failure has occurred. In an airline, this could be a missed connection flagged by the operations control center.

2. **R2 — Resolve:** The organization must *fix* the problem. The airline rebooks the passenger on the next available flight, arranges lounge access, and provides meal vouchers.

3. **R3 — Reimburse / Redress:** The organization must *compensate* the customer. The airline offers a partial refund or loyalty miles as per its published service guarantee.

**Why order matters:** Recognition must come first because unresolved problems cannot be compensated away. Compensation without resolution leaves the underlying problem intact. Resolution without recognition is reactive, not proactive.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A logistics company has a "Delivery in 24 hours or free" guarantee. Monthly data:
- Total shipments = 40,000
- Failure rate = 2.5%
- Payout per failure = ₹150
- Average CLV of a customer = ₹8,000
- Recovery paradox multiplier = 1.6
- WOM multiplier = 4
- Average acquisition value of a new customer = ₹1,200

Compute:
1. Total monthly cost of the guarantee.
2. Total monthly benefit from the Recovery Paradox.
3. Total monthly benefit from positive WOM.
4. Net monthly benefit of the guarantee.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Total monthly cost.**

$$
\text{CoG} \;=\; (40{,}000 \times 0.025) \times 150
$$

$$
\text{CoG} \;=\; 1{,}000 \times 150 \;=\; ₹1{,}50{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Recovery Paradox benefit.**

Recovered customers = 1,000
Extra loyalty per recovered customer = $CLV \times m$

$$
\text{RP Benefit} \;=\; 1{,}000 \times (8{,}000 \times 1.6)
$$

$$
\text{RP Benefit} \;=\; 1{,}000 \times 12{,}800 \;=\; ₹1{,}28{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Word-of-Mouth benefit.**

$$
\text{WOM Benefit} \;=\; 1{,}000 \times 4 \times 1{,}200
$$

$$
\text{WOM Benefit} \;=\; 4{,}000 \times 1{,}200 \;=\; ₹48{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — Net monthly benefit.**

$$
\text{Net Benefit} \;=\; (\text{RP Benefit} + \text{WOM Benefit}) \;-\; \text{CoG}
$$

$$
\text{Net Benefit} \;=\; (1{,}28{,}00{,}000 + 48{,}00{,}000) \;-\; 1{,}50{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Net Benefit} \;=\; 1{,}76{,}00{,}000 \;-\; 1{,}50{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Net Benefit} \;=\; ₹1{,}74{,}50{,}000 \text{ per month}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{\text{Net Monthly Benefit} \;=\; ₹1.745 \text{ Crore}}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The guarantee is extraordinarily profitable, with benefits outweighing costs by a factor of over **100×** when recovery paradox and WOM effects are accounted for.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Service Guarantee" with "Service Recovery"** — A guarantee is the *pre-event* promise; recovery is the *post-event* action. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting the 3Rs in order** — Many students write "Reimburse" before "Resolve." The correct sequence is **Recognize → Resolve → Reimburse**.
> 3. **Not showing units in numerical answers** — Always write **₹, %**, or **count** next to your final numbers.
> 4. **Skipping the interpretation step** — KTU examiners allocate **1 mark specifically for interpretation** of the numerical result. Never end with a bare number; always add a one-line conclusion.
> 5. **Using vague terms** — "Service guarantee helps customer" gets 0 marks. Use precise phrases: *"increases perceived risk-reduction,"* *"operationalizes the service quality standard,"* *"tangibilizes the intangible."*
> 6. **Mixing up formulas** — CoG uses $N \times f \times p$; POR uses (Claims ÷ Total) × 100. Memorize them as two distinct boxes.
> 7. **Forgetting the feedback loop** — When asked to "design a guarantee," always close with the *organizational learning* step: data from payouts must feed back into service redesign.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Service Guarantee**

- **Definition:** A formal, written, unconditional promise of service performance with pre-defined compensation.
- **Origin:** Conceptualized by **Christopher Lovelock and Leonard Berry** as a service-quality mechanism.
- **The 3Rs:** **Recognize → Resolve → Reimburse** (in this order, always).
- **Characteristics (7):** Unconditional, Meaningful, Easy to Understand, Easy to Invoke, Easy to Collect, Credible, Quantifiable.
- **Types (6):** Unconditional Satisfaction, Performance-Specific, Timeliness, Attribute-Specific, Money-Back, Satisfaction Pledge.
- **Benefits:** Trust, Loyalty, WOM, Operational Discipline, Service Recovery Paradox, Customer Empowerment.
- **Costs:** Direct Payouts, Administration, Process Upgrades, Potential Abuse.
- **Service Recovery Paradox:** Recovered customers can be *more loyal* than customers who never had a problem. Multiplier **1.5 to 2.0**.
- **Key Formulas:**
  - $\text{CoG} = N \times f \times p$
  - $\text{POR} = \frac{\text{Claims}}{\text{Total}} \times 100\%$
  - $\text{Net Benefit} = \text{Benefits} - \text{Costs}$
  - $\text{ROI} = \frac{\text{Net Benefit}}{\text{Cost}} \times 100\%$
  - $\text{LVR} = CLV \times (1 + m)$
- **Famous Examples:** Domino's (30 min), FedEx (overnight), L.L. Bean (unconditional returns), JetBlue (Bill of Rights), Nordstrom (unconditional satisfaction).
- **Common Mistakes in Design:** Fine print, vague standards, hard-to-claim process, slow payouts, untrainable standards.
- **Decision Rule:** Introduce a guarantee when (a) failure is detectable, (b) standard is measurable, and (c) recovery cost < customer lifetime value.
- **The Feedback Loop:** Every payout generates data → data drives process improvement → improvement raises the standard → standard tightens the guarantee → cycle continues.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:**
> When asked a 14-mark question, structure your answer as: **Definition → 3Rs/Characteristics → Types → Real Example → Numerical (if any) → Interpretation → Conclusion (linking to service strategy).** This 7-part structure aligns with KTU's **CO (Course Outcome) mapping** for PECBT861 and is the proven template for full marks.

---

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Service Recovery** is the set of actions and processes a service organization undertakes to address, rectify, and learn from customer complaints and service failures in order to restore customer satisfaction, trust, and loyalty. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus framework (rooted in the works of Valarie A. Zeithaml, A. Parasuraman, and Leonard L. Berry), service recovery is positioned as a **critical component of service quality management** and is considered the *last opportunity* for a service firm to retain a dissatisfied customer before defection occurs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone):**
> *"Service recovery refers to the actions a service provider takes in response to a service failure. Effective service recovery can transform dissatisfied customers into loyal advocates, an outcome often called the Service Recovery Paradox."*

### 1.1.1 Service Failure — The Trigger Event

A **service failure** is any service-related problem perceived by the customer that causes dissatisfaction. Failures are classified into three broad categories:

| Failure Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Service Process Failure** | Failure in the core service delivery process | Slow checkout, late delivery |
| **Outcome Failure** | The core service itself fails to perform | Wrong bill, defective product |
| **Employee Response Failure** | Employee behavior or response causes dissatisfaction | Rude staff, indifference |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Restaurant Analogy 🍽️**
> Imagine you are dining at a fine restaurant. Your order arrives 45 minutes late, the soup is cold, and the waiter doesn't apologize. You are furious. This is a **service failure**.
> Now, the manager comes over, sincerely apologizes, offers you a complimentary dessert, comps the bill partially, and the chef personally comes out to apologize. You leave *more* loyal to the restaurant than if the original meal had been perfect. This transformation is the essence of **Service Recovery**.

> **Key Intuition:** Service Recovery is the *bandage* for a wounded customer relationship. Done poorly, it kills the patient (the customer). Done exceptionally, the patient becomes *healthier* than before — this is the famous **Service Recovery Paradox**.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

- **Service Recovery Paradox (SRP):** Phenomenon where customers become *more loyal* after a successful recovery than customers who never experienced any failure.
- **Justice Theory:** Framework explaining customer perceptions of fairness during recovery (Distributive, Procedural, Interactional).
- **Service Guarantee:** A formal pledge promising a specific remedy if service fails.
- **Customer Defection:** When a dissatisfied customer leaves for a competitor.
- **Recovery Performance:** The effectiveness with which a firm resolves a service failure.
- **Word-of-Mouth (WOM):** Customer-to-customer communication, heavily influenced by recovery experiences.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Highlight:** Module 3 questions frequently test the **Service Recovery Paradox**, the **Justice Theory framework**, and the **Service Recovery Process (Acknowledge → Apologize → Fix → Follow-up → Document)**. Memorize these rigorously.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations of Service Recovery

Service recovery is built on three foundational pillars that the KTU examiner expects you to articulate:

### 2.1.1 Pillar 1: Customer Expectations After Failure

When a service failure occurs, customers form a **recovery expectation** — a benchmark for what the firm *should* do to make things right. These expectations typically include:

1. **Acknowledgment** of the problem
2. **Empathy** and a sincere apology
3. **Timely resolution** of the issue
4. **Fair compensation** (refund, replacement, discount)
5. **Follow-up** to ensure the issue does not recur

The gap between **expected recovery** and **perceived recovery** determines post-failure satisfaction:

$$\text{Recovery Satisfaction} = \text{Perceived Recovery} - \text{Expected Recovery}$$

> When this value is **positive**, satisfaction rises. When **negative**, the customer defection probability spikes.

### 2.1.2 Pillar 2: The Service Recovery Paradox (SRP)

Discovered empirically by researchers (notably McCollough, Berry, & Yadav, 2000), the paradox states that:

> **A successfully recovered customer exhibits HIGHER loyalty than a customer who never experienced failure at all.**

This counterintuitive phenomenon occurs because:
- The firm has demonstrated **empathy, responsiveness, and integrity**.
- The customer feels **valued** and emotionally bonded.
- Cognitive dissonance is resolved in the firm's favor.

**Mathematical Representation of SRP:**

$$\text{Loyalty}_{\text{Recovered}} > \text{Loyalty}_{\text{No Failure}}$$

Subject to the condition:

$$\text{Recovery Effectiveness} \geq \text{Threshold}_{\text{Paradox Activation}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The paradox is not automatic.** It activates only when the recovery effort exceeds a quality threshold. A poor recovery makes the customer *more* angry, not more loyal.

### 2.1.3 Pillar 3: Justice Theory in Service Recovery

Customers judge the fairness of recovery on **three dimensions of justice**:

| Justice Type | Customer Question | KTU Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **Distributive Justice** | *"Was the compensation fair?"* | Fairness of the **outcome** (refund, replacement) |
| **Procedural Justice** | *"Was the process fair and fast?"* | Fairness of the **policies and procedures** used |
| **Interactional Justice** | *"Was I treated with respect?"* | Fairness of the **interpersonal treatment** by staff |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked "which type of justice is most important," KTU valuation keys typically award full marks only when the student mentions that **Interactional Justice** is often the *strongest predictor* of post-recovery satisfaction, even outweighing the compensation amount.

## 2.2 The Service Recovery Process — Stepwise Framework

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes a **five-step recovery process** (often called the *Service Recovery Sequence*):

1. **Acknowledge** — Identify and confirm the failure.
2. **Apologize** — Offer a genuine, no-excuse apology.
3. **Fix** — Resolve the issue quickly and efficiently.
4. **Follow-up** — Contact the customer later to confirm satisfaction.
5. **Document** — Record the incident for organizational learning.

> **Why this sequence?** The first three steps address the *immediate* emotional and operational wound. The last two steps build *long-term* trust and *organizational* improvement.

## 2.3 Proactive vs. Reactive Recovery

| Dimension | Proactive Recovery | Reactive Recovery |
|---|---|---|
| **Trigger** | Anticipated failure (anticipate before customer complains) | Actual customer complaint |
| **Timing** | Before failure occurs | After failure occurs |
| **Example** | Hotel sends free upgrade to a flagged VIP room delay | Customer calls, manager offers a free meal |
| **Advantage** | Converts near-failures to loyalty moments | Necessary but often too late |
| **Risk** | Cost of over-recovery | Cost of customer defection |

## 2.4 Service Guarantees as a Recovery Tool

A **Service Guarantee** is an explicit, pre-committed promise that if the service fails, the customer will receive a specified remedy (e.g., "On-time or it's free!").

**Three Conditions for a Successful Guarantee (KTU framework):**

1. **Unconditional** — No hidden fine print.
2. **Easy to Invoke** — No bureaucracy.
3. **Meaningful** — The remedy matters to the customer (e.g., full refund, not a coupon worth 5%).

> [!NOTE]
> **Statistical Insight:** Studies (cited in KTU reference material) suggest that firms with strong service guarantees experience **fewer complaints** (because staff are more careful), and complainers often turn into *enthusiasts* when guarantees are honored.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|
| Recovery Satisfaction (RS) | $RS = P_{rec} - E_{rec}$ | $P_{rec} \geq 0$; $E_{rec} > 0$ |
| Service Recovery Paradox Condition | $L_{recovered} > L_{nofail}$ | Only when recovery effectiveness $R_e \geq T_{paradox}$ |
| Overall Service Quality Gap (5 Gaps) | $Gap_5 = P_s - E_s$ | KTU Module 1/2 retention topic |
| Complaint Resolution Rate (CRR) | $CRR = \dfrac{\text{Resolved Complaints}}{\text{Total Complaints}} \times 100\%$ | Expressed as a percentage |
| Cost of Recovery (COR) | $COR = C_{fix} + C_{compensate} + C_{reputation}$ | Includes intangible reputation cost |
| Customer Effort Score (CES) Weight | $CES_{impact} \propto \dfrac{1}{\text{Effort Customer Makes}}$ | Lower effort = higher satisfaction |
| Repeat Purchase Probability (RPP) post-recovery | $RPP = f(\text{Distributive}, \text{Procedural}, \text{Interactional Justice})$ | All three justices must align |
| Recovery Threshold ($T_{paradox}$) | $T_{paradox} = \alpha \cdot E_{rec} + \beta \cdot E_{effort}$ | Empirical; $\alpha, \beta$ are weights |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation Convention (Anti-Break Safeguard):** All absolute value / condition bars in formulas use the LaTeX command `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent markdown table syntax breakage. For example: $\vert P_{rec} - E_{rec} \vert$ instead of $\vert P_{rec} - E_{rec} \vert$ with literal pipes.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering / Service Industry Utility

Service recovery is universally applicable across industries:

- **Aviation:** Compensation for delayed flights (Distributive Justice in action).
- **Healthcare:** Apology and treatment re-evaluation after misdiagnosis.
- **Hospitality:** Free stay upgrades for poor check-in experiences.
- **E-commerce:** Refunds, replacements, and apology emails for defective deliveries.
- **Telecom/Banking:** Credit for service outages and follow-up calls.

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU 2024, the cross-industry application angle is a **favourite 7-mark question** in Part B examinations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Service Recovery Paradox Loyalty Equation

We begin with the foundational loyalty function of a customer after a service encounter. Let:

- $L_0$ = Baseline loyalty of a customer before any service experience
- $L_{nf}$ = Loyalty of a customer who experienced **no failure**
- $L_f$ = Loyalty of a customer who experienced **failure**
- $L_{rec}$ = Loyalty of a customer who experienced failure **and** recovery
- $R_e$ = Recovery effectiveness (a normalized score, $0 \leq R_e \leq 1$)
- $T_{paradox}$ = Paradox activation threshold (a critical value of $R_e$)
- $S_f$ = Severity of the original failure (a normalized score, $0 < S_f \leq 1$)

### 3.1.1 Step 1: Define Baseline Loyalty After No-Failure Experience

A customer with no service failure typically retains baseline loyalty, perhaps with a small positive nudge from a satisfactory encounter:

$$
L_{nf} = L_0 + \delta
$$

Where $\delta$ is a small satisfaction increment (e.g., $\delta = 0.05 \cdot L_0$ for a typical positive experience).

### 3.1.2 Step 2: Define Loyalty After Failure (Without Recovery)

A failure causes a sharp drop in loyalty, proportional to severity:

$$
L_f = L_0 \cdot (1 - S_f)
$$

**Logic:** The more severe the failure, the greater the loyalty loss. If $S_f = 1$ (catastrophic failure), then $L_f = 0$ (complete defection).

### 3.1.3 Step 3: Define Loyalty After Recovery

Recovery adds back loyalty, scaled by effectiveness, but also has a diminishing-returns property:

$$
L_{rec} = L_0 \cdot (1 - S_f) + L_0 \cdot R_e \cdot \gamma
$$

Where $\gamma$ is a "paradox amplification factor" (typically $1.2 \leq \gamma \leq 2.0$, depending on industry context).

**Logic:** Recovery not only restores lost loyalty ($L_0 \cdot R_e$) but also *amplifies* it by $\gamma$ to reflect the bonding effect of the firm's care.

### 3.1.4 Step 4: Activate the Paradox

The paradox activates when $L_{rec} > L_{nf}$:

$$
L_0 \cdot (1 - S_f) + L_0 \cdot R_e \cdot \gamma > L_0 + \delta
$$

Divide throughout by $L_0$:

$$
(1 - S_f) + R_e \cdot \gamma > 1 + \dfrac{\delta}{L_0}
$$

Rearranging to solve for $R_e$:

$$
R_e \cdot \gamma > 1 + \dfrac{\delta}{L_0} - (1 - S_f)
$$

$$
R_e \cdot \gamma > S_f + \dfrac{\delta}{L_0}
$$

$$
R_e > \dfrac{S_f + \dfrac{\delta}{L_0}}{\gamma}
$$

### 3.1.5 Step 5: Define the Paradox Activation Threshold

The threshold $T_{paradox}$ is therefore:

$$
T_{paradox} = \dfrac{S_f + \dfrac{\delta}{L_0}}{\gamma}
$$

**Interpretation:** To trigger the paradox, recovery effectiveness $R_e$ must exceed this threshold. Higher severity ($S_f$) raises the threshold, requiring *better* recovery to trigger the paradox. A larger amplification factor ($\gamma$) lowers the threshold, making paradox activation easier.

### 3.1.6 Step 6: Numerical Illustration

Suppose $S_f = 0.4$ (moderate failure), $\delta / L_0 = 0.05$ (small baseline satisfaction increment), and $\gamma = 1.5$:

$$
T_{paradox} = \dfrac{0.4 + 0.05}{1.5} = \dfrac{0.45}{1.5} = 0.30
$$

Thus, recovery effectiveness must exceed **30%** for the paradox to activate. If the firm achieves $R_e = 0.50$, the paradox is triggered; if $R_e = 0.20$, the paradox fails and the customer remains dissatisfied.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Recovery Strategies by Failure Type

| Failure Type | Primary Justice Lever | Recommended Recovery Action | Communication Channel | KTU Marking Weight |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Outcome Failure** | Distributive Justice | Refund, replacement, or discount | Phone + Email | High (7 marks) |
| **Process Failure** | Procedural Justice | Process redesign + immediate compensation | Email + In-person | High (7 marks) |
| **Employee Response Failure** | Interactional Justice | Personal apology + staff retraining | In-person call from manager | Very High (10+ marks) |

## 3.3 Cost-Benefit Analysis of Recovery

Let us derive the **Net Recovery Value (NRV)** of a service recovery initiative:

- $C_r$ = Direct cost of recovery (compensation, labor)
- $C_{def}$ = Cost of customer defection (lost lifetime value)
- $P_r$ = Probability of recovery succeeding (depends on $R_e$)
- $V_{rec}$ = Customer lifetime value retained post-recovery
- $V_{loyalty\_boost}$ = Extra value from paradox-driven loyalty

$$
NRV = P_r \cdot (V_{rec} + V_{loyalty\_boost}) - C_r - (1 - P_r) \cdot C_{def}
$$

**Decision Rule:** The firm should execute recovery if $NRV > 0$.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: The Three Justices in Detail

| Justice Type | Customer's Mental Question | Tangible Element | Intangible Element | Impact on Satisfaction |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Distributive** | *"Did I get what I deserved?"* | Refund amount, voucher value | Perceived fairness | Medium-High |
| **Procedural** | *"Was the process quick and fair?"* | Resolution time, ease of process | Transparency, control | High |
| **Interactional** | *"Was I treated with dignity?"* | Polite language, active listening | Empathy, apology tone | Very High |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Analytical Insight:** A common examiner's challenge is: *"If a firm has a limited budget, which justice should it prioritize?"* The correct answer is **Interactional Justice**, because it has the highest correlation with satisfaction per unit of cost (an apology costs nothing, but yields disproportionate loyalty gains).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]
**Define Service Recovery and state its significance in service management.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Service Recovery refers to the systematic actions a service organization takes to address and resolve customer complaints arising from service failures (1 mark). It involves acknowledging the failure, apologizing, fixing the problem, compensating fairly, and following up to restore customer satisfaction (1 mark). Its significance lies in retaining customers, preventing defection, generating positive word-of-mouth, and — when executed exceptionally — activating the *Service Recovery Paradox*, where recovered customers become more loyal than those who never experienced failure (1 mark).

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]
**Briefly explain the concept of the Service Recovery Paradox with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Service Recovery Paradox (SRP) is the phenomenon in which a customer who experiences a service failure that is *successfully recovered* ends up with higher loyalty and satisfaction than a customer who never experienced any failure at all (2 marks). Example: A passenger whose flight is delayed for 3 hours and then receives a full refund, hotel voucher, and personal apology from the airline CEO may book with the same airline more frequently than a passenger whose flight was on time, because the airline demonstrated extraordinary care (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the three dimensions of Justice Theory as applied to service recovery. Which dimension has the strongest impact on post-recovery satisfaction, and why? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the Service Recovery Process in detail with reference to a real-world service industry of your choice. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The three dimensions of Justice Theory in service recovery are (KTU syllabus reference — Zeithaml, Bitner, Gremler):

1. **Distributive Justice (2 marks):** Refers to the perceived fairness of the *outcome* of the recovery. The customer asks, *"Did I receive fair compensation for my loss?"* Examples include the size of a refund, the value of a replacement product, or the extent of a discount offered. A customer who receives a $100 refund for a $50 product feels a strong sense of distributive justice.

2. **Procedural Justice (2 marks):** Refers to the perceived fairness of the *process* used to handle the complaint. Key elements include speed of resolution, ease of complaint filing, transparency of policies, and the customer's ability to provide input. A firm that resolves issues in 24 hours exhibits high procedural justice; a firm that takes 30 days does not.

3. **Interactional Justice (2 marks):** Refers to the perceived fairness of the *interpersonal treatment* the customer receives. This includes politeness, empathy, active listening, courtesy, and respectful communication. A sincere apology, eye contact, and an unhurried tone all build interactional justice.

**Strongest Impact (1 mark):** **Interactional Justice** has the strongest impact on post-recovery satisfaction, as evidenced in KTU-cited research. The reason is that a customer perceives a sincere, empathetic interaction as a sign of genuine care, which is *psychologically* more valuable than the monetary compensation itself. Furthermore, interactional justice is essentially *costless* to the firm (an apology has no direct cost) yet yields disproportionately high loyalty returns.

#### Model Solution for (b):

The Service Recovery Process is a structured, five-step framework used by service organizations to address failures systematically. Applying it to the **airline industry** (3 marks for the industry context, 4 marks for the process):

1. **Acknowledge:** When a flight is delayed, the airline's first step is to acknowledge the delay publicly via announcements, SMS alerts, and app notifications. The gate agent confirms the issue without making excuses (1 mark).

2. **Apologize:** The airline issues a formal apology — both at the gate (in person) and online. The apology is sincere, with no "blame-shifting" to weather, ATC, or operations (1 mark).

3. **Fix:** The airline rebooks passengers on alternative flights, provides meal vouchers, arranges hotel accommodation for overnight delays, and ensures ground transport. The fix is *expedited* — passengers are not left waiting in queues (1 mark).

4. **Compensate:** The airline provides compensation as per regulatory frameworks (e.g., EU261 in Europe) and goes beyond by offering future-travel vouchers, loyalty miles, and priority boarding for the next flight. This addresses *distributive justice* (1 mark).

5. **Follow-up & Document:** Within 48 hours, the airline sends a follow-up email to the affected passenger, asking about their experience and offering additional apology. Internally, the incident is documented in the *Service Recovery Database* for organizational learning — root-cause analysis is conducted, and the procedure is updated to prevent recurrence (1 mark).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Tie-In:** Airlines like **Southwest, Emirates, and Singapore Airlines** are industry benchmarks in service recovery. Their customer loyalty metrics are directly correlated with their recovery performance, validating the Service Recovery Process in a high-stakes, real-time service environment.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Define Service Failure. Classify the major types of service failures with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What are Service Guarantees? Discuss the conditions required for a successful service guarantee with reference to a real-world example. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Definition (2 marks):** A Service Failure is any service-related problem, real or perceived, that causes customer dissatisfaction and triggers a service recovery response. It represents a gap between customer expectations and the actual service delivered.

**Classification (5 marks):** Service failures are typically classified into three major categories:

1. **Service Process Failure (2 marks):** A failure in the *process* by which the service is delivered, even if the outcome is acceptable. The customer's experience of the journey is broken. *Example:* A restaurant's food is excellent, but the customer waits 90 minutes for a table due to poor reservation management. *Engineering analogy:* A perfectly manufactured product that ships late due to logistics breakdown.

2. **Outcome Failure (2 marks):** A failure in the *core service outcome* itself — the very thing the customer paid for. *Example:* A bank debits a customer's account twice for a single transaction, or an online order arrives damaged. *Engineering analogy:* A printed circuit board that passes all inspection tests but fails in the field.

3. **Employee Response Failure (1 mark):** A failure caused by *employee behavior*, attitude, or response to a customer need. *Example:* A retail store employee is rude when a customer asks for help, or a call center agent disconnects the call prematurely. This type of failure is particularly damaging because it adds *emotional* injury on top of any underlying service issue.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Definition (2 marks):** A Service Guarantee is a formal, explicit promise made by a service provider to its customers, pledging a specific level of service quality and a defined remedy (often monetary) if the service fails to meet the promised standard.

**Conditions for a Successful Service Guarantee (4 marks):**

1. **Unconditional (1 mark):** The guarantee must have no hidden fine print, no exceptions, and no loopholes. Customers must feel that the guarantee is iron-clad. *Example:* FedEx's old slogan *"When it absolutely, positively has to be there overnight"* carried an implicit unconditional guarantee of on-time delivery.

2. **Easy to Invoke (1 mark):** The customer should be able to claim the guarantee without bureaucratic obstacles, long forms, or proof-of-purchase difficulties. *Example:* Domino's Pizza's famous *"30 minutes or free"* guarantee was simple to invoke — if the pizza was late, the customer got it free, no questions asked.

3. **Meaningful (1 mark):** The remedy must be valuable enough to matter to the customer. A 5% coupon for a 3-hour delay is insulting; a full refund is meaningful. *Example:* Southwest Airlines' *"bags fly free"* guarantee became meaningful because it saved customers $50+ per checked bag.

4. **Aligned with Service Capability (1 mark):** The firm must be operationally capable of delivering what it guarantees, otherwise the guarantee becomes a liability. *Example:* Domino's eventually retired the "30-minute" guarantee in part because driving pressure led to safety risks — a misalignment between promise and operational reality.

**Real-World Example Discussion (1 mark):** The **Marriott Hotel "Look Us in the Eye" Guarantee** promised that if any guest found the hotel unsatisfactory in any way, the front-desk staff would fix the issue on the spot or reduce the bill — with no manager approval required. This is a textbook example of an unconditional, easy-to-invoke, meaningful service guarantee.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Service Recovery with Service Quality:** Service quality is *preventive*; service recovery is *corrective*. Marks are lost when students use these interchangeably.
> 2. **Treating the Recovery Paradox as automatic:** Many students wrongly claim the paradox *always* occurs. The correct answer is that it activates *only when* recovery effectiveness exceeds a threshold.
> 3. **Listing justices without ranking:** When asked "which is most important," students often list all three without specifying **Interactional Justice** as the highest-impact lever. This costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Skipping the "Why":** KTU valuation keys reward *explanation* (the "why"), not just listing. For 7-mark questions, at least 2–3 sentences of justification are expected per sub-part.
> 5. **No real-world examples:** Generic answers lose marks. Always include a specific industry example (airlines, hotels, e-commerce, banking) in 7-mark answers.
> 6. **Forgetting follow-up and documentation:** The recovery process has *five* steps, not three. Skipping follow-up or documentation is a common 1-mark loss.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Service Recovery (Module 3)**

- ✅ **Definition:** Actions taken to address and resolve service failures, restore customer satisfaction, and prevent defection.
- ✅ **Service Failure Types:** Outcome, Process, Employee Response (memorize examples for each).
- ✅ **Service Recovery Paradox (SRP):** Successful recovery → higher loyalty than no-failure customers; activates only above a threshold.
- ✅ **SRP Threshold Formula:** $T_{paradox} = (S_f + \delta/L_0) / \gamma$.
- ✅ **Justice Theory — Three Dimensions:**
  - Distributive (outcome fairness)
  - Procedural (process fairness)
  - Interactional (interpersonal fairness) — **most important**.
- ✅ **Service Recovery Process (5 steps):** Acknowledge → Apologize → Fix → Follow-up → Document.
- ✅ **Proactive vs. Reactive:** Proactive = anticipate; Reactive = respond after complaint.
- ✅ **Service Guarantees:** Must be unconditional, easy to invoke, meaningful, and operationally feasible.
- ✅ **Net Recovery Value (NRV):** $NRV = P_r \cdot (V_{rec} + V_{loyalty\_boost}) - C_r - (1 - P_r) \cdot C_{def}$. Recovery is justified if NRV > 0.
- ✅ **Common Examples:** Airlines (delays), Hotels (poor rooms), E-commerce (defective goods), Telecom (network outage), Banking (transaction errors).
- ✅ **KTU-Favourite 14-Mark Pattern:** Justice Theory + Recovery Process with industry example.
- ✅ **Valuation Traps:** Don't confuse recovery with quality; don't claim SRP is automatic; rank the three justices; always use real-world examples.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Forecasting Demand for Services
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT861 - SERVICE SCIENCE AND SERVICE OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Forecasting Demand for Services

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forecasting Demand for Services** is the systematic process of estimating future customer demand for intangible service offerings over a specified time horizon, using historical data, statistical models, judgmental inputs, and causal relationships, in order to plan capacity, staffing, inventory of consumables, and service delivery schedules.

According to KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, demand forecasting in a service context is the **proactive anticipation of the volume, timing, and composition of service requests** that a service system will be required to satisfy. Unlike goods, services are **perishable, intangible, inseparable from the provider, heterogeneous, and lack buffer inventory** — making accurate forecasting a *strategic survival tool* rather than just a planning convenience.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 3):**
> Forecasting in services is uniquely challenging because the *product* cannot be stored. A forecast that is too high creates idle capacity and wage costs; a forecast that is too low generates queueing delays, lost goodwill, and customer defection. The **service capacity = demand** equation must be balanced in real time.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Rain Predictor of a Beach Town"

Imagine you own a **lemonade and umbrella rental stand on a Kerala beach** (say, Varkala or Kovalam).

- You cannot *store* a sunny day or *inventory* a breeze.
- On a **cloudy weekday** (low demand), you bring 30 umbrellas and 5 kg of lemons. By evening, most lemons are wasted and the umbrellas sit idle.
- On a **bright Onam weekend** (high demand), you bring 30 umbrellas and 5 kg of lemons — and by 11 AM, you are sold out, customers are angry, and competitors across the beach are laughing.

**Forecasting** is the *weather-prediction skill* you develop:
- Look at last year's same-week patterns,
- Notice the trend (more tourists every year),
- Adjust for the Onam festival (seasonal effect),
- Use a weather app (causal variable),
- Use your gut when patterns break (judgmental input).

In service operations, this same logic — **systematic prediction under uncertainty** — is the foundation of capacity planning, staff scheduling, and revenue management.

## 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Demand** | The quantity of service that customers desire over a period. |
| **Forecast** | A prediction of future demand under stated assumptions. |
| **Forecast Horizon** | The length of time into the future for which a forecast is made (short / medium / long term). |
| **Forecast Error** | The deviation of actual demand from forecasted demand. |
| **MAD** | Mean Absolute Deviation — average of unsigned errors. |
| **MSE** | Mean Squared Error — average of squared errors. |
| **MAPE** | Mean Absolute Percentage Error — average of percentage errors. |
| **Seasonality** | Predictable, repeating demand pattern within a year (e.g., tourist season). |
| **Cyclical** | Longer-term economic ups and downs (multi-year). |
| **Trend** | Long-term upward or downward movement in demand. |
| **Random Variation** | Irregular fluctuations with no identifiable pattern. |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Forecasting Process in Services

A service organization follows a structured **7-step forecasting cycle**:

1. **Define the forecasting objective** — what decision will the forecast support? (e.g., how many doctors to roster next Monday at the OP clinic?).
2. **Collect and clean historical data** — patient footfall, call-centre tickets, restaurant covers, hotel bookings.
3. **Choose the forecasting horizon** — short-term (≤ 1 month), medium-term (1–12 months), long-term (> 1 year).
4. **Select a forecasting method** — qualitative, time-series, causal, or simulation.
5. **Generate the forecast** — produce point estimates and confidence intervals.
6. **Monitor forecast accuracy** — compute MAD, MAPE, or tracking signal.
7. **Refine the model** — recalibrate parameters, switch methods, or add causal variables.

## 2.2 Classification of Forecasting Methods

### A. Qualitative Methods (Judgmental)
Used when **no historical data exists** (new service launch) or when the future is **radically unlike the past** (pandemic, policy shock).

- **Executive opinion (Delphi method)** — structured expert panels.
- **Sales force composite** — frontline staff pool their on-ground insights.
- **Customer survey / Market research** — stated intentions, conjoint analysis.
- **Analogous forecasting** — use a similar service market as proxy.

### B. Quantitative — Time Series Methods
Assume the **future is a function of the past**. Four classical patterns: *Level, Trend, Seasonality, Cycle, Random*.

#### (i) Simple Moving Average (SMA)
Equal weight to the last $n$ periods.

$$F_{t+1} = \frac{D_t + D_{t-1} + D_{t-2} + \dots + D_{t-n+1}}{n}$$

- Best for **stationary demand** (no trend, no seasonality).
- Larger $n$ → smoother but more lagged forecast.

#### (ii) Weighted Moving Average (WMA)
Recent periods get higher weights that sum to 1.

$$F_{t+1} = w_1 D_t + w_2 D_{t-1} + w_3 D_{t-2} + \dots + w_n D_{t-n+1}$$

where $\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = 1$ and $w_1 \geq w_2 \geq \dots \geq w_n$.

#### (iii) Exponential Smoothing (Simple / Single)
Reactive, self-correcting, minimal data storage (only $F_t$ and $\alpha$).

$$F_{t+1} = F_t + \alpha (D_t - F_t) = \alpha D_t + (1 - \alpha) F_t$$

where $\alpha$ is the **smoothing constant**, $0 \leq \alpha \leq 1$.

- **High $\alpha$** (close to 1) → responsive to recent changes.
- **Low $\alpha$** (close to 0) → stable but lagging.

#### (iv) Holt's Linear Trend Method
Captures **level + trend**, two smoothing constants.

$$L_t = \alpha D_t + (1 - \alpha)(L_{t-1} + T_{t-1})$$

$$T_t = \beta (L_t - L_{t-1}) + (1 - \beta) T_{t-1}$$

$$F_{t+m} = L_t + m \cdot T_t$$

where $L_t$ = level, $T_t$ = trend, $m$ = periods ahead.

#### (v) Winters' Triple Exponential Smoothing
Adds a **seasonal index** $S_t$ with period $p$.

$$L_t = \alpha \frac{D_t}{S_{t-p}} + (1 - \alpha)(L_{t-1} + T_{t-1})$$

$$T_t = \beta (L_t - L_{t-1}) + (1 - \beta) T_{t-1}$$

$$S_t = \gamma \frac{D_t}{L_t} + (1 - \gamma) S_{t-p}$$

$$F_{t+m} = (L_t + m T_t) S_{t-p+m}$$

### C. Quantitative — Causal (Associative) Methods
Use **independent variables** that *cause* demand changes.

- **Linear regression**: $D = a + bX + \varepsilon$
  - $X$ may be advertising spend, temperature, fuel price, exam schedule, etc.
- **Multiple regression**: $D = a + b_1 X_1 + b_2 X_2 + \dots$
- **Econometric models** for macro-level service demand.
- **Leading indicator analysis** — use a leading metric (e.g., flight bookings) to predict hotel demand.

### D. Simulation and AI-Based Methods
- **Monte Carlo simulation** for stochastic service demand.
- **ARIMA / SARIMA** for complex auto-correlated patterns.
- **Machine Learning** (Random Forest, LSTM, Prophet) for high-volume digital services.

## 2.3 Service-Specific Challenges in Forecasting

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why service forecasting is harder than goods forecasting:**
> 1. **No inventory buffer** — unmet demand is *lost forever* (perishable capacity).
> 2. **Demand–capacity simultaneity** — customer is part of the process; their presence consumes capacity *while* receiving it.
> 3. **High variability** — arrival pattern, transaction time, request type (Poisson-distributed, log-normal service times).
> 4. **Customer-induced heterogeneity** — each customer interaction is unique.
> 5. **External sensitivity** — weather, festivals, viral social media, school calendars.
> 6. **Self-fulfilling prophecies** — announcing a "long wait" creates more walk-aways, distorting the very data the next forecast relies on.

## 2.4 Forecast Accuracy Metrics — The KTU Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> These four error metrics are **guaranteed 2–3 mark questions** in KTU 2024 ESE. Memorize the formulas and units.

| Metric | Formula | Unit | Sensitivity to Large Errors |
|---|---|---|---|
| **MAD** (Mean Absolute Deviation) | $\text{MAD} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{t=1}^{n} \vert D_t - F_t \vert$ | Same as demand | Linear |
| **MSE** (Mean Squared Error) | $\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{t=1}^{n} (D_t - F_t)^2$ | Demand² | Quadratic (penalizes outliers) |
| **RMSE** (Root MSE) | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\text{MSE}}$ | Same as demand | Quadratic |
| **MAPE** (Mean Absolute Percentage Error) | $\text{MAPE} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{t=1}^{n} \left\vert \frac{D_t - F_t}{D_t} \right\vert \times 100$ | Percentage | Unit-free, comparable |

**Tracking Signal (TS)** — detects systematic bias:

$$\text{TS} = \frac{\text{Cumulative Forecast Error (CFE)}}{\text{MAD}} = \frac{\sum (D_t - F_t)}{\text{MAD}}$$

- $\vert \text{TS} \vert \leq 4$ → forecast is *in control*.
- $\vert \text{TS} \vert > 4$ → model is **biased**, needs recalibration.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Healthcare**: Emergency room staffing, ICU bed forecasting using SARIMA + epidemic data.
- **Hospitality**: Kerala tourism — demand peaks during Onam, Christmas, monsoon off-season.
- **Banking**: ATM cash load forecasting, call-centre staffing at 9 AM salary-credit peak.
- **Telecom**: Cell tower load forecasting, fibre rollout demand.
- **Airlines**: Revenue management — overbooking based on no-show forecasting.
- **Public Services**: KSRTC bus scheduling, Kerala Water Authority pump-hour planning.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Simple & Weighted Moving Average

> **Problem (KTU style):** A diagnostic centre records the following number of MRI bookings over the last 5 days. Compute the **3-day Simple Moving Average (SMA)** forecast for Day 6 and the **3-day Weighted Moving Average (WMA)** using weights 0.5, 0.3, 0.2 for Days 5, 4, 3 respectively.

| Day ($t$) | Demand $D_t$ (bookings) |
|---|---|
| 1 | 20 |
| 2 | 22 |
| 3 | 25 |
| 4 | 21 |
| 5 | 26 |

### Step 1 — Identify the moving window
We need the **last 3 days**: $D_3, D_4, D_5 = 25, 21, 26$.

### Step 2 — Apply the SMA formula

$$F_6 = \frac{D_5 + D_4 + D_3}{3} = \frac{26 + 21 + 25}{3} = \frac{72}{3} = 24 \text{ bookings}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the WMA formula

$$F_6 = w_1 D_5 + w_2 D_4 + w_3 D_3$$

$$F_6 = (0.5 \times 26) + (0.3 \times 21) + (0.2 \times 25)$$

$$F_6 = 13.0 + 6.3 + 5.0$$

$$F_6 = 24.3 \approx 24 \text{ bookings}$$

**Interpretation**: WMA gives slightly more weight to the *most recent* surge (Day 5 = 26) and the result is marginally higher than SMA, reflecting responsiveness.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Single Exponential Smoothing

> **Problem:** Using the same data above, given $\alpha = 0.3$, $F_1 = D_1 = 20$, compute exponential smoothing forecasts for Days 2–6. Also compute **MAD** and **MAPE** to compare accuracy with SMA.

### Step 1 — Initialize
$F_1 = D_1 = 20$

### Step 2 — Iteratively apply $F_{t+1} = \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha) F_t$ for $t = 1 \to 5$

| Day $t$ | $D_t$ | $F_t$ | Computation | $F_{t+1}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 20 | 20.00 | (initial) | $0.3(20) + 0.7(20) = 20.00$ |
| 2 | 22 | 20.00 | $0.3(20) + 0.7(20) = 20.00$ | $F_3 = 20.00$ |
| 3 | 25 | 20.00 | $0.3(22) + 0.7(20) = 20.60$ | $F_4 = 20.60$ |
| 4 | 21 | 20.60 | $0.3(25) + 0.7(20.60) = 21.92$ | $F_5 = 21.92$ |
| 5 | 26 | 21.92 | $0.3(21) + 0.7(21.92) = 21.64$ | $F_6 = 21.64$ |

$$F_6 = 21.64 \approx 22 \text{ bookings}$$

### Step 3 — Compute MAD for the exponential smoothing model
Forecast errors $e_t = D_t - F_t$ for $t = 2,3,4,5$ (since $F_1$ is exact):

$$e_2 = 22 - 20.00 = 2.00$$

$$e_3 = 25 - 20.00 = 5.00$$

$$e_4 = 21 - 20.60 = 0.40$$

$$e_5 = 26 - 21.92 = 4.08$$

$$\text{MAD} = \frac{2.00 + 5.00 + 0.40 + 4.08}{4} = \frac{11.48}{4} = 2.87$$

### Step 4 — Compute MAPE

$$\text{MAPE} = \frac{1}{4}\left(\left\vert\frac{2.00}{22}\right\vert + \left\vert\frac{5.00}{25}\right\vert + \left\vert\frac{0.40}{21}\right\vert + \left\vert\frac{4.08}{26}\right\vert\right) \times 100$$

$$\text{MAPE} = \frac{1}{4}(0.0909 + 0.2000 + 0.0190 + 0.1569) \times 100$$

$$\text{MAPE} = \frac{1}{4}(0.4668) \times 100 = 11.67\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation**: A MAPE of **11.67 %** is generally considered *good* for service demand forecasting (acceptable range 10–20 %).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Linear Regression (Causal Method)

> **Problem (KTU 2024 style):** A Kerala resort wants to forecast monthly room occupancy. The manager believes the **number of tourist arrivals at Cochin International Airport (in thousands)** is a causal driver. Given the data below, build a linear regression model and forecast occupancy for a month with 50 thousand arrivals.

| Month | Airport Arrivals $X$ (thousands) | Occupancy $Y$ (%) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 30 | 45 |
| 2 | 35 | 50 |
| 3 | 40 | 55 |
| 4 | 45 | 65 |
| 5 | 50 | 70 |

### Step 1 — Compute the means

$$\bar{X} = \frac{30+35+40+45+50}{5} = 40$$

$$\bar{Y} = \frac{45+50+55+65+70}{5} = 57$$

### Step 2 — Compute slope $b$

$$b = \frac{\sum (X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y})}{\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2}$$

| $X_i - \bar{X}$ | $Y_i - \bar{Y}$ | Product | $(X_i - \bar{X})^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $-10$ | $-12$ | $120$ | $100$ |
| $-5$ | $-7$ | $35$ | $25$ |
| $0$ | $-2$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| $5$ | $8$ | $40$ | $25$ |
| $10$ | $13$ | $130$ | $100$ |
| **Sum** | | **325** | **250** |

$$b = \frac{325}{250} = 1.30$$

### Step 3 — Compute intercept $a$

$$a = \bar{Y} - b\bar{X} = 57 - (1.30 \times 40) = 57 - 52 = 5$$

### Step 4 — Regression equation

$$\hat{Y} = 5 + 1.30 X$$

### Step 5 — Forecast for $X = 50$ thousand

$$\hat{Y} = 5 + 1.30 \times 50 = 5 + 65 = 70\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 1,000-arrival increase at the airport corresponds to a **1.3 percentage-point** rise in occupancy. The resort can use this to dynamically adjust pricing and housekeeping rosters.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Tracking Signal & Bias Detection

> **Problem:** Compute the Tracking Signal for the exponential smoothing forecast in Example 2. Is the model biased?

| Day | $D_t$ | $F_t$ | Error $e_t$ | $\vert e_t \vert$ | CFE |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 22 | 20.00 | $+2.00$ | 2.00 | 2.00 |
| 3 | 25 | 20.00 | $+5.00$ | 5.00 | 7.00 |
| 4 | 21 | 20.60 | $+0.40$ | 0.40 | 7.40 |
| 5 | 26 | 21.92 | $+4.08$ | 4.08 | 11.48 |

$$\text{TS} = \frac{\text{CFE}}{\text{MAD}} = \frac{11.48}{2.87} = 4.0$$

**Verdict**: $\vert \text{TS} \vert = 4.0$ is at the **upper control limit**. The forecast has a *persistent positive bias* — it consistently *under-predicts* demand. **Action**: increase $\alpha$ to be more responsive, or switch to a trend-aware model (Holt's method).

---

## 3.5 Comparative Decision Matrix — Which Method to Use?

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is a **favourite 7-mark KTU question** ("Compare and contrast time-series and causal methods" / "Which forecasting method would you recommend for a hospital OPD, and why?").

| Criterion | Qualitative | Time-Series (SMA/ES) | Causal (Regression) | AI/ML |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Data needed** | Expert opinion only | Historical demand | Demand + causal variable | Large historical dataset |
| **Setup cost** | Low | Low | Medium | High |
| **Best for** | New services, disruptions | Stable services | Services with known drivers | Digital / high-volume |
| **Horizon** | Short–medium | Short | Medium–long | Short–long |
| **Captures trend?** | Yes (judgmental) | ES-Holt/Winters yes; SMA no | Yes | Yes |
| **Captures seasonality?** | Yes | Only Winters | If $X$ is seasonal | Yes |
| **Service examples** | New branch launch, policy change | ATM cash, call volume | Hotel ↔ tourist arrivals, clinic ↔ flu index | Streaming, e-commerce |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Why is demand forecasting considered *more critical* in services than in manufacturing? Give three reasons.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Perishability of capacity** — Unlike a manufactured good, an unsold service hour (an empty airline seat, an idle hospital bed, a vacant hotel room) is *lost forever*. Manufacturing firms can stockpile inventory to absorb forecast error; service firms cannot.
2. **Customer as co-producer** — The customer's presence *consumes* capacity simultaneously with receiving the service, so demand fluctuations translate directly into waiting times and service quality degradation.
3. **High demand variability and external sensitivity** — Services are highly exposed to weather, festivals, social media, and policy shocks (e.g., COVID-19), making demand patterns volatile and harder to predict.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Differentiate between **qualitative** and **quantitative** forecasting methods. Give one example of each in a service context.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Qualitative | Quantitative |
|---|---|---|
| **Basis** | Judgment, intuition, expert opinion | Historical data, mathematical models |
| **Data requirement** | None / minimal | Large historical dataset |
| **Used when** | New services, no history, disruptions | Stable services with past records |
| **Example (service)** | Delphi method for new airline route demand | Exponential smoothing for daily ATM cash withdrawals |
| **Accuracy** | Subjective, less reproducible | Objective, reproducible |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024]** A retail bank's call centre in Thiruvananthapuram records the following daily call volumes for the last 7 days. The bank wants to forecast call volume for Day 8.

| Day | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Calls | 240 | 260 | 275 | 250 | 280 | 300 | 290 |

**(a)** [7 Marks] Compute the forecast for Day 8 using:
- (i) 3-day Simple Moving Average
- (ii) 3-day Weighted Moving Average with weights 0.5, 0.3, 0.2 for Days 7, 6, 5 respectively
- (iii) Single Exponential Smoothing with $\alpha = 0.4$ and $F_1 = D_1 = 240$

**(b)** [7 Marks] For the exponential smoothing model in (a)(iii), compute **MAD**, **MAPE**, and the **Tracking Signal**. Comment on whether the model is *biased*.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a)(i) — 3-day SMA**

$$F_8 = \frac{D_7 + D_6 + D_5}{3} = \frac{290 + 300 + 280}{3} = \frac{870}{3} = 290 \text{ calls}$$

**Part (a)(ii) — 3-day WMA**

$$F_8 = (0.5 \times 290) + (0.3 \times 300) + (0.2 \times 280)$$

$$F_8 = 145 + 90 + 56 = 291 \text{ calls}$$

**Part (a)(iii) — Exponential Smoothing, $\alpha = 0.4$**

Initialize: $F_1 = 240$.

$$F_2 = 0.4 \times 240 + 0.6 \times 240 = 240.00$$

$$F_3 = 0.4 \times 260 + 0.6 \times 240.00 = 104 + 144 = 248.00$$

$$F_4 = 0.4 \times 275 + 0.6 \times 248.00 = 110 + 148.80 = 258.80$$

$$F_5 = 0.4 \times 250 + 0.6 \times 258.80 = 100 + 155.28 = 255.28$$

$$F_6 = 0.4 \times 280 + 0.6 \times 255.28 = 112 + 153.17 = 265.17$$

$$F_7 = 0.4 \times 300 + 0.6 \times 265.17 = 120 + 159.10 = 279.10$$

$$F_8 = 0.4 \times 290 + 0.6 \times 279.10 = 116 + 167.46 = 283.46 \text{ calls} \approx 283$$

**Part (b) — Error Metrics**

Compute errors for $t = 2$ to $7$ (six periods):

| $t$ | $D_t$ | $F_t$ | $e_t = D_t - F_t$ | $\vert e_t \vert$ | $\vert e_t / D_t \vert \times 100$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 260 | 240.00 | $+20.00$ | 20.00 | 7.69 |
| 3 | 275 | 248.00 | $+27.00$ | 27.00 | 9.82 |
| 4 | 250 | 258.80 | $-8.80$ | 8.80 | 3.52 |
| 5 | 280 | 255.28 | $+24.72$ | 24.72 | 8.83 |
| 6 | 300 | 265.17 | $+34.83$ | 34.83 | 11.61 |
| 7 | 290 | 279.10 | $+10.90$ | 10.90 | 3.76 |
| **Sum** | | | **108.65** | **126.25** | **45.23** |

$$\text{MAD} = \frac{126.25}{6} = 21.04 \text{ calls}$$

$$\text{MAPE} = \frac{45.23}{6} = 7.54\%$$

$$\text{CFE} = 108.65$$

$$\text{TS} = \frac{108.65}{21.04} = 5.16$$

**Comment**: $\vert \text{TS} \vert = 5.16 > 4$, hence the exponential smoothing model is **biased** — it consistently *under-forecasts* call volume. **Recommendation**: increase $\alpha$ (e.g., to 0.6 or 0.7) to make the model more responsive, or switch to **Holt's linear trend method** to capture the upward drift in demand.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** A Kerala-based Ayurvedic resort believes that the number of **monthly tourist arrivals at Calicut International Airport** is a leading indicator of monthly **therapy bookings**. The following data is collected:

| Month | Arrivals $X$ (thousands) | Bookings $Y$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 20 | 50 |
| 2 | 25 | 60 |
| 3 | 30 | 65 |
| 4 | 35 | 80 |
| 5 | 40 | 85 |

**(a)** [7 Marks] Develop a **linear regression model** $\hat{Y} = a + bX$ and interpret the coefficients.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Forecast the number of therapy bookings for a month with **45 thousand** arrivals. Also compute the **correlation coefficient $r$** and comment on the strength of the relationship.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Regression Coefficients**

Means: $\bar{X} = 30$, $\bar{Y} = 68$.

| $X_i - \bar{X}$ | $Y_i - \bar{Y}$ | Product | $(X_i - \bar{X})^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $-10$ | $-18$ | $180$ | $100$ |
| $-5$ | $-8$ | $40$ | $25$ |
| $0$ | $-3$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| $5$ | $12$ | $60$ | $25$ |
| $10$ | $17$ | $170$ | $100$ |
| **Sum** | | **450** | **250** |

$$b = \frac{450}{250} = 1.80$$

$$a = 68 - (1.80 \times 30) = 68 - 54 = 14$$

$$\boxed{\hat{Y} = 14 + 1.80 X}$$

**Interpretation**:
- **Slope $b = 1.80$**: Every 1,000-arrival increase at Calicut airport corresponds to **1.8 additional therapy bookings** per month.
- **Intercept $a = 14$**: The baseline monthly bookings are 14 even when airport arrivals are zero (driven by domestic / repeat customers).

**Part (b) — Forecast and Correlation**

For $X = 45$:

$$\hat{Y} = 14 + (1.80 \times 45) = 14 + 81 = 95 \text{ bookings}$$

**Correlation coefficient**:

$$r = \frac{\sum (X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y})}{\sqrt{\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2 \cdot \sum (Y_i - \bar{Y})^2}}$$

Compute $\sum (Y_i - \bar{Y})^2$:

$$(-18)^2 + (-8)^2 + (-3)^2 + (12)^2 + (17)^2 = 324 + 64 + 9 + 144 + 289 = 830$$

$$r = \frac{450}{\sqrt{250 \times 830}} = \frac{450}{\sqrt{207500}} = \frac{450}{455.52} = 0.988$$

**Comment**: $r = 0.988$ indicates an **extremely strong positive linear relationship** between airport arrivals and therapy bookings. The model is highly reliable for short-term forecasting in this resort's context.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (KTU 2024 Valuation):**
> 1. **Forgetting to mention "perishability"** when asked why service forecasting is hard — examiners expect the *inventory-cannot-be-stored* logic. Lose 1 mark if missed.
> 2. **Conflating MAD and MSE** — MAD uses *absolute* values; MSE uses *squared* values. Mixing them up is a 1-mark killer.
> 3. **Not writing the unit** in MAD and MAPE answers (calls, %, etc.) — KTU deducts ½ mark.
> 4. **Tracking Signal interpretation** — students often say "TS = 5 is good". Wrong! $\vert \text{TS} \vert > 4$ means *biased*, not good. Show the threshold explicitly.
> 5. **Linear regression arithmetic** — the most common error is in computing $a = \bar{Y} - b\bar{X}$ (sign error). Re-verify with one data point.
> 6. **Exponential smoothing iteration** — the *biggest trap* is starting from $F_1 = D_1$ but then indexing incorrectly. Always write the recursion $F_{t+1} = \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha)F_t$ clearly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Forecasting Demand for Services**

- ✅ **Service demand is perishable** — unsold service capacity is a *sunk cost*. Forecasting is the only lever.
- ✅ **Five-step pipeline**: Define → Collect → Choose method → Forecast → Monitor error.
- ✅ **Three method families**: Qualitative (Delphi, sales composite), Time-Series (SMA, WMA, ES, Holt, Winters), Causal (Linear Regression, Econometric).
- ✅ **SMA formula**: $F_{t+1} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} D_{t-i}$ — equal weights, good for stationary demand.
- ✅ **WMA formula**: $F_{t+1} = \sum w_i D_{t-i+1}$, $\sum w_i = 1$ — emphasizes recent data.
- ✅ **Single ES formula**: $F_{t+1} = \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha)F_t$ — reactive, self-correcting.
- ✅ **Holt's method** adds a **trend** term $T_t$ with two smoothing constants $\alpha, \beta$.
- ✅ **Winters' method** adds a **seasonal index** $S_t$ with three constants $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$.
- ✅ **Linear regression**: $\hat{Y} = a + bX$, where $b = \frac{\sum(X-\bar{X})(Y-\bar{Y})}{\sum(X-\bar{X})^2}$ and $a = \bar{Y} - b\bar{X}$.
- ✅ **MAD** uses absolute errors, **MSE** uses squared errors, **MAPE** uses percentage errors.
- ✅ **Tracking Signal** = CFE / MAD; threshold $\pm 4$. If exceeded → model is biased → recalibrate.
- ✅ **Service-specific challenges**: no inventory, demand–capacity simultaneity, high variability, customer heterogeneity, external sensitivity.
- ✅ **MAPE < 10 %** = excellent; **10–20 %** = good; **> 50 %** = poor (rule of thumb for KTU answers).
- ✅ **Choice rule of thumb**: New service → Qualitative; Stable service → Time-Series; Service with known driver → Causal; Digital service → AI/ML.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Managing Capacity and Demand
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT861 - SERVICE SCIENCE AND SERVICE OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Managing Capacity and Demand

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition – Capacity in Services**
> In the context of service operations management, **Capacity** is the maximum level of value-added output that a service system can sustain over a specified period of time under normal operating conditions. It represents the upper limit of service throughput that a firm's resources — people, equipment, facilities, and infrastructure — can reliably deliver.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition – Demand in Services**
> **Demand** refers to the quantity of a service that customers desire and are willing to purchase (or use) at a given price, time, and place. It represents the load placed on the service system by the external market environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Managing Capacity and Demand** is the process of strategically planning, controlling, and reconciling the supply of service capacity with the fluctuating demand of customers to achieve optimal economic performance, service quality, and customer satisfaction.

The fundamental challenge in service operations is that **services are perishable** — an unsold airline seat, an empty hotel room, or an unused hospital bed at 3 PM is a lost revenue opportunity that can never be recovered. This perishability makes the management of capacity and demand a critical strategic function, distinct from manufacturing where inventory can buffer mismatches.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Beach Restaurant** 🏖️🍽️
> Imagine you own a seaside restaurant with **40 seats**. On a sunny Saturday, **120 walk-in customers** arrive between 7 PM and 9 PM. Your **Capacity = 40**, but your **Demand = 120** — a massive shortfall.
> - On a rainy Tuesday, only **15 customers** arrive, but your capacity is still 40 — now you have **idle capacity**.
> - The problem: **You cannot store a sunset view or a plate of pasta for tomorrow.**
> - Solution frameworks (capacity expansion, reservations, dynamic pricing, part-time staff) help you "smooth" the curve so that demand and capacity dance to the same tune.

> [!WARNING]
> **The Core Dilemma:** Service firms face an eternal tug-of-war between the **fixed (or quasi-fixed) nature of capacity** and the **highly variable nature of demand**. Mismatches cost money — lost sales on one side and idle resources on the other.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Highlighted

The key engineering and management metrics used to evaluate capacity-demand alignment are:

- **Capacity Utilization (CU):** The percentage of available capacity that is actually being used.
- **Yield (Y):** The percentage of maximum potential revenue actually realized.
- **Demand Forecast Accuracy (DFA):** A measure of how close forecasts are to actual demand.
- **Service Level (SL):** The probability or proportion of demand that can be met without delay.
- **Peak-to-Trough Ratio:** The ratio of peak-period demand to lowest-period demand.
- **Load Factor (LF):** The percentage of available productive capacity that is filled.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Capacity–Demand Relationship (Structured Logic)

The relationship between capacity and demand follows a structured logic that every KTU examiner expects students to articulate:

- **Step 1 — Establish the Baseline:** Determine the *effective capacity* of the service system using facilities, labour, equipment, and process design.
- **Step 2 — Forecast Demand:** Use historical data, time-series models, and customer behaviour analysis to project future demand.
- **Step 3 — Identify the Gap:** Compare the capacity line with the demand curve over time (hourly, daily, weekly, seasonal).
- **Step 4 — Classify the Mismatch:** Is the gap one of *excess capacity*, *insufficient capacity*, or *volatile mismatch*?
- **Step 5 — Select the Strategy:** Choose between (a) adjusting capacity, (b) adjusting demand, or (c) a hybrid combination.
- **Step 6 — Execute and Measure:** Implement the chosen strategy and continuously monitor KPIs (CU, Yield, SL).
- **Step 7 — Feedback Loop:** Refine the strategy based on observed variances between planned and actual results.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind Each Step:** Without a feedback loop, capacity-demand strategies become static and quickly obsolete in dynamic service environments (e.g., e-commerce surges, airline disruptions, hospital emergencies).

## 2.2 Strategies for Managing Capacity

| # | Strategy | Core Idea | When Best Used |
|---|----------|-----------|----------------|
| 1 | **Level Capacity Strategy** | Build capacity to meet average demand; absorb peaks with buffers | Stable, mature markets (utilities, public transport) |
| 2 | **Chase Demand Strategy** | Adjust capacity (staff, equipment) to match demand | Highly variable demand (call centres, retail) |
| 3 | **Yield Management** | Use differential pricing to allocate fixed capacity to highest-value segments | Perishable inventory (airlines, hotels) |
| 4 | **Flexible Workforce** | Use part-time, on-call, or multi-skilled workers | Demand spikes during specific hours |
| 5 | **Shared Capacity** | Partner with another firm to use spare capacity | B2B services, equipment-intensive operations |
| 6 | **Customer Participation** | Shift tasks to customers (self-checkout, self-service) | Retail, banking, e-commerce |
| 7 | **Reservation Systems** | Pre-book demand to smooth the load | Restaurants, doctors, salons |

## 2.3 Strategies for Managing Demand

| # | Strategy | Mechanism | Example |
|---|----------|-----------|---------|
| 1 | **Differential Pricing** | Higher prices during peaks, lower during troughs | Movie tickets, electricity tariffs |
| 2 | **Promotion & Marketing** | Advertise off-peak discounts | Happy hours, off-season tourism |
| 3 | **Reservation Systems** | Pre-allocate demand across time slots | Restaurants, airlines |
| 4 | **Complementary Services** | Offer ancillary services during low demand | Spa + restaurant combos |
| 5 | **Communication & Signage** | Inform customers of peak times | Bank queue displays |
| 6 | **Loyalty Programs** | Reward off-peak usage | Frequent flyer miles |
| 7 | **Demand Forecasting** | Use predictive analytics to anticipate demand | E-commerce, ride-sharing |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table summarizes every key equation tested in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations for this topic.** All formulas are mathematically verifiable and align with operations management textbooks.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description of Variables |
|---|---------|---------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Capacity Utilization (CU)** | $$CU = \frac{\text{Actual Output}}{\text{Maximum Capacity}} \times 100\%$$ | CU = Utilization %, A = Actual output, C = Maximum capacity |
| 2 | **Effective Capacity** | $$C_{\text{eff}} = C_{\text{design}} \times E \times U$$ | $C_{\text{eff}}$ = Effective capacity, $E$ = Efficiency, $U$ = Utilization |
| 3 | **Yield** | $$Y = \frac{R_{\text{actual}}}{R_{\text{maximum possible}}} \times 100\%$$ | $R$ = Revenue at each point |
| 4 | **Average Load Factor** | $$\bar{L} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i}{n}$$ | $L_i$ = Load factor in period $i$, $n$ = Number of periods |
| 5 | **Break-Even Quantity** | $$Q^{*} = \frac{FC}{P - VC}$$ | $FC$ = Fixed cost, $P$ = Price per unit, $VC$ = Variable cost per unit |
| 6 | **Total Cost** | $$TC = FC + (VC \times Q)$$ | Standard cost equation |
| 7 | **Total Revenue** | $$TR = P \times Q$$ | Revenue from selling $Q$ units at price $P$ |
| 8 | **Peak-to-Trough Ratio** | $$R_{pt} = \frac{D_{\text{peak}}}{D_{\text{trough}}}$$ | $D$ = Demand at peak or trough |
| 9 | **Capacity Cushion** | $$K_{c} = 1 - \text{Utilization}$$ | $K_c$ = Capacity cushion (slack) |
| 10 | **Service Level (M/M/1 approximation)** | $$SL \approx \frac{\mu - \lambda}{\mu}$$ | $\mu$ = Service rate, $\lambda$ = Arrival rate |
| 11 | **Forecast Error (MAPE)** | $$MAPE = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \left\vert \frac{A_i - F_i}{A_i} \right\vert \times 100\%$$ | $A_i$ = Actual, $F_i$ = Forecast |
| 12 | **Revenue per Available Unit** | $$RevPAU = \frac{\text{Total Revenue}}{\text{Available Capacity Units}}$$ | Used in hotels, airlines |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** The **Break-Even Quantity** formula $Q^{*} = \frac{FC}{P - VC}$ is one of the most frequently tested formulas in KTU Module 3 numerical problems. Memorize the derivation path as well.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles of capacity-demand management are applied across virtually every engineering-led service:

- **Aviation Engineering:** Aircraft fleet sizing, route capacity, load factor optimization.
- **Healthcare Engineering:** Hospital bed allocation, MRI machine scheduling, ER staffing.
- **Telecommunications Engineering:** Bandwidth allocation, cell tower load balancing, dynamic spectrum management.
- **Power Systems Engineering:** Demand-side management, peak shaving, smart grid load balancing.
- **Software Engineering:** Server capacity planning, auto-scaling, load balancing, cloud elasticity.
- **Transportation Engineering:** Toll booth staffing, metro train frequency, traffic signal optimization.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Capacity Utilization (CU) — The Core KPI

**Starting Principle:** Capacity utilization measures how much of the available productive output is actually being used by the service system.

**Step 1 — Define the variables symbolically.**
Let:
- $A$ = Actual output of the service system in a given period (units serviced)
- $C_{\text{max}}$ = Maximum possible output under normal operating conditions
- $CU$ = Capacity Utilization, expressed as a fraction (multiply by 100 for %)

**Step 2 — State the fundamental relationship.**
By definition, utilization is the ratio of actual usage to maximum possible usage:

$$
CU = \frac{A}{C_{\text{max}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to percentage form (standard practice in KTU exam scripts).**

$$
CU (\%) = \frac{A}{C_{\text{max}}} \times 100
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result numerically.**
- If $A = 80$ patients/day and $C_{\text{max}} = 100$ patients/day, then:
$$
CU = \frac{80}{100} \times 100 = 80\%
$$
This indicates the hospital is operating at **80% utilization**, leaving a **20% capacity cushion**.

**Conversion Logic:** A utilization of 100% is theoretically ideal but practically dangerous — it leaves zero buffer for breakdowns, surges, or delays.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Break-Even Quantity $Q^{*}$

**Starting Principle:** The break-even point is where **Total Revenue = Total Cost** — the service makes neither profit nor loss.

**Step 1 — Define variables.**
- $P$ = Selling price per unit of service (e.g., ticket price)
- $VC$ = Variable cost per unit (e.g., food cost, fuel)
- $FC$ = Total fixed costs (e.g., rent, salaries)
- $Q$ = Quantity of services sold

**Step 2 — Write the Total Cost equation.**
The total cost is the sum of fixed cost and variable cost:

$$
TC = FC + (VC \times Q)
$$

**Step 3 — Write the Total Revenue equation.**

$$
TR = P \times Q
$$

**Step 4 — Set TR = TC at the break-even point.**

$$
P \times Q^{*} = FC + (VC \times Q^{*})
$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to isolate $Q^{*}$.**

$$
P \times Q^{*} - (VC \times Q^{*}) = FC
$$

**Step 6 — Factor out $Q^{*}$.**

$$
Q^{*} (P - VC) = FC
$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $Q^{*}$.**

$$
Q^{*} = \frac{FC}{P - VC}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The denominator $P - VC$ is called the **contribution margin** — the amount each unit contributes toward covering fixed costs. The greater the contribution margin, the fewer units needed to break even.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU-Style Numerical Example:** A restaurant has $FC = \text{Rs. } 50{,}000$/month, $P = \text{Rs. } 500$/meal, $VC = \text{Rs. } 200$/meal.
> $$Q^{*} = \frac{50{,}000}{500 - 200} = \frac{50{,}000}{300} \approx 167 \text{ meals/month}$$
> The restaurant must serve at least 167 meals to cover its fixed costs.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Yield Management Equation

**Starting Principle:** Yield captures how much of the *maximum possible revenue* from a fixed perishable capacity is actually realized.

**Step 1 — Define variables.**
- $R_{\text{actual}}$ = Total revenue actually earned in the period
- $R_{\text{max}}$ = Maximum revenue that could be earned if every unit of capacity was sold at full price

**Step 2 — Write the yield ratio.**

$$
Y = \frac{R_{\text{actual}}}{R_{\text{max}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to percentage form.**

$$
Y(\%) = \frac{R_{\text{actual}}}{R_{\text{max}}} \times 100
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**
A yield of 75% means the firm captured 75% of the maximum possible revenue from its fixed capacity — the remaining 25% is "leakage" due to discounts, idle capacity, or no-shows.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Capacity–Demand Imbalance Scenarios

| Scenario | Demand vs Capacity | Root Cause | Recommended Strategy |
|----------|-------------------|------------|----------------------|
| **A** | Demand >> Capacity | Under-provisioned resources, unexpected surge | Add capacity (chase), queue management, dynamic pricing |
| **B** | Demand << Capacity | Over-investment, off-peak period | Promotions, complementary services, partial shutdown |
| **C** | Demand = Capacity (stable) | Well-planned system | Maintain, monitor, refine |
| **D** | Demand oscillates wildly | Seasonal business, event-driven | Level strategy + flexible workforce + yield management |
| **E** | Capacity is fixed and perishable | Airlines, hotels, theatres | Yield management, overbooking, differential pricing |

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4: Service Level for an M/M/1 Queue (Light Introduction)

For an M/M/1 queueing system (Poisson arrivals, exponential service times, single server), the steady-state service level — the probability that an arriving customer does not wait — is approximated by:

$$
SL = 1 - \rho = 1 - \frac{\lambda}{\mu} = \frac{\mu - \lambda}{\mu}
$$

**Step 1 — $\rho$ (rho)** is the *traffic intensity* = arrival rate ÷ service rate.
**Step 2 — If $\rho \geq 1$**, the system is unstable — the queue grows without bound.
**Step 3 — The service level** equals the probability of zero customers in the system on arrival.

**Conversion Logic:** As $\lambda \to \mu$ (utilization approaches 100%), service level $\to 0$ — the system becomes saturated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the engineering foundation** behind why call centres, hospitals, and banks aim for a *service level target* of 80% — answering within 20 seconds — rather than 100% utilization, which would create infinite queues.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: The Capacity-Demand Management Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Service System Inputs] --> B[Capacity Planning]
    B --> C[Effective Capacity C eff]
    D[Customer Market] --> E[Demand Forecasting]
    E --> F[Forecasted Demand]
    C --> G[Gap Analysis]
    F --> G
    G --> H{Imbalance Type}
    H -->|Excess Capacity| I[Demand-Side Strategies]
    H -->|Shortage| J[Capacity-Side Strategies]
```
