```mermaid
    H -->|Match| K[Maintain and Monitor]
    I --> L[Pricing, Promotions, Reservations]
    J --> M[Chase, Level, Flexible Staff, Yield Mgmt]
    L --> N[Performance Monitoring]
    M --> N
    K --> N
    N --> O{KPIs Met?}
    O -->|Yes| P[Steady State Operations]
    O -->|No| G
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** The diagram shows the closed-loop feedback system that governs capacity-demand management. Notice how the process re-enters *Gap Analysis* whenever KPIs are not met — this is the **continuous improvement** principle.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Yield Management Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fixed Perishable Capacity e.g. 100 seats] --> B[Forecast Demand by Segment]
    B --> C[Allocate Seats to High-Yield Customers]
    C --> D[Set Differential Prices by Time and Segment]
    D --> E[Monitor Booking Curve]
    E --> F{Unsold Capacity Remaining?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Offer Discount to Price-Sensitive Segment]
    F -->|No| H[Hold Capacity for Late High-Value Bookings]
    G --> E
    H --> E
    E --> I[Compute Yield = Actual Revenue / Max Revenue]
    I --> J{Yield Target Met?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Strategy Successful]
    J -->|No| B
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Yield management is **not** a one-time decision — it is a continuous reallocation of inventory to the most valuable customer segment as the booking window closes.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Capacity Strategy Selection Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start: Analyze Demand Pattern] --> B{Demand Variability}
    B -->|Low and Predictable| C[Level Capacity Strategy]
    B -->|High and Unpredictable| D[Chase Demand Strategy]
    B -->|Seasonal| E[Hybrid: Level + Flexible Workforce]
    C --> F[Buffer Inventory, Cross-Trained Staff]
    D --> G[Part-Time Workers, On-Call Capacity]
    E --> H[Core Staff + Surge Capacity]
    F --> I[Steady Output]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Continuous KPI Monitoring]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: Demand-Side Strategy Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Excess Capacity Detected] --> B[Choose Demand Strategy]
    B --> C[Pricing Levers]
    B --> D[Promotion Levers]
    B --> E[Service Mix Levers]
    B --> F[Information Levers]
    C --> C1[Off-Peak Discounts]
    C --> C2[Loyalty Rewards]
    D --> D1[Targeted Marketing]
    D --> D2[Bundle Offers]
    E --> E1[Complementary Services]
    E --> E2[New Customer Segments]
    F --> F1[Real-Time Wait-Time Displays]
    F --> F2[Digital Queue Management]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Note:** When asked to draw a diagram in the KTU 2024 exam, *label every node, every arrow, and every decision diamond*. Marks are awarded for clarity, not artistic merit.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Instructions (KTU 2024):** Answer in **one or two sentences** with precise terminology. Each question carries **3 marks**. No derivations required.

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the term "Capacity Utilization" in a service context. Why is a 100% utilization rate considered undesirable in most service operations?**

**Model Answer:**
Capacity Utilization is the ratio of actual service output to the maximum possible service output, expressed as a percentage. A 100% utilization rate is undesirable because it leaves **no buffer for unexpected surges, equipment breakdowns, or variability in service time**, leading to long queues, customer dissatisfaction, and system instability.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between the "Level Capacity Strategy" and the "Chase Demand Strategy" with one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Level Capacity Strategy | Chase Demand Strategy |
|--------|------------------------|----------------------|
| Approach | Capacity is set to meet **average demand**; peaks absorbed via buffers | Capacity is **continuously adjusted** to match demand |
| Cost | Lower staffing costs, higher inventory/buffer cost | Higher staffing/training cost, lower buffer cost |
| Example | **Electricity supply** by a power utility | **Call centre staffing** that flexes with call volume |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Instructions (KTU 2024):** Answer **any ONE** of the following. Each carries **14 marks** with sub-parts of 7 marks each. Show all working clearly.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

> **(a)** Explain **five key strategies** for managing **service capacity**, with one real-world example for each. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A regional bank has a fixed cost of **Rs. 2,40,000 per month**. The bank sells a financial advisory service at **Rs. 800 per customer**, with a variable cost of **Rs. 300 per customer**.
> Calculate:
>   (i) The break-even quantity $Q^{*}$.
>   (ii) The total revenue at break-even.
>   (iii) The capacity utilization if the bank serves **400 customers** and its maximum capacity is **500 customers per month**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Five Capacity Management Strategies:**

1. **Level Capacity Strategy:** Set capacity to match *average* demand. Example: A municipal water supply plant built to meet average daily consumption; excess capacity is reserved for droughts.
2. **Chase Demand Strategy:** Match capacity hour-by-hour to fluctuating demand. Example: A delivery company hiring more riders during festive seasons.
3. **Yield Management:** Allocate fixed perishable capacity to the highest-revenue customer segment using dynamic pricing. Example: An airline charging business-class fares close to departure.
4. **Flexible Workforce:** Use part-time, multi-skilled, or on-call employees. Example: A hospital deploying on-call doctors during weekends.
5. **Customer Participation:** Shift tasks to customers to increase effective capacity. Example: Self-check-in kiosks at airports, which increase the number of passengers processed per hour.

> *(Note: Students may include 'Shared Capacity' as a sixth strategy if space permits.)*

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $FC = \text{Rs. } 2{,}40{,}000$ per month
- $P = \text{Rs. } 800$ per customer
- $VC = \text{Rs. } 300$ per customer
- $A = 400$ customers (actual)
- $C_{\text{max}} = 500$ customers (maximum)

**(i) Break-Even Quantity:**

$$
Q^{*} = \frac{FC}{P - VC} = \frac{2{,}40{,}000}{800 - 300} = \frac{2{,}40{,}000}{500} = 480 \text{ customers}
$$

**(ii) Total Revenue at Break-Even:**

$$
TR^{*} = P \times Q^{*} = 800 \times 480 = \text{Rs. } 3{,}84{,}000
$$

**Verification of TC at break-even:**

$$
TC = FC + (VC \times Q^{*}) = 2{,}40{,}000 + (300 \times 480) = 2{,}40{,}000 + 1{,}44{,}000 = \text{Rs. } 3{,}84{,}000 \;\checkmark
$$

**(iii) Capacity Utilization:**

$$
CU = \frac{A}{C_{\text{max}}} \times 100 = \frac{400}{500} \times 100 = 80\%
$$

The bank is operating at **80% utilization**, with a **20% capacity cushion**.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Supplementary Model Paper 2024]**

> **(a)** Describe **five demand-side management strategies** that a service firm can use to smooth out demand. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A hotel has **120 rooms**. The full tariff is **Rs. 3,000 per room per night**. The variable cost per occupied room is **Rs. 500** (laundry, utilities, amenities). Fixed costs total **Rs. 1,50,000 per day**.
> Calculate:
>   (i) The break-even occupancy rate (in %).
>   (ii) The profit (or loss) if the hotel achieves **80% occupancy**.
>   (iii) Comment on what management should do if occupancy drops to **40%** for a sustained period. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Five Demand-Side Strategies:**

1. **Differential Pricing:** Charge different prices at different times to shift demand from peak to off-peak. Example: Lower movie ticket prices on weekday afternoons.
2. **Promotional Offers and Discounts:** Run advertising campaigns during off-peak hours. Example: "Happy Hours" at restaurants.
3. **Reservation Systems:** Pre-allocate demand to specific time slots. Example: Restaurant reservation apps.
4. **Complementary Services:** Bundle slow-moving services with popular ones. Example: Free spa session with a five-night hotel stay.
5. **Loyalty and Reward Programs:** Reward customers for using the service during off-peak periods. Example: Frequent flyer miles that double for Tuesday flights.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Total rooms $N = 120$
- $P = \text{Rs. } 3{,}000$ per room
- $VC = \text{Rs. } 500$ per occupied room
- $FC = \text{Rs. } 1{,}50{,}000$ per day

**(i) Break-Even Occupancy Rate:**

First, compute the break-even number of rooms:

$$
Q^{*} = \frac{FC}{P - VC} = \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{3{,}000 - 500} = \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{2{,}500} = 60 \text{ rooms}
$$

The break-even occupancy rate is:

$$
\text{Occupancy Rate}^{*} = \frac{60}{120} \times 100 = 50\%
$$

**(ii) Profit at 80% Occupancy:**

Occupied rooms at 80%:

$$
Q = 0.80 \times 120 = 96 \text{ rooms}
$$

Total Revenue:

$$
TR = P \times Q = 3{,}000 \times 96 = \text{Rs. } 2{,}88{,}000
$$

Total Cost:

$$
TC = FC + (VC \times Q) = 1{,}50{,}000 + (500 \times 96) = 1{,}50{,}000 + 48{,}000 = \text{Rs. } 1{,}98{,}000
$$

Profit:

$$
\pi = TR - TC = 2{,}88{,}000 - 1{,}98{,}000 = \text{Rs. } 90{,}000 \text{ per day}
$$

**(iii) Comment on Sustained 40% Occupancy:**

Occupied rooms at 40%:

$$
Q = 0.40 \times 120 = 48 \text{ rooms}
$$

$$
TR = 3{,}000 \times 48 = \text{Rs. } 1{,}44{,}000
$$

$$
TC = 1{,}50{,}000 + (500 \times 48) = 1{,}50{,}000 + 24{,}000 = \text{Rs. } 1{,}74{,}000
$$

$$
\pi = 1{,}44{,}000 - 1{,}74{,}000 = -\text{Rs. } 30{,}000 \text{ (loss per day)}
$$

**Recommended Actions:**
- Aggressive differential pricing (offer 30–40% off-peak discounts to push occupancy above 50%).
- Targeted promotion via online travel agencies (OTAs) and loyalty programs.
- Add complementary services (breakfast bundles, airport transfers) to increase per-room revenue.
- Consider temporary closure of a portion of the rooms to reduce variable cost and focus on higher service quality.
- Explore partnerships with corporate clients for guaranteed volume.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Exams:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion:** Students often forget to convert the break-even quantity to an *occupancy rate* or *percentage*. KTU examiners explicitly test this conversion.
> 2. **Formula Misquote:** Writing $Q^{*} = \frac{FC}{P + VC}$ (with a plus sign) is a fatal error — always subtract VC from P.
> 3. **Forgetting the Feedback Loop:** In theory questions, students list capacity strategies but omit that they are part of a *continuous improvement cycle*. This costs up to 2 marks.
> 4. **Confusing Capacity Cushion with Capacity Utilization:** Cushion = $1 - \text{Utilization}$. Mixing these up is a frequent error.
> 5. **Not Stating Assumptions:** In numerical problems, students should state *“assuming linear cost behaviour and constant selling price”* to earn full method marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Managing Capacity and Demand**

- ☐ **Capacity** = maximum service output; **Demand** = customer request volume.
- ☐ Capacity–demand imbalance is the **central operational problem** of services.
- ☐ **Three core strategies:** Adjust Capacity, Adjust Demand, or Both.
- ☐ **Level Strategy** suits stable demand; **Chase Strategy** suits volatile demand.
- ☐ **Yield Management** is essential when inventory is perishable and fixed.
- ☐ Key formulas to memorize cold:
  - $CU = \dfrac{A}{C_{\max}} \times 100$
  - $Q^{*} = \dfrac{FC}{P - VC}$
  - $Y = \dfrac{R_{\text{actual}}}{R_{\text{max}}} \times 100$
- ☐ **Service Level** in M/M/1 = $\dfrac{\mu - \lambda}{\mu}$ — high utilization ⇒ low service level.
- ☐ The **capacity cushion** is the safety margin left intentionally unused.
- ☐ Demand-side strategies: pricing, promotion, reservation, complementary services, loyalty.
- ☐ Capacity-side strategies: level, chase, yield, flexible workforce, shared capacity, customer participation.
- ☐ In every KTU numerical, **state assumptions** and **verify the answer** (e.g., check $TR = TC$ at break-even).
- ☐ Always link strategies to a **feedback loop** for continuous improvement.
- ☐ Use **clear, labelled diagrams** in theory questions — examiners award 1–2 marks for visual clarity.

---

**End of Module 3 Notes — Managing Capacity and Demand** ✅
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Psychology of Waiting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT861 - SERVICE SCIENCE AND SERVICE OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Psychology of Waiting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Psychology of Waiting** is a sub-domain of **Service Operations Management** that studies the cognitive, emotional, and behavioural responses of customers to delays in receiving a service. It analyses the **disparity between *actual* waiting time (clock time)** and ***perceived* waiting time (subjective time)**, and provides a set of managerial principles to design waiting experiences that align customer perception with operational reality.

The term is most formally associated with **David H. Maister's seminal 1985 essay, *“The Psychology of Waiting Lines.”*** In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, it is treated as a **service-quality design tool** under the broader umbrella of *Service Encounter Design* and *Customer Experience (CX) Management*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric Highlighted by KTU Examiners:**
> The two metrics that must NEVER be confused in an exam answer are:
> - **Actual Wait Time (AWT)** – The objective, clock-measured duration a customer spends in the queue.
> - **Perceived Wait Time (PWT)** – The subjective duration experienced by the customer, which is governed by psychological factors.
> Almost every KTU 14-mark question revolves around the gap **Δ = PWT − AWT**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

Imagine you are sitting in a **Government RTO office** waiting for an Aadhaar update. The board shows token number **47**, and yours is **112**. The clock ticks 25 minutes, and you feel it has been an **eternity**.

Now imagine the same 25 minutes spent **scrolling Instagram reels** in an airport lounge while waiting for a boarding call. The 25 minutes **vanish** — yet both durations are mathematically identical.

> **The intuitive rule:** *The mind does not measure waiting in minutes; it measures it in **boredom, anxiety, uncertainty, and unfairness**.*

A customer in a service queue is essentially paying a **psychological tax** on every minute of inactivity. The job of a service manager is to **minimise that tax**, even when the physical queue length cannot be reduced.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Exams — "O.P.A.U.U.S.G.V":**
> The eight pillars of waiting psychology — **O**ccupied time, **P**re-process wait, **A**nxiety, **U**ncertainty, **U**nexplained waits, **S**olo vs group, **G**roup waits, **V**alue of service. Use this sequence to structure any 14-mark descriptive answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Maister's Eight Propositions of Waiting (Decomposed)

The theoretical backbone of this topic is **Maister's First Principles of Waiting Lines**. Each proposition explains *why* a psychological gap emerges between objective and subjective wait time.

### Proposition 1 — Occupied Time Feels Shorter Than Unoccupied Time
- **Logic:** Distraction engages the brain's sensory cortex, reducing the cognitive load of clock-watching.
- **Operational Application:** Provide Wi-Fi, music, refreshments, magazines, or interactive kiosks in waiting areas.
- **Why it matters:** Airlines such as Emirates stream entertainment *before* boarding to shrink *perceived* boarding time.

### Proposition 2 — People Want to Get Started (Pre-Process Wait)
- **Logic:** Anxiety peaks *before* the service begins. Once the customer is in the service "core," the wait is associated with progress.
- **Operational Application:** Offer **pre-service engagement** — forms to fill, greeters to brief, or status updates.

### Proposition 3 — Anxiety Makes Waits Seem Longer
- **Logic:** Health-clinic, airport-security, and bank queues trigger anxiety, distorting time perception.
- **Operational Application:** Reassure customers, show professionalism, and create a calm ambience.

### Proposition 4 — Uncertain Waits Are Longer Than Known, Finite Waits
- **Logic:** A wait of "5–7 minutes" feels longer than a wait of *"exactly 5 minutes"*, even when the actual durations are similar.
- **Operational Application:** Publish estimated wait times (e.g., "Your turn in 8 minutes").

### Proposition 5 — Unexplained Waits Are Longer Than Explained Waits
- **Logic:** Lack of information triggers suspicion and resentment.
- **Operational Application:** Announce delays with a **reason**: *"The doctor is delayed due to an emergency case."*

### Proposition 6 — Unfair Waits Feel Longer Than Equitable Waits
- **Logic:** A customer who sees another person "cut in" experiences anger that *amplifies* perceived wait.
- **Operational Application:** Implement **FIFO (First-In-First-Out)** queue discipline, single-line systems, and visible token numbers.

### Proposition 7 — The More Valuable the Service, the Longer the Customer Will Wait
- **Logic:** High-value service justifies higher tolerance. The trade-off is **value vs. wait**.
- **Operational Application:** Premium customers (business-class check-in) accept longer waits if the experience is dignified.

### Proposition 8 — Solo Waits Feel Longer Than Group Waits
- **Logic:** Social interaction reduces boredom and the perception of time.
- **Operational Application:** Create **communal waiting zones** with seating arrangements that encourage conversation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Heuristic:** A 7-mark sub-part almost always expects **four to five** of the above propositions **with a one-line operational example for each**. Memorising examples for the same is worth a full distinction band.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Boundary / Limiting Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Perceived Wait Differential | $\Delta_{W} = T_{PWT} - T_{AWT}$ | minutes (min) | $\Delta_{W} \geq 0$ for poor design; $\Delta_{W} \leq 0$ for excellent design |
| 2 | Perceived Wait Ratio | $R_{PW} = \dfrac{T_{PWT}}{T_{AWT}}$ | dimensionless | $R_{PW} = 1$ ideal; $R_{PW} > 1$ indicates anxiety/idleness |
| 3 | Little's Law (Queue Length) | $L = \lambda \cdot W$ | customers | $L$ = avg. no. in system, $\lambda$ = arrival rate, $W$ = avg. time in system |
| 4 | Average Wait in M/M/1 Queue | $W_{q} = \dfrac{\rho}{\mu - \lambda}$ | minutes | Valid when $\rho = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu} < 1$ |
| 5 | Service Utilisation | $\rho = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | $\rho \to 1$ implies system saturation; perceived wait diverges |
| 6 | Occupied-Time Correction Factor | $T_{PWT} = T_{AWT} \cdot (1 - f_{occ})$ | minutes | $0 \leq f_{occ} \leq 1$ is the **distraction fraction** |
| 7 | Anxiety Multiplier | $T_{PWT} = T_{AWT} \cdot k_{anx}$ | minutes | $k_{anx} \in [1.0, 2.5]$ empirically; $k_{anx} = 1$ for routine tasks |
| 8 | Customer Tolerance Threshold | $T_{tol} = \alpha \cdot V_{svc}$ | minutes | $V_{svc}$ = perceived value; $\alpha$ = service-class constant |
| 9 | Queue Fairness Index (Coefficient of Variation of Effective Wait) | $CVE_{W} = \dfrac{\sigma_{T_{PWT}}}{\mu_{T_{PWT}}}$ | dimensionless | $CVE_{W} \to 0$ indicates perfectly fair perceived waits |
| 10 | Service Level (Probability of Wait $\leq$ $t_{0}$) | $SL(t_{0}) = P(W \leq t_{0})$ | probability | $0 \leq SL \leq 1$; KTU benchmark: $SL \geq 0.85$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Pipe Rule:** The vertical bar $\vert$ and $\mid$ in formulas above are used **inside math mode only**. Never write raw `|` in markdown tables — it breaks the table parser.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Service Engineering

- **Healthcare:** Emergency rooms use **triage-based queuing** to reduce perceived unfairness.
- **Aviation:** Self-service kiosks reduce *pre-process* wait by initiating the service journey.
- **Banking:** Token systems + digital display boards = explained, fair, finite wait.
- **Hospitality:** Lobby entertainment, refreshment vouchers, and concierge greetings apply Proposition 1.
- **E-Services:** Progress bars, animated loading icons, and "skeleton screens" apply Proposition 4 (known wait).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This module is **descriptive-analytical**, not algebraic. Hence "derivations" here are **logical step-decompositions** of psychological and operational chains, with embedded quantitative examples for the KTU numerical sub-parts.

## 3.1 Derivation: Linking Perceived Wait to Maister's Propositions

**Goal:** Express $T_{PWT}$ as a function of the eight psychological drivers.

### Step 1 — Begin with the Baseline Identity
By definition, **perceived wait** is **actual wait scaled by a perception multiplier**:

$$
T_{PWT} \;=\; T_{AWT} \cdot k_{tot}
$$

Here, $k_{tot}$ is the **composite perception multiplier**, dimensionless, with an ideal value of **1.0**.

### Step 2 — Decompose the Multiplier
Each of Maister's propositions contributes a multiplicative factor:

$$
k_{tot} \;=\; k_{occ} \cdot k_{pre} \cdot k_{anx} \cdot k_{unc} \cdot k_{expl} \cdot k_{fair} \cdot k_{solo}
$$

Where:
- $k_{occ}$ — Occupied time correction (Proposition 1)
- $k_{pre}$ — Pre-process wait penalty (Proposition 2)
- $k_{anx}$ — Anxiety multiplier (Proposition 3)
- $k_{unc}$ — Uncertainty penalty (Proposition 4)
- $k_{expl}$ — Unexplained-wait penalty (Proposition 5)
- $k_{fair}$ — Fairness distortion (Proposition 6)
- $k_{solo}$ — Solo/group adjustment (Proposition 8)

### Step 3 — Insert Functional Forms
Based on empirical service-research literature (Maister, 1985; McDonnell, 2007):

$$
k_{occ} = 1 - f_{occ}
$$

Here, $f_{occ}$ is the fraction of waiting time during which the customer is engaged in a distraction. The logic is that distraction *subtracts* from perceived wait proportionally.

$$
k_{anx} = 1 + \beta \cdot A_{score}
$$

Here, $A_{score}$ is an **anxiety index** ranging from 0 to 1, and $\beta$ is a sensitivity coefficient. High-anxiety settings (ER, immigration) yield $A_{score} \to 1$ and $\beta \approx 1.5$, giving $k_{anx} \to 2.5$ — meaning the perceived wait is 2.5× the actual wait.

$$
k_{unc} = 1 + \gamma \cdot U_{score}
$$

Here, $U_{score}$ is the **uncertainty index**. Announcing a definite time reduces $U_{score} \to 0$ and forces $k_{unc} \to 1$.

### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example

**Scenario:** A bank customer waits 10 minutes at a counter.
- $f_{occ} = 0.4$ (40 % time spent on phone)
- $A_{score} = 0.3$, $\beta = 1.2$
- $U_{score} = 0.5$, $\gamma = 0.8$
- Fairness $k_{fair} = 1.0$ (well-managed FIFO)
- Pre-process $k_{pre} = 1.1$
- Solo $k_{solo} = 1.0$ (single customer)

**Compute each factor:**

$$
k_{occ} = 1 - 0.4 = 0.6
$$

$$
k_{anx} = 1 + (1.2)(0.3) = 1.36
$$

$$
k_{unc} = 1 + (0.8)(0.5) = 1.40
$$

**Total multiplier:**

$$
k_{tot} = (0.6) \cdot (1.1) \cdot (1.36) \cdot (1.40) \cdot (1.0) \cdot (1.0) \cdot (1.0)
$$

$$
k_{tot} = 0.6 \times 1.1 = 0.66
$$

$$
0.66 \times 1.36 = 0.8976
$$

$$
0.8976 \times 1.40 = 1.2566
$$

**Perceived wait:**

$$
T_{PWT} = 10 \times 1.2566 = 12.57 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Interpretation:** Even though the customer was distracted (which usually shortens wait), the **anxiety and uncertainty together inflate the perceived wait by 25.66 %**, making a 10-minute wait feel like 12.5 minutes.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Trick:** In any KTU 14-mark question that gives a numerical scenario, you should present the answer in the form **$T_{AWT} \rightarrow T_{PWT}$** with a one-line **strategic recommendation** to bring $T_{PWT}$ closer to $T_{AWT}$.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Propositions to Service Interventions

| Proposition | Psychological Driver | Symptom in Queue | Recommended Intervention | Expected $k$ Reduction |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Occupied time | Boredom | Clock-watching, complaints | Wi-Fi, magazines, refreshments | $k_{occ} \downarrow 30 - 50 \%$ |
| 2. Pre-process | Anticipation anxiety | Impatience at the entrance | Pre-service greeters, forms | $k_{pre} \downarrow 20 - 30 \%$ |
| 3. Anxiety | Stress | Visible discomfort, abandonment | Calm environment, status updates | $k_{anx} \downarrow 15 - 25 \%$ |
| 4. Uncertainty | Unknown duration | Repeated staff queries | Display estimated wait | $k_{unc} \downarrow 40 - 60 \%$ |
| 5. Unexplained | Lack of information | Resentment, negativity | Public announcements | $k_{expl} \downarrow 30 - 45 \%$ |
| 6. Unfairness | Perceived queue-jumping | Anger, complaints | Single-line FIFO, token system | $k_{fair} \downarrow 50 - 70 \%$ |
| 7. Value trade-off | High stakes | Tolerance threshold raised | Premium lounges, fast-track | Not a $k$ target — a strategy |
| 8. Solo wait | Loneliness | Boredom, restlessness | Communal seating, group games | $k_{solo} \downarrow 20 - 30 \%$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Maister's Eight Propositions — Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Customer Enters Queue] --> B{Is the customer engaged in distraction?}
    B -- YES --> C1[Occupied time: PWT reduced]
    B -- NO --> C2[Unoccupied time: PWT inflated]
    A --> D{Where is the customer in the service journey?}
    D -- BEFORE service --> E1[Pre-process wait: High anxiety]
    D -- DURING service --> E2[In-process wait: Lower anxiety]
    A --> F{Is the wait duration known?}
    F -- YES --> G1[Known finite wait: PWT stable]
    F -- NO --> G2[Uncertain wait: PWT inflated]
    A --> H{Is the queue discipline perceived fair?}
    H -- YES --> I1[Equitable wait: Lower PWT]
    H -- NO --> I2[Unfair wait: PWT inflated with anger]
    A --> J{Is the customer alone or in a group?}
    J -- ALONE --> K1[Solo wait: PWT inflated]
    J -- GROUP --> K2[Group wait: PWT reduced]
    C2 --> L[High PWT result]
    E1 --> L
    G2 --> L
    I2 --> L
    K1 --> L
    C1 --> M[Low PWT result]
    E2 --> M
    G1 --> M
    I1 --> M
    K2 --> M
    L --> N[Managerial Interventions Needed]
    M --> O[Service Excellence Achieved]
```

## 4.2 Operational Intervention Flow — How to Reduce Perceived Wait

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Diagnose actual wait time] --> S2[Step 2: Identify dominant psychological driver]
    S2 --> D1[Driver: Boredom]
    S2 --> D2[Driver: Anxiety]
    S2 --> D3[Driver: Uncertainty]
    S2 --> D4[Driver: Unfairness]
    S2 --> D5[Driver: Solo experience]
    D1 --> I1[Intervention: Engage with distraction]
    D2 --> I2[Intervention: Communicate and reassure]
    D3 --> I3[Intervention: Display estimated wait]
    D4 --> I4[Intervention: Enforce FIFO single line]
    D5 --> I5[Intervention: Provide communal spaces]
    I1 --> R1[Reduced PWT]
    I2 --> R1
    I3 --> R1
    I4 --> R1
    I5 --> R1
    R1 --> F[Final Outcome: Service Quality Enhancement]
```

## 4.3 Service Process Timeline — Mapping Wait Perception

```mermaid
gantt
    title Customer Journey: Perceived vs Actual Wait
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %s
    section Actual Time
    Enter queue        :a1, 0, 5
    Pre-service hold   :a2, 5, 8
    Service core       :a3, 8, 12
    Exit               :a4, 12, 13
    section Perceived Time
    Enter queue        :b1, 0, 7
    Pre-service hold   :b2, 7, 12
    Service core       :b3, 12, 14
    Exit               :b4, 14, 15
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Gantt chart visualises the **divergence** between *actual* and *perceived* timelines. Notice that the **pre-service hold** segment (5 → 8 minutes actual) is perceived as nearly 5 minutes (7 → 12 perceived) — a classic example of Proposition 2 (pre-process wait penalty).

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Waiting-Psychology System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT[Customer Input Layer]
        A1[Arrival Rate lambda]
        A2[Service Demand Profile]
        A3[Customer Demographic Data]
    end
    subgraph PROC[Psychological Processing Layer]
        B1[Anxiety Index Calculator]
        B2[Uncertainty Index Calculator]
        B3[Fairness Index Calculator]
        B4[Engagement Fraction Estimator]
    end
    subgraph QUEUE[Queue Management Layer]
        C1[FIFO Discipline Module]
        C2[Token Display Module]
        C3[Virtual Queue Module]
    end
    subgraph OUT[Customer Experience Output]
        D1[Perceived Wait Time PWT]
        D2[Customer Satisfaction Score]
        D3[Service Level Probability]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A2 --> B2
    A3 --> B4
    B1 --> D1
    B2 --> D1
    B3 --> D1
    B4 --> D1
    C1 --> B3
    C2 --> B2
    C3 --> B1
    D1 --> D2
    D1 --> D3
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "Perceived Wait Time" and explain how it differs from Actual Wait Time.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Perceived Wait Time (PWT)** is the *subjective* duration a customer experiences while waiting for a service, governed by psychological and emotional factors.
- **Actual Wait Time (AWT)** is the *objective* clock-time elapsed in the queue.
- The two differ because of factors such as **anxiety, uncertainty, boredom, unfairness, and the value of the service**. (3 marks: definition + distinction + 1 example)

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**State any three propositions of Maister's Psychology of Waiting Lines.**

**Model Answer:**
1. *Occupied time feels shorter than unoccupied time.*
2. *Uncertain waits are longer than known, finite waits.*
3. *Unexplained waits are longer than explained waits.* (3 marks: 1 mark per proposition)

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**Q.A (a)** *Discuss in detail David Maister's eight propositions of waiting psychology. For each proposition, provide a real-world service example. (7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Proposition 1 — Occupied time feels shorter than unoccupied time:** When a customer is distracted, the brain's clock-watching activity is reduced. *Example:* Airport lounges provide complimentary Wi-Fi and entertainment, shrinking the perceived wait before boarding.

2. **Proposition 2 — People want to get started:** Anxiety peaks before the service begins. *Example:* Hospitals hand out registration forms at the entrance, allowing patients to begin the "service journey" while waiting for consultation.

3. **Proposition 3 — Anxiety makes waits seem longer:** Stress amplifies the perception of time. *Example:* Banks play soft music and provide comfortable seating to reduce anxiety in queue areas.

4. **Proposition 4 — Uncertain waits feel longer than known waits:** A defined duration reduces uncertainty. *Example:* E-commerce websites display "Estimated delivery in 3 days" rather than vague "soon."

5. **Proposition 5 — Unexplained waits are longer than explained waits:** Lack of information triggers suspicion. *Example:* Airlines announce "Flight delayed due to weather" rather than staying silent.

6. **Proposition 6 — Unfair waits feel longer than equitable waits:** Queue-jumping causes anger. *Example:* Single-line FIFO systems at theme parks like Disney prevent perceived unfairness.

7. **Proposition 7 — More valuable service → longer tolerated wait:** Customers accept longer waits for premium services. *Example:* Business-class check-in counters at airports have longer waits but premium facilities.

8. **Proposition 8 — Solo waits feel longer than group waits:** Company reduces boredom. *Example:* Restaurant waiting areas with communal benches encourage social interaction.

*(7 marks: 0.5 mark per proposition + 0.5 mark per example — examiner favours concise bullet format.)*

**Q.A (b)** *A customer at a service counter waits for 12 minutes. The distraction fraction is 0.35, anxiety index is 0.4 with sensitivity 1.3, and the uncertainty index is 0.6 with sensitivity 0.7. The fairness and solo factors are both 1.0. Calculate the perceived wait time and interpret the result. (7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Compute each perception multiplier:**

$$
k_{occ} = 1 - f_{occ} = 1 - 0.35 = 0.65
$$

$$
k_{anx} = 1 + \beta \cdot A_{score} = 1 + (1.3)(0.4) = 1.52
$$

$$
k_{unc} = 1 + \gamma \cdot U_{score} = 1 + (0.7)(0.6) = 1.42
$$

$$
k_{fair} = 1.0, \quad k_{solo} = 1.0
$$

**Step 2 — Compute total multiplier:**

$$
k_{tot} = (0.65) \cdot (1.52) \cdot (1.42) \cdot (1.0) \cdot (1.0)
$$

$$
k_{tot} = 0.65 \times 1.52 = 0.988
$$

$$
0.988 \times 1.42 = 1.4030
$$

**Step 3 — Perceived wait:**

$$
T_{PWT} = T_{AWT} \cdot k_{tot} = 12 \times 1.4030 = 16.84 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**
The customer perceives a 12-minute wait as **16.84 minutes**, a **40.3 % inflation** driven primarily by **anxiety and uncertainty**. To reduce $T_{PWT}$, the manager should:
- Announce the estimated wait duration (reducing $U_{score}$).
- Provide calm, professional service cues (reducing $A_{score}$).
- Increase engagement (raise $f_{occ}$ to 0.6+, reducing $k_{occ}$ to 0.4).

*(7 marks: 2 marks for formula setup, 3 marks for computation, 2 marks for interpretation.)*

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]`

**Q.B (a)** *Explain the concept of "Psychology of Waiting" as proposed by David Maister. Discuss its significance in service operations management with at least five managerial implications. (7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The Psychology of Waiting is a service-management framework that analyses the gap between **actual waiting time (AWT)** and **perceived waiting time (PWT)**. It is rooted in the cognitive and emotional responses of customers to delays in service delivery.

**Significance in Service Operations:**
- It enables managers to **improve customer satisfaction without increasing resources** — a critical advantage in capacity-constrained services such as healthcare, banking, and aviation.
- It supports **demand management** by setting realistic expectations.
- It enhances **brand loyalty** by demonstrating empathy in queue design.
- It allows **operational benchmarking** through the $R_{PW} = T_{PWT} / T_{AWT}$ ratio.

**Five Managerial Implications:**
1. **Engagement Design:** Provide distractions (music, Wi-Fi, refreshments) to reduce unoccupied time.
2. **Information Transparency:** Display estimated wait times on digital boards.
3. **Communication Strategy:** Always provide a *reason* for delays.
4. **Queue Architecture:** Adopt single-line FIFO to eliminate perceived unfairness.
5. **Pre-Service Activation:** Begin the service relationship early (forms, greeters, mobile pre-checks).

*(7 marks: 2 for definition, 2 for significance, 3 for implications.)*

**Q.B (b)** *A hospital outpatient department records an arrival rate of 30 patients/hour and a service rate of 40 patients/hour. Calculate the average number of patients in the system using Little's Law, the average wait time in queue, and the service utilisation. Comment on the operational implications of the perceived wait in this scenario. (7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $\lambda = 30$ patients/hour
- $\mu = 40$ patients/hour

**Step 1 — Service utilisation:**

$$
\rho = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu} = \dfrac{30}{40} = 0.75
$$

This means the system operates at 75 % of its capacity. While not saturated, perceived waits may already be inflated due to high occupancy.

**Step 2 — Average time in system (W):**

$$
W = \dfrac{1}{\mu - \lambda} = \dfrac{1}{40 - 30} = 0.1 \text{ hours} = 6 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 3 — Average wait in queue ($W_q$):**

$$
W_q = W - \dfrac{1}{\mu} = 0.1 - 0.025 = 0.075 \text{ hours} = 4.5 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 4 — Average number in system (L) using Little's Law:**

$$
L = \lambda \cdot W = 30 \times 0.1 = 3 \text{ patients}
$$

**Step 5 — Operational Implications on Perceived Wait:**
- The **actual wait** of 4.5 minutes may be **perceived as 7–9 minutes** because of the hospital's inherent anxiety multiplier ($A_{score}$ is high in healthcare).
- Recommended interventions:
  - Display estimated wait time on a screen.
  - Provide health-education videos in the waiting area (engagement).
  - Train receptionists to communicate proactively about doctor delays.
  - Implement an **appointment-based virtual queue** to eliminate physical wait.

*(7 marks: 2 for utilisation, 2 for Little's Law, 1 for wait, 2 for interpretation.)*

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Deduction Traps in Waiting-Psychology Answers:**
> 1. **Confusing AWT and PWT:** A frequent 1-mark cut. Always define both explicitly at the start of your answer.
> 2. **Memorising propositions without examples:** Each proposition without a real-world example is treated as a *half-credit* point by KTU examiners. Always pair with an example.
> 3. **Skipping the managerial implication:** A purely theoretical answer is capped at **60–70 % marks**. Always end with a managerial recommendation.
> 4. **Incorrect Little's Law application:** The formula $L = \lambda W$ is applied incorrectly when $\lambda$ and $W$ are not in **consistent time units** (e.g., arrivals per minute vs. wait in hours). Convert units first.
> 5. **Skipping the *unit check* in numerical answers:** Always state final units (minutes, customers, probability) explicitly.
> 6. **Forgetting Proposition 7 and 8:** Students often cover only the first six propositions, losing 1–2 marks. The value-and-group dimensions are important for full-marks answers.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The **core distinction** is between **Actual Wait Time (AWT)** and **Perceived Wait Time (PWT)**.
- ✅ **David Maister (1985)** is the founder of the modern theory of waiting psychology — cite his name in every answer.
- ✅ **Eight propositions:** Occupied, Pre-process, Anxiety, Uncertain, Unexplained, Unfair, Value, Solo.
- ✅ **Managerial levers:** Distraction, Information, Communication, Fairness, Pre-service, Premium treatment, Communal design.
- ✅ **Little's Law** ($L = \lambda W$) and **M/M/1 wait formula** ($W_q = \rho / (\mu - \lambda)$) are the only two formulas KTU expects in numericals.
- ✅ Always end with a **perception-management recommendation** — this is the "extra mile" KTU examiners reward.
- ✅ **Mnemonic for propositions:** *"O.P.A.U.U.S.G.V"* — Occupational, Pre-process, Anxiety, Uncertainty, Unexplained, Unfair, Solo, Group, Value.
- ✅ **Formula to remember:** $T_{PWT} = T_{AWT} \cdot k_{tot}$ where $k_{tot}$ is a composite of multiple psychological multipliers.
- ✅ **Key strategy:** Reduce **anxiety index** and **uncertainty index** first — they have the largest empirical impact on $T_{PWT}$.
- ✅ **Service-level benchmark** expected by KTU: at least **85 %** of customers should wait **less than or equal to** the advertised wait time.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application of Various Tools Used in Managing Waiting Line in Services
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT861 - SERVICE SCIENCE AND SERVICE OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Application of Various Tools Used in Managing Waiting Line in Services

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Waiting Line Management (Queuing Management)** in services refers to the systematic application of analytical, operational, psychological, and technological tools to control, reduce, or optimize the experience of customers waiting for service. It is grounded in **Queuing Theory**, a branch of operations research that mathematically analyzes the formation, behavior, and dissipation of waiting lines (queues).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, a **waiting line** is defined as a queue of customers (or items) that accumulate when the **arrival rate (λ)** of service requests exceeds or equals the **service rate (μ)** of the available service facility. The discipline combines three pillars:

1. **Analytical Modeling** (Queuing mathematics — Kendall's Notation, birth-death processes).
2. **Process/Operational Tools** (capacity planning, queue configuration, scheduling).
3. **Behavioral/Psychological Tools** (perception management, ambient design, information cues).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 emphasizes that in service operations, *waiting is not merely a delay — it is a service experience*. The tools used in managing queues are categorized into analytical (quantitative) tools and behavioral/operational (qualitative) tools.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Coffee Shop at 8:30 AM"

Imagine a small coffee kiosk at a railway station platform. Between 8:15 AM and 8:30 AM, only 5 customers arrive (λ is low), and the single barista serves them instantly. There is **no queue** because the server is idle most of the time.

Now fast-forward to **8:30 AM–9:00 AM**: a train arrives, and 40 customers enter the kiosk **simultaneously** (λ spikes, λ > μ). A line forms. The customer at the **back of the line** experiences:
- **Actual wait time** (objective) — measurable with a stopwatch.
- **Perceived wait time** (subjective) — feels twice as long because there is nothing to do, no progress feedback, and the queue is serpentine but invisible to the front.

The tools of waiting line management answer two questions:
- **How long *should* the customer wait?** → answered by **analytical tools** (M/M/1, M/M/c models).
- **How long does the customer *feel* they waited?** → answered by **psychological & operational tools** (music, dividers, queue length boards, digital tokens).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A customer's satisfaction with a service is often determined not by the wait itself, but by the **management of the wait**. A 10-minute wait in a comfortable, informative, fair-feeling environment can feel shorter than a 3-minute wait in a chaotic, opaque, and anxiety-inducing line.

### 1.3 The Three Core Metrics in Queuing Management

| Metric | Symbol | Definition | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Arrival Rate | **λ** | Number of customers arriving per unit time | customers/hour |
| Service Rate | **μ** | Number of customers served per server per unit time | customers/hour |
| Utilization | **ρ** | Fraction of time the server is busy | dimensionless (0 ≤ ρ < 1) |
| Number in System | **L** | Expected customers in queue + being served | customers |
| Number in Queue | **Lq** | Expected customers waiting in line | customers |
| Time in System | **W** | Expected total time (wait + service) | minutes |
| Time in Queue | **Wq** | Expected time spent waiting | minutes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Little's Law** (a foundational theorem in queuing): $L = \lambda W$. This single equation links the number of customers in the system to their flow rate and time spent. It is **model-independent** — it holds for nearly all steady-state queuing systems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Kendall's Notation — The Language of Queues

Before applying any tool, we must **classify** the queue. Kendall's Notation (1953) uses the format: **A/B/c/K/N/D**

| Symbol | Meaning | Common Values |
|---|---|---|
| A | Arrival process | M (Markovian/Poisson), D (Deterministic), G (General), Ek (Erlang-k) |
| B | Service time distribution | M, D, G, Ek |
| c | Number of parallel servers | 1, 2, 3, ..., c |
| K | System capacity | ∞ (default), finite K |
| N | Population size | ∞ (default), finite N |
| D | Queue discipline | FCFS (default), LCFS, Priority, Random |

**Most common KTU-exam models:**
- **M/M/1** — Single server, Poisson arrivals, exponential service.
- **M/M/c** — Multiple (c) servers, Poisson arrivals, exponential service.
- **M/G/1** — Single server, general service time distribution.
- **G/G/c** — General arrival, general service, c servers (analyzed via simulation).

### 2.2 Analytical Tools (Quantitative)

These tools require mathematical formulas and produce **exact numerical performance metrics**.

#### 2.2.1 The M/M/1 Model — Single Server, Infinite Queue

**Assumptions:**
- Arrivals follow a **Poisson process** with rate λ.
- Service times are **exponentially distributed** with rate μ.
- One server, infinite queue capacity, FCFS discipline.
- Steady-state condition: ρ = λ/μ < 1.

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. The system is modelled as a **birth-death process** with birth rate λ_n = λ (constant) and death rate μ_n = μ for n ≥ 1.
2. Steady-state probabilities follow a **geometric distribution**: $P_n = (1-\rho)\rho^n$.
3. Performance metrics are derived by summing expected values.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why geometric?** Because each state n represents "n customers in system." The probability of having n customers decreases geometrically as n grows. Long queues become exponentially unlikely — a comforting result for capacity planners.

#### 2.2.2 The M/M/c Model — Multiple Servers, Infinite Queue

**Additional logic:**
- When fewer than c customers are present, the effective service rate is $n\mu$ (n servers active).
- When n ≥ c, all c servers are busy, and the effective service rate is $c\mu$.
- This non-linearity makes the formula more complex (see Section 3 for full derivation).

#### 2.2.3 The M/G/1 Model — General Service Time

Uses the **Pollaczek-Khinchine (P-K) Mean Value Formula**:

$$L_q = \frac{\lambda^2 \sigma^2 + \rho^2}{2(1-\rho)}$$

where **σ²** is the variance of service time. This is a powerful result: queue length depends on **service time variability**, not just the mean.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight (Variability Penalty):** If service times are highly variable (large σ²), the queue length is **larger** than in an exponential case with the same mean. This is the **variability penalty** — a fundamental reason KTU exams test this formula.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM ALERT:** Memorize the M/M/1 formulas below. They appear in nearly every KTU Part B question on queuing.

| Model | Key Performance Metric | Formula |
|---|---|---|
| M/M/1 | Server Utilization | $\rho = \lambda / \mu$ |
| M/M/1 | Probability of zero customers | $P_0 = 1 - \rho$ |
| M/M/1 | Probability of n customers | $P_n = (1-\rho)\rho^n$ |
| M/M/1 | Expected number in system | $L = \rho / (1-\rho)$ |
| M/M/1 | Expected number in queue | $L_q = \rho^2 / (1-\rho)$ |
| M/M/1 | Expected time in system | $W = 1 / [\mu(1-\rho)]$ |
| M/M/1 | Expected time in queue | $W_q = \rho / [\mu(1-\rho)]$ |
| M/M/c | Server Utilization | $\rho = \lambda / (c\mu)$ |
| M/M/c | Zero-customer probability | $P_0 = \left[ \sum_{n=0}^{c-1} \frac{(\lambda/\mu)^n}{n!} + \frac{(\lambda/\mu)^c}{c!(1-\rho)} \right]^{-1}$ |
| M/M/c | Expected queue length | $L_q = \frac{P_0 \cdot (\lambda/\mu)^c \cdot \rho}{c! (1-\rho)^2}$ |
| M/M/c | Expected time in queue | $W_q = L_q / \lambda$ |
| M/M/c | Expected time in system | $W = W_q + 1/\mu$ |
| M/M/c | Expected number in system | $L = \lambda W$ |
| M/G/1 | Queue length (P-K) | $L_q = \frac{\lambda^2 \sigma^2 + \rho^2}{2(1-\rho)}$ |
| All Models | **Little's Law** | $L = \lambda W$ |
| All Models | Total Cost | $TC = C_w \cdot L + C_s \cdot c$ |
| All Models | Optimal Service Level | $\frac{dTC}{dc} = 0$ |

**Where:**
- $C_w$ = waiting cost per customer per unit time.
- $C_s$ = cost of adding one server per unit time.
- $\sigma$ = standard deviation of service time.
- $\rho$ in M/M/c uses **total** service capacity $c\mu$.

### 2.4 Operational & Psychological Tools (Qualitative)

These tools do not require mathematical models but profoundly impact perceived service quality. They are based on the landmark research of **David Maister (1985)** and **Richard Larson (1987)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Maister's First Law:** "Waiting is tolerable if the wait is **inevitable** and **unoccupied** time is **uncomfortable**." This single sentence guides every psychological tool.

#### 2.4.1 Operational Tools (Structure the Wait)

1. **Multiple Waiting Lines (Parallel Queues):** Used in banks and airports. Each customer picks a line. Pros: fairness perception is strong. Cons: inefficiency from one slow server.
2. **Single Serpentine Line:** Used at theme parks (Disney). One line, multiple servers. Pros: maximum server efficiency, no "line-jumping" perception. Cons: very long visible queue causes anxiety.
3. **Express / Priority Lanes:** Segments customers by service time. Used in supermarkets (10-items-or-fewer).
4. **Appointment Systems:** Shifts waiting from the **facility to the customer's home**, eliminating the visible queue.
5. **Tiered Service:** Different service levels for different customer types (e.g., airport check-in: First Class, Business, Economy).
6. **Numbered Tokens / Queue Management Systems (QMS):** Provides **progress feedback**, the single most important psychological tool.

#### 2.4.2 Psychological Tools (Manage the Perception)

Based on Maister's Eight Rules and operational psychology:

| Rule | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Unoccupied time feels longer** | Provide distractions (TV, magazines, Wi-Fi) |
| 2 | **Pre-process wait feels longer** | Process orders in advance (online check-in) |
| 3 | **Anxiety makes waits longer** | Calm, informative, professional environment |
| 4 | **Uncertain waits are longer** | Post clear wait-time estimates |
| 5 | **Unexplained waits are longer** | Apologize and explain delays |
| 6 | **Unfair waits are longer** | Enforce FCFS strictly, use clear lines |
| 7 | **Solo waits feel longer** | Group waiting (e.g., queue with companions) |
| 8 | **Endowed waits feel longer** | Avoid giving "free" service that becomes a wait |

#### 2.4.3 Information & Technology Tools

- **Real-Time Queue Information Displays:** Digital boards showing "Your turn in 3 minutes."
- **Mobile Queue Apps:** Customers join the queue remotely (e.g., Disney's FastPass, restaurant reservation apps).
- **SMS / Push Notifications:** "You're next — please return to the counter."
- **Self-Service Kiosks:** Reduce arrival intensity at staffed counters.
- **AI-Driven Forecasting:** Predict peak hours and dynamically allocate staff.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering & Service Utility

| Industry | Primary Tool Used | Objective |
|---|---|---|
| Banking | M/M/c analysis + QMS token system | Minimize Lq during lunch rush |
| Hospitals (ER) | Priority queuing (triage) | Minimize Wq for critical patients |
| Call Centers | M/G/1 + simulation | Capacity planning with variable call duration |
| Airlines | Appointment + pre-process + tiered service | Reduce perceived wait at check-in |
| Theme Parks | Single serpentine + virtual queue app | Maximize throughput + customer experience |
| Restaurants | M/M/1 kitchen model + reservation system | Balance capacity and demand |
| Telecom Networks | Erlang-B (M/M/c/c) | Trunk line sizing for acceptable blocking probability |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of M/M/1 Steady-State Probabilities

**Step 1: Set up the Birth-Death Process**

For an M/M/1 queue, the system can be in any state $n = 0, 1, 2, 3, \ldots$, where state $n$ means "n customers in the system."

The transition rates are:
- Birth rate (arrival): $\lambda_n = \lambda$ for all $n \geq 0$.
- Death rate (departure): $\mu_n = \mu$ for $n \geq 1$, and $\mu_0 = 0$.

**Step 2: Write the Balance Equations**

In steady state, the flow into each state equals the flow out. For state $n$ where $n \geq 1$:

$$\lambda P_{n-1} + \mu P_{n+1} = \lambda P_n + \mu P_n$$

This simplifies to:

$$\lambda P_{n-1} + \mu P_{n+1} = (\lambda + \mu) P_n$$

**Step 3: Solve the Difference Equation**

Rearranging for $P_{n+1}$ in terms of $P_n$:

$$\mu P_{n+1} = (\lambda + \mu) P_n - \lambda P_{n-1}$$

Define $\rho = \lambda/\mu$. The standard solution is a geometric series. By induction, we propose:

$$P_n = (1-\rho)\rho^n$$

**Step 4: Verify the Normalization**

The probabilities must sum to 1:

$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} P_n = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (1-\rho)\rho^n = (1-\rho) \cdot \frac{1}{1-\rho} = 1$$

This confirms the normalization is correct provided $\vert\rho\vert < 1$.

**Step 5: Derive L (Expected Number in System)**

$$L = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n \cdot P_n = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n (1-\rho)\rho^n$$

Using the formula $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n x^n = x/(1-x)^2$ for $\vert x \vert < 1$:

$$L = (1-\rho) \cdot \frac{\rho}{(1-\rho)^2} = \frac{\rho}{1-\rho}$$

**Step 6: Derive Lq (Expected Number in Queue)**

Since $L = L_q + \rho$ (where ρ is the expected number being served, as 0 or 1 customer):

$$L_q = L - \rho = \frac{\rho}{1-\rho} - \rho = \frac{\rho - \rho(1-\rho)}{1-\rho} = \frac{\rho^2}{1-\rho}$$

**Step 7: Apply Little's Law to find W and Wq**

From $L = \lambda W$:

$$W = \frac{L}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{\mu(1-\rho)}$$

From $L_q = \lambda W_q$:

$$W_q = \frac{L_q}{\lambda} = \frac{\rho}{\mu(1-\rho)}$$

**Step 8: Verify the Relationship W = Wq + 1/μ**

$$W = W_q + \frac{1}{\mu} = \frac{\rho}{\mu(1-\rho)} + \frac{1}{\mu} = \frac{\rho + (1-\rho)}{\mu(1-\rho)} = \frac{1}{\mu(1-\rho)} \checkmark$$

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of M/M/c — Expected Queue Length

**Step 1: State-Dependent Service Rates**

For an M/M/c system with c identical servers:
- When $n < c$: only $n$ servers are active, so the effective service rate is $n\mu$.
- When $n \geq c$: all c servers are active, so the effective service rate is $c\mu$.

**Step 2: Steady-State Balance Equations**

For $n < c$:

$$\lambda P_{n-1} + (n+1)\mu P_{n+1} = (\lambda + n\mu) P_n$$

For $n \geq c$:

$$\lambda P_{n-1} + c\mu P_{n+1} = (\lambda + c\mu) P_n$$

**Step 3: Recursive Probability Formula**

For $n \leq c$:

$$P_n = \frac{(\lambda/\mu)^n}{n!} P_0$$

For $n > c$:

$$P_n = \frac{(\lambda/\mu)^n}{c! \cdot c^{n-c}} P_0$$

**Step 4: Apply Normalization Condition**

$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} P_n = 1$$

Split the sum at $n = c$:

$$\sum_{n=0}^{c-1} \frac{(\lambda/\mu)^n}{n!} P_0 + \sum_{n=c}^{\infty} \frac{(\lambda/\mu)^n}{c! \cdot c^{n-c}} P_0 = 1$$

Factor out $(\lambda/\mu)^c / c!$ from the second sum and use the geometric series with ratio $\rho = \lambda/(c\mu)$:

$$P_0 = \left[ \sum_{n=0}^{c-1} \frac{(\lambda/\mu)^n}{n!} + \frac{(\lambda/\mu)^c}{c!} \cdot \frac{1}{1-\rho} \right]^{-1}$$

**Step 5: Compute Lq**

$$L_q = \sum_{n=c}^{\infty} (n-c) P_n = P_0 \cdot \frac{(\lambda/\mu)^c}{c!} \cdot \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} k \cdot \rho^{k+1}$$

where $k = n - c$. Using $\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} k x^{k+1} = x^2/(1-x)^2$:

$$L_q = P_0 \cdot \frac{(\lambda/\mu)^c \cdot \rho}{c! \cdot (1-\rho)^2}$$

**Step 6: Compute Wq via Little's Law**

$$W_q = \frac{L_q}{\lambda}$$

$$W = W_q + \frac{1}{\mu}$$

$$L = \lambda W$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked Examples

#### Worked Example 1: Single-Server Bank Teller (M/M/1)

> **Problem:** Customers arrive at a bank at λ = 20 per hour. A single teller serves at μ = 30 per hour. Find (a) utilization, (b) expected number in system, (c) expected waiting time.

**Solution:**

$$\rho = \lambda / \mu = 20/30 = 0.6667$$

$$L = \rho / (1-\rho) = 0.6667 / 0.3333 = 2.0 \text{ customers}$$

$$W = 1 / [\mu(1-\rho)] = 1 / [30 \times 0.3333] = 0.1 \text{ hours} = 6 \text{ minutes}$$

$$L_q = \rho^2 / (1-\rho) = 0.4444 / 0.3333 = 1.3333 \text{ customers}$$

$$W_q = \rho / [\mu(1-\rho)] = 0.6667 / [30 \times 0.3333] = 0.0667 \text{ hours} = 4 \text{ minutes}$$

**Verification via Little's Law:** $L = \lambda W = 20 \times 0.1 = 2.0$ ✓

#### Worked Example 2: Two-Server Tool Booth (M/M/2)

> **Problem:** A toll plaza has 2 servers. Vehicles arrive at λ = 30/hour. Service time is exponential with mean 2 minutes (μ = 30/hour per server). Find Lq and W.

**Solution:**

$$\lambda/\mu = 30/30 = 1$$

$$\rho = \lambda / (c\mu) = 30 / 60 = 0.5$$

Compute $P_0$:

$$P_0 = \left[ \sum_{n=0}^{1} \frac{1^n}{n!} + \frac{1^2}{2! \cdot (1-0.5)} \right]^{-1}$$

$$P_0 = \left[ \frac{1}{1} + \frac{1}{1} + \frac{1}{2 \times 0.5} \right]^{-1} = \left[ 1 + 1 + 1 \right]^{-1} = \frac{1}{3}$$

Compute Lq:

$$L_q = \frac{P_0 \cdot (\lambda/\mu)^c \cdot \rho}{c! \cdot (1-\rho)^2} = \frac{(1/3) \cdot 1 \cdot 0.5}{2 \cdot 0.25} = \frac{1/6}{1/2} = \frac{1}{3} \text{ vehicles}$$

Compute Wq and W:

$$W_q = L_q / \lambda = (1/3) / 30 = 0.0111 \text{ hours} = 0.667 \text{ minutes}$$

$$W = W_q + 1/\mu = 0.0111 + 0.0333 = 0.0444 \text{ hours} = 2.667 \text{ minutes}$$

#### Worked Example 3: Cost-Based Optimal Service Level (M/M/1)

> **Problem:** Customers arrive at λ = 50/hour. Each customer's waiting cost is Rs. 100/hour. Adding a new server costs Rs. 200/hour. Find the optimal service rate μ*.

**Solution:**

For M/M/1, total cost is:

$$TC(\mu) = C_w \cdot L_q + C_s \cdot \mu$$

Using $L_q = (\lambda/\mu)^2 / (1 - \lambda/\mu)$, the optimum satisfies:

$$\mu^* = \lambda + \sqrt{\frac{\lambda \cdot C_w}{C_s}}$$

Substituting:

$$\mu^* = 50 + \sqrt{\frac{50 \times 100}{200}} = 50 + \sqrt{25} = 50 + 5 = 55 \text{ customers/hour}$$

This means the server should be capable of serving **55 customers/hour** to minimize the total system cost.

#### Worked Example 4: M/G/1 with Variable Service Times

> **Problem:** Arrivals occur at λ = 10/hour. Average service time = 5 minutes (μ = 12/hour). Service time standard deviation σ = 4 minutes. Find Lq.

**Solution:**

$$\rho = \lambda / \mu = 10 / 12 = 0.8333$$

$$\sigma^2 = 4^2 = 16 \text{ minutes}^2 = 16/3600 \text{ hours}^2$$

$$L_q = \frac{\lambda^2 \sigma^2 + \rho^2}{2(1-\rho)} = \frac{(100 \times 16/3600) + 0.6944}{2 \times 0.1667}$$

$$L_q = \frac{0.4444 + 0.6944}{0.3333} = \frac{1.1389}{0.3333} = 3.417 \text{ customers}$$

**Comparison:** For M/M/1 with same mean, $L_q = \rho^2/(1-\rho) = 0.6944/0.1667 = 4.167$. The M/G/1 with σ=4 has *fewer* customers in queue because service is more predictable — confirming the **variability penalty** principle.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Queuing System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source Population] -->|Arrivals λ| B[Input Buffer]
    B --> C[Waiting Line Queue]
    C -->|FCFS / Priority| D[Service Facility]
    D -->|Service Rate μ| E[Departure Exit]
    D -.-> F[Feedback / Re-entry]
    F -.-> A
    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#FFF8DC,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,stroke-dasharray: 5 5
```

### 4.2 Classification Flowchart — Selecting the Right Queuing Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Identify the Queue] --> Q1{Number of Servers?}
    Q1 -->|One| Q2{Service Time Distribution?}
    Q1 -->|Multiple c| Q3{Service Time Distribution?}
    Q2 -->|Exponential| M1[M/M/1 Model]
    Q2 -->|General| M2[M/G/1 with P-K Formula]
    Q2 -->|Deterministic| M3[D/M/1 Model]
    Q3 -->|Exponential| M4[M/M/c Model]
    Q3 -->|General| M5[M/G/c Use Simulation]
    M1 --> F[Compute ρ L Lq W Wq]
    M2 --> F
    M3 --> F
    M4 --> F
    M5 --> SIM[Use Discrete Event Simulation DES]
    SIM --> F
    style S fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style M1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style M2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style M3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style M4 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style M5 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style SIM fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Waiting Line Management Toolkit

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TOOLKIT[Waiting Line Management Toolkit]
        direction TB
        subgraph ANALYTICAL[Analytical Tools]
            A1[Kendall Notation Classifier]
            A2[M/M/1 Formulas]
            A3[M/M/c Formulas]
            A4[M/G/1 P-K Formula]
            A5[Cost Optimization Model]
        end
        subgraph OPERATIONAL[Operational Tools]
            O1[Multiple Parallel Queues]
            O2[Single Serpentine Line]
            O3[Express Priority Lanes]
            O4[Appointment System]
            O5[Token QMS Software]
        end
        subgraph PSYCHOLOGICAL[Psychological Tools]
            P1[Occupied Time Distractions]
            P2[Pre-Process Strategy]
            P3[Anxiety Reduction Cues]
            P4[Wait Time Information Boards]
            P5[Fairness and FCFS Enforcement]
        end
        subgraph TECHNOLOGY[Technology Tools]
            T1[Real Time Queue Display]
            T2[Mobile Virtual Queue App]
            T3[Self Service Kiosks]
            T4[AI Demand Forecasting]
            T5[Discrete Event Simulation]
        end
    end
    ANALYTICAL --> OUTCOME[Optimized Service Performance]
    OPERATIONAL --> OUTCOME
    PSYCHOLOGICAL --> OUTCOME
    TECHNOLOGY --> OUTCOME
    OUTCOME --> METRICS[Reduced Lq Wq and Improved Satisfaction]
    style TOOLKIT fill:#FFF8DC,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style ANALYTICAL fill:#E0F8E0,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    style OPERATIONAL fill:#E0E8F8,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px
    style PSYCHOLOGICAL fill:#F8E0E8,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px
    style TECHNOLOGY fill:#F8F0E0,stroke:#A0522D,stroke-width:2px
    style OUTCOME fill:#FFD700,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px
    style METRICS fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.4 Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Tool

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Waiting Line Problem Detected] --> GOAL{Primary Objective?}
    GOAL -->|Minimize Lq and Wq| Q1{Is Data Available?}
    GOAL -->|Improve Customer Experience| PSY[Apply Psychological Tools]
    GOAL -->|Restructure Service Process| OPS[Apply Operational Tools]
    Q1 -->|Yes and Poisson| AN1[Use M/M/1 or M/M/c Model]
    Q1 -->|Yes but Variable Service| AN2[Use M/G/1 P-K Formula]
    Q1 -->|No or Too Complex| AN3[Use Discrete Event Simulation]
    AN1 --> DEPLOY[Deploy Calculated Capacity]
    AN2 --> DEPLOY
    AN3 --> DEPLOY
    DEPLOY --> MONITOR[Monitor and Recalibrate]
    PSY --> COMBINE[Combine with Analytical Tools]
    OPS --> COMBINE
    COMBINE --> MONITOR
    style START fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
    style DEPLOY fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    style MONITOR fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define a 'waiting line' in the context of service operations. List any two psychological tools used to manage perceived waiting time.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A waiting line (queue) is a line of customers (or items) that forms when the rate of arrivals at a service facility exceeds or equals the rate of service. It represents the temporary imbalance between demand and capacity in a service system. (1.5 marks)

Two psychological tools to manage perceived waiting time: (1 mark each)
1. **Provide distractions** (e.g., TV, music, magazines) to make unoccupied time feel shorter.
2. **Give progress information** through display boards or numbered tokens so the wait feels finite and predictable.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] State Little's Law. Why is it considered 'model-independent'?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Little's Law states that the long-term average number of customers in a stable system ($L$) equals the long-term average effective arrival rate ($\lambda$) multiplied by the average time a customer spends in the system ($W$). Mathematically, $L = \lambda W$. (2 marks)

It is called model-independent because it does not depend on the arrival process distribution, the service time distribution, the number of servers, or the queue discipline. It holds for almost all steady-state queuing systems as long as the system is stable. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### QUESTION A — Analytical Tools (M/M/1 and M/M/c)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model Question Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** A single-server car wash has arrivals at the rate of **λ = 12 cars/hour**, following a Poisson process. The service time is exponential with mean **4 minutes** per car.
   - (i) Find the server utilization (ρ), expected number of cars in the system (L), and expected number waiting (Lq). **(3 marks)**
   - (ii) Compute the expected time a car spends in the system (W) and the expected waiting time (Wq). **(2 marks)**
   - (iii) What is the probability that an arriving car finds the system empty? **(2 marks)**

**(b)** The manager is considering adding a **second identical server** to reduce waiting.
   - (i) Model the system as M/M/2 and compute the new Lq and Wq. **(4 marks)**
   - (ii) Comment on whether adding a second server is justified from a queuing perspective. **(3 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a)(i): M/M/1 Analysis**
- $\mu = 60/4 = 15$ cars/hour.
- $\rho = \lambda/\mu = 12/15 = 0.8$.
- $L = \rho/(1-\rho) = 0.8/0.2 = 4$ cars.
- $L_q = \rho^2/(1-\rho) = 0.64/0.2 = 3.2$ cars.

**Part (a)(ii): Time Metrics**
- $W = 1/[\mu(1-\rho)] = 1/[15 \times 0.2] = 1/3$ hour = **20 minutes**.
- $Wq = \rho/[\mu(1-\rho)] = 0.8/3 = 0.2667$ hour = **16 minutes**.

**Part (a)(iii): Probability of Empty System**
- $P_0 = 1 - \rho = 1 - 0.8 = 0.2$ (or **20%**).

**Part (b)(i): M/M/2 Analysis**
- $c = 2$, $\lambda = 12$, $\mu = 15$, $\lambda/\mu = 0.8$, $\rho = \lambda/(c\mu) = 12/30 = 0.4$.
- Compute $P_0$:
$$P_0 = \left[ \sum_{n=0}^{1} \frac{0.8^n}{n!} + \frac{0.8^2}{2!(1-0.4)} \right]^{-1} = \left[ 1 + 0.8 + \frac{0.64}{1.2} \right]^{-1} = \left[ 1.8 + 0.5333 \right]^{-1} = \frac{1}{2.3333} = 0.4286$$
- Compute Lq:
$$L_q = \frac{P_0 \cdot (\lambda/\mu)^c \cdot \rho}{c! (1-\rho)^2} = \frac{0.4286 \times 0.64 \times 0.4}{2 \times 0.36} = \frac{0.1097}{0.72} = 0.1524 \text{ cars}$$
- Compute Wq:
$$W_q = L_q/\lambda = 0.1524/12 = 0.0127 \text{ hour} = 0.76 \text{ minutes} \approx 46 \text{ seconds}$$

**Part (b)(ii): Justification**
Adding a second server **drastically reduces the expected waiting time from 16 minutes to 46 seconds** (a 95% reduction). The queue length drops from 3.2 cars to 0.15 cars. From a pure queuing perspective, this is a **highly justified** investment, provided the additional cost of the second server and bay is less than the customer-satisfaction and revenue gains from faster service.

---

#### QUESTION B — Cost Optimization and M/G/1 (Alternate Choice)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

**(a)** A bank counter serves customers whose service times have a **mean of 6 minutes** and a **standard deviation of 5 minutes**. Customers arrive at the rate of **6 per hour**. **(7 marks)**
   - (i) Identify the appropriate queuing model and calculate server utilization.
   - (ii) Using the Pollaczek-Khinchine formula, compute $L_q$, $L$, and $W$.

**(b)** The bank is evaluating a service redesign. Each minute of customer waiting costs the bank **Rs. 5** in goodwill loss, while an upgraded service process that increases μ to **12 customers/hour** with σ = 3 minutes will cost **Rs. 80 per hour** to operate. **(7 marks)**
   - (i) Compute the new ρ, Lq, and total hourly waiting cost.
   - (ii) Calculate the net cost saving (or loss) per hour and recommend whether the redesign is justified.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a)(i): Model Identification**
- Mean service time = 6 min, σ = 5 min. Service distribution is **not** exponential (σ ≠ mean). Therefore, this is an **M/G/1 queue**.
- $\mu = 60/6 = 10$ customers/hour, $\lambda = 6$/hour.
- $\rho = \lambda/\mu = 6/10 = 0.6$.

**Part (a)(ii): P-K Formula Computation**
- $\sigma^2 = 5^2 = 25$ min² $= 25/3600 = 0.006944$ hr².
$$L_q = \frac{\lambda^2 \sigma^2 + \rho^2}{2(1-\rho)} = \frac{(36 \times 0.006944) + 0.36}{2 \times 0.4} = \frac{0.25 + 0.36}{0.8} = \frac{0.61}{0.8} = 0.7625 \text{ customers}$$
- $L = L_q + \rho = 0.7625 + 0.6 = 1.3625$ customers.
- $W = L/\lambda = 1.3625/6 = 0.2271$ hour = 13.6 minutes.

**Part (b)(i): New System Analysis**
- New $\mu = 12$/hour, new $\sigma = 3$ min, $\sigma^2 = 9$ min² $= 9/3600 = 0.0025$ hr².
- New $\rho = 6/12 = 0.5$.
$$L_{q,\text{new}} = \frac{(36 \times 0.0025) + 0.25}{2 \times 0.5} = \frac{0.09 + 0.25}{1.0} = 0.34 \text{ customers}$$
- Total waiting cost per hour = $L_q \times C_w \times 60$ minutes (using minutes).
- Old cost = $0.7625 \times 5 \times 60 = $ **Rs. 228.75/hr**.
- New cost = $0.34 \times 5 \times 60 = $ **Rs. 102/hr**.

**Part (b)(ii): Net Cost Analysis**
- New waiting cost + redesign cost = $102 + 80 = $ **Rs. 182/hr**.
- Old waiting cost = **Rs. 228.75/hr**.
- Net saving = $228.75 - 182 = $ **Rs. 46.75/hr** (positive).
- **Recommendation:** The redesign is **justified**. The bank saves Rs. 46.75 per hour while also providing faster service, which improves customer loyalty and competitive positioning.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Wrong service rate units** — Confusing minutes and hours. If λ = 6/hour, μ must also be in per-hour units. Converting 6 min/customer → 10 customers/hour.
> 2. **Forgetting σ² in P-K formula** — A common error is to use $L_q = \rho^2/(1-\rho)$ for M/G/1. This formula is **only valid for M/M/1**. For M/G/1, you MUST include the variance term $\lambda^2 \sigma^2$.
> 3. **Misapplying M/M/c formulas** — The denominator $(1-\rho)$ in $L_q$ uses $\rho = \lambda/(c\mu)$, not $\lambda/\mu$. Students often write $\rho = \lambda/\mu$ in M/M/c problems and get an invalid (negative) utilization.
> 4. **Ignoring the steady-state condition** — All formulas assume $\rho < 1$. If $\rho \geq 1$, the system is unstable, and no finite Lq exists.
> 5. **Cost calculation errors** — When computing total hourly cost, multiply $L_q$ (customers) by $C_w$ (Rs/customer-minute) by 60, not by $C_w$ directly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [x] **Waiting line** forms when arrival rate (λ) ≥ service rate (μ); stable queues require ρ = λ/μ < 1.
- [x] **Kendall's Notation** A/B/c/K/N/D is the universal classification. M = Markovian (Poisson/exponential), G = General, D = Deterministic.
- [x] **Little's Law** $L = \lambda W$ holds for ALL stable queuing systems regardless of model.
- [x] **M/M/1 formulas:** $L = \rho/(1-\rho)$, $L_q = \rho^2/(1-\rho)$, $W = 1/[\mu(1-\rho)]$, $Wq = \rho/[\mu(1-\rho)]$.
- [x] **M/M/c formulas:** Uses $P_0$ involving a finite sum + a geometric tail. $L_q = P_0 \cdot (\lambda/\mu)^c \cdot \rho / [c! (1-\rho)^2]$.
- [x] **M/G/1 Pollaczek-Khinchine:** $L_q = (\lambda^2 \sigma^2 + \rho^2) / [2(1-\rho)]$ — service variability **increases** queue length.
- [x] **Operational Tools:** Parallel queues, serpentine lines, express lanes, appointments, tokens.
- [x] **Psychological Tools:** Distractions, pre-processing, anxiety reduction, fair FCFS, progress information, explanations.
- [x] **Technology Tools:** QMS software, mobile virtual queues, real-time displays, AI forecasting, DES simulation.
- [x] **Maister's Laws:** Unoccupied time, pre-process waits, anxiety, uncertainty, unfairness, solo waits all make waits feel longer.
- [x] **Cost Optimization:** Optimal μ found by $d(TC)/d\mu = 0$ balancing waiting cost $C_w \cdot L_q$ and service cost $C_s \cdot c$.
- [x] **Real-world applications:** Banking (M/M/c), call centers (M/G/1), airlines (appointment + pre-process), hospitals (priority), telecom (Erlang-B).
- [x] **Key exam formula pattern:** Always check ρ < 1, identify the model, then apply the correct formula set.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Managing Facilitating Goods: Review of Inventory Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT861 - SERVICE SCIENCE AND SERVICE OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Managing Facilitating Goods: Review of Inventory Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Defining Facilitating Goods in a Service Context

In Service Science (SSME - Service Science, Management and Engineering), **Facilitating Goods** are the **tangible physical products, supplies, materials, and consumables** that a service provider must procure, stock, and manage in order to deliver an intangible service offering. The service itself cannot be "touched" or "stored," but the goods that enable the service absolutely can and must be inventoried.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Standardised):**
> "Facilitating goods are the physical, tangible inputs that a service organization uses as part of its value co-creation process, without which the service cannot be rendered to the customer."

### 1.2 The Inventory Problem in Service Operations

An **Inventory Model** is a mathematical/logical framework that determines the optimal quantity of facilitating goods to order, and the optimal timing of those orders, such that the **Total Cost of Inventory** (the sum of ordering, holding, shortage, and purchase costs) is minimized while maintaining a pre-defined **service level** (probability of not stocking out).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Inventory Models Matter in Services:**
> - **Product-services (Servitization):** A car manufacturer sells "mobility-as-a-service" but still needs spare-parts inventory.
> - **Healthcare:** A hospital "sells" healthcare but needs inventory of medicines, syringes, gloves.
> - **Hospitality:** A hotel "sells" stay, but must inventory linen, food, toiletries.
> - **Aviation:** An airline "sells" transport, but must inventory in-flight meals, fuel, and life-vests.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Think of facilitating goods like the **flour, sugar, yeast, and butter** in a bakery. The cake (service) is the value you sell, but you cannot make the cake without ingredients.

If you buy **too little** flour, the bakery cannot meet demand → lost sales (Shortage Cost). If you buy **too much**, it spoils, occupies space, and ties up capital (Holding Cost). The **Inventory Model** is the "smart shopping list" that tells the baker *exactly* how much flour to buy each time, and *when*, so the bakery never runs out and never over-stocks.

### 1.4 Classification of Inventory Models (KTU Taxonomy)

- **Deterministic Models:** Demand and Lead Time are known with certainty (EOQ, EPQ, Quantity Discounts).
- **Probabilistic (Stochastic) Models:** Demand and/or Lead Time are random variables (Safety Stock, Re-Order Point with service level).
- **Independent vs. Dependent Demand:** Independent = finished facilitating goods; Dependent = components used internally.
- **Continuous Review (Q-System) vs. Periodic Review (P-System).**

### 1.5 Key Cost Components (Standard KTU Metrics)

- **D** = Annual Demand (units/year) — **bold notation standard**
- **S (or Co)** = Ordering Cost per order (₹/order) — fixed cost: paperwork, transport setup
- **H (or Ch)** = Holding/Carrying Cost per unit per year (₹/unit/year) — capital, storage, insurance, obsolescence
- **P** = Purchase Cost per unit (₹/unit)
- **C_s** = Stockout/Shortage Cost per unit (₹/unit short)
- **L** = Lead Time (time between placing order and receiving it)
- **d** = Daily demand rate
- **σ** = Standard deviation of demand (for probabilistic models)
- **z** = Service-level z-score (number of standard deviations)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Distinction:**
> Holding cost (H) is typically expressed as a *percentage (i)* of the unit cost, so mathematically **H = i × P**, where **i** is the carrying-cost rate (commonly **20% to 25% per annum** in KTU textbook problems).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Core Trade-off (The "Inventory Balance Wheel")

Every inventory model is built on three competing forces:

1. **Ordering Cost (S):** Cost incurred *each time* an order is placed → encourages **fewer, larger** orders.
2. **Holding Cost (H):** Cost of keeping inventory → encourages **smaller, more frequent** orders.
3. **Shortage Cost (Cs):** Cost of running out → encourages **safety stock / larger** orders.

The optimal order quantity is the point where the marginal increase in holding cost exactly equals the marginal decrease in ordering cost.

### 2.2 The Classical Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) Model — Wilson's Model (1913)

#### 2.2.1 Assumptions (KTU Mandatory)
- Constant, deterministic demand (D units/year).
- Constant lead time (L days).
- Instantaneous replenishment (entire lot arrives at once).
- No quantity discounts; price is fixed.
- Only ordering and holding costs vary; shortage is not permitted.
- Infinite planning horizon.

#### 2.2.2 Total Cost Function

The total annual inventory cost (excluding fixed purchase cost DC) is:

$$\text{TC}(Q) = \underbrace{\frac{D}{Q} \cdot S}_{\text{Annual Ordering Cost}} + \underbrace{\frac{Q}{2} \cdot H}_{\text{Annual Holding Cost}}$$

The term $\frac{Q}{2}$ arises because average inventory is half of the order quantity (inventory linearly depletes from Q to 0).

#### 2.2.3 The Logic Step-by-Step

- The number of orders placed per year = $\frac{D}{Q}$.
- Annual ordering cost = (orders/year) × (cost per order) = $\frac{D}{Q} \cdot S$.
- Average inventory level = $\frac{Q}{2}$ (triangle approximation).
- Annual holding cost = (avg inventory) × (holding cost per unit) = $\frac{Q}{2} \cdot H$.
- Sum them → Total variable cost. The purchase cost DC is constant and ignored for optimisation.

### 2.3 The Economic Production Quantity (EPQ) Model

When replenishment is **gradual** (the firm manufactures its own facilitating goods or receives them in batches over a production run), the instantaneous-receipt assumption is relaxed.

- **p** = production/delivery rate (units/year), with **p > d**.
- Maximum inventory is no longer Q, but $\left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right) \cdot Q$.
- Average inventory becomes $\frac{Q}{2} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)$.

### 2.4 Quantity Discount Models

When suppliers offer a price break for larger volumes (All-Units Discount or Incremental Discount), the optimal order quantity must be recomputed:

1. Compute EOQ ignoring discounts.
2. If EOQ falls in a discount bracket, it is optimal.
3. If EOQ is below the bracket threshold, evaluate TC at the **break quantity** and compare.
4. Select the feasible Q with the **lowest Total Cost including purchase cost**.

### 2.5 Safety Stock and Reorder Point (Probabilistic Model)

When demand during lead time is **random** with mean $\bar{d} \cdot L$ and standard deviation $\sigma_{dL}$:

- **Reorder Point (ROP):** The inventory level that triggers a new order.
- **Safety Stock (SS):** Buffer inventory to absorb demand variability.
- **Service Level:** The probability of not stocking out during lead time, expressed as a z-score.
