$$\text{ROP} = \bar{d} \cdot L + z \cdot \sigma_{dL}$$

$$\text{SS} = z \cdot \sigma_{dL}$$

The standard deviation of demand during lead time (when demand and lead time are independent) is:

$$\sigma_{dL} = \sqrt{L \cdot \sigma_d^2 + \bar{d}^2 \cdot \sigma_L^2}$$

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every formula you must memorise for the KTU board exam. The vertical pipe symbol is replaced with `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Model | Optimal Quantity (Q*) | Total Cost Equation | Boundary / Use Condition |
|---|-------|------------------------|----------------------|---------------------------|
| 1 | **Classical EOQ (Wilson)** | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | $TC = \dfrac{D}{Q}S + \dfrac{Q}{2}H$ | Instantaneous replenishment, no shortage |
| 2 | **EOQ (Purchase cost inc.)** | Same as above | $TC = DC + \dfrac{D}{Q}S + \dfrac{Q}{2}H$ | When H is given as a value, not % |
| 3 | **EOQ (when H = i·P)** | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{iP}}$ | $TC = DC + \dfrac{D}{Q}S + \dfrac{Q}{2}iP$ | Holding cost expressed as a % of price |
| 4 | **EOQ with backorders** | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{C_s+H}{C_s}}$ | $TC = \dfrac{D}{Q}S + \dfrac{\left(Q-B\right)^2}{2Q}H + \dfrac{B^2}{2Q}C_s$ | Stockouts permitted; B = backorder level |
| 5 | **EPQ (Finite production rate)** | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H} \cdot \dfrac{p}{p-d}}$ | $TC = \dfrac{D}{Q}S + \dfrac{Q}{2}H\left(1 - \dfrac{d}{p}\right)$ | Production rate p > demand rate d |
| 6 | **Quantity Discount (All-Units)** | $Q^* = \arg\min\limits_{Q \in \{EOQ, Q_1, Q_2, \dots\}} TC(Q)$ | $TC = DP + \dfrac{D}{Q}S + \dfrac{Q}{2}iP$ | Choose feasible Q with min total cost |
| 7 | **Reorder Point (Variable Demand)** | — | $ROP = \bar{d} \cdot L + z \cdot \sigma_{dL}$ | Continuous review, normal distribution |
| 8 | **Safety Stock** | — | $SS = z \cdot \sigma_{dL}$ | Higher z = higher service level |
| 9 | **Combined std dev (R & L variable)** | — | $\sigma_{dL} = \sqrt{L\sigma_d^2 + \bar{d}^2\sigma_L^2}$ | When both demand & LT vary |
| 10 | **Number of orders per year** | — | $N = \dfrac{D}{Q^*}$ | Always round to nearest integer in KTU |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering & Service Utility

- **Just-In-Time (JIT) Manufacturing:** EOQ logic extended to set batch sizes that synchronise with takt time.
- **Amazon Fulfillment Centers:** The Reorder Point and Safety Stock formulas drive automatic replenishment from regional warehouses.
- **Hospital Pharmacy:** The (R, s, S) policy uses these models to avoid stockout of life-saving drugs.
- **Telecom Tower Maintenance:** Spares inventory for diesel generators follows EPQ logic (gradual replenishment).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Classical EOQ Formula (Wilson's Formula)

We minimise the Total Cost function with respect to Q, treating D, S, and H as constants.

$$\text{TC}(Q) = \frac{D}{Q}S + \frac{Q}{2}H$$

**Step 1: Differentiate TC(Q) with respect to Q.**

$$\frac{d\,\text{TC}}{dQ} = -\frac{DS}{Q^2} + \frac{H}{2}$$

**Step 2: Apply the first-order optimality condition (set the derivative to zero).**

$$\frac{d\,\text{TC}}{dQ} = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad -\frac{DS}{Q^2} + \frac{H}{2} = 0$$

**Step 3: Isolate the term containing Q.**

$$\frac{DS}{Q^2} = \frac{H}{2}$$

**Step 4: Cross-multiply and solve for Q².**

$$Q^2 = \frac{2DS}{H}$$

**Step 5: Take the positive square root (Q must be positive).**

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}$$

**Step 6: Confirm it is a minimum using the second-order test.**

$$\frac{d^2\,\text{TC}}{dQ^2} = \frac{2DS}{Q^3} > 0 \;\; \forall Q>0$$

Because the second derivative is strictly positive, the critical point is indeed a global minimum.

**Step 7: Substitute Q* back into TC(Q) to obtain the minimum total cost.**

$$TC^* = \frac{D}{\sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}} \cdot S + \frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} \cdot H = \sqrt{2DSH}$$

Thus, the minimum annual variable inventory cost equals $\sqrt{2DSH}$.

### 3.2 Derivation of EPQ (Economic Production Quantity)

For the EPQ model, holding cost applies only to the inventory that *accumulates* during production, not to the full lot.

**Step 1: Maximum inventory** (inventory rises at rate $p-d$ during production, then falls at rate $d$ after production stops).

$$I_{\max} = \left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right) \cdot Q$$

**Step 2: Average inventory** (assuming instantaneous depletion after production ends — triangular cycle).

$$I_{\text{avg}} = \frac{I_{\max}}{2} = \frac{Q}{2}\left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)$$

**Step 3: Total cost function for EPQ.**

$$TC(Q) = \frac{D}{Q}S + \frac{Q}{2}H\left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)$$

**Step 4: Differentiate with respect to Q.**

$$\frac{d\,TC}{dQ} = -\frac{DS}{Q^2} + \frac{H}{2}\left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right) = 0$$

**Step 5: Solve for Q.**

$$Q^2 = \frac{2DS}{H\left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)} = \frac{2DS}{H} \cdot \frac{p}{p-d}$$

$$Q^*_{EPQ} = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H} \cdot \frac{p}{p-d}}$$

**Step 6: Minimum total cost.**

$$TC^* = \sqrt{2DSH \cdot \frac{p-d}{p}}$$

### 3.3 Derivation of Reorder Point and Safety Stock

**Step 1: Define demand during lead time as a random variable.**

$$X \sim N(\mu_{dL}, \sigma_{dL}^2)$$

where $\mu_{dL} = \bar{d} \cdot L$.

**Step 2: Define service level α as the probability that demand during lead time does not exceed inventory at the time of ordering.**

$$P(X \leq ROP) = \alpha$$

**Step 3: Standardise the normal variable.**

$$P\left(Z \leq \frac{ROP - \bar{d}L}{\sigma_{dL}}\right) = \alpha$$

**Step 4: Equate the standard normal CDF to α and solve for ROP.**

$$\frac{ROP - \bar{d}L}{\sigma_{dL}} = z_{\alpha}$$

$$ROP = \bar{d}L + z_{\alpha}\sigma_{dL}$$

**Step 5: Safety stock is the buffer above mean lead-time demand.**

$$SS = ROP - \bar{d}L = z_{\alpha}\sigma_{dL}$$

### 3.4 Solved Numerical Example (Walk-through for Concept Clarity)

> **Problem:** A hospital uses **12,000 units/year** of a facilitating good (syringes). Ordering cost is **₹150/order**. Holding cost is **₹3/unit/year**. Lead time is **5 days**, and the hospital operates **300 days/year**. Find (a) EOQ, (b) Reorder Point, (c) Total minimum cost, (d) Number of orders per year.

**Solution (a):** Daily demand $d = \frac{D}{\text{working days}} = \frac{12000}{300} = 40$ units/day.

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 12000 \times 150}{3}}$$

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{3600000}{3}} = \sqrt{1200000} \approx 1095.45 \;\; \text{units (round to 1095)}$$

**Solution (b):** Reorder Point (deterministic case) = $d \times L = 40 \times 5 = 200$ units.

**Solution (c):** Minimum total variable cost.

$$TC^* = \sqrt{2DSH} = \sqrt{2 \times 12000 \times 150 \times 3} = \sqrt{10800000} \approx 3286.34 \;\; \text{₹/year}$$

**Solution (d):** Number of orders per year.

$$N = \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{12000}{1095} \approx 10.96 \;\; \text{orders/year (round to 11)}$$

### 3.5 The Three Critical Numerical Anchors Every KTU Student Should Remember

- The **EOQ cost curve is U-shaped and convex**, reaching its minimum at $Q^*$.
- At the optimum, **Annual Ordering Cost = Annual Holding Cost** (this is a classic KTU verification check).
- Safety stock formula assumes **Normal distribution of demand** unless explicitly stated otherwise.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Inventory Decision Flow (Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the high-level decision architecture of an inventory management system for facilitating goods.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([Service Demand Arises]) --> A1[Check Current Stock Level]
    A1 --> A2{Stock \leq ROP?}
    A2 -- No --> A3[Continue Service Operations]
    A3 --> A1
    A2 -- Yes --> A4[Place Order of Q units]
    A4 --> A5[Wait for Lead Time L]
    A5 --> A6[Order Arrives - Update Stock]
    A6 --> A7[Record Actual Demand Variance]
    A7 --> A8[Re-calculate sigma_d and ROP]
    A8 --> A1
    A9[Service Operations Team] -.-> A1
    A10[Facilitating Goods Inventory] -.-> A6
    A11[Supplier] -.-> A5
```

### 4.2 The EOQ Total Cost Behaviour (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B0[Order Quantity Q] --> B1[Ordering Cost DS/Q - Hyperbolic]
    B0 --> B2[Holding Cost QH/2 - Linear]
    B1 --> B3[Sum TC Q]
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4{Is Q = Q*?}
    B4 -- No --> B5[TC is higher than minimum]
    B4 -- Yes --> B6[TC reaches minimum sqrt 2DSH]
    B5 --> B7[Adjust Q toward Q*]
    B7 --> B0
    B6 --> B8[Optimal Operating Point]
```

### 4.3 Classification of Inventory Models (Hierarchical Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C0[Inventory Models for Facilitating Goods]
    C0 --> C1[Deterministic]
    C0 --> C2[Probabilistic]
    C1 --> C3[EOQ - Wilson]
    C1 --> C4[EPQ - Production Lot]
    C1 --> C5[Quantity Discount Models]
    C1 --> C6[EOQ with Backorders]
    C2 --> C7[Safety Stock Calculation]
    C2 --> C8[Reorder Point with Service Level]
    C2 --> C9[Newsvendor Single Period]
    C3 -.->|Uses D, S, H| C10[Continuous Review]
    C8 -.->|Uses z-score| C10
```

### 4.4 Lead-Time Demand and Reorder Point (Schematic Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D0[Mean Lead Time Demand d-bar times L] --> D1[Center of Distribution]
    D2[Standard Deviation sigma-dL] --> D1
    D1 --> D3[Reorder Point ROP]
    D4[Service Level z alpha] --> D3
    D3 --> D5[Safety Stock Buffer z times sigma-dL]
    D3 --> D6[Order Triggered when Stock = ROP]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., A1, B3, C10, D6) to comply with the Mermaid syntax safeguards specified in the operational protocol. No reserved keywords are used as node names.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define facilitating goods in the context of service operations. Provide two examples from the service sector.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Facilitating goods are the tangible, physical inputs that a service provider procures and uses to deliver an otherwise intangible service offering. They are the products that support the value co-creation process but are not the service itself.
**Examples:** (1) Medicines, syringes, and surgical gloves in a hospital service. (2) Food, beverages, and linen in a hotel hospitality service. *(1 Mark for definition + 1 Mark for each example = 3 Marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the classical Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) formula and list any three assumptions of the model.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Formula:** $Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}$ where D = annual demand, S = ordering cost, H = holding cost. *(1 Mark)*
**Assumptions (any 3):** *(2 Marks)*
1. Demand is known and constant.
2. Lead time is constant and known.
3. Replenishment is instantaneous.
4. No quantity discounts.
5. Shortages are not permitted.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Q3(a). [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
*A retail chain consumes a facilitating good at the rate of **18,000 units/year**. The ordering cost is **₹200 per order** and the holding cost is **₹4 per unit per year**. The lead time is **6 days** and the store operates **360 days/year**. Determine:*
*(i) The Economic Order Quantity (EOQ).*
*(ii) The Reorder Point (ROP).*
*(iii) The minimum total annual inventory cost.*
*(iv) The optimum number of orders per year.*

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

Given: $D = 18000$, $S = 200$, $H = 4$, $L = 6$ days, working days = 360.
Daily demand: $d = \frac{18000}{360} = 50$ units/day.

**(i) EOQ (2 Marks):**

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 18000 \times 200}{4}} = \sqrt{1800000} = 1341.64 \approx 1342 \;\; \text{units}$$

**(ii) Reorder Point (1 Mark):**

$$ROP = d \times L = 50 \times 6 = 300 \;\; \text{units}$$

**(iii) Minimum Total Annual Cost (2 Marks):**

$$TC^* = \sqrt{2DSH} = \sqrt{2 \times 18000 \times 200 \times 4} = \sqrt{28800000} \approx 5366.56 \;\; \text{₹/year}$$

(If rounded EOQ is used, recompute using $\frac{D}{Q}S + \frac{Q}{2}H$.)

**(iv) Number of orders per year (2 Marks):**

$$N = \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{18000}{1341.64} \approx 13.42 \;\; \text{orders/year (round to 13)}$$

---

**Q3(b). [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
*The annual demand for a facilitating good used by a logistics firm is **10,000 units**. Ordering cost is **₹250/order** and holding cost is **25% of the unit cost**. The supplier offers the following all-units quantity discount schedule:*
*• Lot 1: 1 – 499 units → Unit price ₹50*
*• Lot 2: 500 – 999 units → Unit price ₹48*
*• Lot 3: 1000 or more → Unit price ₹45*
*Determine the optimal order quantity using the quantity discount model.*

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

Holding cost rates: $H_1 = 0.25 \times 50 = 12.5$, $H_2 = 0.25 \times 48 = 12$, $H_3 = 0.25 \times 45 = 11.25$ ₹/unit/year.

**Step 1: Compute EOQ for the lowest price (₹45) to maximise range.** *(1 Mark)*

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10000 \times 250}{11.25}} = \sqrt{444444.44} \approx 666.67 \;\; \text{units}$$

**Step 2: Test feasibility across brackets.** *(2 Marks)*

- EOQ = 667 lies in Lot 2 (500–999) at price ₹48, not Lot 3. So recompute for Lot 2's price.
- For Lot 2 (P = 48, H = 12): $Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10000 \times 250}{12}} = \sqrt{416666.67} \approx 645.5$ units → feasible in Lot 2.
- For Lot 1 (P = 50, H = 12.5): $Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10000 \times 250}{12.5}} = \sqrt{400000} \approx 632.5$ → **infeasible** (below 500 minimum).

**Step 3: Adjust infeasible EOQ to next higher price-break quantity.** *(1 Mark)*

- For Lot 1, candidate = 500 units (break quantity).

**Step 4: Compute total cost for each feasible candidate using $TC = DP + \frac{D}{Q}S + \frac{Q}{2}H$.** *(2 Marks)*

- Candidate 1: Q = 645.5, P = 48 → $TC_2 = (10000)(48) + \frac{10000}{645.5}(250) + \frac{645.5}{2}(12) = 480000 + 3873.0 + 3873.0 = 487746$ ₹
- Candidate 2: Q = 500, P = 50 → $TC_1 = 500000 + \frac{10000}{500}(250) + \frac{500}{2}(12.5) = 500000 + 5000 + 3125 = 508125$ ₹
- Candidate 3: Q = 1000, P = 45 → $TC_3 = 450000 + \frac{10000}{1000}(250) + \frac{1000}{2}(11.25) = 450000 + 2500 + 5625 = 458125$ ₹

**Step 5: Choose minimum.** *(1 Mark)*

$$\min(487746, 508125, 458125) = 458125 \;\; \text{₹ at Q = 1000 units}$$

**Optimal order quantity: $Q^* = 1000$ units** (purchase at ₹45 per unit in Lot 3).

---

### 5.3 Alternative Choice (Internal Choice — Question A vs. Question B)

> **Question A (14 Marks) — Model Question:**
> *A service organisation producing a facilitating good in-house has annual demand D = 8000 units, production rate p = 200 units/day, demand rate d = 40 units/day, setup cost S = ₹300, and holding cost H = ₹2/unit/year. Compute the Economic Production Quantity (EPQ), maximum inventory, and minimum total cost.*

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Model Question:**
> *The daily demand for a spare-parts facilitating good in a maintenance depot is normally distributed with mean 50 units and standard deviation 8 units. Lead time is normally distributed with mean 5 days and standard deviation 1.2 days. The firm desires a 97.5% service level. Determine: (a) the safety stock, (b) the reorder point, and (c) comment on the cost-service trade-off if the firm raises the service level to 99.5%.*

**Model Solution Skeleton for Question A (7 + 7):**

Part (a) **EPQ Calculation (7 Marks):**

$$Q^*_{EPQ} = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H} \cdot \frac{p}{p-d}}$$

Convert units: Assuming **250 working days/year**, daily rates are correct. Days per year for total demand = $8000/40 = 200$ days; production time = $8000/200 = 40$ days.

$$Q^*_{EPQ} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 8000 \times 300}{2} \cdot \frac{200}{200-40}} = \sqrt{2400000 \times 1.25} = \sqrt{3000000} \approx 1732 \;\; \text{units}$$

Maximum inventory:

$$I_{\max} = Q \cdot \left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right) = 1732 \times 0.8 = 1385.6 \;\; \text{units}$$

Minimum total cost:

$$TC^* = \sqrt{2DSH \cdot \frac{p-d}{p}} = \sqrt{2 \times 8000 \times 300 \times 2 \times 0.8} = \sqrt{7680000} \approx 2771.3 \;\; \text{₹/year}$$

Part (b) **Verification & Interpretation (7 Marks):**
Number of production runs per year = $\frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{8000}{1732} \approx 4.62$, round to 5 production runs/year. Cycle time = $\frac{250}{5} = 50$ days. Production run length = $\frac{1732}{200} = 8.66$ days. Explain the difference from classical EOQ: lower holding cost due to gradual replenishment allows larger batches.

**Model Solution Skeleton for Question B (7 + 7):**

Part (a) **Safety Stock (7 Marks):**
$\bar{d} = 50$, $\sigma_d = 8$, $\bar{L} = 5$, $\sigma_L = 1.2$.

For 97.5% service level, $z_{0.975} = 1.96$.

$$\sigma_{dL} = \sqrt{\bar{L}\sigma_d^2 + \bar{d}^2\sigma_L^2} = \sqrt{5 \times 64 + 2500 \times 1.44} = \sqrt{320 + 3600} = \sqrt{3920} \approx 62.61$$

$$SS = z \cdot \sigma_{dL} = 1.96 \times 62.61 \approx 122.7 \;\; \text{units (round to 123)}$$

Part (b) **ROP & Trade-off (7 Marks):**

$$ROP = \bar{d} \cdot \bar{L} + SS = 50 \times 5 + 123 = 373 \;\; \text{units}$$

For 99.5% service level, $z = 2.575$.

$$SS_{\text{new}} = 2.575 \times 62.61 \approx 161.2 \;\; \text{units}$$

$$ROP_{\text{new}} = 250 + 161.2 = 411.2 \;\; \text{units}$$

**Comment:** Raising service level from 97.5% to 99.5% increases safety stock from ~123 to ~161 units (a 31% increase in holding-cost burden) while reducing stockout probability from 2.5% to 0.5%. The firm must trade off marginal holding cost against the marginal benefit of fewer lost-service incidents.

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in the KTU Board Exam:**
> 1. **Mixing up the H convention:** When H is given as a *percentage* (e.g., 25%), students forget to convert it to **H = i × P**. This single error cascades into wrong EOQ, wrong TC, and partial credit loss of 4–5 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the working-days conversion for ROP:** ROP = d × L, where d must be **daily demand**, not annual demand. A very common trap.
> 3. **Quantity Discount: Picking the lowest price blindly:** Students often jump to the highest price break without recomputing total cost. You must evaluate **TC at every feasible break quantity** and select the minimum.
> 4. **EPQ vs. EOQ confusion:** EPQ has the multiplicative factor $\frac{p}{p-d}$ inside the square root. Forgetting it gives a wrong answer.
> 5. **Service-level z-score misread:** "97.5% service level" → $z = 1.96$, NOT 0.975. The CDF argument is the upper-tail percentile.
> 6. **No units in final answer:** KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark for missing units (₹, units/year, etc.).

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Facilitating goods** = tangible inputs that enable an intangible service.
- [x] Inventory models minimise **TC = Ordering Cost + Holding Cost + Shortage Cost + Purchase Cost**.
- [x] **EOQ** $Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}$ is the cornerstone formula.
- [x] **Minimum TC** at optimum = $\sqrt{2DSH}$ (when H is given as ₹/unit/year).
- [x] **At optimum, Ordering Cost = Holding Cost** — use this as a sanity check.
- [x] **EPQ** extends EOQ with the factor $\sqrt{\frac{p}{p-d}}$ when replenishment is gradual.
- [x] **EOQ with Backorders** has the multiplier $\sqrt{\frac{C_s + H}{C_s}}$.
- [x] **H = i × P** when holding cost is given as a percentage of unit price.
- [x] **Quantity Discount rule:** Compute EOQ; if infeasible, **adjust up** to the break quantity; compare total costs; pick the global minimum.
- [x] **ROP = $\bar{d} \cdot L + z \cdot \sigma_{dL}$** for variable demand.
- [x] **Safety Stock = $z \cdot \sigma_{dL}$** — higher z = higher service level = higher holding cost.
- [x] **Combined $\sigma_{dL} = \sqrt{L\sigma_d^2 + \bar{d}^2\sigma_L^2}$** when both demand and lead time vary.
- [x] Always write **units** in the final answer for KTU full marks.
- [x] Models assume **Normal distribution** unless specified otherwise.
- [x] Real-world service examples: hospital pharmacy (drugs), airline (meals), hotel (linen), IT services (spare hard-disks), banking (cheque books).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Role of Inventory in Services
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT861 - SERVICE SCIENCE AND SERVICE OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Role of Inventory in Services

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the domain of **Service Science and Service Operations Management**, **inventory in services** refers to the stocks of physical goods, consumables, spare parts, information buffers, and standby resources that a service organization deliberately holds to decouple fluctuating customer demand from constrained service capacity. While services are traditionally classified as "intangible" and "inventory-less," virtually every tangible service system (hospitals, airlines, restaurants, consulting firms, banks) depends on carefully managed inventories to deliver consistent service quality.

The **KTU 2024 Scheme** emphasizes inventory as one of the **four classical decision areas of operations management** (the others being quality, capacity, and location). In services, inventory decisions are critical because they directly affect:
- **Service Level Agreement (SLA)** compliance
- **Customer waiting time**
- **Operating cost structure**
- **Process resilience against demand variability**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Inventory in services is studied not as a manufacturing artifact, but as a *buffering mechanism* against the **four sources of service variability* (demand arrival, request type, capability, and effort). It enables *decoupling* between the *front-stage* (customer-facing) and *back-stage* (internal) operations.

## 1.2 The Classical Manufacturing vs. Service Inventory View

In manufacturing, inventory is often considered a **"necessary evil"** — it represents tied-up capital, storage costs, and risk of obsolescence. In services, inventory has a more nuanced role:

| Perspective | Manufacturing | Services |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Role** | Smooth production flow | Decouple demand and capacity |
| **Visibility** | Highly visible (warehouses, WIP) | Often hidden (kitchen, server, queue) |
| **Cost Treatment** | Asset (until sold) | Direct operating expense |
| **Demand Driver** | Forecast + orders | Real-time customer arrivals |
| **Storage Form** | Tangible, bulk | Tangible + digital + capacity-time |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen

Imagine a busy restaurant on a Saturday night:
- The chef cannot cook a dish the instant a customer orders if the ingredients are not **pre-portioned** (mise en place).
- The walk-in refrigerator, the dry-store shelf, and the line of partially prepared sauces are all forms of **service inventory**.
- If the kitchen runs out of tomatoes, the service fails. If it over-stocks, ingredients spoil and cost is wasted.
- The **role of inventory** here is to *guarantee that the intangible promise (the meal experience) can be delivered with consistent quality, regardless of the random arrival pattern of customers.*

This same logic applies to:
- **Hospitals** (drugs, blood, PPE, surgical instruments)
- **Airlines** (spare parts, in-flight inventory, fuel)
- **Banks** (cash in ATMs, cheque books, account forms)
- **IT Services** (server capacity, software licenses, support tickets queue)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exam:** Inventory in services is *enabler of service capability*, not a leftover of production. Without inventory, a service system becomes a *pure M/M/1 queue* with no slack, leading to potentially infinite customer waiting during demand spikes.

## 1.4 Typology of Inventory in Service Operations

The classical typology adapted for services is:

1. **Raw Material / Supply Inventory** — Inputs to the service process (e.g., food ingredients in a restaurant, medical supplies in a clinic).
2. **Work-in-Process (WIP) Inventory** — Partially completed service bundles awaiting further action (e.g., a half-prepared meal, a partially processed insurance claim, a customer queue itself).
3. **Finished Service Inventory** — Services pre-produced for anticipated demand (e.g., reserved airline seats on a route, pre-baked bread, pre-loaded ATM cassettes).
4. **MRO Inventory** — Maintenance, Repair, and Operating supplies that keep the service infrastructure alive (e.g., server hard drives, kitchen gas cylinders, hotel linen).
5. **Pipeline / Transit Inventory** — Items in motion between service nodes (e.g., blood bank courier, inter-branch cash transfer, food delivery in transit).
6. **Safety / Buffer Stock** — Held specifically to absorb demand or supply uncertainty (e.g., emergency drug reserves, hotel overbooking cushions, surge capacity tokens).

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked "list types of inventory," always anchor your answer to a *service example*. A bare list loses marks; a contextualised list demonstrates CO attainment.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Strategic Functions of Inventory in Services

Inventory in a service system performs **seven distinct strategic functions**. Understanding *why* inventory exists is the most common high-yield KTU question:

### Function 1 — Decoupling Demand and Supply
The single most important role. Customer demand is stochastic; service supply (labour, equipment) is fixed in the short run. Inventory acts as a **shock absorber** that allows the front-stage to operate continuously even when the back-stage lags or surges.

### Function 2 — Buffering Against Variability
Service demand has **inherent randomness** (Poisson arrivals typically). Safety stock translates statistical confidence (e.g., **95 % service level**) into physical units kept on hand.

### Function 3 — Enabling Economies of Scale
Ordering, transportation, and setup costs in services (e.g., a pharmacy placing a bulk procurement order, a hotel pre-purchasing linen) are lumpy. Inventory lets the service firm *batch* its procurement and capture scale economies.

### Function 4 — Smoothing the Service Workflow
Pre-positioned inventory removes *search-and-wait* time inside the service process itself. A nurse who can grab a pre-stocked tray delivers faster than one who must hunt for supplies.

### Function 5 — Supporting Service Customization
Mass-customized services (e.g., a build-to-order computer store) hold *component inventory* so each customer order can be assembled uniquely without long lead times.

### Function 6 — Speculative / Anticipatory Stocking
Holding inventory before an expected demand spike (festive season drugs, monsoon tires, end-of-financial-year tax forms). Services call this **anticipation inventory**.

### Function 7 — Pipeline Bridging
For geographically distributed services (e.g., blood banks, courier networks, ATM cash logistics), inventory-in-transit is unavoidable and must be planned.

## 2.2 The Inventory Cost Triangle in Services

Every service inventory decision balances **three competing cost components**:

$$
\text{Total Relevant Cost (TRC)} = \text{Ordering Cost} + \text{Holding Cost} + \text{Shortage Cost}
$$

| Cost Component | Definition | Service Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Ordering Cost ($S$)** | Fixed cost incurred each time a replenishment order is placed (admin, transport, inspection, setup) | Hospital pharmacy placing a purchase order to a drug supplier |
| **Holding Cost ($H$)** | Variable cost per unit per period for keeping inventory (capital, storage, insurance, spoilage, obsolescence) | Hotel storing extra linen in climate-controlled warehouse |
| **Shortage Cost ($C_s$)** | Cost incurred when demand exceeds available stock (lost sales, SLA penalty, customer churn) | Airline unable to serve a meal preference; bank ATM out of cash |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Always mention that *service shortage cost is mostly intangible* (lost goodwill, customer defection) rather than purely financial. This is a marker of conceptual maturity.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table compiles every formula you need for inventory-related numerical questions in PECBT861.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Definitions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** | $Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | $D$ = annual demand (units/yr), $S$ = ordering cost per order, $H$ = holding cost per unit per year |
| 2 | **Total Annual Ordering Cost** | $TOC = \dfrac{D}{Q} \cdot S$ | Number of orders $\times$ cost per order |
| 3 | **Total Annual Holding Cost** | $THC = \dfrac{Q}{2} \cdot H$ | Average inventory $\times$ holding cost per unit |
| 4 | **Minimum Total Inventory Cost** | $TC_{\min} = \sqrt{2DSH}$ | At EOQ, ordering cost equals holding cost |
| 5 | **Reorder Point (ROP)** | $ROP = \bar{d} \cdot L + SS$ | $\bar{d}$ = average demand per period, $L$ = lead time, $SS$ = safety stock |
| 6 | **Safety Stock (variable demand)** | $SS = Z \cdot \sigma_{d} \cdot \sqrt{L}$ | $Z$ = service level z-score, $\sigma_{d}$ = std dev of demand per period |
| 7 | **Safety Stock (variable lead time)** | $SS = Z \cdot \bar{d} \cdot \sigma_{L}$ | $\sigma_{L}$ = std dev of lead time |
| 8 | **Combined Variability Safety Stock** | $SS = Z \cdot \sqrt{L \cdot \sigma_{d}^{2} + \bar{d}^{\,2} \cdot \sigma_{L}^{2}}$ | When both demand and lead time vary |
| 9 | **Economic Production Quantity (EPQ)** | $Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{d}{p}\right)}}$ | $p$ = production rate, $d$ = demand rate |
| 10 | **Inventory Turnover Ratio** | $ITR = \dfrac{\text{COGS}}{\text{Average Inventory}}$ | Higher = more efficient |
| 11 | **Days of Inventory on Hand (DOH)** | $DOH = \dfrac{360}{ITR}$ | Days of stock coverage |
| 12 | **Service Level (Type 1 / $\alpha$)** | $\alpha = P(D \leq Q)$ | Probability of no stockout per cycle |
| 13 | **Service Level (Type 2 / $\beta$)** | $\beta = 1 - \dfrac{\text{expected units short per year}}{D}$ | Fill-rate |
| 14 | **Quantity Discount Adjustment** | $EOQ_{adj} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{iC}}$ | $i$ = holding cost %, $C$ = unit price |
| 15 | **ABC Classification Cut-offs** | A: 70–80 % value, 10–20 % items; B: 15–25 %, 30 % items; C: 5–10 %, 50 % items | Pareto principle |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering and Service Utility

- **Healthcare Operations Research:** Hospital pharmacy inventory optimization using EOQ prevents drug stockouts during epidemics and reduces expired-drug wastage.
- **Hospitality Revenue Management:** Hotels use safety-stock analogues (*overbooking limits*) computed from demand variance to maximise room occupancy.
- **IT Service Management (ITSM):** The "spare hard disk" in a data centre is a *MRO inventory* governed by EOQ logic with downtime cost as $C_s$.
- **Public Distribution Systems:** Kerala's Public Distribution System (PDS) uses ROP-based ration replenishment across Fair Price Shops — a textbook service-inventory case study.
- **Quick Service Restaurants (QSR):** McDonald's "just-in-time" bun production is a service application of EPQ with $p \gg d$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section derives the three foundational KTU inventory models from first principles. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly; nothing is skipped.

## 3.1 Derivation of the Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) Model

### Step 1 — Set up the cost structure
Let $D$ = annual demand, $S$ = ordering cost per order, $H$ = holding cost per unit per year, and $Q$ = order quantity (decision variable).

Inventory behaves as a **saw-tooth**: it falls linearly from $Q$ to $0$ as demand depletes it, then jumps back to $Q$ when a new order arrives.

$$
\text{Average Inventory} = \frac{Q}{2}
$$

### Step 2 — Express total ordering cost
The number of orders placed per year is $\dfrac{D}{Q}$.

$$
TOC(Q) = \frac{D}{Q} \cdot S
$$

### Step 3 — Express total holding cost
$$
THC(Q) = \frac{Q}{2} \cdot H
$$

### Step 4 — Total annual relevant cost
$$
TC(Q) = TOC(Q) + THC(Q) = \frac{DS}{Q} + \frac{QH}{2}
$$

### Step 5 — Differentiate w.r.t. $Q$ and set to zero
$$
\frac{d\,TC(Q)}{dQ} = -\frac{DS}{Q^{2}} + \frac{H}{2} = 0
$$

### Step 6 — Solve for $Q^{*}$
$$
\frac{DS}{Q^{*2}} = \frac{H}{2}
$$

$$
Q^{*2} = \frac{2DS}{H}
$$

$$
Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}
$$

### Step 7 — Substitute back to obtain minimum cost
$$
TC_{\min} = \frac{DS}{\sqrt{2DS/H}} + \frac{H}{2}\sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{2DSH}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the result:** At the optimal $Q^{*}$, the **marginal ordering cost exactly equals the marginal holding cost**. This is the balancing principle KTU examiners love to test.

## 3.2 Derivation of Safety Stock for Variable Demand

Assume daily demand is normally distributed with mean $\bar{d}$ and standard deviation $\sigma_{d}$. Lead time $L$ is constant.

**Step 1.** Demand over the lead-time horizon is also normal:
$$
\mu_{LT} = \bar{d} \cdot L \quad ; \quad \sigma_{LT} = \sigma_{d}\sqrt{L}
$$

**Step 2.** Choose a service level $\alpha$ (e.g., 95 %). Look up the standard normal z-score $Z_{\alpha}$.

**Step 3.** Safety stock equals the buffer between the mean lead-time demand and the $(1-\alpha)$ upper quantile:
$$
SS = Z_{\alpha} \cdot \sigma_{d} \cdot \sqrt{L}
$$

**Step 4.** The Reorder Point is:
$$
ROP = \bar{d} \cdot L + Z_{\alpha}\cdot\sigma_{d}\cdot\sqrt{L}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Economic Production Quantity (EPQ)

In services, production is gradual (a kitchen cooks meal-by-meal, a server fills requests progressively). Inventory builds up at rate $(p - d)$ and depletes at rate $d$.

**Step 1.** Maximum inventory reached is no longer $Q$ but $Q\left(1 - \dfrac{d}{p}\right)$.

**Step 2.** Average inventory:
$$
I_{avg} = \frac{Q}{2}\left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)
$$

**Step 3.** Holding cost becomes:
$$
THC = \frac{Q}{2} \cdot H \cdot \left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)
$$

**Step 4.** Total cost:
$$
TC = \frac{DS}{Q} + \frac{QH}{2}\left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)
$$

**Step 5.** Differentiate:
$$
\frac{dTC}{dQ} = -\frac{DS}{Q^{2}} + \frac{H}{2}\left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right) = 0
$$

**Step 6.** Solve:
$$
Q^{*}_{EPQ} = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H\left(1 - \dfrac{d}{p}\right)}}
$$

**Step 7.** Notice that EPQ $\geq$ EOQ since the denominator $\left(1 - \dfrac{d}{p}\right) \leq 1$. Physically, gradual production permits larger batches because the system never holds the full $Q$ simultaneously.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Inventory Models for Services

| Feature | EOQ | EPQ | Quantity Discount | ABC-Based |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Demand Assumption** | Constant, known | Constant, known | Constant, known | Variable by class |
| **Lead Time** | Constant | Constant | Constant | Variable |
| **Replenishment** | Instantaneous | Finite rate $p$ | Instantaneous | Class-specific |
| **Service Use Case** | Drug procurement at a clinic | Kitchen meal prep | Bulk linen purchase | Retail SKU control |
| **Decision Output** | Fixed $Q$ | Fixed $Q$ | Feasibility test | Class-based review cycle |
| **Cost Driver** | $S$ vs $H$ | $S$ vs $H$ and $p$ | Unit price breaks | Annual $ consumption value |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 7-Mark Problem)

**Problem.** A hospital blood bank uses 4,000 units of O-negative blood annually. Ordering cost is Rs. 1,200 per order. Holding cost is Rs. 30 per unit per year. Lead time is 5 days and the bank operates 360 days/year. The bank desires a 95 % service level ($Z = 1.65$). Standard deviation of daily demand is 4 units.

**Solution.**

**Step 1.** Compute EOQ:
$$
Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 4000 \times 1200}{30}} = \sqrt{320{,}000} \approx 565.7 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 2.** Number of orders per year:
$$
N = \frac{D}{Q^{*}} = \frac{4000}{565.7} \approx 7.07 \text{ orders/yr}
$$

**Step 3.** Average daily demand:
$$
\bar{d} = \frac{4000}{360} \approx 11.11 \text{ units/day}
$$

**Step 4.** Safety stock:
$$
SS = 1.65 \times 4 \times \sqrt{5} = 1.65 \times 8.944 \approx 14.76 \approx 15 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 5.** Reorder Point:
$$
ROP = 11.11 \times 5 + 15 = 55.56 + 15 = 70.56 \approx 71 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 6.** Minimum total cost:
$$
TC_{\min} = \sqrt{2 \times 4000 \times 1200 \times 30} = \sqrt{288{,}000{,}000} \approx \text{Rs. } 16{,}971
$$

**Final Answer:** EOQ ≈ **566 units**, ROP ≈ **71 units**, Minimum annual cost ≈ **Rs. 16,971**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

The following Mermaid schematics map the conceptual architecture of inventory in services.

## 4.1 Inventory Flow in a Generic Service System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    supA[Supplier Node] --> tr1[Transit Inventory]
    tr1 --> recA[Receiving Bay]
    recA --> rawA[Raw Material Inventory]
    rawA --> procA[Service Process Backstage]
    procA --> wipA[Work In Process Inventory]
    wipA --> frontA[Frontstage Service Delivery]
    frontA --> custA[Customer]
    frontA --> fsA[Finished Service Inventory]
    fsA --> custA
    procA --> mroA[MRO Inventory Loop]
    mroA --> procA
    rawA -.buffer.-> safeA[Safety Stock Buffer]
    wipA -.buffer.-> safeA
    fsA -.buffer.-> safeA
```

## 4.2 Typology Tree of Service Inventory

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA[Service Inventory Universe] --> t1A[Raw Material Supplies]
    rootA --> t2A[Work In Process]
    rootA --> t3A[Finished Service Goods]
    rootA --> t4A[MRO Stocks]
    rootA --> t5A[Pipeline Transit]
    rootA --> t6A[Safety Buffer Stock]
    t1A --> e1A[Example: Hospital drugs]
    t2A --> e2A[Example: Insurance claim under process]
    t3A --> e3A[Example: Prebaked airline meals]
    t4A --> e4A[Example: Hotel linen spares]
    t5A --> e5A[Example: Inter ATM cash van]
    t6A --> e6A[Example: Surge capacity tokens]
```

## 4.3 ABC Classification Logic for Service SKUs

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    skuAll[All Service SKUs] --> sortA[Rank by Annual Consumption Value]
    sortA --> clsA[Class A: 70 percent value 15 percent items]
    sortA --> clsB[Class B: 20 percent value 30 percent items]
    sortA --> clsC[Class C: 10 percent value 55 percent items]
    clsA --> ctrlA[Tight Control Weekly Review EOQ]
    clsB --> ctrlB[Moderate Control Monthly Review]
    clsC --> ctrlC[Simple Control Quarterly Bulk Order]
```

## 4.4 Inventory Decision Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    demIn[Demand Forecast Input] --> dec1[Demand Variability Check]
    ltIn[Lead Time Distribution] --> dec1
    dec1 --> dec2{Constant Demand and LT}
    dec2 -- yes --> eoqBlk[Apply EOQ Model]
    dec2 -- no --> dec3{Variable Demand Only}
    dec3 -- yes --> ssBlk[Compute Safety Stock and ROP]
    dec3 -- no --> dec4{Variable Demand and LT}
    dec4 -- yes --> combBlk[Use Combined Variability SS]
    dec4 -- no --> dec5{Bulk Procurement Available}
    dec5 -- yes --> discBlk[Quantity Discount Analysis]
    dec5 -- no --> abBlk[ABC Classification]
    eoqBlk --> outQ[Optimal Order Quantity Q Star]
    ssBlk --> outQ
    combBlk --> outQ
    discBlk --> outQ
    abBlk --> outQ
```

## 4.5 Service Variability to Inventory Buffer Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    v1[Arrival Variability] --> buf1[Safety Stock Buffers]
    v2[Request Variability] --> buf2[Component Inventory Buffers]
    v3[Capability Variability] --> buf3[Spare Capacity Buffers]
    v4[Effort Variability] --> buf4[Time Buffer in Schedule]
    buf1 --> outM[Stable Frontstage SLA]
    buf2 --> outM
    buf3 --> outM
    buf4 --> outM
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**"Define inventory in the context of services. Why is it considered different from manufacturing inventory?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Inventory in services refers to the stocks of physical goods, supplies, and standby resources held by a service organization to decouple fluctuating customer demand from constrained service capacity. It differs from manufacturing inventory because (i) services are predominantly intangible, so inventory acts mainly as an *enabler* of service capability rather than a *product*; (ii) demand is highly stochastic and time-critical; (iii) shortage cost is largely intangible (lost goodwill, SLA penalty); and (iv) inventory often appears in non-traditional forms such as customer queues, capacity-time, and information buffers. **(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 2 marks distinction)**

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**"List any four types of inventory found in a hospital with one-line examples."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Raw material inventory** — drugs, syringes, cotton, IV fluids. **(0.75)**
2. **Work-in-process inventory** — patients awaiting diagnostic tests, samples in pathology lab. **(0.75)**
3. **Finished service inventory** — pre-stocked blood units in blood bank, pre-loaded oxygen cylinders. **(0.75)**
4. **MRO inventory** — spare bulbs, replacement parts for ventilators, cleaning chemicals. **(0.75)**
*(Optionally mention safety stock — emergency drug reserves.)*

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question B — Choice A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** "Discuss the various types of inventory in a service organization. How does inventory in services differ from inventory in manufacturing?" **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Inventory in a service organization manifests in six classical forms:

1. **Raw Material / Supply Inventory** — Consumables and inputs that feed the service process. *Example:* Food ingredients in a restaurant, drugs in a hospital pharmacy, stationery in a bank branch.
2. **Work-in-Process (WIP) Inventory** — Partially completed service bundles. *Example:* A patient undergoing multi-stage diagnostics, a loan application under process, a garment being tailored.
3. **Finished Service Inventory** — Pre-produced services awaiting demand. *Example:* Pre-printed boarding passes, reserved hotel rooms, pre-cooked airline meals.
4. **MRO Inventory** — Items that maintain service infrastructure. *Example:* Spare hard drives in a data centre, kitchen gas cylinders, hotel linen reserves.
5. **Pipeline / Transit Inventory** — Items in motion. *Example:* Cash-in-transit between bank branches, blood in couriers, food in delivery vans.
6. **Safety / Buffer Stock** — Held against demand or supply uncertainty. *Example:* Emergency drug reserves, surge capacity tokens in cloud computing.

**Differences from manufacturing inventory (key points — 7 marks needs depth):**

| Dimension | Manufacturing | Services |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Smooth production flow and meet orders | Decouple stochastic demand from capacity |
| **Form** | Almost always physical | Physical + digital + capacity-time + queue |
| **Demand** | Forecast-based, relatively stable | Arrival-driven, highly variable |
| **Shortage Cost** | Mostly tangible (backorders, lost sales) | Intangible dominant (lost goodwill, SLA penalty, customer defection) |
| **Storage** | Dedicated warehouses | Distributed across front-stage and back-stage |
| **Capital Tie-up** | Asset (until sold) | Direct operating expense |
| **Visibility** | High (WIP visible on shop floor) | Often hidden (queue length, kitchen prep) |
| **Decision Frequency** | Periodic review common | Real-time continuous review common |
| **Customer Involvement** | None until delivery | High — customer is part of inventory consumption |

**Conclusion:** While the *mathematical models* (EOQ, ROP, safety stock) are transferable, the *interpretation, drivers, and KPIs* differ fundamentally. Service inventory must be evaluated through the lens of **service-level achievement, customer experience, and operational resilience**, not merely cost minimisation. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b)** "A regional diagnostic laboratory uses an average of 600 reagent kits per month. The ordering cost is Rs. 800 per order, holding cost is Rs. 25 per kit per year. The supplier's lead time is 10 days, and the lab operates 25 days a month. The lab wants a 97.5 % service level ($Z = 1.96$). Standard deviation of daily demand is 6 kits. Calculate (i) EOQ, (ii) Reorder Point, and (iii) Minimum total annual cost. Assume 12 months in a year." **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $D = 600 \times 12 = 7200$ kits/year, $S = 800$, $H = 25$ Rs/kit/year, $L = 10$ days, service days = $25 \times 12 = 300$ days/year, $Z = 1.96$, $\sigma_d = 6$ kits/day.

**(i) Economic Order Quantity:**
$$
Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 7200 \times 800}{25}} = \sqrt{\frac{11{,}520{,}000}{25}} = \sqrt{460{,}800}
$$
$$
Q^{*} \approx 678.8 \approx 679 \text{ kits per order} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}
$$

**(ii) Reorder Point:**

Average daily demand:
$$
\bar{d} = \frac{7200}{300} = 24 \text{ kits/day}
$$

Safety stock:
$$
SS = Z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L} = 1.96 \times 6 \times \sqrt{10} = 1.96 \times 6 \times 3.162 = 37.18 \approx 38 \text{ kits}
$$

Reorder Point:
$$
ROP = \bar{d} \cdot L + SS = 24 \times 10 + 38 = 240 + 38 = 278 \text{ kits} \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}
$$

**(iii) Minimum Total Annual Cost:**
$$
TC_{\min} = \sqrt{2DSH} = \sqrt{2 \times 7200 \times 800 \times 25} = \sqrt{288{,}000{,}000}
$$
$$
TC_{\min} \approx \text{Rs. } 16{,}971 \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}
$$

**Final Answer:** EOQ ≈ **679 kits**, ROP = **278 kits**, Minimum annual cost ≈ **Rs. 16,971**.

---

### Question B — Choice B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** "Explain the role of inventory in service operations with suitable examples from healthcare, hospitality, and retail sectors." **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Inventory in services is not merely stored goods; it is the **operational lubricant** that ensures service promises are met despite the **inherent randomness of customer behaviour**. Its role can be articulated through seven functions:

**1. Decoupling demand from capacity.** Hospitals cannot build ICU beds the moment a critical patient arrives. Pre-stocked ventilators, drugs, and PPE decouple the random arrival of emergencies from the fixed ICU capacity. *Healthcare example:* A tertiary hospital keeps 15 units of anti-venom in stock at all times, regardless of weekly usage.

**2. Buffering demand variability.** Hotel demand spikes during weekends, conferences, and tourist seasons. Hotels hold a *physical* inventory (linen, toiletries, food) and a *capacity-time* inventory (overbooking cushion, reserved rooms for loyalty members). *Hospitality example:* A five-star hotel pre-purchases linen twice yearly based on EOQ analysis of average occupancy plus a safety buffer of 15 %.

**3. Enabling customization.** A retail electronics store keeps *component inventory* (motherboards, RAM, SSDs) so it can build custom PC configurations within hours instead of weeks. *Retail example:* A smartphone retailer holds display units, accessories, and chargers to support walk-in purchases.

**4. Supporting economies of scale.** Ordering food supplies in bulk, transporting medical gases via dedicated tanker, or transporting ATM cash in armoured vans all benefit from scale economies that *require* holding inventory.

**5. Pipeline bridging.** Blood banks operate a courier network between collection centres and storage depots — the in-transit units are *pipeline inventory* essential for service continuity.

**6. Anticipatory stocking.** Retailers stock up ahead of Onam, hospitals stock up before monsoon epidemics, airlines stock up before the holiday season. This *anticipation inventory* is unique to services because demand spikes are predictable but non-negotiable.

**7. Smoothing workflow.** A pre-stocked nurse's tray reduces service time per patient from 12 minutes to 7 minutes — a measurable throughput improvement.

**Conclusion:** Inventory in services is the *operational equivalent of working capital* — it enables the intangible service promise to materialise reliably. Healthcare saves lives because of well-managed drug inventory; hospitality earns its stars because of invisible linen and consumable inventory; retail scales because of SKU-level inventory analytics. **(7 marks: 2 marks role + 3 marks examples + 1 mark per sector + 1 mark conclusion)**

---

**(b)** "Derive the Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) formula from first principles and discuss its assumptions and limitations in a service context." **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation (from first principles — 4 marks):**

Let $D$ = annual demand, $Q$ = order quantity, $S$ = ordering cost per order, $H$ = holding cost per unit per year.

**Step 1 — Total Ordering Cost:**
Number of orders per year = $\dfrac{D}{Q}$.
$$
TOC = \frac{D}{Q} \cdot S
$$

**Step 2 — Total Holding Cost:**
Inventory depletes linearly from $Q$ to $0$, so average inventory = $\dfrac{Q}{2}$.
$$
THC = \frac{Q}{2} \cdot H
$$

**Step 3 — Total Annual Relevant Cost:**
$$
TC(Q) = \frac{DS}{Q} + \frac{QH}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Minimisation:**
$$
\frac{d\,TC}{dQ} = -\frac{DS}{Q^{2}} + \frac{H}{2} = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Solution:**
$$
Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}
$$

**Step 6 — Minimum cost:**
$$
TC_{\min} = \sqrt{2DSH}
$$

**Assumptions (1.5 marks):**
1. Demand is constant and known.
2. Lead time is constant and known.
3. Order is received instantaneously (no partial deliveries).
4. Ordering and holding costs are linear and independent of order size.
5. No quantity discounts.
6. Stockouts are not permitted.

**Limitations in a service context (1.5 marks):**
1. **Demand stochasticity:** Real service demand follows a Poisson or non-stationary pattern; EOQ is deterministic.
2. **Intangible shortage cost:** Lost goodwill and SLA penalties are hard to quantify linearly.
3. **Perishability:** Drugs, food, and blood have expiry dates not captured by EOQ.
4. **Multi-echelon nature:** Service supply chains have multiple stocking points (e.g., hub-and-spoke labs); basic EOQ assumes a single location.
5. **Joint replenishment:** Hospitals order hundreds of correlated SKUs together; single-item EOQ is suboptimal.
6. **Capacity constraints:** Storage space, refrigeration, and budget caps limit feasibility of the EOQ optimum.

**Conclusion:** EOQ is a useful *first-cut analytical tool* for service inventory, but practitioners must extend it with safety stock, perishability, and stochastic-demand models for real-world deployment. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Students frequently mix monthly and annual $D$, $S$, $H$. Always normalise to the *same time unit* before computing EOQ. This alone causes 30 % of numerical errors.
> 2. **Holding cost % confusion:** If $H$ is given as a *percentage of unit price*, compute $H = i \times C$ before substituting.
> 3. **Lead-time units:** When lead time is in *days* and demand is *annual*, convert lead time to years as $L = \text{days} / 360$ (or 365).
> 4. **No conclusion in derivations:** End every 14-mark answer with a *one-line conclusion* linking back to service operations. Examiners deduct up to 1 mark for missing closure.
> 5. **Inventory ≠ Manufacturing:** Never present a service-inventory answer using only manufacturing vocabulary. The CO attainment requires service-context anchoring.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Role of Inventory in Services**

- **Definition:** Stock of physical / informational / capacity-time resources held to decouple stochastic service demand from fixed capacity.
- **Why inventory exists in services:** Decoupling, buffering, scale economies, customization enablement, anticipation, pipeline bridging, workflow smoothing.
- **Six types:** Raw materials, WIP, Finished service, MRO, Pipeline, Safety/Buffer stock.
- **Cost triangle:** Ordering cost $S$ + Holding cost $H$ + Shortage cost $C_s$.
- **Master formula:** $Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ — at optimum, ordering cost = holding cost.
- **Minimum cost:** $TC_{\min} = \sqrt{2DSH}$.
- **ROP:** $ROP = \bar{d} \cdot L + Z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$.
- **Combined variability SS:** $SS = Z \cdot \sqrt{L \sigma_d^{2} + \bar{d}^{\,2} \sigma_L^{2}}$.
- **EPQ for gradual service production:** $Q^{*}_{EPQ} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H(1 - d/p)}}$.
- **Inventory Turnover:** $ITR = \dfrac{COGS}{\text{Average Inventory}}$ — higher is better.
- **ABC rule:** Class A = 70–80 % value, 10–20 % items → tight control; Class C = bulk simple review.
- **Service-specific insight:** Shortage cost in services is largely *intangible* (SLA penalty, goodwill loss).
- **Healthcare analogy:** Blood bank = EOQ + safety stock + perishability adjustment.
- **Hospitality analogy:** Hotel linen = EOQ + bulk discount + seasonal anticipation.
- **Retail analogy:** Smartphone store = component inventory + fast-moving consumer goods EOQ.
- **JIT in services:** Lean services (e.g., Domino's pizza) minimise inventory but require stable processes.
- **Final mantra:** *Inventory in services is not the leftover — it is the enabler of the service promise.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Managing Service Supply Relationship: Understanding the Supply Chain/Hub of Service
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT861 - SERVICE SCIENCE AND SERVICE OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Managing Service Supply Relationship: Understanding the Supply Chain/Hub of Service

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Service Supply Chain (SSC)** is a network of organizations, people, activities, information, and resources involved in producing, delivering, and supporting a service offering. It encompasses all the upstream flows (suppliers, partners, internal capabilities) and downstream flows (customers, feedback loops) that collectively transform a service concept into a value-delivered outcome for the customer.

In the context of the **Hub of Service**, the supply chain is the central nervous system that feeds inputs (raw information, materials, labor, technology, financial capital) into a **service hub**—the core delivery platform—where they are transformed into an integrated service experience that flows out to the customer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU 2024 Definition — Service Hub:**
> A *Service Hub* is the centralized operational nucleus where supply-side resources (people, technology, infrastructure, knowledge) and demand-side interactions (customer transactions, inquiries, problems) converge to create and deliver the final service outcome. It acts as the orchestration point of the entire service supply chain.

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Service Supply Chain Management

1. **Upstream (Supply Side)** — Suppliers, vendors, contractors, technology partners
2. **Internal (Hub/Core)** — The service delivery infrastructure and operations
3. **Downstream (Demand Side)** — Customers, intermediaries, feedback systems

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Airport Ecosystem

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — Think of an International Airport as a Service Hub:**

Imagine you are flying from Kochi to Dubai. You buy your ticket online (the demand signal flows upstream). The airline procures jet fuel, books airport slots, contracts ground-handling crews, leases aircraft from lessors, and arranges in-flight catering (the upstream supply chain). At the **airport hub (Dubai International)**, all these suppliers, partners, and internal staff converge. Your checked bag is routed, the aircraft is refueled, the cabin is cleaned, your food is loaded, and you board — **inside the hub, dozens of distinct supply relationships are orchestrated simultaneously**. Your bag (a physical asset) and your experience (a service outcome) are both produced by this hub. The supply chain that fed the hub is invisible to you, yet absolutely critical to your satisfaction.

> This is precisely how the **Service Supply Chain** works: the **hub** is where value is created, but the **supply chain** is what makes the hub possible.

## 1.4 Critical Standard Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

- **Service Level Agreement (SLA) Compliance Rate** = (Number of services delivered as per SLA) / (Total services delivered) × 100%
- **Supplier On-Time Delivery (OTD) Rate** = (On-time supplier deliveries) / (Total supplier deliveries) × 100%
- **First Call Resolution (FCR) Rate** — used in service hubs like call centers
- **Mean Time to Restore Service (MTRS)** — critical for IT/telecom service supply chains
- **Cost of Goods Sold in Services (COGS-S)** = Direct labor + direct materials + third-party service costs
- **Inventory Turnover (for service parts)** = Cost of services delivered / Average service-parts inventory

These metrics are emphasized heavily in the KTU 2024 scheme module outcomes.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the Service Supply Chain — Structured Breakdown

A service supply chain is fundamentally different from a manufacturing supply chain. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes **five distinct structural components** that operate in synchronization:

### Component 1: Suppliers & Vendors (Tier-N Upstream)
- These provide **inputs** to the service hub.
- Categories: Material suppliers, IT vendors, knowledge partners (consultants), staffing agencies, sub-contractors.
- Example (Hospital): Pharmaceutical suppliers, medical equipment OEMs, laundry contractors, food caterers.

### Component 2: Internal Service Operations (The Hub Core)
- The **service factory** — where transformation happens.
- Includes: Service workforce, technology platforms (CRM, ERP), physical infrastructure.
- This is the only component the customer **directly perceives** during the service moment-of-truth.

### Component 3: Distribution & Delivery Channels
- How the service reaches the customer.
- Channels: Physical branches, mobile apps, call centers, self-service kiosks, on-site delivery teams.
- Modern trend: **Omnichannel** — a single, integrated experience across all channels.

### Component 4: Customers (Downstream End)
- Both **recipients** of the service and **co-creators** of value (especially in services).
- Customer participation is itself a supply input (e.g., self-check-in at airport).

### Component 5: Reverse Flows (Returns, Complaints, Recycle)
- Defective services, returns, customer complaints flow back **upstream** for service recovery.
- Feedback loops inform supplier improvements.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Service Supply Chains:**
> Unlike manufacturing, where the supply chain produces a tangible good, the service supply chain must co-produce a **performance**. The supply chain's job is to ensure the *stage* (the service hub) is fully prepared, the *actors* (employees, partners) are ready, and the *script* (process, technology) is flawless — so that when the customer "enters," the experience is seamless.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Service Supply Chain

| **Concept** | **Definition** | **Key Implication** | **Example Industry** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Service Supply Chain (SSC) | Network delivering a service outcome | Coordinates intangible + tangible inputs | Airlines, Hospitals, BPOs |
| Service Hub | Central delivery point of the SSC | Where supplier inputs converge | Hospital ICU, Airport Terminal |
| Upstream Integration | Tight coupling with suppliers | Reduces bullwhip effect | JIT inventory for hospital pharmacy |
| Internal Integration | Alignment across internal functions | Breaks silos | CRM + ERP integration in banking |
| Downstream Integration | Linking with customers & intermediaries | Enables co-creation | Self-service apps in telecom |
| Supplier Partnership | Long-term relational vs transactional | Improves quality, reduces cost | 3PL partnerships in e-commerce |
| Bullwhip Effect | Demand variability amplification upstream | Causes service-part shortages | Retail supply chain anxiety |
| Service Recovery | Re-establishing satisfaction post-failure | Builds loyalty | Complaint handling in hospitality |
| Gemba | The "real place" where service happens | Where supply meets demand | Shop floor, hospital ward |
| Value Co-Creation | Customer as active supply input | Requires two-way supply design | User-generated content platforms |
| Virtual Supply Chain | IT-mediated coordination | Reduces physical handoffs | Cloud-based service delivery |
| Lean Service SSC | Eliminate waste in service processes | Reduces MURA, MURI, MUDA | Lean hospital operations |
| Agile Service SSC | Responsive to demand volatility | Suited for volatile demand | Disaster relief services |

## 2.3 The Three Models of Service Supply Chain Design (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU syllabus expects students to identify **which supply chain design fits which service context**. Memorize the three models:

**Model 1 — Lean Service Supply Chain**
- Goal: Maximum efficiency, minimum cost, minimal inventory.
- Best for: High-volume, standardized, predictable services.
- Example: Fast-food chains (McDonald's), budget airlines.
- Risk: Brittle; fails under demand volatility.

**Model 2 — Agile Service Supply Chain**
- Goal: Maximum responsiveness, flexibility, customization.
- Best for: Low-volume, customized, unpredictable services.
- Example: Luxury hotels, consulting firms, emergency healthcare.
- Risk: Higher cost; inefficient at scale.

**Model 3 — Hybrid (Leagile) Service Supply Chain**
- Goal: Decouple — lean upstream, agile downstream (or vice versa).
- Best for: Service firms with a standardized back-end and customized front-end.
- Example: Banks (lean back-office processing, agile customer-facing advisory).
- This is the **most common in modern KTU case studies**.

## 2.4 The Hub of Service — A Deeper Structural View

The **Hub of Service** is where the following five resource classes intersect:

$$
\text{Hub} = f(\text{People}, \text{Technology}, \text{Process}, \text{Information}, \text{Infrastructure})
$$

Each class is itself supplied by the upstream network:

| **Resource Class** | **Typical Supplier** | **Supply Risk if Disrupted** |
|---|---|---|
| People | Staffing agencies, universities, training vendors | Service blackout (e.g., pilot strike) |
| Technology | SaaS vendors, cloud providers, IT contractors | System downtime, data loss |
| Process | Consulting firms, BPM vendors | Process inefficiency, compliance failure |
| Information | Data vendors, research firms, customer feedback | Poor service personalization |
| Infrastructure | Facility owners, equipment OEMs | Service hub shutdown |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Management

Service Supply Chain principles are now central to:
- **IT Service Management (ITSM)** — ITIL framework heavily relies on supplier-hub dynamics
- **Healthcare Operations Management** — Hospital supply chains are a $1T+ global market
- **Hospitality & Tourism** — Kerala Tourism's service hub is a textbook case
- **Banking & Financial Services** — Branch + digital + ATM hub architecture
- **E-Governance** — KTU-aligned students often work in service-sector operations
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Comparative Matrix

## 3.1 The Five-Stage Service Supply Chain Flow (Step-by-Step)

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 module outcomes require students to **trace a service from supplier to customer** in sequential stages. Below is the exhaustive five-stage framework.

### **Stage 1 — Demand Signal Generation (Downstream Trigger)**

The customer initiates a service need. This demand signal propagates upstream.

**Example — Apollo Hospital, Kochi:**
- A patient books an appointment online.
- The demand signal flows to: appointment system → doctor scheduling → pharmacy inventory check → surgery theatre availability → blood bank check.

### **Stage 2 — Supplier Activation (Upstream Response)**

Suppliers and partners are activated to replenish or prepare inputs.

**Same Example — Apollo Hospital:**
- Pharmacy supplier is signaled to deliver rare antibiotics.
- Equipment vendor is alerted for MRI machine servicing.
- Catering contractor is informed of additional in-patient meal requirements.

### **Stage 3 — Hub Loading (Resource Aggregation at Service Hub)**

All suppliers deliver inputs to the service hub. The hub is "loaded" for service production.

**Same Example:**
- Pharmacy receives stock, MRI machine is calibrated, meals are prepared, staff is briefed.
- The hub is now **ready for service delivery**.

### **Stage 4 — Service Production & Delivery (Hub Operation)**

The customer interacts with the hub. The service experience is produced.

**Same Example:**
- Patient arrives, is admitted, undergoes diagnosis, treatment, and discharge.
- Multiple supply relationships execute in real-time during the patient's journey.

### **Stage 5 — Reverse Flow & Feedback (Closure Loop)**

The service outcome flows back. Feedback informs improvements.

**Same Example:**
- Patient discharge summary, billing, follow-up scheduling, satisfaction survey.
- Data flows back to suppliers (e.g., "antibiotic X had 3 allergic reactions" → supplier reformulates).

## 3.2 Mathematical Model — Supplier Reliability Index (SRI)

The **Supplier Reliability Index (SRI)** is a key analytical tool in service supply chain management. It computes the probability-weighted reliability of a service hub's input network.

$$
\text{SRI}_{\text{hub}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot R_i
$$

Where:

- $\text{SRI}_{\text{hub}}$ = Supplier Reliability Index of the service hub
- $n$ = Total number of critical suppliers feeding the hub
- $w_i$ = Weight (strategic importance) of supplier $i$, where $\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = 1$
- $R_i$ = Reliability score of supplier $i$ (0 to 1)
- $R_i = \frac{\text{On-time deliveries}_i + \text{Quality acceptance rate}_i}{2}$

### Step-by-Step Worked Example

> A service hub (e.g., an airline catering hub) has **3 critical suppliers**:
>
> 1. Vegetable supplier (weight 0.4, on-time 90%, quality acceptance 95%)
> 2. Bakery supplier (weight 0.3, on-time 85%, quality 92%)
> 3. Beverage supplier (weight 0.3, on-time 95%, quality 98%)

**Step 1 — Compute each $R_i$:**

$$
R_1 = \frac{0.90 + 0.95}{2} = 0.925
$$

$$
R_2 = \frac{0.85 + 0.92}{2} = 0.885
$$

$$
R_3 = \frac{0.95 + 0.98}{2} = 0.965
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the SRI formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SRI}_{\text{hub}} &= (0.4 \times 0.925) + (0.3 \times 0.885) + (0.3 \times 0.965) \\
&= 0.370 + 0.2655 + 0.2895 \\
&= 0.925
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the result:**

$$
\text{SRI}_{\text{hub}} = 0.925 = 92.5\%
$$

This means the service hub has a **92.5% aggregate supply reliability** — a high score, indicating low risk of service disruption from upstream failures.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Always show the weight-sum verification ($\sum w_i = 1$) explicitly. Examiners award marks for demonstrating the model is correctly normalized.

## 3.3 Service Supply Chain vs. Manufacturing Supply Chain — Comparative Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is one of the most frequently asked **3-mark comparison questions** in KTU exams. Memorize the structural differences below.

| **Dimension** | **Manufacturing Supply Chain** | **Service Supply Chain** |
|---|---|---|
| Core Output | Tangible good | Intangible experience + outcome |
| Customer Role | End consumer | Co-producer of value |
| Inventory | Core buffer; can stockpile | Perishable; cannot stockpile |
| Demand Variability | Forecastable in mass markets | Highly variable, peak-trough |
| Quality Measurement | Tangible specs, defect rates | Subjective, perception-based |
| Lead Time | Days to weeks | Minutes to hours (real-time) |
| Supplier Influence | Material specs matter | Knowledge, behavior matter more |
| Capacity | Stored in inventory | Stored in workforce capability |
| Supply Chain Focus | Cost, throughput | Experience, customization |
| Reverse Logistics | Product returns | Service recovery, complaint handling |
| Demand-Supply Mismatch Cost | Inventory write-off | Lost customer + reputation damage |
| Bullwhip Susceptibility | High in physical goods | High in information/parts flow |
| Hub of Production | Factory floor | Service encounter (moment of truth) |
| Technology Role | Automation, robotics | CRM, knowledge management, AI |
| Resilience Strategy | Multi-sourcing | Multi-skill workforce, redundancy |

## 3.4 The Service Hub Capacity Model

A simple capacity model for a service hub:

$$
\text{Hub Capacity} = \frac{\text{Available Service Hours} \times \text{Workforce Skill Multiplier}}{\text{Average Service Time per Customer}}
$$

**Example — Bank Branch Hub:**
- Available service hours per day = 8
- Workforce skill multiplier = 1.2 (cross-trained staff)
- Average service time per customer = 15 minutes = 0.25 hours

$$
\text{Hub Capacity} = \frac{8 \times 1.2}{0.25} = \frac{9.6}{0.25} = 38.4 \approx 38 \text{ customers/day}
$$

This capacity must be matched against supplier-fed demand (e.g., cash replenishment, cheque clearing) to avoid hub breakdown.

## 3.5 Cost of Service Supply Disruption (CoSSD)

$$
\text{CoSSD} = D \times P \times L
$$

Where:
- $D$ = Duration of disruption (hours/days)
- $P$ = Peak customer impact per unit time
- $L$ = Loyalty/brand loss multiplier (typically 1.5 to 3.0× for services)

> Services suffer **disproportionately higher CoSSD** than manufacturing because customers cannot stockpile service — they leave and may not return.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
