# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define Service Supply Chain. Differentiate it from a manufacturing supply chain in two key aspects.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Service Supply Chain (SSC)** is a network of organizations, people, activities, information, and resources involved in producing, delivering, and supporting an **intangible service offering** to the end customer. It coordinates upstream inputs (suppliers, knowledge, technology) into a service hub for value creation.

**Two Key Differences from Manufacturing SC:**

| **Aspect** | **Manufacturing SC** | **Service SC** |
|---|---|---|
| Output Type | Tangible, storable product | Intangible, perishable experience |
| Customer Role | Passive end-consumer | Active co-producer of value |

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for two valid differences = 3 marks)*

---

### **Question 2: What is meant by the 'Hub of Service'? Give a suitable example.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **Hub of Service** is the centralized operational nucleus where supply-side resources (people, technology, infrastructure, information, processes) and customer demand converge to produce a service outcome. It is the **moment-of-truth** location where the service experience is actually created and delivered.

**Example:** The **outpatient department of a multi-specialty hospital** is a service hub — it integrates doctors, nurses, diagnostic equipment, pharmacy inputs, IT systems, and patient flows into a single coordinated care experience.

*(1.5 marks for definition + 1.5 marks for relevant example = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive SSC Analysis** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

> **"Service organizations must design their supply chains differently from manufacturing firms. With respect to a hospital, explain the structure of the service supply chain. Discuss the role of the 'Hub of Service' and the challenges in managing upstream supplier relationships."**

**Structure: (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**

#### **Part (a) — Structure of the Hospital Service Supply Chain & Role of the Hub (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A hospital is a **classic service hub**, integrating complex upstream supplier relationships with downstream patient care. The structure comprises:

**1. Upstream Suppliers (Tier 1, 2, 3):**
- **Pharmaceutical companies** — supply medicines, vaccines, surgical consumables.
- **Medical equipment OEMs** — supply MRI, CT, X-ray, ventilators, monitors.
- **IT vendors** — Hospital Information System (HIS), ERP, PACS imaging.
- **Human resource partners** — medical colleges, nursing institutes, locum agencies.
- **Support service contractors** — laundry, food/catering, housekeeping, biomedical waste management.
- **Blood banks and pathology labs** — external diagnostics and transfusion support.

**2. The Hospital Hub (Service Hub):**
The hub is the **hospital campus itself**, comprising interconnected departments:
- **Front-of-house:** Reception, OPD, emergency department.
- **Core clinical hub:** OT (operation theatre), ICU, wards, diagnostics, pharmacy.
- **Back-of-house:** Stores, CSSD (Central Sterile Supply Department), kitchen, admin.
- **Support systems:** Power backup, medical gas pipelines, oxygen plants.

**3. Distribution Channels:**
- In-person consultation, telemedicine, home healthcare, ambulance services, mobile diagnostic vans.

**4. Customers (Downstream):**
- Patients, attendants, insurance/TPA partners, referring doctors, corporate clients.

**5. Reverse Flows:**
- Discharge feedback, readmission data, complaint management, biomedical waste reverse logistics, equipment recall.

**Role of the Service Hub (Synthesis):**

The hospital hub **orchestrates** all upstream inputs in real-time to produce patient outcomes. Unlike a factory, the patient (customer) physically enters the hub, making the hub's design — layout, workflow, signage, wait-time management — directly part of the **service experience**. The hub's **service encounter quality** (e.g., empathy of nurse, cleanliness of ward, accuracy of diagnosis) determines the patient's perceived value, not just the clinical outcome.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for structure, 2 marks for hub explanation, 2 marks for role, 1 mark for diagram-style example)*

#### **Part (b) — Challenges in Managing Upstream Supplier Relationships (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Managing upstream supplier relationships in hospital service supply chains presents **six key challenges**:

**1. Demand Volatility:**
Hospitals face unpredictable demand — emergency admissions, epidemic surges. Suppliers must be flexible. *Mitigation:* Use **Vendor-Managed Inventory (VMI)** with safety stock agreements.

**2. Quality and Compliance Risk:**
A single defective implant or contaminated IV fluid can cause patient harm. *Mitigation:* Stringent **incoming quality assurance**, supplier audits, regulatory compliance (NABH, JCI, Drugs and Cosmetics Act).

**3. Bullwhip Effect:**
Small demand variations in the OPD amplify into large procurement orders. *Mitigation:* Collaborative Planning, Forecasting, and Replenishment (**CPFR**) systems.

**4. Supplier Concentration Risk:**
Specialty drugs and high-end equipment often have few suppliers. *Mitigation:* Strategic partnerships, multi-sourcing where possible, long-term contracts.

**5. Information Asymmetry:**
Suppliers often have more information about product availability than the hospital. *Mitigation:* Real-time data sharing via cloud-based **procurement portals**.

**6. Cost vs Service Trade-off:**
The cheapest supplier may compromise on delivery reliability. *Mitigation:* **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** analysis rather than unit-price focus.

**Conclusion:** Successful hospital SSC management requires moving from a **transactional** to a **strategic partnership** model with key suppliers — building trust, transparency, and shared risk-reward frameworks.

*(7 marks: 1 mark per challenge + 1 mark for conclusion = 7 marks)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **"The Hub of Service is the strategic center of any service supply chain. Examine the design of a hotel's service hub, and evaluate how the choice between Lean, Agile, and Leagile supply chain models impacts service delivery performance."**

**Structure: (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**

#### **Part (a) — Design of a Hotel Service Hub (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A hotel is a **multi-stage service hub** with both tangible (rooms, food) and intangible (hospitality, ambience) dimensions.

**Hub Design Components:**

| **Layer** | **Elements** | **Supplier Source** |
|---|---|---|
| People | Front desk, housekeeping, chefs, concierge | Staffing agencies, hospitality institutes |
| Technology | PMS (Property Management System), CRM, smart room controls | SaaS vendors, IoT providers |
| Process | Check-in, room service, billing, complaint resolution | Internal SOPs, consulting firms |
| Information | Guest profiles, booking history, reviews | OTAs (Booking.com), loyalty programs |
| Infrastructure | Building, rooms, kitchen, laundry, parking | Contractors, equipment OEMs |

**Hub Flow:**
1. **Pre-arrival:** Reservation (downstream) → housekeeping prep (internal) → vendor activation (upstream).
2. **Arrival:** Welcome → check-in → room allocation → service initiation.
3. **Stay:** Dining, housekeeping, concierge, business services.
4. **Departure:** Billing, feedback, reverse flow of loyalty data.
5. **Post-stay:** Reviews, repeat booking, CRM feedback.

**Distinguishing Feature:** Unlike a hospital, the hotel hub is **experience-centric** — even a perfect room fails if the welcome is cold. Hence, the **moment-of-truth** at front office disproportionately defines perceived value.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for hub layers, 2 marks for flow, 2 marks for distinguishing feature, 1 mark for synthesis)*

#### **Part (b) — Lean vs Agile vs Leagile in Service Delivery (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Lean Service Supply Chain:**
- **Goal:** Maximize efficiency, minimize waste (Muda), standardize service.
- **Suited for:** Budget hotels (e.g., OYO, Ibis), standardized room service.
- **Advantages:** Low cost, predictable quality, scalable.
- **Disadvantages:** Inflexible to special requests; fails during peak demand.

**Agile Service Supply Chain:**
- **Goal:** Maximize responsiveness, customization.
- **Suited for:** Luxury hotels (e.g., Taj, Oberoi), boutique resorts, Ayurvedic retreats.
- **Advantages:** Highly personalized, premium experience.
- **Disadvantages:** High cost, lower capacity utilization, complex supplier coordination.

**Leagile (Hybrid) Service Supply Chain:**
- **Goal:** Lean upstream (bulk procurement of linens, food), agile downstream (customized guest experience).
- **Suited for:** Most mid-to-upper scale hotels; the **industry standard**.
- **Strategic Decoupling Point:** Set at the **room allocation and service delivery** boundary.

**Performance Impact:**

| **Model** | **Cost** | **Speed** | **Customization** | **Resilience** | **Customer Loyalty** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Lean | Low | Fast | Low | Low | Moderate |
| Agile | High | Variable | Very High | Moderate | High |
| Leagile | Medium | Fast | High | High | High |

**Conclusion:** The Leagile model offers the optimal balance for hotels — operational efficiency in the back-of-house and emotional engagement in the front-of-house. The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically highlights this **decoupling** concept as a high-yield topic.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for lean, 2 marks for agile, 2 marks for leagile, 1 mark for comparative conclusion)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Service Hub" with "Service Center"** — A service hub is a strategic orchestration point, not just a physical location. Marks deducted for shallow definitions.
> 2. **Listing suppliers without linking to the hub** — Examiners expect you to *explain the flow* from supplier → hub → customer, not just list parties.
> 3. **Mixing up Lean and Agile** — Lean = efficiency (Toyota Production System origin); Agile = responsiveness. Do not interchange them.
> 4. **Ignoring the customer as a supply input** — In service SSCs, customers co-produce. Omitting this loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Forgetting the reverse flow** — Always mention feedback, service recovery, and reverse logistics in SSC diagrams.
> 6. **Skipping the Decoupling Point** — When discussing Leagile models, explicitly state *where* the lean-to-agile transition occurs.
> 7. **No diagram in 14-mark questions** — KTU examiners expect at least one structural diagram (worth 1–2 marks) in long answers.
> 8. **Ignoring KTU context** — Relating examples to Kerala-based services (Kerala Tourism, KSRTC, KSFE, district hospitals) earns contextual appreciation marks.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Points for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- ✅ **Service Supply Chain (SSC)** = Network producing + delivering a service (intangible-dominant).
- ✅ **Service Hub** = Central orchestration point where supplier inputs meet customer demand.
- ✅ **Five components of SSC:** Suppliers, Hub Operations, Distribution, Customers, Reverse Flows.
- ✅ **Three SSC designs:** Lean (efficiency), Agile (responsiveness), Leagile (hybrid + decoupling).
- ✅ **Lean SC** → High volume, low variability, standardized services (fast food, budget airlines).
- ✅ **Agile SC** → Low volume, high variability, customized services (luxury hotels, consulting).
- ✅ **Leagile SC** → Most common; decoupling point at hub-customer interface.
- ✅ **Supplier Reliability Index (SRI)** = $\sum w_i \cdot R_i$ (weighted reliability score, 0 to 1).
- ✅ **Bullwhip Effect** in SSC = demand variability amplification upstream; mitigated by CPFR.
- ✅ **Customer as co-producer** — a unique feature of service SSCs.
- ✅ **Reverse flows** — feedback, complaints, service recovery, reverse logistics.
- ✅ **Hub Capacity Formula** = (Service Hours × Skill Multiplier) / Avg Service Time.
- ✅ **CoSSD** = Disruption Duration × Peak Impact × Loyalty Loss Multiplier.
- ✅ **Five hub resource classes:** People, Technology, Process, Information, Infrastructure.
- ✅ **Supplier relationship spectrum:** Adversarial → Cooperative → Partnership → Strategic → Vertical Integration.
- ✅ **Kerala Context** = Kerala Tourism, district hospitals, KSRTC, KSFE banks, Ayurveda hubs.
- ✅ **Real-world example to remember:** Airport = textbook service hub (suppliers converge, customer enters, experience delivered).
- ✅ **Key difference from manufacturing:** No inventory buffer, customer is part of process, capacity stored in workforce.
- ✅ **Always draw at least one diagram** in 14-mark answers on SSC.
- ✅ **Mention the decoupling point explicitly** when discussing Leagile models.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT861 - SERVICE SCIENCE AND SERVICE OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Strategies for Managing Suppliers of Service

# 📘 Strategies for Managing Suppliers of Service

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 🎯 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Service Operations Management**, *suppliers of service* (often called **service vendors**, **third-party providers**, or **strategic service partners**) are external entities that deliver intangible value-creating activities on behalf of a focal firm. According to the **Service-Dominant (S-D) Logic** (Vargo & Lusch) and the **Triple-A Supply Chain Framework** (Lee, 2004), managing these suppliers is no longer a transactional procurement function but a **strategic capability-building exercise** aimed at co-creating value, mitigating operational risk, and sustaining competitive advantage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 3 Focus):**
> Strategies for managing suppliers of service refer to the **systematic set of policies, frameworks, contractual instruments, and relational mechanisms** that a service organization uses to **select, evaluate, develop, integrate, and control** external providers of services to ensure they consistently meet the firm's strategic objectives and customer promise.

### 🌐 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you own a **five-star restaurant in Kochi** but you are not a master baker. You buy your bread and pastries from a specialist supplier "Daily Crust Bakers."

- If you treat them as just a **commodity vendor** who must simply deliver bread at the lowest price, you get inconsistent quality and angry customers.
- If you treat them as a **strategic service partner** — sharing customer feedback, involving them in menu design, signing a **Service Level Agreement (SLA)** for on-time warm delivery, and jointly innovating a new sourdough recipe — your restaurant's reputation soars.

The "bread supplier" in service industries is your **IT vendor, logistics partner, BPO call-center, cloud-services provider, or facilities management firm**. *Strategy* is how you *manage* that partnership.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Conceptual Shift (KTU High-Yield):**
> In goods supply chains → focus is on **price + quality + delivery**.
> In service supply chains → focus is on **value co-creation + relationship + experience consistency + risk sharing**.

### 🏷️ Standard Metrics & Boundary Conditions

The following **standard service-supplier management metrics** are bolded as they are KTU examinable:

- **Service Level Agreement (SLA) Compliance Rate** — measured in **%**.
- **Mean Time to Restore Service (MTRS)** — measured in **hours/minutes**.
- **First Call Resolution (FCR)** — measured in **%**.
- **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** of supplier interaction — measured on a scale of **-100 to +100**.
- **Supplier Performance Index (SPI)** — dimensionless score **0 to 1**.
- **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** for the service — measured in **INR/USD per unit of output**.

---

### 🔄 The Service-Supplier Ecosystem — Core Stakeholders

| Stakeholder | Role | KTU Strategic Relevance |
|-------------|------|--------------------------|
| **Focal Service Firm** | The "principal" who contracts the service | Owns customer relationship |
| **Tier-1 Service Supplier** | Direct vendor (e.g., IT services firm) | Delivers core outsourced process |
| **Tier-2 / Sub-suppliers** | Vendors of the vendor | Create supply-chain depth & risk |
| **Customer (End-user)** | Receiver of co-created value | Ultimate judge of supplier performance |
| **Regulatory Body** | Sets compliance boundaries | Imposes non-negotiable service standards |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 🧠 The Strategic Management Framework (SMF) for Service Suppliers

The management of service suppliers can be deconstructed into **five sequential strategic phases**, each of which is a KTU high-yield area:

#### **Phase 1 — Strategic Sourcing & Make-or-Buy Decision**

Before selecting a supplier, the firm must answer: *"Should this service be produced in-house (insourcing) or bought (outsourcing/co-sourcing)?"*

This is evaluated using the **Make-or-Buy Analytical Matrix (MBAM)** with three dimensions:

1. **Strategic Importance** of the service activity.
2. **Supplier Market Maturity** — number of competent vendors available.
3. **Internal Capability Gap** — gap between current and required competence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule of Thumb (Module 3 High-Yield):**
> *If Strategic Importance = HIGH and Internal Capability = LOW → Outsource to a Strategic Partner.*
> *If Strategic Importance = LOW and Internal Capability = HIGH → Insource.*

#### **Phase 2 — Supplier Selection & Segmentation**

Once outsourcing is decided, suppliers are evaluated using the **Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP)** or the **Kraljic Matrix adapted for services**. Selection criteria commonly include:

- Technical competence
- Financial stability
- Cultural fit
- Innovation capability
- Geographic proximity
- Compliance and security posture
- Track record in SLA compliance

The **Kraljic Matrix for Services** classifies suppliers into four quadrants:

| Quadrant | Service Type | Strategy |
|----------|--------------|----------|
| **Strategic** | Core, high-value, low-substitutability | Partnership, joint innovation, long-term contracts |
| **Leverage** | High-value, many suppliers available | Competitive bidding, aggressive negotiation |
| **Bottleneck** | Low-value, few suppliers available | Develop alternatives, secure continuity, manage risk |
| **Non-critical** | Low-value, many suppliers available | Standardize, automate, transactional contracts |

#### **Phase 3 — Contracting & SLA Design**

The contractual backbone of service-supplier management is the **Service Level Agreement (SLA)**. It must contain:

- **Service Description** (the "what")
- **Service Level Targets (SLTs)** (the "how much/many") — measurable KPIs
- **Roles & Responsibilities** (RACI matrix)
- **Penalty / Service Credit (SC) Mechanism**
- **Escalation & Governance Procedure**
- **Exit / Termination Clauses**
- **Information Security & Compliance Addendums**

#### **Phase 4 — Supplier Performance Monitoring & Governance**

Performance is tracked through a **Supplier Performance Scorecard (SPS)** built on the **BSC (Balanced Scorecard)** dimensions:

1. **Financial** — cost variance, invoice accuracy, TCO reduction
2. **Operational** — SLA compliance, MTRS, FCR
3. **Customer** — end-user NPS, complaint rate
4. **Learning & Growth** — innovation pipeline, training, certification

> [!NOTE]
> **Governance Cadence (Industry Standard):**
> Daily → operational dashboards; Weekly → service review meetings; Monthly → executive governance; Quarterly → strategic business review (QBR).

#### **Phase 5 — Supplier Development & Continuous Improvement**

This phase converts a *vendor* into a *partner*. Tools include:

- **Kaizen Workshops** with supplier teams
- **Supplier Capability Maturity Model Integration (SCMMi)**
- **Joint Investment in Technology (e.g., shared cloud platforms)**
- **Supplier Recognition & Reward Programs**

### 📐 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Metric | Equation | Unit | Application |
|---|------------------|----------|------|-------------|
| 1 | **SLA Compliance Rate** | $$\text{SLA\%} = \frac{\text{Number of SLA Met}}{\text{Total SLAs Measured}} \times 100$$ | **%** | Measures adherence to contracted service targets |
| 2 | **Mean Time to Restore Service** | $$\text{MTRS} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_{\text{restore},i}}{n}$$ | **minutes / hours** | Average downtime duration after incident |
| 3 | **First Call Resolution** | $$\text{FCR\%} = \frac{\text{Issues Resolved on First Contact}}{\text{Total Contacts}} \times 100$$ | **%** | Service effectiveness in contact centres |
| 4 | **Service Credit (Penalty)** | $$\text{SC} = \sum_{k=1}^{m} \big( \text{Breach}_k \times \text{Monthly Fee} \times \text{Weight}_k \big)$$ | **Currency** | Financial compensation for SLA breach |
| 5 | **Supplier Performance Index** | $$\text{SPI} = \sum_{j=1}^{p} w_j \cdot s_j,\quad \sum w_j = 1$$ | **0 to 1** | Weighted composite supplier score |
| 6 | **Net Promoter Score** | $$\text{NPS} = \%\text{Promoters} - \%\text{Detractors}$$ | **-100 to +100** | End-user loyalty to the service |
| 7 | **Total Cost of Ownership** | $$\text{TCO} = C_{\text{acq}} + C_{\text{op}} + C_{\text{trans}} + C_{\text{exit}}$$ | **Currency** | Holistic cost view of supplier |
| 8 | **Supplier Risk Score** | $$\text{Risk} = P_{\text{fail}} \times I_{\text{impact}}$$ | **Index** | Probability × Impact of supplier failure |
| 9 | **Cost Variance Ratio** | $$\text{CVR} = \frac{C_{\text{actual}} - C_{\text{budget}}}{C_{\text{budget}}} \times 100$$ | **%** | Budgetary control of supplier spend |
| 10 | **Defect Density in Service** | $$\text{DD} = \frac{\text{Defects Logged}}{\text{Service Units Delivered}}$$ | **defects/unit** | Quality of service output |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Avoid Markdown Table Breaking:** When using absolute values or conditions within table formulas, use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` instead of `\|x\|` to prevent table syntax corruption.

### 🌍 Real-World Utility

These formulas and frameworks are used in:

- **Banking & Financial Services** — managing cloud and IT service suppliers (e.g., Infosys, TCS engagements)
- **Healthcare** — managing outsourced radiology, lab services, and facility management
- **Hospitality** — managing third-party housekeeping, catering, and tour operators in Kerala tourism
- **Telecommunications** — managing tower service providers (e.g., Indus Towers, ATC)
- **Public Sector** — managing e-governance service vendors (Kerala KSITM, C-DIT)

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 🔬 Worked Example 1 — SLA Service Credit Calculation

**Problem:**
A retail bank has outsourced its **24×7 customer support** to ServiceSupplier Pvt. Ltd. for a monthly fee of **₹50,00,000**. The SLA contains **three weighted KPIs** as given below. The actual performance for April 2024 is shown. Compute the total **Service Credit** payable by the supplier.

| KPI | Weight | Target | Actual | Breach Formula |
|-----|--------|--------|--------|----------------|
| Call Answer Time (CAT) | 0.5 | ≤ 30 sec | 45 sec | ₹2,00,000 for every 10-second breach beyond 30 sec, capped at 100% of monthly fee |
| First Call Resolution (FCR) | 0.3 | ≥ 80% | 75% | 1% of monthly fee for every 1% shortfall, capped at 50% of monthly fee |
| System Uptime | 0.2 | ≥ 99.5% | 99.0% | 0.5% of monthly fee for every 0.1% shortfall, capped at 30% of monthly fee |

#### Step 1 — Identify the monthly fee basis
$$M = \text{₹ }50{,}00{,}000$$

#### Step 2 — Compute the CAT Service Credit
The breach duration is:
$$\Delta_{\text{CAT}} = 45 - 30 = 15 \text{ seconds}$$

This corresponds to 1.5 units of "10-second breach":
$$n_{\text{CAT}} = \frac{15}{10} = 1.5$$

$$\text{SC}_{\text{CAT}} = n_{\text{CAT}} \times \text{₹ }2{,}00{,}000 = 1.5 \times \text{₹ }2{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ }3{,}00{,}000$$

Check cap: Maximum cap = 100% of $M$ = ₹ 50,00,000. ₹ 3,00,000 < cap, so the value stands.

#### Step 3 — Compute the FCR Service Credit
The shortfall percentage is:
$$\Delta_{\text{FCR}} = 80 - 75 = 5\%$$

Service Credit for FCR:
$$\text{SC}_{\text{FCR}} = 5 \times 1\% \times M = 0.05 \times \text{₹ }50{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ }2{,}50{,}000$$

Check cap: Maximum cap = 50% of $M$ = ₹ 25,00,000. ₹ 2,50,000 < cap, so the value stands.

#### Step 4 — Compute the System Uptime Service Credit
The shortfall in uptime is:
$$\Delta_{\text{up}} = 99.5 - 99.0 = 0.5\%$$

Number of "0.1% shortfall" units:
$$n_{\text{up}} = \frac{0.5}{0.1} = 5$$

Service Credit for uptime:
$$\text{SC}_{\text{up}} = 5 \times 0.5\% \times M = 2.5\% \times \text{₹ }50{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ }1{,}25{,}000$$

Check cap: Maximum cap = 30% of $M$ = ₹ 15,00,000. ₹ 1,25,000 < cap, so the value stands.

#### Step 5 — Aggregate the Total Service Credit
$$\text{SC}_{\text{total}} = \text{SC}_{\text{CAT}} + \text{SC}_{\text{FCR}} + \text{SC}_{\text{up}}$$

$$\text{SC}_{\text{total}} = \text{₹ }3{,}00{,}000 + \text{₹ }2{,}50{,}000 + \text{₹ }1{,}25{,}000 = \text{₹ }6{,}75{,}000$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The supplier owes the bank a total service credit of **₹ 6,75,000** for April 2024, equivalent to **13.5%** of the monthly contract value. This is a major input to the quarterly supplier performance review (QBR).

---

### 🔬 Worked Example 2 — Supplier Performance Index (SPI) Computation

**Problem:**
A hospital evaluates its outsourced **Housekeeping Service Vendor (GreenClean)** using three KPIs. The weights and raw scores (0 to 1 scale) for Q1 2024 are:

- SLA Compliance — weight 0.5, raw score 0.92
- MTRS — weight 0.3, raw score 0.85
- End-user NPS normalized — weight 0.2, raw score 0.78

Compute the SPI.

#### Step 1 — Verify Weights Sum to 1
$$w_1 + w_2 + w_3 = 0.5 + 0.3 + 0.2 = 1.0 \;\; ✅$$

#### Step 2 — Apply the SPI Formula
$$\text{SPI} = \sum_{j=1}^{p} w_j \cdot s_j$$

$$\text{SPI} = (0.5 \times 0.92) + (0.3 \times 0.85) + (0.2 \times 0.78)$$

$$\text{SPI} = 0.4600 + 0.2550 + 0.1560$$

$$\text{SPI} = 0.8710$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A SPI of **0.871** (or **87.1%**) places GreenClean in the **"Preferred Partner"** band (>0.80). The hospital may offer a contract extension and a performance bonus.

---

### 🔬 Worked Example 3 — Kraljic Matrix Quadrant Identification for a Service

**Problem:**
Classify the following service-supplier relationships into the Kraljic Matrix:

1. **Cloud Hosting Services** (AWS / Azure) for a fintech startup — high impact, high cost, few large suppliers.
2. **Office Stationery Supply & Printing** for a corporate office — low impact, low cost, many suppliers.
3. **Specialized Cybersecurity Audit Firm** — high impact, low cost, very few qualified firms in India.
4. **Corporate Travel & Ticketing Service** — moderate impact, high cost, many options.

| Scenario | Quadrant | Strategic Action |
|----------|----------|------------------|
| 1. Cloud Hosting | **Strategic** | Long-term partnership, joint roadmap, co-investment |
| 2. Office Stationery | **Non-critical** | E-procurement, standardization, transactional PO |
| 3. Cybersecurity Audit | **Bottleneck** | Develop in-house capability, multi-source, secure continuity |
| 4. Corporate Travel | **Leverage** | Competitive bidding, volume leverage, optimize price |

---

### 📊 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Three Major Supplier-Management Strategies

| Dimension | **Outsourcing Strategy** | **Strategic Alliance Strategy** | **Co-Creation / Partnership Strategy** |
|-----------|--------------------------|---------------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| **Duration** | Short to medium term (1–3 years) | Medium to long term (3–5 years) | Long term (5+ years) |
| **Contract Type** | Transactional SLA | Master Service Agreement + Framework | Alliance Charter + Shared P&L |
| **Risk Allocation** | Transferred to vendor | Shared between parties | Mutually absorbed |
| **Innovation Role** | Low — execution only | Moderate — joint problem-solving | High — joint R&D and IP creation |
| **Best For** | Non-core, commoditized services | Important but not core services | Core, differentiating services |
| **KTU Example** | Outsourced payroll processing | Outsourced IT help-desk with shared SLAs | Joint cloud-platform development with AWS |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 🗺️ Diagram 1 — The Service-Supplier Management Lifecycle (SSML)

This flowchart visualizes the closed-loop, five-phase strategic process for managing service suppliers end-to-end.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Phase 1: Strategic Sourcing and Make or Buy Decision"] --> B["Phase 2: Supplier Selection and Segmentation using Kraljic Matrix"]
    B --> C["Phase 3: Contracting and SLA Design with KPIs and Service Credits"]
    C --> D["Phase 4: Performance Monitoring via Supplier Performance Scorecard"]
    D --> E["Phase 5: Supplier Development and Continuous Improvement"]
    E --> F["Quarterly Business Review and Governance"]
    F --> D
    F --> G["Contract Renewal or Exit Decision"]
    G --> A
```

