## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the term "Service Level Agreement (SLA)" and list any four of its key components.**

**Model Answer:**

An **SLA** is a formal, contractual document between a service provider and a customer (or principal firm) that explicitly defines the **scope, quality, responsibilities, and measurable performance targets** of the service to be delivered.

Four key components of an SLA are:
1. **Service Description** — the nature and scope of the service.
2. **Service Level Targets (SLTs)** — measurable KPIs such as uptime, response time.
3. **Penalty / Service Credit Mechanism** — financial consequences for breach.
4. **Roles and Responsibilities (RACI)** — who does what.
*(Acceptable alternatives: Governance/Escalation procedure, Reporting cadence, Exit clause, Information security addendum.)*

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Key:** A complete answer mentions **"measurable"** and **"consequences for breach"** — these two phrases alone can fetch 2 of the 3 marks.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain briefly the "Kraljic Matrix" as applied to service suppliers. Name its four quadrants.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Kraljic Matrix** is a strategic procurement tool that classifies suppliers based on two dimensions: **Supply Risk (scarcity of suppliers)** and **Profit Impact (spend/value)**. When adapted to service suppliers, it helps a firm decide *how* to manage each type of vendor.

The four quadrants are:
1. **Strategic** — high impact, high scarcity → long-term partnership.
2. **Leverage** — high impact, low scarcity → competitive bidding.
3. **Bottleneck** — low impact, high scarcity → secure continuity.
4. **Non-critical** — low impact, low scarcity → standardize and automate.

---

### 📝 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### ✍️ Question A (14 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Discuss the **five-phase Strategic Management Framework for Service Suppliers** with a suitable real-world example from the IT / BPO / Healthcare / Hospitality industry. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An outsourced **logistics service vendor** charges a fixed monthly fee of **₹20,00,000**. The SLA defines two weighted KPIs:
- **On-Time Delivery (OTD):** weight 0.6, target ≥ 95%, actual = 90%. Penalty: 2% of monthly fee for every 1% shortfall, capped at 40% of monthly fee.
- **Damage-Free Delivery (DFD):** weight 0.4, target ≥ 98%, actual = 96%. Penalty: 1.5% of monthly fee for every 1% shortfall, capped at 30% of monthly fee.

Compute the total **Service Credit** the vendor must pay. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

The **five-phase Strategic Management Framework (SMF)** for service suppliers is as follows:

**Phase 1 — Strategic Sourcing & Make-or-Buy Decision:**
The firm first decides *whether* to insource or outsource. For example, a Kochi-based hospital may decide to **outsource its diagnostic lab services** to a third-party NABL-accredited lab (e.g., Metropolis) because in-house capability is limited and patient demand is variable.

**Phase 2 — Supplier Selection & Segmentation:**
The hospital uses the Kraljic Matrix. Diagnostic services are **high impact** (affect patient outcome) and there are only a few NABL-accredited labs, so this falls into the **Strategic Quadrant**. The hospital chooses Metropolis based on competence, accreditation, and cultural fit.

**Phase 3 — Contracting & SLA Design:**
A detailed **SLA** is signed specifying TAT (Turn-Around Time) of 6 hours for routine tests, daily report delivery, 99% accuracy, and a **service credit mechanism** for breaches. A RACI matrix is also defined.

**Phase 4 — Performance Monitoring & Governance:**
A monthly **Supplier Performance Scorecard (SPS)** tracks TAT, accuracy, complaint rate, and patient feedback (NPS). Weekly operational reviews and quarterly QBRs are held.

**Phase 5 — Supplier Development & Continuous Improvement:**
The hospital and Metropolis co-invest in a **digital report-delivery platform**, joint training of phlebotomists, and Kaizen workshops to reduce TAT further. Over time, Metropolis becomes a **strategic partner** rather than a vendor.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Valuation Note (7-mark question):** All five phases must be named and briefly explained. Using a *single consistent example* across all five phases (rather than 5 disjoint examples) fetches higher marks.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given Data:**
Monthly Fee $M$ = ₹ 20,00,000
OTD: target = 95%, actual = 90%, weight 0.6
DFD: target = 98%, actual = 96%, weight 0.4

**Step 1 — Compute OTD Shortfall**
$$\Delta_{\text{OTD}} = 95 - 90 = 5\%$$

Service Credit for OTD:
$$\text{SC}_{\text{OTD}} = 5 \times 2\% \times M = 10\% \times \text{₹ }20{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ }2{,}00{,}000$$

Cap check: 40% of $M$ = ₹ 8,00,000 → ₹ 2,00,000 is within cap ✅

**Step 2 — Compute DFD Shortfall**
$$\Delta_{\text{DFD}} = 98 - 96 = 2\%$$

Service Credit for DFD:
$$\text{SC}_{\text{DFD}} = 2 \times 1.5\% \times M = 3\% \times \text{₹ }20{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ }60{,}000$$

Cap check: 30% of $M$ = ₹ 6,00,000 → ₹ 60,000 is within cap ✅

**Step 3 — Total Service Credit**
$$\text{SC}_{\text{total}} = \text{SC}_{\text{OTD}} + \text{SC}_{\text{DFD}}$$

$$\text{SC}_{\text{total}} = \text{₹ }2{,}00{,}000 + \text{₹ }60{,}000 = \text{₹ }2{,}60{,}000$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The vendor must pay **₹ 2,60,000** (i.e., **13%** of the monthly fee) as service credits. This amount is typically deducted from the next month's invoice.

---

### ✍️ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Differentiate between **Outsourcing**, **Strategic Alliance**, and **Co-Creation** as strategies for managing service suppliers. Use a comparative analytical matrix and a relevant industry example for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A B-school evaluates its outsourced **campus IT services vendor** using three KPIs with weights as given. Compute the **Supplier Performance Index (SPI)** and recommend whether the vendor qualifies for the **"Preferred Partner"** band (SPI ≥ 0.80).

| KPI | Weight | Raw Score (0–1) |
|-----|--------|-----------------|
| Network Uptime | 0.4 | 0.88 |
| Help-desk FCR | 0.4 | 0.75 |
| User NPS (normalized) | 0.2 | 0.82 |

**(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) of Question B

A clear comparative matrix is presented below (also covered in Section 3 — Analytical Matrix). Explanation follows.

| Strategy | Duration | Risk Allocation | Innovation | Best For |
|----------|----------|-----------------|------------|----------|
| Outsourcing | Short–medium | Transferred to vendor | Low | Non-core commoditized services |
| Strategic Alliance | Medium–long | Shared | Moderate | Important but non-core services |
| Co-Creation | Long-term | Mutually absorbed | High | Core differentiating services |

**Industry Examples:**
- **Outsourcing** — A Kerala bank outsources its ATM cash-replenishment logistics to a third-party cash management firm.
- **Strategic Alliance** — Infosys and a UK bank sign a 5-year Master Services Agreement for IT modernization with shared KPIs.
- **Co-Creation** — Apple and TSMC co-create custom silicon chips; the relationship is so deep that TSMC is a strategic co-creator of Apple's product roadmap.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Key:** Students often lose marks by giving *only definitions*. Examiners explicitly reward **industry linkage** and **comparative analysis** in such 7-mark questions.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) of Question B

**Step 1 — Verify Weights Sum to 1**
$$0.4 + 0.4 + 0.2 = 1.0 \;\; ✅$$

**Step 2 — Apply the SPI Formula**
$$\text{SPI} = (0.4 \times 0.88) + (0.4 \times 0.75) + (0.2 \times 0.82)$$

$$\text{SPI} = 0.3520 + 0.3000 + 0.1640$$

$$\text{SPI} = 0.8160$$

**Step 3 — Compare with "Preferred Partner" Threshold**
$$\text{SPI} = 0.816 \ge 0.80 \;\; ✅$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Recommendation:** The vendor **qualifies for the "Preferred Partner" band**, but only marginally. The B-school should issue a **Performance Improvement Plan (PIP)** focused on improving **Help-desk FCR** (the weakest KPI at 0.75) before contract renewal.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "vendor" and "supplier"** — In service context, the terms are interchangeable, but in KTU answers, students must use **"service supplier"** consistently.
> 2. **Forgetting the cap** in Service Credit calculation — failing to check the maximum cap clause leads to **−2 mark deduction** even when the percentage is correct.
> 3. **Listing KPIs without units** — always write **"MTRS = 4 hours"** rather than just **"MTRS = 4"**.
> 4. **Not verifying weight sum = 1** in SPI problems — the examiner specifically tests this.
> 5. **Mixing up Kraljic quadrants** — students often swap "Strategic" and "Leverage"; memorize the *spend-vs-scarcity* mapping.
> 6. **Omitting the regulatory layer** in service-supply-chain diagrams — this is a guaranteed 1-mark loss in diagram-based questions.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Service supplier management** is a **strategic capability**, not a transactional procurement activity.
- ✅ The **five-phase SMF**: Sourcing → Selection → Contracting → Monitoring → Development.
- ✅ The **Kraljic Matrix** has four quadrants: **Strategic, Leverage, Bottleneck, Non-critical**.
- ✅ An **SLA** must have measurable KPIs, **service credit mechanism**, RACI, governance, and exit clauses.
- ✅ **SLA Compliance %**, **MTRS**, **FCR**, **SPI**, **NPS**, and **TCO** are the most frequently tested metrics.
- ✅ **SPI** is a **weighted composite score**; weights **must sum to 1**.
- ✅ **Service Credit** is the financial penalty for SLA breach, subject to **capped percentages**.
- ✅ **Multi-tier service supply chains** have Tier-1 vendors and Tier-2 sub-suppliers; risk grows with depth.
- ✅ **Governance cadence**: daily dashboards → weekly reviews → monthly ops review → **quarterly QBR**.
- ✅ **Strategic alliances** differ from **outsourcing** in that risk, innovation, and reward are **shared**.
- ✅ **Co-creation** is the highest form of supplier engagement — used only for **core differentiating services**.
- ✅ Always **link theory to a real industry** in exam answers (banking, healthcare, IT, hospitality) for full marks.
- ✅ In diagrams, always show the **principal**, **Tier-1**, **Tier-2**, **customer**, and **regulatory** layers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vehicle Routing Problem: Managing After Sales Service
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT861 - SERVICE SCIENCE AND SERVICE OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Vehicle Routing Problem: Managing After Sales Service

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP)** is a combinatorial optimization and integer programming problem that seeks to determine the **optimal set of routes** for a fleet of vehicles starting and ending at one or more depots, in order to serve a set of geographically dispersed customers, while minimizing the total transportation cost (distance, time, or fuel) subject to a set of operational constraints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** VRP, in the context of service operations, is a classical logistics problem where the goal is to plan the most efficient delivery or service routes, ensuring that all customer demands are met while respecting vehicle capacity, time windows, and service-level agreements (SLAs).

When applied to **After-Sales Service Management**, the VRP is extended to address the routing and scheduling of **field service engineers (FSEs)**, **technicians**, and **service vans** carrying spare parts and tools to customer locations for installation, repair, maintenance, or warranty service.

The **After-Sales Service Vehicle Routing Problem (AS-VRP)** is formally defined as a network optimization problem on a complete graph $G = (V, A)$, where:
- $V = \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, n\}$ is the set of vertices, with vertex 0 representing the **service depot** and the remaining vertices representing **customer service requests**.
- $A = \{(i, j) \mid i, j \in V, i \neq j\}$ is the set of arcs (directed edges) connecting the vertices.
- Each arc $(i,j)$ has an associated cost $c_{ij}$ (travel time or distance).
- Each customer $i$ has a demand $q_i$ (representing the service time, skill requirement, or parts to be delivered).
- Each vehicle has a capacity $Q$ and an operational time window.

The **objective function** is:

$$\min \sum_{i \in V} \sum_{j \in V} c_{ij} x_{ij}$$

where $x_{ij}$ is a binary decision variable equal to 1 if a vehicle traverses arc $(i,j)$, and 0 otherwise.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Pizza Delivery Problem on Steroids":** Imagine you run a chain of pizza outlets. You have 5 delivery boys, 30 hungry customers, and 2 hours before the pizza goes cold. You must decide *which boy delivers to which customers, in what order*, so that no customer is missed, no boy is overloaded, and the total time is minimized. Now, replace pizzas with **broken air conditioners**, delivery boys with **AC technicians**, and pizzas with **spare parts** — that is essentially the Vehicle Routing Problem applied to After-Sales Service.

In a real-world scenario, consider a company like **LG India** or **Bosch** that has thousands of service requests daily across a city. Each request requires a specific **type of technician** (e.g., refrigerator vs. washing machine specialist), a specific set of **spare parts**, and must be served within a customer-promised **time window** (e.g., 9 AM – 12 PM). The dispatch manager must decide:
- **Which technician** to assign to which customer?
- **In what order** should the technician visit the customers?
- **What spare parts** should be pre-loaded in the service van?
- **How to minimize** travel distance, fuel cost, and SLA violations?

This is the **Vehicle Routing Problem for After-Sales Service** — a hybrid of combinatorial optimization, workforce management, and service-level engineering.

## 1.3 Key Terminology and Constants

| Term | Definition | Unit |
|------|-----------|------|
| **Depot (0)** | Central location where vehicles start/end | — |
| **Demand ($q_i$)** | Service requirement of customer $i$ (time/parts) | minutes/units |
| **Capacity ($Q$)** | Maximum load a vehicle can carry | kg or service-hours |
| **Service Time ($s_i$)** | Time spent at customer $i$ | minutes |
| **Time Window ($[a_i, b_i]$)** | Allowed service interval for customer $i$ | clock-time |
| **SLA** | Service Level Agreement — contractual commitment | %/hours |
| **MTTR** | Mean Time To Repair | hours |
| **KPI** | Key Performance Indicator (e.g., First-Time-Fix-Rate) | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bolded standard metric:** In KTU 2024 scheme problems, the most common objective is to minimize **Total Travel Distance (km)** or **Total Operational Cost (₹)**, subject to **Capacity Constraint** and **Time Window Constraint**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Formulation of the Standard VRP

The standard Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem (CVRP) is formulated as follows:

### Decision Variables
- $x_{ijk} \in \{0, 1\}$ — equals 1 if vehicle $k$ travels directly from node $i$ to node $j$, and 0 otherwise.
- $y_{ik} \in \{0, 1\}$ — equals 1 if customer $i$ is assigned to vehicle $k$, and 0 otherwise.
- $u_i$ — auxiliary variable for subtour elimination (Miller–Tucker–Zemlin constraints).

### Objective Function

$$\min Z = \sum_{k \in K} \sum_{i \in V} \sum_{j \in V} c_{ij} \, x_{ijk}$$

This minimizes the total routing cost across all vehicles $k \in K$ in the fleet.

### Constraints

**1. Each customer is visited exactly once:**

$$\sum_{k \in K} \sum_{j \in V} x_{ijk} = 1 \quad \forall i \in V \setminus \{0\}$$

**2. Flow conservation at each customer node:**

$$\sum_{j \in V} x_{ijk} = \sum_{j \in V} x_{jik} = y_{ik} \quad \forall i \in V \setminus \{0\}, \; \forall k \in K$$

**3. Vehicle capacity constraint:**

$$\sum_{i \in V \setminus \{0\}} q_i \, y_{ik} \leq Q \quad \forall k \in K$$

**4. Every vehicle departs from and returns to the depot:**

$$\sum_{j \in V \setminus \{0\}} x_{0jk} = 1 \quad \text{and} \quad \sum_{i \in V \setminus \{0\}} x_{i0k} = 1 \quad \forall k \in K$$

**5. Subtour elimination (MTZ formulation):**

$$u_i - u_j + n \cdot x_{ijk} \leq n - 1 \quad \forall i, j \in V \setminus \{0\}, \; i \neq j, \; \forall k \in K$$

$$2 \leq u_i \leq n \quad \forall i \in V \setminus \{0\}$$

## 2.2 The Clarke–Wright Savings Algorithm

This is the most widely taught **heuristic method** for solving the VRP in KTU examinations. It works by merging routes intelligently.

### Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Initialization:** Initially, assign one vehicle to each customer. Each vehicle travels from depot (0) to customer $i$ and back: route $0 \to i \to 0$.
2. **Compute savings** for every pair of customers $(i, j)$:

$$s_{ij} = d_{0i} + d_{0j} - d_{ij}$$

3. **Sort savings** $s_{ij}$ in descending order.
4. **Merge routes:** Iterate through sorted savings. If merging route containing $i$ and route containing $j$ does not violate capacity or other constraints, merge them. The merge is valid only if both $i$ and $j$ are *end customers* (not interior) of their respective routes.
5. **Stop** when no more feasible merges are possible.

### Why it Works (The Intuition)
- $d_{0i} + d_{0j}$ = cost of two separate round-trips from depot to $i$ and depot to $j$.
- $d_{ij}$ = cost of going directly from $i$ to $j$.
- The **saving** $s_{ij}$ represents the distance *saved* by combining both customers on a single trip instead of two separate trips.

## 2.3 VRP Variants Relevant to After-Sales Service

| Variant | Full Name | After-Sales Application |
|---------|-----------|-------------------------|
| **CVRP** | Capacitated VRP | Van carries limited spare parts inventory |
| **VRPTW** | VRP with Time Windows | Customer requests service in a specific time slot |
| **VRPPD** | VRP with Pickup and Delivery | Old part picked up, new part delivered |
| **VRPSDP** | VRP with Split Deliveries | Multiple technicians may co-serve a complex job |
| **DVRP** | Dynamic VRP | New requests arrive in real-time (live dispatch) |
| **VRPB** | VRP with Backhauls | Service vehicle returns with faulty parts |
| **MVRP** | Multi-Depot VRP | Service hubs in multiple cities |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is **critical for KTU 2024 ESE preparation**. Memorize the units and constraints carefully.

| Formula | Description | Variables/Units |
|---------|-------------|-----------------|
| $Z = \sum_{i} \sum_{j} c_{ij} x_{ij}$ | Total routing cost objective | $c_{ij}$ in ₹ or km, dimensionless $x_{ij}$ |
| $s_{ij} = d_{0i} + d_{0j} - d_{ij}$ | Clarke–Wright savings value | distance (km) |
| $\sum_i q_i \cdot y_{ik} \leq Q$ | Vehicle load capacity | $q_i$ in kg or service-hr, $Q$ in kg or service-hr |
| $a_i \leq t_i \leq b_i$ | Time window constraint | $t_i$ in hours, $[a_i, b_i]$ in clock-time |
| $t_j = t_i + s_i + c_{ij}$ | Arrival time propagation | hours |
| $\text{SLA} = 1 - \frac{\text{Late Arrivals}}{\text{Total Jobs}}$ | Service Level Agreement metric | dimensionless ratio (0 to 1) |
| $\text{MTTR} = \frac{\sum \text{Repair Time}}{\text{No. of Repairs}}$ | Mean Time To Repair | hours |
| $\text{FTFR} = \frac{\text{Fixed on First Visit}}{\text{Total Visits}}$ | First-Time-Fix Rate | dimensionless ratio |
| $\text{Cost}_{\text{fuel}} = D \times \rho \times p$ | Fuel cost for route of distance $D$ | ₹ (INR) |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Service Engineering

The VRP for After-Sales Service is used by:
- **Consumer durables** (LG, Samsung, Whirlpool) — appliance repair scheduling.
- **Automotive OEMs** (Maruti, Tata Motors) — warranty service and roadside assistance.
- **IT & Telecom** (Jio, Airtel, Cisco) — on-site installation and maintenance.
- **Medical equipment** (GE Healthcare, Siemens) — hospital equipment uptime.
- **HVAC companies** (Daikin, Voltas) — installation and AMC visits.

A well-optimized VRP can **reduce fuel cost by 15–25%**, **increase SLA compliance by 20%**, and **improve technician utilization by 30%** (industry benchmark figures).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Clarke–Wright Savings Algorithm (Full KTU-Style Problem)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A service depot (node 0) has 4 customer service requests. Distances from depot and between customers are given below. Each service van has a **capacity of 10 units**. Find the optimal set of routes using the **Clarke–Wright Savings Algorithm**.
>
> **Distance Matrix (in km):**
> | From\To | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | 0 | 0 | 4 | 6 | 9 | 5 |
> | 1 | 4 | 0 | 7 | 8 | 6 |
> | 2 | 6 | 7 | 0 | 5 | 4 |
> | 3 | 9 | 8 | 5 | 0 | 7 |
> | 4 | 5 | 6 | 4 | 7 | 0 |
>
> **Customer Demands (in service units):** $q_1 = 3, \; q_2 = 4, \; q_3 = 5, \; q_4 = 2$. All vehicles have capacity $Q = 10$.

### Step 1: Initial Solution (One Vehicle per Customer)

Initially, every customer is served by a separate vehicle making a round trip from the depot:

| Route | Sequence | Total Distance (km) |
|-------|----------|---------------------|
| 1 | 0 → 1 → 0 | $4 + 4 = 8$ |
| 2 | 0 → 2 → 0 | $6 + 6 = 12$ |
| 3 | 0 → 3 → 0 | $9 + 9 = 18$ |
| 4 | 0 → 4 → 0 | $5 + 5 = 10$ |

**Total Initial Distance:** $Z_0 = 8 + 12 + 18 + 10 = 48$ km

### Step 2: Compute Savings $s_{ij}$ for All Customer Pairs

Using the formula $s_{ij} = d_{0i} + d_{0j} - d_{ij}$:

$$s_{12} = d_{01} + d_{02} - d_{12} = 4 + 6 - 7 = 3 \text{ km}$$

$$s_{13} = d_{01} + d_{03} - d_{13} = 4 + 9 - 8 = 5 \text{ km}$$

$$s_{14} = d_{01} + d_{04} - d_{14} = 4 + 5 - 6 = 3 \text{ km}$$

$$s_{23} = d_{02} + d_{03} - d_{23} = 6 + 9 - 5 = 10 \text{ km}$$

$$s_{24} = d_{02} + d_{04} - d_{24} = 6 + 5 - 4 = 7 \text{ km}$$

$$s_{34} = d_{03} + d_{04} - d_{34} = 9 + 5 - 7 = 7 \text{ km}$$

### Step 3: Sort Savings in Descending Order

| Rank | Pair $(i,j)$ | Saving $s_{ij}$ (km) |
|------|--------------|-----------------------|
| 1 | (2, 3) | 10 |
| 2 | (2, 4) | 7 |
| 3 | (3, 4) | 7 |
| 4 | (1, 3) | 5 |
| 5 | (1, 2) | 3 |
| 6 | (1, 4) | 3 |

### Step 4: Iterative Route Merging

**Iteration 1 — Merge (2, 3) [saving = 10 km]:**
- Check: Both 2 and 3 are end customers of their own routes (each is in a separate route). ✓
- Check Capacity: $q_2 + q_3 = 4 + 5 = 9 \leq 10$. ✓
- New Route: $0 \to 2 \to 3 \to 0$. Distance = $6 + 5 + 9 = 20$ km.
- **Status:** Routes = {0→2→3→0}, {0→1→0}, {0→4→0}.

**Iteration 2 — Merge (2, 4) [saving = 7 km]:**
- Customer 2 is **interior** of route $0 \to 2 \to 3 \to 0$ (not an endpoint). Merge **not allowed** by Clarke–Wright rule. ✗
- Skip.

**Iteration 3 — Merge (3, 4) [saving = 7 km]:**
- Customer 3 is an **endpoint** of route $0 \to 2 \to 3 \to 0$. Customer 4 is endpoint of own route. ✓
- Check Capacity: $q_2 + q_3 + q_4 = 4 + 5 + 2 = 11 > 10$. **Capacity violated.** ✗
- Skip.

**Iteration 4 — Merge (1, 3) [saving = 5 km]:**
- Customer 1 is an endpoint of its route. Customer 3 is an endpoint of route $0 \to 2 \to 3 \to 0$. ✓
- Check Capacity: $q_1 + q_2 + q_3 = 3 + 4 + 5 = 12 > 10$. **Capacity violated.** ✗
- Skip.

**Iteration 5 — Merge (1, 2) [saving = 3 km]:**
- Customer 1 is an endpoint. Customer 2 is **interior** of route $0 \to 2 \to 3 \to 0$. ✗
- Skip.

**Iteration 6 — Merge (1, 4) [saving = 3 km]:**
- Both 1 and 4 are endpoints of separate routes. ✓
- Check Capacity: $q_1 + q_4 = 3 + 2 = 5 \leq 10$. ✓
- New Route: $0 \to 1 \to 4 \to 0$. Distance = $4 + 6 + 5 = 15$ km.
- **Status:** Routes = {0→2→3→0}, {0→1→4→0}.

### Step 5: Final Solution

| Route | Sequence | Customers | Total Load | Distance (km) |
|-------|----------|-----------|-----------|----------------|
| 1 | 0 → 2 → 3 → 0 | 2, 3 | 9 | 20 |
| 2 | 0 → 1 → 4 → 0 | 1, 4 | 5 | 15 |

**Total Optimized Distance:** $Z^* = 20 + 15 = 35$ km

**Distance Saved vs. Initial:** $48 - 35 = 13$ km (**27.08% reduction**). ✓

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Mathematical Formulation Setup (For KTU 14-Mark Questions)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** A depot serves 3 customers. There is 1 service van with capacity $Q = 15$ units. Demands: $q_1 = 6, q_2 = 5, q_3 = 4$. Distance matrix is symmetric with $d_{0i} = 5, 7, 4$ for $i = 1, 2, 3$ and $d_{12} = 6, d_{13} = 5, d_{23} = 8$.
>
> **Formulate the VRP as a 0-1 Integer Linear Program.**

### Step-by-Step Formulation

**Step 1 — Define Sets and Parameters:**

- Set of customers: $V = \{1, 2, 3\}$
- Set of nodes including depot: $V_0 = \{0, 1, 2, 3\}$
- Distance parameter: $c_{ij} = d_{ij}$
- Demand: $q_1 = 6, q_2 = 5, q_3 = 4$
- Capacity: $Q = 15$

**Step 2 — Define Decision Variable:**

$$x_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if arc } (i,j) \text{ is used} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 3 — Write Objective Function:**

$$\min Z = c_{01}x_{01} + c_{02}x_{02} + c_{03}x_{03} + c_{10}x_{10} + c_{12}x_{12} + c_{13}x_{13} + c_{20}x_{20} + c_{21}x_{21} + c_{23}x_{23} + c_{30}x_{30} + c_{31}x_{31} + c_{32}x_{32}$$

Substituting values:

$$\min Z = 5x_{01} + 7x_{02} + 4x_{03} + 5x_{10} + 6x_{12} + 5x_{13} + 7x_{20} + 6x_{21} + 8x_{23} + 4x_{30} + 5x_{31} + 8x_{32}$$

**Step 4 — Subject to Constraints:**

**(a) Each customer has exactly one incoming arc:**

$$x_{01} + x_{21} + x_{31} = 1 \quad \text{(for customer 1)}$$

$$x_{02} + x_{12} + x_{32} = 1 \quad \text{(for customer 2)}$$

$$x_{03} + x_{13} + x_{23} = 1 \quad \text{(for customer 3)}$$

**(b) Each customer has exactly one outgoing arc:**

$$x_{10} + x_{12} + x_{13} = 1 \quad \text{(for customer 1)}$$

$$x_{20} + x_{21} + x_{23} = 1 \quad \text{(for customer 2)}$$

$$x_{30} + x_{31} + x_{32} = 1 \quad \text{(for customer 3)}$$

**(c) Capacity Constraint (Subtour Loading):**

$$6(x_{10} + x_{12} + x_{13}) + 5(x_{20} + x_{21} + x_{23}) + 4(x_{30} + x_{31} + x_{32}) \leq 15$$

**(d) Depot balance:**

$$x_{01} + x_{02} + x_{03} = 1 \quad \text{(one vehicle leaves depot)}$$

$$x_{10} + x_{20} + x_{30} = 1 \quad \text{(one vehicle returns to depot)}$$

**(e) Subtour elimination via MTZ auxiliary variable $u_i$:**

$$u_1 \geq 1, \quad u_1 \leq 15 - 6 = 9, \quad \text{etc.}$$

$$u_i - u_j + 3 \cdot x_{ij} \leq 2 \quad \forall i \neq j, \; i, j \in \{1, 2, 3\}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing VRP Solution Methods

| Method | Type | Optimality | Complexity | Best Use Case |
|--------|------|-----------|-----------|----------------|
| **Clarke–Wright** | Heuristic | Approximate | $O(n^2 \log n)$ | Quick, large-scale, classroom problems |
| **Nearest Neighbor** | Heuristic | Approximate | $O(n^2)$ | Very fast, low-quality baseline |
| **Sweep Algorithm** | Heuristic | Approximate | $O(n \log n)$ | Geometric/clustered customers |
| **Branch and Bound** | Exact | Optimal | Exponential (worst) | Small instances ($n \leq 25$) |
| **Set Partitioning** | Exact/Heuristic | Near-optimal | High | Large fleets with structured routes |
| **Tabu Search** | Metaheuristic | Near-optimal | $O(n^2)$ | Real-world after-sales service |
| **Genetic Algorithm** | Metaheuristic | Near-optimal | $O(n \cdot g \cdot p)$ | Dynamic/dynamic VRP with time windows |
| **Simulated Annealing** | Metaheuristic | Near-optimal | $O(n \cdot T)$ | Noisy/discrete search spaces |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — After-Sales Service VRP System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[Customer Service Request] --> nodeB[Request Ingestion Module]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Service Request Database]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Demand & Skill Classifier]
    nodeD --> nodeE[VRP Optimization Engine]
    nodeF[Technician Database] --> nodeE
    nodeG[Spare Parts Inventory] --> nodeE
    nodeH[Traffic and Distance Matrix] --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeI[Route Planning Module]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Technician Assignment Dispatcher]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Field Service Engineer]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Customer Site Visit]
    nodeL --> nodeM[Service Completion and Feedback]
    nodeM --> nodeN[SLA Performance Dashboard]
    nodeN --> nodeO[Continuous Improvement Loop]
    nodeO --> nodeE
```

## 4.2 Clarke–Wright Algorithm — Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start: Read Inputs] --> nodeB[Initialize One Route per Customer]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Compute Savings s_ij for All Pairs]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Sort Savings in Descending Order]
    nodeD --> nodeE{Pick Highest Saving Pair i,j}
    nodeE --> nodeF{Are i and j Endpoints?}
    nodeF -->|No| nodeI[Skip This Pair]
    nodeF -->|Yes| nodeG{Capacity Constraint Satisfied?}
    nodeG -->|No| nodeI
    nodeG -->|Yes| nodeH[Merge Routes i and j]
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ{More Pairs to Check?}
    nodeJ -->|Yes| nodeE
    nodeJ -->|No| nodeK[Output Final Routes]
    nodeK --> nodeL[End]
```

## 4.3 VRP with Time Windows — Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Service Request with Time Window a_i to b_i] --> nodeB{Compute Arrival Time t_i}
    nodeB --> nodeC{t_i less than a_i?}
    nodeC -->|Yes| nodeD[Wait at Customer Site]
    nodeC -->|No| nodeE{t_i greater than b_i?}
    nodeE -->|Yes| nodeF[SLA Violation - Replan Route]
    nodeE -->|No| nodeG[Begin Service]
    nodeD --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeH[Re-optimize using Metaheuristic]
    nodeG --> nodeI[Service Complete at Time t_i + s_i]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Move to Next Customer]
```

## 4.4 After-Sales Service Routing — Component Interaction Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph InputLayer[Input Layer]
        nodeA1[Customer Requests]
        nodeA2[Technician Skills]
        nodeA3[Parts Inventory]
        nodeA4[Geographic Data]
    end
    subgraph ProcessingLayer[VRP Processing Layer]
        nodeB1[Request Clustering]
        nodeB2[Skill Matching]
        nodeB3[Route Optimization]
        nodeB4[Schedule Builder]
    end
    subgraph OutputLayer[Output Layer]
        nodeC1[Daily Route Sheets]
        nodeC2[Parts Picking List]
        nodeC3[Customer Notification SMS]
        nodeC4[Technician Mobile App Update]
    end
    InputLayer --> ProcessingLayer
    ProcessingLayer --> OutputLayer
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define the **Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP)**. List any **two real-world after-sales service scenarios** where VRP is applied.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** The Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP) is a combinatorial optimization problem that determines the optimal set of routes for a fleet of vehicles starting from a central depot to serve a set of geographically dispersed customers, with the objective of minimizing total transportation cost (distance, time, or fuel) while satisfying constraints such as vehicle capacity, time windows, and service-level requirements.

**Two real-world after-sales scenarios (1 mark):**
1. **White goods/appliance repair** — LG/Samsung dispatching field engineers for refrigerator or AC repair.
2. **Automobile warranty service** — Maruti Suzuki scheduling service vans for scheduled maintenance and breakdown assistance.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** What is the **Clarke–Wright savings value**? Write its formula and explain its significance in solving VRP.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Formula (2 marks):** The Clarke–Wright savings value for merging two customer routes is given by:

$$s_{ij} = d_{0i} + d_{0j} - d_{ij}$$

where $d_{0i}$ and $d_{0j}$ are the distances from the depot to customers $i$ and $j$, and $d_{ij}$ is the direct distance between $i$ and $j$.

**Significance (1 mark):** The savings value represents the reduction in total travel distance achieved by combining customers $i$ and $j$ on a single route instead of serving them with two separate round trips. Larger savings indicate more beneficial merges, and sorting these in descending order guides the heuristic to build efficient routes.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3 Choice A]**

**(a)** [7 marks] A service depot must serve 4 customers. The distance matrix (in km) and customer demands are given below. Each service van has a capacity of **8 units**. Solve the VRP using the **Clarke–Wright Savings Algorithm** and find the **total route distance**.

| From\To | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|---------|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0       | 0 | 3 | 5 | 8 | 4 |
| 1       | 3 | 0 | 6 | 7 | 5 |
| 2       | 5 | 6 | 0 | 4 | 3 |
| 3       | 8 | 7 | 4 | 0 | 6 |
| 4       | 4 | 5 | 3 | 6 | 0 |

Customer demands: $q_1 = 3, \; q_2 = 4, \; q_3 = 5, \; q_4 = 2$. Capacity $Q = 8$ units.

**(b)** [7 marks] Explain any **three key differences** between the standard **CVRP** and the **VRP with Time Windows (VRPTW)**. Provide a real-world after-sales service example illustrating why time windows are critical in service operations.

#### Solution to (a) — Clarke–Wright Savings Algorithm

**Step 1: Initial Solution (One Route per Customer):**

| Route | Sequence | Distance (km) |
|-------|----------|---------------|
| 1 | 0 → 1 → 0 | $3 + 3 = 6$ |
| 2 | 0 → 2 → 0 | $5 + 5 = 10$ |
| 3 | 0 → 3 → 0 | $8 + 8 = 16$ |
| 4 | 0 → 4 → 0 | $4 + 4 = 8$ |

**Initial Total Distance:** $Z_0 = 6 + 10 + 16 + 8 = 40$ km

**Step 2: Compute Savings $s_{ij} = d_{0i} + d_{0j} - d_{ij}$:**

$$s_{12} = 3 + 5 - 6 = 2 \text{ km}$$

$$s_{13} = 3 + 8 - 7 = 4 \text{ km}$$

$$s_{14} = 3 + 4 - 5 = 2 \text{ km}$$

$$s_{23} = 5 + 8 - 4 = 9 \text{ km}$$

$$s_{24} = 5 + 4 - 3 = 6 \text{ km}$$

$$s_{34} = 8 + 4 - 6 = 6 \text{ km}$$

**Step 3: Sort Savings in Descending Order:**

| Rank | Pair | Saving (km) |
|------|------|-------------|
| 1 | (2, 3) | 9 |
| 2 | (2, 4) | 6 |
| 3 | (3, 4) | 6 |
| 4 | (1, 3) | 4 |
| 5 | (1, 2) | 2 |
| 6 | (1, 4) | 2 |

**Step 4: Iterative Merging:**

**Iteration 1 — (2,3), saving = 9:**
- Both are endpoints of separate routes. ✓
- Capacity: $q_2 + q_3 = 4 + 5 = 9 > 8$. **VIOLATION.** ✗
- Skip.

**Iteration 2 — (2,4), saving = 6:**
- Both endpoints. ✓
- Capacity: $q_2 + q_4 = 4 + 2 = 6 \leq 8$. ✓
- **Merge:** New route $0 \to 2 \to 4 \to 0$. Distance = $5 + 3 + 4 = 12$ km.
- Active routes: {0→2→4→0}, {0→1→0}, {0→3→0}.

**Iteration 3 — (3,4), saving = 6:**
- 3 is endpoint of own route; 4 is endpoint of $0 \to 2 \to 4 \to 0$ (right end). ✓
- Capacity: $q_2 + q_3 + q_4 = 4 + 5 + 2 = 11 > 8$. **VIOLATION.** ✗
- Skip.

**Iteration 4 — (1,3), saving = 4:**
- 1 and 3 are endpoints. ✓
- Capacity: $q_1 + q_3 = 3 + 5 = 8 \leq 8$. ✓
- **Merge:** New route $0 \to 1 \to 3 \to 0$. Distance = $3 + 7 + 8 = 18$ km.
- Active routes: {0→2→4→0}, {0→1→3→0}.

**Iteration 5 — (1,2), saving = 2:**
- 2 is **interior** of route $0 \to 2 \to 4 \to 0$. ✗
- Skip.

**Iteration 6 — (1,4), saving = 2:**
- 1 is endpoint of $0 \to 1 \to 3 \to 0$ (left end); 4 is endpoint of $0 \to 2 \to 4 \to 0$ (right end). ✓
- Capacity: $q_1 + q_2 + q_3 + q_4 = 3 + 4 + 5 + 2 = 14 > 8$. **VIOLATION.** ✗
- Skip.

**Step 5: Final Routes:**

| Route | Sequence | Customers | Total Load | Distance (km) |
|-------|----------|-----------|-----------|----------------|
| 1 | 0 → 2 → 4 → 0 | 2, 4 | 6 | 12 |
| 2 | 0 → 1 → 3 → 0 | 1, 3 | 8 | 18 |

**Total Optimized Distance:** $Z^* = 12 + 18 = 30$ km

**Distance Saved:** $40 - 30 = 10$ km (**25% reduction**).

---

#### Solution to (b) — CVRP vs VRPTW

| S.No. | Aspect | CVRP (Capacitated VRP) | VRPTW (VRP with Time Windows) |
|-------|--------|------------------------|--------------------------------|
| 1 | **Constraint Type** | Only vehicle capacity constraint | Adds time window $[a_i, b_i]$ for each customer |
| 2 | **Objective Complexity** | Minimize total distance/cost | Minimize distance *and* minimize tardiness/waiting |
| 3 | **Solution Method** | Clarke–Wright, sweep algorithms | Requires metaheuristics (Tabu, GA) due to higher complexity |
| 4 | **Real-world fit** | Bulk delivery (e.g., parts transport) | Time-critical after-sales service visits |

**Real-world after-sales example (2 marks):** A customer reports a malfunctioning washing machine and demands service between **10 AM and 12 PM** because they have a doctor appointment at 12:30 PM. The dispatcher must assign this request to a technician who can arrive within this narrow 2-hour window, which is a **VRPTW** scenario. If only CVRP is used, the technician may arrive at 2 PM, breaking the SLA and causing customer dissatisfaction.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3 Choice B]**

**(a)** [7 marks] Formulate the **Capacitated VRP (CVRP)** as a **0-1 Integer Linear Program (ILP)**. Clearly state the decision variables, objective function, and at least **four constraints** using proper mathematical notation. Use the notation: $V$ = set of customers, $V_0$ = set including depot, $K$ = set of vehicles, $Q$ = vehicle capacity, $q_i$ = demand of customer $i$, $c_{ij}$ = cost of travel from $i$ to $j$, $x_{ijk}$ = binary decision variable.

**(b)** [7 marks] With reference to **after-sales service management**, explain the role of the following: **(i)** Service Level Agreement (SLA), **(ii)** First-Time-Fix Rate (FTFR), **(iii)** Mean Time To Repair (MTTR). For each, explain how VRP optimization impacts the metric.

#### Solution to (a) — CVRP Formulation

**Decision Variables (1 mark):**

$$x_{ijk} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if vehicle } k \text{ travels from node } i \text{ to node } j \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} \quad \forall i, j \in V_0, \; \forall k \in K$$

**Objective Function (2 marks):** Minimize total travel cost:

$$\min Z = \sum_{k \in K} \sum_{i \in V_0} \sum_{j \in V_0} c_{ij} \, x_{ijk}$$

**Constraints (4 marks — at least 4 needed):**

1. **Each customer is visited exactly once:**

$$\sum_{k \in K} \sum_{j \in V_0} x_{ijk} = 1 \quad \forall i \in V$$

2. **Flow conservation (what comes in must go out):**

$$\sum_{j \in V_0} x_{ijk} = \sum_{j \in V_0} x_{jik} \quad \forall i \in V_0, \; \forall k \in K$$

3. **Vehicle capacity constraint:**

$$\sum_{i \in V} q_i \sum_{j \in V_0} x_{ijk} \leq Q \quad \forall k \in K$$

4. **Each vehicle starts and ends at depot (at most once):**

$$\sum_{j \in V} x_{0jk} \leq 1 \quad \text{and} \quad \sum_{i \in V} x_{i0k} \leq 1 \quad \forall k \in K$$

5. **Binary constraint:**

$$x_{ijk} \in \{0, 1\} \quad \forall i, j \in V_0, \; \forall k \in K$$

---

#### Solution to (b) — Role of SLA, FTFR, MTTR in After-Sales Service

**(i) Service Level Agreement (SLA) — 2 marks:**

**Definition:** A formal, contractual commitment between the service provider and customer specifying the maximum response time or resolution time. Example: "Technician must reach the customer within 4 hours of complaint registration."

**VRP Impact:** A well-optimized VRP minimizes travel time and assigns the nearest qualified technician, directly improving SLA compliance rate. Industry data shows VRP-based dispatching can improve SLA from 75% to 92%.

**(ii) First-Time-Fix Rate (FTFR) — 2.5 marks:**

**Definition:** The percentage of service visits where the technician successfully resolves the issue in a single visit without requiring a return trip.

$$\text{FTFR} = \frac{\text{Number of issues resolved in first visit}}{\text{Total number of service visits}} \times 100\%$$

**VRP Impact:** VRP systems integrate **parts inventory optimization** — the van is pre-loaded with the right spare parts based on historical failure data. This dramatically increases FTFR, often from 60% to 85%, reducing repeat visits and saving cost.

**(iii) Mean Time To Repair (MTTR) — 2.5 marks:**

**Definition:** The average time taken to repair a faulty unit, measured from the moment the technician arrives to the moment the unit is operational.

$$\text{MTTR} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Repair Time}_i}{n}$$

**VRP Impact:** By clustering nearby service requests and assigning skilled specialists, VRP minimizes both travel time and diagnostic time. The **skill-matching constraint** in modern VRP variants ensures the right technician is dispatched, which is the single biggest factor in reducing MTTR.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mistakes Students Make:**
> 1. **Forgetting to check endpoint status** before merging routes in Clarke–Wright — a 2-mark penalty per wrong merge.
> 2. **Not verifying capacity constraint** after every merge — even if the merge is "endpoint-valid," capacity can still be violated.
> 3. **Omitting binary constraint** $x_{ijk} \in \{0,1\}$ in ILP formulation — full mark deduction for the formulation question.
> 4. **Confusing $d_{ij}$ with $c_{ij}$** — $d$ is *distance*, $c$ is *cost* (which may include time, fuel, toll).
> 5. **Writing MTZ constraints incorrectly** — remember $u_i$ is bounded by $2 \leq u_i \leq n$, and the constraint is $u_i - u_j + n \cdot x_{ij} \leq n-1$.
> 6. **Not stating units** in the final answer (km, hours, ₹) — KTU examiners often deduct 0.5 marks for missing units.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 3, VRP for After-Sales Service:**

- ⭐ **VRP is NP-hard** — exact methods fail for $n > 25$; use heuristics/metaheuristics for real instances.
- ⭐ **Clarke–Wright Savings Formula:** $s_{ij} = d_{0i} + d_{0j} - d_{ij}$ — sorted in descending order.
- ⭐ **Endpoint Rule:** A merge is valid **only if both $i$ and $j$ are endpoints** of their respective routes.
- ⭐ **Capacity Rule:** $\sum q_i \leq Q$ for every merged route.
- ⭐ **CVRP vs VRPTW:** Time windows add $[a_i, b_i]$ constraints and increase complexity dramatically.
- ⭐ **Three Pillars of After-Sales VRP:** (1) **Routing** (which order), (2) **Scheduling** (when), (3) **Assignment** (who).
- ⭐ **Key Service KPIs:** **SLA** (% on-time), **FTFR** (% first-visit fix), **MTTR** (avg repair time).
- ⭐ **Decision Variable:** $x_{ijk} \in \{0,1\}$ — arc-based binary variable.
- ⭐ **Auxiliary Variable:** $u_i$ for **MTZ subtour elimination** in ILP formulation.
- ⭐ **Real-world industry usage:** LG, Samsung, Maruti, Bosch, Jio, Daikin — all use VRP-based dispatching.
- ⭐ **Savings achieved:** Optimized VRP gives **15–30% cost reduction** and **20% SLA improvement**.
- ⭐ **Variants to remember:** **CVRP, VRPTW, DVRP (dynamic), VRPPD (pickup-delivery), VRPB (backhauls), MVRP (multi-depot)**.
- ⭐ **Common algorithms:** Clarke–Wright (savings), Nearest Neighbor, Sweep, Tabu Search, Genetic Algorithm, Simulated Annealing.
- ⭐ **For KTU 14-mark questions:** Always show **(i) initial solution, (ii) savings computation table, (iii) sorted savings, (iv) iterative merge with capacity check, (v) final routes and total distance**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Understanding Services that Involve Transportation of People and Vehicle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT861 - SERVICE SCIENCE AND SERVICE OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Understanding Services that Involve Transportation of People and Vehicle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Transportation Services** constitute a sub-category of services within the service sector that are primarily concerned with the **spatial relocation of people, goods, or vehicles** from one geographical point (origin) to another (destination). According to service operations management literature, these services are classified under **People Processing Services (PPS)** when they involve the physical movement of customers (passengers), and under **Possession Processing Services (PPS-P)** when they involve the movement of physical goods, vehicles, or freight.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, **services involving transportation of people and vehicles** are understood as the integrated set of operational, infrastructural, and managerial activities that facilitate the **movement of human beings (passenger transport) and physical assets (vehicle/freight transport)** through various modes such as road, rail, air, and water.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 3):** The focus of this topic is to help students understand the **service architecture**, **operational characteristics**, **capacity management**, and **quality dimensions** specific to mobility-based services. This forms the foundation for advanced topics like service design, capacity-demand balancing, and service quality management in transportation systems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Understanding

Imagine you walk into a **Kerala State Road Transport Corporation (KSRTC) bus station in Thiruvananthapuram** and ask for a ticket to Kochi. Within a few minutes, you are seated in an air-conditioned bus, and six hours later, you disembark at your destination. What just happened?

- The bus (the **service vehicle**) was scheduled, routed, and dispatched.
- You (the **passenger**, i.e., the "service being processed") were transported physically.
- The fare you paid covered the **operational cost** of moving that bus, including fuel, driver wages, maintenance, and infrastructure.
- The seat you occupied was a **perishable service capacity** — once the bus left, that seat's revenue opportunity was gone forever.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Moving Restaurant" Model:**
> Think of an **airline flight** as a **flying restaurant with wings**. The "food" here is the service of taking you from city A to city B. The "kitchen" is the aircraft. The "chefs" are the pilots and crew. The "customers" must be physically present inside the kitchen to be "served." This is exactly what makes transportation services unique — **production and consumption occur simultaneously** (a property known as *simultaneity* or *inseparability*).

This analogy helps us grasp the four foundational characteristics of transportation services:

| Characteristic | What it Means in Transportation |
|---|---|
| **Intangibility** | You cannot touch or store the "service of being transported" — it is experienced, not possessed. |
| **Inseparability** | The service is produced and consumed at the same instant the vehicle is in motion. |
| **Heterogeneity** | Every trip is different — traffic, weather, passenger behaviour, route conditions. |
| **Perishability** | An empty seat on a departed bus is a lost, unrecoverable revenue opportunity. |

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics Used in Transportation Services

> [!NOTE]
> The following **operational metrics** are the standard units of measurement used globally by airlines, railways, bus operators, and logistics firms. Memorize these — they appear frequently in KTU university examinations.

- **Load Factor (LF)** — measured as a **percentage (%)**
- **On-Time Performance (OTP)** — measured as a **percentage (%)**
- **Revenue per Available Seat Kilometer (RASM/RASK)** — measured in **INR/USD per ASK**
- **Cost per Available Seat Kilometer (CASK)** — measured in **INR/USD per ASK**
- **Passengers per Vehicle Hour (PPVH)** — measured in **passengers/hour**
- **Vehicle Utilization Rate (VUR)** — measured as a **percentage (%)**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Transportation Services

Transportation services can be classified along two primary dimensions — **what is being moved** and **how it is moved**.

### 2.1.1 Classification by Object of Movement

**(A) Passenger Transportation Services (People Processing)**

These services focus on moving **human beings** from origin to destination. The customer is physically present inside the service delivery system.

- **Public Transport:** Buses (KSRTC, BMTC), Metro Rail (Kochi Metro), Suburban Railways, Monorail.
- **Private Transport:** Taxis (Ola, Uber, Rapido), Rental Cars (Zoomcar), Personal Vehicles.
- **Mass Long-Distance:** Airlines (Air India, IndiGo), Inter-city trains (Rajdhani, Shatabdi, Vande Bharat), Inter-state buses.
- **Last-Mile Mobility:** Auto-rickshaws, e-scooters (Bounce, Vogo), bike-sharing (Yulu), walking infrastructure.
- **Premium/Charter:** Private jets, Luxury coaches, Cruise ships.

**(B) Vehicle & Freight Transportation Services (Possession Processing)**

These services focus on moving **goods, vehicles, or other physical assets** on behalf of owners.

- **Surface Freight:** Trucking (TCI, Delhivery), Rail freight (CONCOR), Last-mile delivery (Amazon, Flipkart).
- **Air Cargo:** Dedicated freighters, Belly cargo in passenger aircraft.
- **Water Transport:** Container shipping (Maersk, MSC), Inland waterways, Coastal shipping.
- **Pipeline Transport:** Petroleum, natural gas, water supply networks.
- **Vehicle-Specific Services:** Car-carrier trucks, vehicle transport via railways (Roll-on/Roll-off), Ambulance services, Tow trucks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to differentiate, emphasize that **passenger transport** is a *People Processing Service* where the customer enters the **service factory** (the vehicle), whereas **freight/vehicle transport** is a *Possession Processing Service* where the customer's physical asset enters the service system.

### 2.1.2 Classification by Mode of Transport

The **multi-modal framework** of transport includes:

1. **Road Transport** — Most flexible, door-to-door service, high last-mile reach.
2. **Rail Transport** — High capacity, energy-efficient, suitable for bulk and mass transit.
3. **Air Transport** — Fastest, highest cost, suitable for high-value/time-sensitive cargo and long-distance passenger travel.
4. **Water Transport** — Lowest cost per ton-km, slowest, suited for bulk commodities.
5. **Pipeline Transport** — Continuous flow, restricted to specific commodities (oil, gas).

## 2.2 Core Service Operations Framework for Transportation

The service operations of a transportation system revolve around **five pillars**:

### Pillar 1: Capacity Planning
- Capacity in transportation = **Number of seats (passenger)** or **Tonnage/Volume (freight)** available over a defined time period.
- Capacity is **perishable** (cannot be inventoried) and **stochastic** (subject to demand fluctuations).
- **Peak-hour problem:** Demand during 8–10 AM and 5–8 PM may be 2–3× the off-peak average, yet fleet size is largely fixed.

### Pillar 2: Demand Forecasting
- Uses historical data, seasonality (Onam, Christmas, summer holidays in Kerala), and events (festivals, IPL matches).
- Forecasting accuracy directly affects load factor and revenue.

### Pillar 3: Fleet Management & Scheduling
- **Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP):** Optimal assignment of vehicles to routes.
- **Crew Scheduling:** Pilot/Driver duty roster generation (subject to Flight Time Limitations or Drivers' Hours rules).
- **Maintenance Scheduling:** A-check, B-check, C-check intervals for aircraft; fitness certificates for buses.

### Pillar 4: Service Quality Management
- Quality dimensions specific to transportation (developed from **SERVQUAL** and **SERVPERF** models):
  - **Tangibles:** Cleanliness of vehicle, comfort of seats, appearance of crew.
  - **Reliability:** Adherence to schedule, frequency of service.
  - **Responsiveness:** Speed of ticket booking, complaint resolution.
  - **Assurance:** Safety record, competence of driver/pilot.
  - **Empathy:** Special care for elderly, differently-abled, women, children.
  - **PLUS two transport-specific dimensions:**
    - **Safety:** Accident rate, fatalities per million km.
    - **Accessibility:** Coverage area, ease of reaching the station/stop.

### Pillar 5: Revenue Management & Yield Optimization
- **Dynamic Pricing:** Surge pricing in Ola/Uber during peak hours.
- **Yield Management:** Charging different prices to different customer segments (berth classes in trains, economy vs business in flights).
- **Load Factor Maximization:** Filling empty seats with discounted fares rather than letting them go empty.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these formulas for KTU University Examinations.** The table below uses `\vert` and `\mid` instead of vertical pipe characters `|` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Metric / Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Load Factor (LF)** | $$ LF = \frac{\text{Actual Passengers Transported}}{\text{Available Seat Capacity}} \times 100\% $$ | Measures capacity utilization of a transport service. |
| 2 | **Vehicle Utilization Rate (VUR)** | $$ VUR = \frac{\text{Total Revenue Hours}}{\text{Total Available Hours}} \times 100\% $$ | Fraction of time a vehicle is in productive (revenue) service. |
| 3 | **On-Time Performance (OTP)** | $$ OTP = \frac{\text{Number of On-Time Trips}}{\text{Total Number of Trips}} \times 100\% $$ | Schedule adherence percentage. |
| 4 | **Revenue per Available Seat Kilometer (RASK)** | $$ RASK = \frac{\text{Total Operating Revenue}}{\text{Available Seat Kilometers (ASK)}} $$ | Revenue productivity of the fleet. ASK = Seats × Distance. |
| 5 | **Cost per Available Seat Kilometer (CASK)** | $$ CASK = \frac{\text{Total Operating Cost}}{\text{Available Seat Kilometers (ASK)}} $$ | Cost efficiency of the fleet. |
| 6 | **Operating Margin per ASK** | $$ \text{Profit per ASK} = RASK - CASK $$ | Indicates financial health of the service. |
| 7 | **Passenger Kilometers Flown/Run (RPK/RKM)** | $$ RPK = \text{Passengers} \times \text{Distance Travelled} $$ | Total passenger-distance demand served. |
| 8 | **Revenue per Passenger Kilometer (RPK Rev)** | $$ \text{Rev/RPK} = \frac{\text{Total Revenue}}{RPK} $$ | Average fare per passenger-km. |
| 9 | **Break-Even Load Factor** | $$ LF_{BE} = \frac{CASK - \text{Non-Fare Revenue/ASK}}{RASK} \times 100\% $$ | Minimum LF required to cover all costs. |
| 10 | **Service Productivity** | $$ \text{Productivity} = \frac{\text{Output (Passenger-km)}}{\text{Input (Cost, Labour, Fuel)}} $$ | Operational efficiency of the service. |
| 11 | **Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI)** | $$ CSI = \frac{\sum (\text{Importance}_i \times \text{Satisfaction}_i)}{\sum \text{Importance}_i} $$ | Weighted average of customer ratings across service attributes. |
| 12 | **Fleet Availability** | $$ FA = \frac{\text{Vehicles Available for Service}}{\text{Total Fleet Size}} \times 100\% $$ | Operational readiness of the fleet. |
| 13 | **Average Wait Time (AWT)** | $$ AWT = \frac{\sum (\text{Headway intervals})}{2} \times \text{Service Frequency Factor} $$ | Average time a passenger spends waiting for the next service. |
| 14 | **Turnaround Time (TAT)** | $$ TAT = T_{arrival} - T_{departure} \text{ (at terminal)} $$ | Time a vehicle spends at the terminal between two trips. |
| 15 | **Vehicle Kilometers per Day** | $$ VKD = \sum_{i=1}^{n} D_i \quad \text{where } D_i = \text{distance of trip } i $$ | Total daily mileage of the fleet. |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility and Engineering Relevance

- **Operations Research (OR) algorithms** are deployed in transportation for routing, scheduling, and crew assignment (e.g., the **Travelling Salesman Problem**, **Vehicle Routing Problem**).
- **IoT and Telematics** enable real-time vehicle tracking, predictive maintenance, and dynamic dispatch (used by Uber, Ola, KSRTC-SWIFT).
- **Data Science / Machine Learning** powers demand forecasting, dynamic pricing engines, and ETA prediction (Google Maps, Namma Yatri).
- **Industrial Engineering** principles (queueing theory, Little's Law) are applied at airports, bus stations, and toll plazas to optimize passenger flow.

> **Real-World Utility:** When KSRTC deploys a new Volvo multi-axle service from Kochi to Bangalore, it uses these exact formulas to decide fleet size, frequency, fare, and break-even passenger count.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Load Factor and Revenue Calculations for a KSRTC Bus Route

**Problem Statement:**
A KSRTC **Garuda Plus** bus operates between **Thiruvananthapuram (TVM)** and **Kochi (EKM)**. The bus has **40 seats** and operates **3 round trips per day** (each one-way distance = **220 km**). Ticket price is **₹450 per passenger**.

On a particular day:
- Trip 1 (TVM→EKM): **32 passengers**
- Trip 2 (EKM→TVM): **38 passengers**
- Trip 3 (TVM→EKM): **25 passengers**
- Trip 4 (EKM→TVM): **40 passengers**
- Trip 5 (TVM→EKM): **30 passengers**
- Trip 6 (EKM→TVM): **35 passengers**

**Calculate:**
(a) Daily Load Factor
(b) Total Revenue
(c) Available Seat Kilometers (ASK) for the day
(d) Revenue per ASK (RASK)
(e) Passenger Kilometers (RPK) and check if LF = RPK/ASK.

---

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1: Total Passengers Transported (Day)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Passengers} &= 32 + 38 + 25 + 40 + 30 + 35 \\
&= 200 \text{ passengers}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* Add passenger counts from all 6 one-way trips performed by the bus in a single day.

---

**Step 2: Total Available Seat Capacity (Day)**

Each trip has 40 seats. The bus makes 6 trips per day.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Seat Capacity} &= 40 \times 6 \\
&= 240 \text{ seat-trips}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* Multiply seats per trip by the number of trips to get the day's potential carrying capacity.

---

**Step 3: Daily Load Factor (a)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LF &= \frac{\text{Actual Passengers}}{\text{Available Seats}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{200}{240} \times 100\% \\
&= 83.33\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* Load factor is the ratio of actual utilization to maximum potential. A LF of 83.33% is considered good for long-distance road transport.

---

**Step 4: Daily Revenue (b)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Revenue} &= \text{Total Passengers} \times \text{Fare per Passenger} \\
&= 200 \times 450 \\
&= ₹90{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* Direct multiplication of passengers and unit fare.

---

**Step 5: Available Seat Kilometers — ASK (c)**

ASK = Seats × Distance for each trip, summed over all trips.

$$
\begin{aligned}
ASK &= 40 \times 220 \times 6 \\
&= 8{,}800 \times 6 \\
&= 52{,}800 \text{ seat-km}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* Each of the 40 seats is "available" to travel 220 km on each of 6 trips, so the product gives the total available passenger-distance supply.

---

**Step 6: Revenue per ASK — RASK (d)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RASK &= \frac{\text{Total Revenue}}{ASK} \\
&= \frac{90{,}000}{52{,}800} \\
&= ₹1.7045 \text{ per seat-km}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* RASK measures how much revenue is earned per unit of available capacity, used by airlines and bus operators to compare route profitability.

---

**Step 7: Passenger Kilometers — RPK and Cross-Verification (e)**

RPK = Σ (Passengers in trip × Distance of trip). Distance = 220 km for all trips.

$$
\begin{aligned}
RPK &= (32 + 38 + 25 + 40 + 30 + 35) \times 220 \\
&= 200 \times 220 \\
&= 44{,}000 \text{ passenger-km}
\end{aligned}
$$

Cross-verification using RPK and ASK:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{RPK}{ASK} \times 100\% &= \frac{44{,}000}{52{,}800} \times 100\% \\
&= 83.33\%
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches the Load Factor calculated in Step 3. **Verified ✓**

> *Logic Insight:* This cross-verification is a **KTU examiner favourite** — it tests whether the student understands that Load Factor, RPK/ASK, and average seat occupancy are mathematically equivalent expressions of the same concept.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Break-Even Load Factor for a Regional Airline

**Problem Statement:**
A regional airline operates an **ATR-72 aircraft** with **70 seats** on the **Kochi–Mangalore** sector (one-way distance = **400 km**). The airline operates **2 flights per day** each direction.

- Operating Cost per flight (one-way) = **₹4,20,000**
- Non-fare revenue (cargo, baggage, etc.) per flight = **₹30,000**
- Average ticket price = **₹3,500**

**Find:**
(a) Total Available Seat Kilometers per day.
(b) Total Operating Cost per day.
(c) Break-Even Load Factor.

---

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1: Available Seat Kilometers per Day (a)**

One-way distance = 400 km. Total daily flights (one-way) = 2 (Kochi→Mangalore) + 2 (Mangalore→Kochi) = **4 flights**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
ASK_{\text{day}} &= 70 \times 400 \times 4 \\
&= 112{,}000 \text{ seat-km}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* Total seat-km supplied to the market in one day.

---

**Step 2: Total Operating Cost per Day (b)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost}_{\text{day}} &= 4{,}20{,}000 \times 4 \\
&= ₹16{,}80{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Step 3: Cost per Available Seat Kilometer (CASK)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CASK &= \frac{16{,}80{,}000}{1{,}12{,}000} \\
&= ₹15.00 \text{ per seat-km}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Step 4: Revenue per Available Seat Kilometer (RASK) at 100% LF**

At 100% load, the airline fills all 70 seats, earning 70 × ₹3,500 = ₹2,45,000 per one-way flight.

$$
\begin{aligned}
RASK_{\text{full}} &= \frac{2{,}45{,}000}{70 \times 400} \\
&= \frac{2{,}45{,}000}{28{,}000} \\
&= ₹8.75 \text{ per seat-km}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Step 5: Net Revenue per ASK after Non-Fare Revenue (c)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Non-fare/ASK} &= \frac{30{,}000}{28{,}000} \\
&= ₹1.0714 \text{ per seat-km}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net RASK} &= RASK + \text{Non-fare/ASK} \\
&= 8.75 + 1.0714 \\
&= ₹9.8214 \text{ per seat-km}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Step 6: Break-Even Load Factor**

The Break-Even LF is the LF at which **RASK × LF = CASK**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
LF_{BE} &= \frac{CASK}{\text{Net RASK}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{15.00}{9.8214} \times 100\% \\
&= 152.73\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic Insight:* The break-even LF exceeds 100%! This indicates the route is **structurally unprofitable** at current fare and cost levels. The airline must either:
> (i) Increase ticket price,
> (ii) Reduce operating cost (e.g., use a smaller aircraft, reduce crew, optimize fuel),
> (iii) Increase non-fare revenue (ancillary services),
> (iv) Or discontinue the route.

**Conclusion:** The Kochi–Mangalore sector at the given parameters would not be viable.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing People vs Vehicle Transportation Services

| Dimension | People Transportation (PT) | Vehicle/Freight Transportation (VT) |
|---|---|---|
| **Service Classification** | People Processing | Possession Processing |
| **Customer Presence** | Mandatory (passenger must board) | Not required (goods move autonomously) |
| **Capacity Unit** | Seats / Standing rooms | Cubic meters / Tonnes / TEU |
| **Revenue Driver** | Passenger fares | Freight charges, contracts, subscriptions |
| **Key Performance Indicator** | Load Factor, OTP, NPS | Tonnage-km, Delivery OTP, Damage Rate |
| **Major Cost** | Crew, fuel, station costs | Fuel, toll, packaging, last-mile |
| **Peak Demand** | Rush hours, holidays | Harvest seasons, e-commerce sale days |
| **Primary Risk** | Safety, passenger complaints | Cargo damage, theft, delays |
| **Technology Focus** | Ticketing apps, real-time tracking, AI dispatch | WMS, TMS, IoT, blockchain tracking |
| **Regulation** | Passenger safety, fare control, accessibility | Vehicle permits, weight limits, hazardous goods |
| **Yield Strategy** | Multiple fare classes, surge pricing | Tiered service (express, standard, economy) |
| **Example Companies** | KSRTC, IndiGo, Kochi Metro | Delhivery, TCI, Maersk, DHL |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Transportation Service Ecosystem — High-Level Block Diagram

This Mermaid diagram shows the **interacting components** of a transportation service system, mapping inputs, processes, and outcomes.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Customer Demand] --> B1[Service Booking Channel]
    A2[Fleet Assets] --> B2[Fleet Management]
    A3[Infrastructure Roads Rails Airports Ports] --> B3[Network Operations]
    A4[Human Resources Drivers Pilots Crew] --> B4[Crew Scheduling]
    A5[Fuel Energy Capital] --> B5[Cost Management]
    
    B1 --> C1[Service Operations Control Tower]
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C1
    B5 --> C1
    
    C1 --> D1[Vehicle Dispatch and Routing]
    C1 --> D2[Real-time Tracking and Monitoring]
    C1 --> D3[Pricing and Revenue Management]
    
    D1 --> E1[Service Delivery to Passenger or Vehicle]
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1
    
    E1 --> F1[Quality of Service Measurement]
    E1 --> F2[Billing and Settlement]
    E1 --> F3[Feedback Loop Customer Satisfaction]
    
    F1 --> G1[Continuous Improvement]
    F2 --> G1
    F3 --> G1
    
    G1 --> C1
```

> *Reading the diagram:* Customer demand, fleet, infrastructure, crew, and capital feed into a central **Operations Control Tower**, which orchestrates dispatch, tracking, and pricing. The service is delivered, and quality/billing/feedback loops feed back into continuous improvement.

---

## 4.2 Passenger Transportation Service Process Flow

This Mermaid flowchart illustrates the **end-to-end journey** of a passenger using a transportation service.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S([Start Customer Need to Travel]) --> P1[Search and Compare Options]
    P1 --> P2[Book and Pay]
    P2 --> P3{Booking Confirmed?}
    P3 -->|No| P4[Failure - Try Alternative]
    P3 -->|Yes| P5[Travel to Boarding Point]
    P5 --> P6[Boarding and Security Check]
    P6 --> P7[In-transit Service]
    P7 --> P8[Arrival and Disembarkation]
    P8 --> P9[Last-mile Connectivity]
    P9 --> P10[Post-trip Feedback]
    P10 --> P11[Service Quality Score and NPS]
    P11 --> E([End])
    P4 --> P1
```

> *Reading the diagram:* A linear journey with a **decision point** at booking confirmation and a **feedback loop** at the end that influences future service improvement. The **in-transit service** is the core value-adding stage.

---

## 4.3 Vehicle/Freight Transportation Service Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SA([Start Shipper Order]) --> V1[Order Receipt and Documentation]
    V1 --> V2[Pickup Scheduling]
    V2 --> V3[Loading and Consignment Check]
    V3 --> V4[In-transit Movement Multi-modal]
    V4 --> V5[Tracking and Exception Handling]
    V5 --> V6{Any Exception?}
    V6 -->|Yes| V7[Reroute or Rebook]
    V6 -->|No| V8[Arrival at Hub]
    V7 --> V4
    V8 --> V9[Customs and Clearance if applicable]
    V9 --> V10[Unloading and Delivery]
    V10 --> V11[Proof of Delivery and POD]
    V11 --> V12[Billing and Settlement]
    V12 --> V13[Customer Feedback and CSI]
    V13 --> EA([End])
```

> *Reading the diagram:* The freight process has **more compliance and exception-handling touchpoints** than passenger transport — customs, damage checks, multi-modal transfers. The **Proof of Delivery (POD)** is the freight equivalent of a passenger's arrival confirmation.

---

## 4.4 Capacity-Demand Balancing Model (Block Architecture)

This block diagram models the **dynamic interplay** between supply (capacity) and demand in transportation services.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DC[Demand Curve - Time of Day / Season] --> FB[Forecasting Engine]
    FB --> FC[Forecasted Demand]
    FC --> CB[Capacity Buffer Decision]
    CB --> CI[Capacity Provisioned Seats / Vehicles]
    CI --> OPR[Operations - Run the Service]
    OPR --> ACT[Actual Demand Realized]
    ACT --> LF[Load Factor Calculation]
    LF --> PM[Performance Monitoring]
    PM --> DEC{Performance vs Target?}
    DEC -->|Under| ADJ1[Increase Frequency or Capacity]
    DEC -->|Over| ADJ2[Dynamic Pricing or Demand Shaping]
    DEC -->|Match| ADJ3[Maintain Current Strategy]
    ADJ1 --> FB
    ADJ2 --> FB
    ADJ3 --> FB
```

> *Reading the diagram:* A **closed-loop control system**. Forecasts drive capacity decisions. Operations produce real data. Performance is measured and fed back to adjust either capacity (more buses/trains) or demand (dynamic pricing, incentives).

---
