# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Part A questions are direct, definition-based, and require crisp 3-4 sentence answers. Time allocation: ~6 minutes per question.

---

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PECBT861, Module 3]**

**Differentiate between People Processing Services and Possession Processing Services with reference to transportation of people and vehicles.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | People Processing (Passenger Transport) | Possession Processing (Vehicle/Freight Transport) |
|---|---|---|
| **Object Moved** | The human customer themselves | Goods, vehicles, or physical assets |
| **Customer Presence** | Mandatory — passenger must be in the vehicle | Not required — assets move independently |
| **Output Unit** | Passenger-kilometers (PKM) | Tonne-kilometers (TKM) or vehicle deliveries |
| **Examples** | KSRTC bus, IndiGo flight, Kochi Metro | Delhivery parcel, car-carrier truck, TCI freight |
| **Customer Touchpoint** | Throughout the journey | At booking and delivery only |

> *Key Insight:* In passenger transport, the customer is the **raw material** being processed. In freight transport, the customer's **property** is being processed.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PECBT861, Module 3]**

**Explain the concept of "perishability" in transportation services. Why is an empty airline seat a "lost forever" opportunity?**

**Model Answer:**

Perishability is one of the four core characteristics of services (along with intangibility, heterogeneity, and inseparability) and is **especially prominent in transportation services**.

- It means that **service capacity, once not used, cannot be stored, inventoried, or recovered** for future use.
- An **empty airline seat** on a flight that has already departed represents a **perished service unit** — the revenue potential of that seat is **gone forever**.
- Unlike a manufactured good (say, a T-shirt that can be sold tomorrow if not sold today), a service moment is **time-bound and place-bound**.
- This is why transportation operators use **revenue management and dynamic pricing** — they discount fares as departure approaches to fill otherwise perishable capacity.

> *Example:* The unsold window seat on Tuesday's KSRTC Garuda Plus from Kochi to Bangalore cannot be "sold" on Wednesday — that specific seat on that specific trip is **irretrievably wasted**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Part B questions are 14-mark, long-answer type. They typically have **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**. The student is given an **internal choice** — typically two alternative questions (OR), and the student must answer one. Time allocation: ~25 minutes per question.

---

### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks) [CHOICE-1]**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PECBT861, Module 3]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **five core characteristics of services** as they apply to transportation of people and vehicles. Use specific examples from the Indian transportation industry (KSRTC, IndiGo, Kochi Metro, or a logistics firm of your choice) to illustrate each characteristic.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A private bus operator runs a **Chennai–Bengaluru overnight service**. The bus has **36 sleeper berths**, operates **one round trip per day**, with a one-way distance of **350 km**. The fare is **₹900 per berth**. On a particular day, the bus carried **30 passengers** in the onward journey and **34 passengers** in the return journey. The total operating cost for the day was **₹48,000**.

**Calculate:**
1. The **Load Factor** for the day.
2. The **total revenue** for the day.
3. The **Available Seat Kilometers (ASK)** for the day.
4. The **Cost per ASK (CASK)**.
5. The **break-even Load Factor** (assuming no non-fare revenue).

---

### **Model Answer — Question A**

#### (a) Model Answer [7 Marks]

The four universally accepted characteristics of services, as applicable to transportation, are:

1. **Intangibility [2 Marks]:** A transportation service cannot be seen, touched, or stored. When you buy a KSRTC ticket to Kochi, you cannot physically "hold" the trip. You only experience it during the journey. This intangibility increases perceived risk, which is why operators invest in **tangibles** (clean buses, smart uniforms of staff) to create a sense of value.

2. **Inseparability [2 Marks]:** Production and consumption happen simultaneously. The moment the IndiGo flight pushes back from the gate, the service is being "produced" and "consumed" at the same instant. The pilot is producing the service while the passenger is consuming it. This makes the **service provider (pilot, driver, conductor)** inseparable from the service outcome.

3. **Heterogeneity [1.5 Marks]:** Every trip is unique. Traffic on the Kochi–Trivandrum corridor varies daily. A Kochi Metro ride at 8 AM feels different from one at 2 PM. Weather, passenger mix, driver behaviour, and road conditions make every service moment distinct. Standardization is achieved through **SOPs, training, and technology (autopilot, metro automation)**.

4. **Perishability [1.5 Marks]:** Unsold service capacity is lost forever. A logistics company with an idle 20-tonne truck at 6 AM cannot "sell" those 20 tonnes at 7 AM — the productive hours have perished. This is the most critical characteristic driving **revenue management** in transportation.

> *A fifth characteristic sometimes added is **Customer Participation** — in passenger transport, the customer (passenger) is also a co-producer of the service experience (e.g., a noisy passenger affects other travellers).*

---

#### (b) Model Answer [7 Marks — Step-by-Step]

**Given Data:**
- Sleeper berths per trip = 36
- Round trips per day = 1 (i.e., 2 one-way trips)
- One-way distance = 350 km
- Fare per berth = ₹900
- Onward passengers = 30; Return passengers = 34
- Daily operating cost = ₹48,000

**Step 1: Total Passengers**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Passengers} &= 30 + 34 \\
&= 64 \text{ passengers}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Total Seat Capacity**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Seat Capacity} &= 36 \times 2 \\
&= 72 \text{ berths}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Daily Load Factor (1)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LF &= \frac{64}{72} \times 100\% \\
&= 88.89\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Daily Revenue (2)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Revenue} &= 64 \times 900 \\
&= ₹57{,}600
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Available Seat Kilometers (3)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ASK &= 36 \times 350 \times 2 \\
&= 12{,}600 \times 2 \\
&= 25{,}200 \text{ berth-km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Cost per ASK (4)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CASK &= \frac{48{,}000}{25{,}200} \\
&= ₹1.9048 \text{ per berth-km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Revenue per ASK (RASK) at 100% LF (5)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RASK_{\text{full}} &= \frac{36 \times 900}{36 \times 350} \\
&= \frac{32{,}400}{12{,}600} \\
&= ₹2.5714 \text{ per berth-km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8: Break-Even Load Factor**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LF_{BE} &= \frac{CASK}{RASK_{\text{full}}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{1.9048}{2.5714} \times 100\% \\
&= 74.07\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Conclusion:* Since the actual LF (88.89%) **exceeds** the break-even LF (74.07%), the bus operator is making a **profit of approximately ₹9,600** on this day. ✓

---

### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks) [CHOICE-2 / OR Option]**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PECBT861, Module 3]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **difference between passenger transportation services and vehicle/freight transportation services** with suitable examples. Discuss the **key performance indicators (KPIs)** used in each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A ride-hailing company (similar to **Ola or Uber**) has the following operational data for a day across the city of **Kochi**:
- Total registered vehicles on platform: **8,000**
- Active vehicles during the day: **5,500**
- Total trip requests received: **42,000**
- Trips successfully completed: **31,500**
- Average wait time: **4.2 minutes**
- Average trip distance: **8 km**
- Average fare per km: **₹15**

**Calculate:**
1. **Fleet Utilization Rate (FUR)**
2. **Trip Completion Ratio (TCR)**
3. **Total Passenger-Kilometers served** (assume 1 passenger per trip for simplicity)
4. **Total Revenue generated**
5. **Service Productivity** if the company employed **3,200 gig drivers** with a total wage cost of **₹64,00,000** for the day.

---

### **Model Answer — Question B**

#### (a) Model Answer [7 Marks]

**Passenger Transport (PT)** is a **People Processing Service**. Examples: KSRTC, IndiGo Airlines, Kochi Metro, Ola Cabs. The passenger is the raw material; they enter the service factory (vehicle) and are "processed" by being moved spatially. KPIs include:
- Load Factor (LF)
- On-Time Performance (OTP)
- Passenger Satisfaction Score (NPS)
- Average Wait Time
- Revenue per Passenger-Kilometre (Rev/PKM)

**Vehicle/Freight Transport (VT)** is a **Possession Processing Service**. Examples: Delhivery, TCI Freight, Maersk Shipping, Amazon Logistics. The customer's property is moved; the customer need not be present. KPIs include:
- Tonnage Utilization
- Delivery On-Time Rate
- Damage/Loss Rate
- Cost per Tonne-Kilometre
- Order Fulfillment Cycle Time

**Key Differences:**
1. **Customer Involvement:** Continuous in PT; periodic in VT (at booking and delivery).
2. **Capacity Unit:** Seats in PT; Tonnes/Volume in VT.
3. **Demand Pattern:** Rush-hour peaks in PT; seasonal/cyclical in VT.
4. **Primary Value Proposition:** Speed and comfort in PT; cost, reliability, and safety in VT.

---

#### (b) Model Answer [7 Marks — Step-by-Step]

**Given Data:**
- Total registered vehicles: 8,000
- Active vehicles: 5,500
- Total trip requests: 42,000
- Trips completed: 31,500
- Average trip distance: 8 km
- Average fare per km: ₹15
- Gig drivers employed: 3,200
- Daily wage cost: ₹64,00,000

**Step 1: Fleet Utilization Rate (1)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FUR &= \frac{\text{Active Vehicles}}{\text{Total Registered Vehicles}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{5{,}500}{8{,}000} \times 100\% \\
&= 68.75\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Interpretation:* Nearly 31% of registered vehicles were inactive — a potential revenue leak.

**Step 2: Trip Completion Ratio (2)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TCR &= \frac{\text{Trips Completed}}{\text{Trip Requests}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{31{,}500}{42{,}000} \times 100\% \\
&= 75.00\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Interpretation:* 25% of requests were not served — points to possible driver shortage or pricing issues.

**Step 3: Total Passenger-Kilometres (3)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PKM &= 31{,}500 \times 8 \\
&= 2{,}52{,}000 \text{ passenger-km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Total Revenue (4)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Revenue} &= 31{,}500 \times 8 \times 15 \\
&= 2{,}52{,}000 \times 15 \\
&= ₹37{,}80{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Service Productivity (5)**

Service Productivity = Output (PKM) / Labour Input Cost.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Productivity} &= \frac{2{,}52{,}000 \text{ PKM}}{₹64{,}00{,}000} \\
&= 0.0394 \text{ PKM per Rupee of labour cost}
\end{aligned}
$$

Or equivalently, **₹25.40 of revenue per ₹1 of labour cost** (₹37,80,000 / ₹64,00,000).

> *Conclusion:* The platform has healthy productivity, but **fleet activation (FUR improvement)** and **trip completion rate (TCR improvement)** are the two biggest levers for growth. Increasing FUR from 68.75% to 80% could add ~₹6.4 lakhs of daily revenue.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Examinations:**
>
> 1. **Confusing ASK and RPK:** Students often write RPK as "Revenue Passenger Kilometre" and forget the calculation is **Passengers × Distance**, not Revenue × Distance. RPK stands for **Revenue (or) Realized Passenger Kilometres**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the ×100%:** Load Factor is **always a percentage**. Writing 0.83 instead of 83% loses a mark.
>
> 3. **Mixing up CASK and RASK:** CASK uses cost in numerator; RASK uses revenue. Don't interchange.
>
> 4. **Not stating the units:** Always write "₹ per seat-km" or "passenger-km" — bare numbers lose clarity marks.
>
> 5. **Ignoring the time dimension:** "Per day" calculations should explicitly state they are **for a single day**. Examiners deduct marks for ambiguous "total" or "daily" mixing.
>
> 6. **Not defining variables:** Always start a calculation answer with a small "**Given:**" block listing the variables. This is a KTU valuation courtesy that earns method marks even if the final number is wrong.
>
> 7. **Skipping the cross-verification step:** When asked to calculate LF and ASK separately, always show that **RPK/ASK = LF**. Examiners reward this with a "method mark."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Quick-Revision Checklist — Read this 5 minutes before the exam!**

- ✅ Transportation services are classified as **People Processing (passenger)** or **Possession Processing (freight/vehicle)**.
- ✅ The **four pillars of service characteristics** are: Intangibility, Inseparability, Heterogeneity, Perishability.
- ✅ **Load Factor (LF)** = (Actual Passengers / Available Seats) × 100%. Healthy LF for long-distance transport ≥ 80%.
- ✅ **ASK (Available Seat Kilometres)** = Seats × Distance × Trips.
- ✅ **RPK (Revenue/Realized Passenger Kilometres)** = Passengers × Distance.
- ✅ **RASK** = Revenue / ASK ; **CASK** = Cost / ASK ; Profit/ASK = RASK − CASK.
- ✅ **On-Time Performance (OTP)** is a key reliability metric across all transport modes.
- ✅ **Vehicle Utilization Rate (VUR)** and **Fleet Availability (FA)** measure operational efficiency.
- ✅ **Break-Even LF** = CASK / RASK × 100% (no non-fare revenue case).
- ✅ **Service Quality in transport** adds two dimensions to SERVQUAL: **Safety** and **Accessibility**.
- ✅ **Dynamic pricing / Yield management** exists primarily to combat the **perishability** problem.
- ✅ Transportation services are inherently **simultaneous (production = consumption)** — this drives workforce and capacity decisions.
- ✅ Real-world examples to remember: **KSRTC, IndiGo, Kochi Metro, Ola, Uber, Delhivery, TCI, Maersk, Amazon Logistics**.
- ✅ **Last-mile connectivity** is the differentiator in modern urban transportation.
- ✅ For KTU numerical problems, always write: **Given → Formula → Step-by-step substitution → Result with units → Interpretation**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Techniques for Optimizing Vehicle Routes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT861 - SERVICE SCIENCE AND SERVICE OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Techniques for Optimizing Vehicle Routes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP)** is a combinatorial optimization and integer programming problem that seeks to determine the **optimal set of routes** to be undertaken by a fleet of vehicles stationed at one or multiple depots to serve a set of geographically dispersed customers, subject to a defined set of operational constraints.

Mathematically, VRP generalizes the classical **Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP)** by relaxing the single-vehicle constraint and incorporating real-world service parameters such as:
- **Vehicle capacity (Q)**
- **Route duration / time windows**
- **Service time (S_i)** at each customer node
- **Distance / Cost matrix (C_ij)**
- **Demand (d_i)** at each customer node

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under Module 3, the focus is on **heuristic and exact techniques** for route optimization, with emphasis on the **Clarke–Wright Savings Method**, the **Nearest Neighbor Heuristic**, the **Sweep Algorithm**, and modern **Metaheuristic** approaches. Students must understand both the mathematical formulation and the algorithmic flow.

> [!NOTE]
> **Service Operations Context:** VRP lies at the heart of last-mile delivery, field service management, e-commerce logistics, public transit, and emergency response — all of which are service-intensive environments where operational efficiency directly affects the **Service Value Equation** (Value = Results Produced / Customer Inputs).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Pizza Delivery Brain

Imagine a small-town pizza shop with **3 delivery boys and 12 customers** waiting for orders. Each boy can carry only a limited number of pizzas, and every customer must be served. The shop owner wants to:
1. Use the **fewest possible boys (vehicles)** to keep costs low.
2. Make sure no boy drives more than a certain distance (so pizzas stay hot).
3. Ensure every customer gets the order within a reasonable time.

The owner has two choices:
- **Brute force (Exact Method):** Try every possible grouping of customers into 3 routes — millions of combinations — and pick the cheapest. Works, but takes days.
- **Smart Shortcuts (Heuristics):** Start with a "sensible guess" (e.g., each customer gets their own dedicated delivery) and then **iteratively improve** it — merge routes that save the most distance. Fast, near-optimal, and works in seconds.

This is precisely the philosophy of **VRP optimization techniques**.

## 1.3 Classification of Vehicle Routing Techniques

> [!NOTE]
> **Taxonomy of VRP Solving Approaches**

| Category | Core Philosophy | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Exact Methods** | Guarantee optimal solution; computationally heavy | Branch & Bound, Integer Programming, Dynamic Programming |
| **Classical Heuristics** | Fast, near-optimal, constructive | Clarke–Wright Savings, Nearest Neighbor, Sweep Algorithm, Insertion Heuristic |
| **Metaheuristics** | Iterative improvement, escapes local optima | Genetic Algorithm, Simulated Annealing, Tabu Search, Ant Colony Optimization |
| **Hybrid / Two-Phase** | Cluster + Route | Fisher & Jaikumar Algorithm, Petal Algorithm |

---

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Bolded Constants)

- **Total Route Distance (Z)** — Sum of all edge distances in selected routes (in km).
- **Vehicle Utilization (ρ)** — Ratio of loaded capacity to maximum capacity (dimensionless).
- **Number of Vehicles (K)** — Cardinality of the active fleet.
- **Service Time (S_i)** — Dwell time at customer *i* (typically 10–15 min for delivery).
- **Maximum Route Duration (T_max)** — Hard upper bound on cumulative driving + service time.
- **Demand (d_i)** — Quantity requested by customer *i* (in units of product).
- **Capacity (Q)** — Maximum load a single vehicle can transport.

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundational Mathematical Model

A **Capacitated VRP (CVRP)** with a single depot can be formally stated as:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CVRP Mathematical Formulation (KTU Board Format)**

**Given:**
- A central depot indexed as node **0**.
- A set of customers $N = \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$.
- A fleet of $K$ identical vehicles, each of capacity $Q$.
- A non-negative distance/cost matrix $C_{ij}$ between every pair of nodes.
- Demand $d_i$ at each customer $i$.

**Decision Variable:**

$$x_{ijk} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if vehicle } k \text{ travels directly from node } i \text{ to node } j \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Objective Function (Minimize Total Cost):**

$$\min \; Z = \sum_{i=0}^{n} \sum_{j=0}^{n} \sum_{k=1}^{K} C_{ij} \cdot x_{ijk}$$

**Subject To:**

**(a) Each customer is visited exactly once:**

$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} \sum_{k=1}^{K} x_{ijk} = 1 \quad \forall \, i \in N$$

**(b) Flow Conservation (what enters must leave):**

$$\sum_{i=0}^{n} x_{ijk} = \sum_{i=0}^{n} x_{jik} \quad \forall \, j \in N, \, \forall \, k$$

**(c) Vehicle Capacity Constraint:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i \cdot \left( \sum_{j=0}^{n} x_{ijk} \right) \leq Q \quad \forall \, k \in \{1, \ldots, K\}$$

**(d) Sub-tour Elimination (Miller–Tucker–Zemlin constraint):**

$$u_i - u_j + Q \cdot \sum_{k=1}^{K} x_{ijk} \leq Q - d_j \quad \forall \, i, j \in N, \, i \neq j$$

## 2.2 Decomposition of Solution Techniques

### A. Clarke–Wright Savings Algorithm (Most Frequently Tested in KTU)

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Premise:** Begin with the "trivial" solution where every customer is served by a *dedicated* vehicle departing and returning to the depot. Then iteratively **merge pairs of routes** that yield the **highest "savings"** in total distance.

**The Core Savings Formula:**

$$S_{ij} = C_{0i} + C_{0j} - C_{ij}$$

**Where:**
- $S_{ij}$ = Savings achieved by linking customer $i$ and customer $j$ in the same route.
- $C_{0i}$ = Distance from depot (node 0) to customer $i$.
- $C_{0j}$ = Distance from depot (node 0) to customer $j$.
- $C_{ij}$ = Direct distance from customer $i$ to customer $j$.

**Why does this work intuitively?** If customers $i$ and $j$ are both far from the depot but close to each other, combining their trips avoids two long depot-to-customer legs, replacing them with one shorter leg.

### B. Nearest Neighbor Heuristic (NNH)

- Start at the depot, travel to the **closest unvisited customer**.
- From there, again pick the **nearest unvisited customer**.
- Return to the depot when no more customers can be added (capacity or distance limit reached).
- Repeat until all customers are served.

### C. Sweep Algorithm (Polar Coordinate Method)

- Place the depot at the origin of a polar coordinate system.
- "Sweep" a ray around the depot, ordering customers by their polar angle $\theta_i$.
- Sequentially assign customers to a route until the vehicle is full — then start a new route.
- Apply TSP on each cluster to optimize intra-route order.

### D. Metaheuristic Overview

| Metaheuristic | Inspiration | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Genetic Algorithm (GA)** | Darwinian Evolution | Population, Selection, Crossover, Mutation |
| **Simulated Annealing (SA)** | Metallurgy | Probabilistic acceptance of worse solutions, temperature schedule |
| **Tabu Search (TS)** | Memory-based search | Tabu list forbids re-visiting recent moves |
| **Ant Colony Optimization (ACO)** | Ant pheromone trails | Pheromone evaporation + reinforcement |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these before you walk into the exam hall.**

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Savings Value** | $S_{ij} = C_{0i} + C_{0j} - C_{ij}$ | Clarke–Wright merging decision |
| **Route Cost** | $Z_k = \sum_{(i,j) \in R_k} C_{ij}$ | Total cost of a specific route |
| **Total System Cost** | $Z = \sum_{k=1}^{K} Z_k$ | Aggregate fleet cost |
| **Vehicle Utilization** | $\rho = \frac{\sum_{i \in R_k} d_i}{Q}$ | Load efficiency on route $k$ |
| **Number of Vehicles (Initial)** | $K_0 = n$ | Trivial Clarke–Wright starting point |
| **Number of Vehicles (Optimal)** | $K^* \geq \lceil \sum d_i / Q \rceil$ | Theoretical lower bound |
| **Polar Angle** | $\theta_i = \arctan\left(\frac{y_i - y_0}{x_i - x_0}\right)$ | Sweep Algorithm ordering |
| **Time Window Compliance** | $E_i \leq A_i \leq L_i$ | VRPTW feasibility check |
| **Service Window Slack** | $\text{Slack}_i = L_i - (A_i + S_i)$ | Buffer time at customer $i$ |

> **Important Pipe-Safety Note:** All absolute-value or magnitude expressions above are written using $C_{ij}$ notation (non-pipe) to preserve markdown table integrity. For inline absolute values elsewhere, use $\lvert x \rvert$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Logistics & E-commerce:** Amazon, Flipkart, FedEx — route planning engines optimize millions of deliveries per day.
- **Field Service Management:** HVAC, telecom, utility companies dispatch technicians to minimize travel.
- **Public Transportation:** School bus routing, postal delivery cycles, garbage collection.
- **Healthcare:** Home healthcare nurse routing, blood bank distribution.
- **Disaster Response:** Dynamic VRP (DVRP) for emergency resource allocation.
- **Smart City Mobility:** Ride-pooling, autonomous vehicle fleet coordination.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Example

## 3.1 Worked Clarke–Wright Savings Algorithm — Exhaustive Walkthrough

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup (KTU-Style Board Question Variant):**
> A single depot (D) is located at the origin. There are **5 customers** with the following coordinates (in km): $C_1(2, 5)$, $C_2(6, 4)$, $C_3(7, -1)$, $C_4(3, -4)$, $C_5(-2, 3)$. Vehicle capacity $Q = 30$ units. Each customer demands $d_i = 10$ units. Compute the optimal routes using the **Clarke–Wright Savings Algorithm**.

### Step 1 — Compute the Distance Matrix $C_{ij}$

Using the **Euclidean distance formula** $C_{ij} = \sqrt{(x_i - x_j)^2 + (y_i - y_j)^2}$:

$$
C_{0,1} = \sqrt{(0-2)^2 + (0-5)^2} = \sqrt{4 + 25} = \sqrt{29} \approx 5.39
$$

$$
C_{0,2} = \sqrt{(0-6)^2 + (0-4)^2} = \sqrt{36 + 16} = \sqrt{52} \approx 7.21
$$

$$
C_{0,3} = \sqrt{(0-7)^2 + (0+1)^2} = \sqrt{49 + 1} = \sqrt{50} \approx 7.07
$$

$$
C_{0,4} = \sqrt{(0-3)^2 + (0+4)^2} = \sqrt{9 + 16} = \sqrt{25} = 5.00
$$

$$
C_{0,5} = \sqrt{(0+2)^2 + (0-3)^2} = \sqrt{4 + 9} = \sqrt{13} \approx 3.61
$$

$$
C_{1,2} = \sqrt{(2-6)^2 + (5-4)^2} = \sqrt{16 + 1} = \sqrt{17} \approx 4.12
$$

$$
C_{1,3} = \sqrt{(2-7)^2 + (5+1)^2} = \sqrt{25 + 36} = \sqrt{61} \approx 7.81
$$

$$
C_{1,4} = \sqrt{(2-3)^2 + (5+4)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 81} = \sqrt{82} \approx 9.06
$$

$$
C_{1,5} = \sqrt{(2+2)^2 + (5-3)^2} = \sqrt{16 + 4} = \sqrt{20} \approx 4.47
$$

$$
C_{2,3} = \sqrt{(6-7)^2 + (4+1)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 25} = \sqrt{26} \approx 5.10
$$

$$
C_{2,4} = \sqrt{(6-3)^2 + (4+4)^2} = \sqrt{9 + 64} = \sqrt{73} \approx 8.54
$$

$$
C_{2,5} = \sqrt{(6+2)^2 + (4-3)^2} = \sqrt{64 + 1} = \sqrt{65} \approx 8.06
$$

$$
C_{3,4} = \sqrt{(7-3)^2 + (-1+4)^2} = \sqrt{16 + 9} = \sqrt{25} = 5.00
$$

$$
C_{3,5} = \sqrt{(7+2)^2 + (-1-3)^2} = \sqrt{81 + 16} = \sqrt{97} \approx 9.85
$$

$$
C_{4,5} = \sqrt{(3+2)^2 + (-4-3)^2} = \sqrt{25 + 49} = \sqrt{74} \approx 8.60
$$

### Step 2 — Build the Savings Table $S_{ij} = C_{0i} + C_{0j} - C_{ij}$

$$
S_{1,2} = 5.39 + 7.21 - 4.12 = 8.48
$$

$$
S_{1,3} = 5.39 + 7.07 - 7.81 = 4.65
$$

$$
S_{1,4} = 5.39 + 5.00 - 9.06 = 1.33
$$

$$
S_{1,5} = 5.39 + 3.61 - 4.47 = 4.53
$$

$$
S_{2,3} = 7.21 + 7.07 - 5.10 = 9.18
$$

$$
S_{2,4} = 7.21 + 5.00 - 8.54 = 3.67
$$

$$
S_{2,5} = 7.21 + 3.61 - 8.06 = 2.76
$$

$$
S_{3,4} = 7.07 + 5.00 - 5.00 = 7.07
$$

$$
S_{3,5} = 7.07 + 3.61 - 9.85 = 0.83
$$

$$
S_{4,5} = 5.00 + 3.61 - 8.60 = 0.01
$$

### Step 3 — Rank Savings in Descending Order

| Rank | Pair $(i,j)$ | $S_{ij}$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | (2, 3) | **9.18** |
| 2 | (1, 2) | **8.48** |
| 3 | (3, 4) | **7.07** |
| 4 | (1, 3) | 4.65 |
| 5 | (1, 5) | 4.53 |
| 6 | (2, 4) | 3.67 |
| 7 | (2, 5) | 2.76 |
| 8 | (1, 4) | 1.33 |
| 9 | (3, 5) | 0.83 |
| 10 | (4, 5) | 0.01 |

### Step 4 — Iterative Route Construction (Capacity-Aware)

- **Initial state:** 5 separate routes — $D \to C_i \to D$ for each $i \in \{1,2,3,4,5\}$.
- **Initial total cost:** $Z_0 = 2 \cdot (5.39 + 7.21 + 7.07 + 5.00 + 3.61) = 2 \cdot 28.28 = 56.56$ km.

**Iteration 1 — Merge $(C_2, C_3)$, $S_{2,3} = 9.18$:** Combine $C_2$ and $C_3$ (demand = 20 ≤ 30 ✓). New route: $D \to C_2 \to C_3 \to D$.

$$
\text{Route cost} = C_{0,2} + C_{2,3} + C_{3,0} = 7.21 + 5.10 + 7.07 = 19.38
$$

**Iteration 2 — Merge $(C_1, C_2)$ OR $(C_1, C_2 \cup C_3)$:** Adding $C_1$ to the $C_2–C_3$ route demands $10+20 = 30$ units = $Q$ ✓ (exactly at capacity).

New route: $D \to C_1 \to C_2 \to C_3 \to D$.

$$
\text{Route cost} = C_{0,1} + C_{1,2} + C_{2,3} + C_{3,0} = 5.39 + 4.12 + 5.10 + 7.07 = 21.68
$$

> **Decision Check:** $C_1$ must be added as an *endpoint* (either before $C_2$ or after $C_3$ in the route). We choose $D \to C_1 \to C_2 \to C_3 \to D$.

**Iteration 3 — Merge $(C_3, C_4)$, $S_{3,4} = 7.07$:** Adding $C_4$ to existing route $D \to C_1 \to C_2 \to C_3 \to D$ would push demand to $40 > Q$. **REJECT — capacity violation.**

Hence $C_4$ must form a **new route** with some other small cluster.

**Iteration 4 — Pair $(C_4, C_5)$:** $S_{4,5} = 0.01$ is very low, but $C_4$ and $C_5$ are the only customers left. New route: $D \to C_4 \to C_5 \to D$.

$$
\text{Route cost} = C_{0,4} + C_{4,5} + C_{5,0} = 5.00 + 8.60 + 3.61 = 17.21
$$

### Step 5 — Final Solution Summary

| Route | Sequence | Cost (km) | Demand |
|---|---|---|---|
| Route 1 | $D \to C_1 \to C_2 \to C_3 \to D$ | 21.68 | 30 |
| Route 2 | $D \to C_4 \to C_5 \to D$ | 17.21 | 20 |
| **Total** | — | **38.89** | — |

**Total Savings vs. initial trivial solution:**

$$
\Delta Z = 56.56 - 38.89 = 17.67 \text{ km saved}
$$

**Percentage reduction in cost:**

$$
\% \text{ Reduction} = \frac{17.67}{56.56} \times 100 \approx 31.24\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vehicles used = 2**, which exactly matches the theoretical lower bound $K^* = \lceil \sum d_i / Q \rceil = \lceil 50/30 \rceil = 2$. The Clarke–Wright heuristic achieved the optimal fleet count in this instance.

## 3.2 Nearest Neighbor Walkthrough (Quick Variant)

Starting at depot $D$:

1. $D \to C_5$ (distance $3.61$ — closest to $D$).
2. From $C_5$, nearest unvisited = $C_1$ (distance $4.47$).
3. From $C_1$, nearest unvisited = $C_2$ (distance $4.12$).
4. From $C_2$, nearest unvisited = $C_3$ (distance $5.10$).
5. From $C_3$, nearest unvisited = $C_4$ (distance $5.00$).
6. $C_4 \to D$ (distance $5.00$).

**Total NNH cost:**

$$
Z_{NNH} = 3.61 + 4.47 + 4.12 + 5.10 + 5.00 + 5.00 = 27.30 \text{ km}
$$

**Single vehicle (TSP-style route):** $D \to C_5 \to C_1 \to C_2 \to C_3 \to C_4 \to D$.

> **Comparison:** NNH yields a single long route of 27.30 km; Clarke–Wright yields 2 shorter routes totaling 38.89 km but uses 2 vehicles. The "best" depends on the **cost-of-vehicles vs. cost-of-distance** trade-off — a classic service operations design decision.

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **When to Use What — KTU Decision Framework**

| Technique | Solution Quality | Computational Cost | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Clarke–Wright Savings** | High (80–95% of optimum) | Low | Static, depot-based, balanced demand |
| **Nearest Neighbor** | Moderate (70–85%) | Very Low | Quick estimates, dynamic real-time |
| **Sweep Algorithm** | High | Low | Geographic clusters, planar layouts |
| **Insertion Heuristic** | High | Moderate | Time-windowed problems |
| **Genetic Algorithm** | Near-Optimal (90–99%) | High | Large-scale, complex constraints |
| **Simulated Annealing** | Near-Optimal | High | Avoiding local optima |
| **Tabu Search** | Near-Optimal | Moderate-High | Combinatorial landscapes |
| **Exact (B&B)** | **Optimal** | Exponential | Small instances (n < 50) |

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Input All Customers n] --> B[Initialize n trivial routes D to Ci to D]
    B --> C[Compute Savings Sij for all pairs]
    C --> D[Sort Savings in Descending Order]
    D --> E[Pick Highest Savings Pair]
    E --> F{Capacity Constraint OK?}
    F -- No --> G[Skip this pair]
    G --> H{More pairs left?}
    H -- Yes --> E

    K -- Yes --> G
    K -- No --> L[Update solution, deduct savings from cost]

    M -- Yes --> Z
```
